| 8 | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | $0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{Cash Flow}_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} - \text{Investment}$ | % | ESCO financing |
| 9 | Energy Use Intensity (EUI) | $\text{EUI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Floor Area}}$ | kWh/m²/year | Building benchmarking |
| 10 | Power Factor Correction Savings | $\Delta C_{\text{PF}} = P \times ( \tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2 ) \times h \times C_k$ | ₹/year | PFC capacitor sizing |
| 11 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{steam}} \times (h_{\text{steam}} - h_{\text{feedwater}})}{m_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{GCV}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | % | Thermal audit |
| 12 | Electrical Load Factor | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}} \times 100$ | % | Demand analysis |
| 13 | CO₂ Emission Reduction | $\Delta \text{CO}_2 = E_{\text{saved}} \times 0.82$ | kg CO₂/year | Environmental impact |
| 14 | Demand-Side Management (DSM) Ratio | $\text{DSM Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Peak Cut}}{\text{Old Peak}}$ | % | Load management |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** When writing absolute value or modulus in your exam sheet, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — **never** write $\lvert x \rvert$ with literal pipes, as it breaks markdown table rendering. This is a KTU answer script formatting trap.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ESCO (Energy Service Company) Industry:** All ESCO projects originate from a Type 2/3 audit. The ECMs identified are financed, implemented, and the savings shared between ESCO and client.
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme:** BEE assigns SEC targets to DCs. Energy audits establish the baseline SEC against which compliance is measured. Under-performing units buy **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**.
- **Green Building Ratings:** IGBC, GRIHA, LEED certifications all require a baseline energy audit as a prerequisite.
- **Carbon Credit Markets:** Audited, verified, M&V-confirmed savings are the only acceptable evidence for monetizing carbon credits.
- **Industrial IoT & Digital Twins:** Modern audits feed into digital twins for continuous optimization beyond the initial audit window.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) — The Core Benchmarking Metric

**Given:**
- A cement plant produces $P = 1{,}000$ tonnes of clinker per day
- Total electrical energy consumed in 30 days = $E_{\text{month}} = 6{,}00{,}000$ kWh
- Total thermal energy consumed in 30 days (in coal terms) = $E_{\text{thermal}} = 1{,}500$ tonnes of coal
- GCV of coal = $5{,}500$ kcal/kg
- Plant operates 30 days × 24 hours = 720 hours/month

**Step 1 — Convert thermal energy into common energy unit (kcal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{thermal, kcal}} &= \text{Mass of coal} \times \text{GCV} \\
&= 1{,}500 \times 1000 \text{ kg} \times 5{,}500 \text{ kcal/kg} \\
&= 8.25 \times 10^9 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert electrical energy into kcal (using 1 kWh = 860 kcal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{electrical, kcal}} &= 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 860 \text{ kcal/kWh} \\
&= 5.16 \times 10^8 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy in kcal:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total, kcal}} &= E_{\text{thermal}} + E_{\text{electrical}} \\
&= 8.25 \times 10^9 + 5.16 \times 10^8 \\
&= 8.766 \times 10^9 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total production:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 1{,}000 \text{ t/day} \times 30 \text{ days} \\
&= 30{,}000 \text{ tonnes of clinker}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute SEC (kcal per tonne of clinker):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SEC} &= \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{total}}} \\
&= \dfrac{8.766 \times 10^9 \text{ kcal}}{30{,}000 \text{ t}} \\
&= 2.922 \times 10^5 \text{ kcal/t} \\
&= 292{,}200 \text{ kcal/tonne of clinker}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to GJ/tonne (1 kcal = 4.186 × 10⁻⁶ GJ):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SEC (GJ/t)} &= 2.922 \times 10^5 \times 4.186 \times 10^{-6} \\
&\approx 1.223 \text{ GJ/tonne of clinker}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Indian cement industry benchmark is **3.0 – 3.4 GJ/tonne** for thermal + electrical combined. Our plant at **1.223 GJ/t** is in the best-in-class range — indicating either an exceptionally efficient plant OR missing baseline data. Audit cross-verification needed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Simple Payback Period (SPP) and ROI for an ECM

**Given:**
- Proposed ECM: Replace 50 existing standard-efficiency motors (η₁ = 85%) with IE3 premium-efficiency motors (η₂ = 90%)
- Total connected motor load = $P_{\text{connected}} = 500$ kW
- Average load factor = 0.7, Operating hours = 6000 h/year
- Tariff = ₹8/kWh
- Cost per IE3 motor (including rewiring, mounting) = ₹35,000
- Old motor scrap value = ₹5,000 each (residual salvage)

**Step 1 — Calculate original annual energy consumption (with standard motors):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg, old}} &= P_{\text{connected}} \times \text{LF} \\
&= 500 \times 0.7 = 350 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{input, old}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{avg, old}}}{\eta_1} = \dfrac{350}{0.85} = 411.76 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{old, annual}} &= 411.76 \times 6000 = 24.706 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate new annual energy consumption (with IE3 motors):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{input, new}} &= \dfrac{350}{0.90} = 388.89 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{new, annual}} &= 388.89 \times 6000 = 23.333 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate annual energy saved:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{saved}} &= E_{\text{old}} - E_{\text{new}} \\
&= 24.706 \times 10^5 - 23.333 \times 10^5 \\
&= 1.373 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/year} \\
&\approx 1.373 \text{ lakh kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate annual monetary savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= E_{\text{saved}} \times \text{Tariff} \\
&= 1.373 \times 10^5 \times 8 \\
&= \text{₹ } 10.984 \text{ lakh/year} \approx \text{₹ } 10.98 \text{ lakhs/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate total net investment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{total}} &= (50 \times 35{,}000) - (50 \times 5{,}000) \\
&= 17.5 \text{ lakhs} - 2.5 \text{ lakhs} \\
&= \text{₹ } 15 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Simple Payback Period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPP} &= \dfrac{I_{\text{total}}}{\Delta C} \\
&= \dfrac{15 \text{ lakhs}}{10.98 \text{ lakhs/year}} \\
&\approx 1.366 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate ROI (% per year):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{\Delta C}{I_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{10.98}{15} \times 100 \\
&\approx 73.2 \% \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** SPP < 2 years and ROI > 70% classifies this as a **"Must-Implement"** ECM under standard ESCO acceptance criteria (typical threshold: SPP ≤ 3 years, ROI ≥ 30%).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Factor Correction — Annual Savings Calculation

**Given:**
- Plant average active power = $P = 800$ kW
- Existing power factor = $\cos \phi_1 = 0.78$
- Target power factor after correction = $\cos \phi_2 = 0.95$
- Tariff = ₹7.5/kWh
- Operating hours = 5000 h/year

**Step 1 — Compute reactive power before and after correction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \phi_1 &= \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.78)) = \tan(38.7°) \approx 0.8023 \\
\tan \phi_2 &= \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.95)) = \tan(18.2°) \approx 0.3287
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute kVAR required for compensation (capacitor rating):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{c}} &= P \times (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2) \\
&= 800 \times (0.8023 - 0.3287) \\
&= 800 \times 0.4736 \\
&= 378.88 \text{ kVAR (capacitor bank size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the reduced apparent power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \dfrac{P}{\cos \phi_1} = \dfrac{800}{0.78} = 1025.64 \text{ kVA} \\
S_2 &= \dfrac{P}{\cos \phi_2} = \dfrac{800}{0.95} = 842.11 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute kVA reduction (which avoids demand charges):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &= S_1 - S_2 \\
&= 1025.64 - 842.11 = 183.53 \text{ kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute annual savings (assuming ₹150/kVA/month demand charge):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \Delta S \times 150 \times 12 \\
&= 183.53 \times 150 \times 12 \\
&\approx \text{₹ } 3.30 \text{ lakhs/year (in demand charges alone)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute payback on capacitor cost (assume ₹800/kVAR installed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 378.88 \times 800 = \text{₹ } 3.03 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{SPP} &= \dfrac{3.03}{3.30} \approx 0.92 \text{ years} \approx 11 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Power Factor Correction is one of the most frequently asked "numerical" in KTU Energy Management papers. Always show both the kVAR formula derivation and the payback calculation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Energy Audit Methodologies Side-by-Side

| Parameter | Walk-Through (Level 1) | Detailed (Level 2) | Investment-Grade (Level 3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Audit Team Size** | 1–2 auditors | 3–6 auditors + specialists | 6–15 + M&V experts |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days on-site | 2–6 weeks on-site | 1–3 months on-site |
| **Instruments Used** | Visual + basic clamp meter | Power analyzer, thermography, flue gas, lux meter | All L2 + vibration, harmonics, dynamic modeling software |
| **Data Collection** | Utility bills + spot checks | Continuous logged data (1–4 weeks) | Multi-month, multi-season data |
| **ECM Identification Depth** | Quick wins, obvious wastes | Quantified, technically verified | Bankable, vendor-priced, M&V-planned |
| **Cost-Benefit** | Order-of-magnitude | ±10%–20% accurate | ±5%–10% accurate |
| **Investment Cost (Approx.)** | ₹25K – ₹1L | ₹2L – ₹10L | ₹5L – ₹25L |
| **Output Document** | 5–15 page report | 30–80 page report | 100+ page report with appendices |
| **Best Application** | Initial screening, M&V support | Most industrial / commercial audits | ESCO projects, large retrofits |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks):** Define an Energy Audit. List any four needs for conducting an energy audit in an industrial facility.

**Model Answer:**

An Energy Audit is a systematic, planned, and documented procedure to obtain information on the energy consumption profile of a system, identify energy conservation opportunities (ECMs), and recommend technically and economically viable energy-saving measures with quantified savings, investment, and payback.

**Four key needs:**

1. **Economic Need:** Energy accounts for 5–15% of operating cost in manufacturing; reducing it improves profit margins directly.
2. **Regulatory Need:** Compliance with the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** and the **PAT (Perform-Achieve-Trade)** scheme under BEE.
3. **Environmental Need:** Each kWh saved avoids ~**0.82 kg CO₂** emission, supporting ESG and carbon neutrality goals.
4. **Operational Need:** Identifies hidden wastes (motor over-loading, compressed air leaks, idle equipment) that compromise efficiency.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1 mark, Each need = 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks):** Differentiate between Walk-Through Audit and Detailed Energy Audit.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Walk-Through (Level 1) | Detailed (Level 2) |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| Accuracy of savings | ±30%–40% | ±10%–20% |
| Instruments | Visual + basic clamp meter | Calibrated power analyzer, flue gas, IR camera, lux meter |
| Data | Utility bills + spot checks | Continuous logged data (1–4 weeks) |
| Output | List of quick wins | Quantified ECMs with full cost-benefit |

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Regulation:** *"Answer any one full question from each module. Each full question carries 14 marks, with internal choice between sub-parts (a) and (b) where applicable."*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Comprehensive Energy Audit Methodology

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

Explain in detail the various **types of energy audits** as per **ASHRAE classification**. Compare their scope, methodology, duration, and typical output accuracy.

**Model Answer:**

**ASHRAE classifies energy audits into four progressive levels:**

**Level 0 — Benchmarking Audit:**
- **Scope:** Compare facility's Energy Use Intensity (EUI in kWh/m²/year) against peer facilities using ENERGY STAR, CBECS, or CII benchmarking databases.
- **Methodology:** Compile monthly utility bills for 12 months; compute EUI; compare statistically.
- **Duration:** 1–2 days (mostly desk work)
- **Output Accuracy:** ±50% — only identifies whether a deeper audit is justified.
- **Use Case:** Initial screening for large building portfolios (banks, hotels, IT parks).

**Level 1 — Walk-Through Audit:**
- **Scope:** Visual + spot-check survey of all energy systems. Identify "low-hanging fruit."
- **Methodology:** On-site walk-through with checklists, operator interviews, basic spot measurements (ampere, voltage, surface temperature, illumination).
- **Duration:** 1–3 days on-site
- **Output Accuracy:** ±30%–40% for identified savings
- **Use Case:** Quick ECM identification, deciding if Level 2 is needed.

**Level 2 — Detailed Energy Survey (Audit):**
- **Scope:** Full quantification of all energy systems with measured data over a representative period.
- **Methodology:** Deploy calibrated instruments (power quality analyzers, flue gas analyzers, ultrasonic leak detectors, data loggers) for 1–4 weeks. Construct detailed energy balance (Sankey diagram). Build computer models where needed.
- **Duration:** 2–6 weeks on-site + 2–3 weeks analysis
- **Output Accuracy:** ±10%–20% — sufficient for most industrial decisions.
- **Use Case:** Standard industrial energy audits, PAT compliance, ISO 50001 baseline.

**Level 3 — Investment-Grade Audit:**
- **Scope:** Bankable, vendor-priced ECMs with full financial modeling for major capital projects.
- **Methodology:** All Level 2 activities + dynamic building/process simulation (eQUEST, EnergyPlus, ASPEN), vendor quotes, IPMVP-compliant M&V plan, financial modeling (NPV, IRR, lifecycle cost).
- **Duration:** 1–3 months
- **Output Accuracy:** ±5%–10% — financial-grade
- **Use Case:** ESCO projects, large retrofits, capex proposals to board/bankers.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Feature | Level 0 | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Depth | Benchmark | Walk-through | Detailed | Investment-grade |
| Cost | < ₹25K | ₹25K–1L | ₹2L–10L | ₹5L–25L |
| Accuracy | ±50% | ±30%–40% | ±10%–20% | ±5%–10% |
| Decision Support | Need for audit? | What to study? | Which ECMs to do? | How to finance? |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

Discuss the **need for energy audit** under six categories. List the **common instruments** used during a detailed energy audit and the parameter each measures.

**Model Answer:**

**Need for Energy Audit (6 categories):**

1. **Economic Need** — Reduce energy bills (often 5–15% of operating cost).
2. **Regulatory Need** — Energy Conservation Act 2001, BEE notifications, PAT cycle compliance.
3. **Environmental Need** — CO₂ reduction, carbon credits, ESG, sustainability reporting.
4. **Operational Need** — Identify equipment inefficiency, downtime, hidden losses.
5. **Strategic Need** — Capacity creation (avoid new transformer/boiler purchase), competitive edge via lower unit cost.
6. **Social / Legal Need** — Demonstrate corporate responsibility, comply with pollution control norms, maintain license to operate.

**Common Instruments Used in Detailed Energy Audit:**

| Instrument | Parameter Measured | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Power Analyzer (3-phase) | V, I, kW, kVA, kVAR, PF, harmonics | Motor and transformer loading |
| Flue Gas Analyzer | O₂, CO, CO₂, NOₓ, flue temperature | Boiler efficiency |
| Ultrasonic Leak Detector | High-frequency sound of leaks | Compressed air / steam leak detection |
| IR Thermometer / Camera | Surface temperature (non-contact) | Insulation gaps, electrical hot spots |
| Lux Meter | Illumination (lux) | Lighting audit |
| Tachometer | Rotational speed (rpm) | Motor / fan speed verification |
| Combustion Analyzer | Excess air, stack loss | Furnace tuning |
| Manometer / Pitot Tube | Pressure, velocity | Air / gas flow measurement |
| Vane Anemometer | Air velocity | HVAC ducts |
| Energy Management System (EMS) | Real-time kWh logging | Continuous monitoring |
| Data Logger | Multi-channel analog/digital logging | Long-term load profiling |
| Pressure Gauge (calibrated) | Steam / air / water pressure | Distribution losses |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Energy Audit Questions:**
> 1. **Do not write a generic essay.** Always structure with clear **headings** (Need, Types, Methodology, Instruments). Examiners scan for keywords first.
> 2. **Never miss a comparison table** in "Differentiate" questions. A missing table = ~2 mark loss even if the prose is correct.
> 3. **Avoid mixing up ASHRAE levels with the old "Preliminary/Detailed/Investment-grade"** classification. Either can be asked — be ready with both.
> 4. **For numerical questions, always write units** (kWh, ₹, years, %). Missing units = 0.5 mark lost per step.
> 5. **Don't confuse "energy conservation" and "energy efficiency"** — they are related but distinct. Conservation = behavioral/operational, Efficiency = technological.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** Energy Audit Economics

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A factory operates 50 induction motors each of 15 kW rating, currently running at an average **80% loading** with motor **efficiency 88%**. The plant operates **6000 hours/year** at an **electricity tariff of ₹7.50/kWh**. The management is evaluating replacement with **IE3 premium efficiency motors (efficiency 92%)** at a cost of **₹22,000 per motor** (installation included) and scrap value of old motors at **₹4,000 each**.

Calculate: **(i)** Annual energy saved, **(ii)** Annual monetary savings, **(iii)** Simple Payback Period, and **(iv)** ROI.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute average output power per motor and total connected output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{out, each}} &= 15 \text{ kW} \times 0.80 = 12 \text{ kW} \\
P_{\text{out, total}} &= 12 \times 50 = 600 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute input power (electrical drawn) with old motors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{in, old, each}} &= \dfrac{12}{0.88} = 13.636 \text{ kW} \\
P_{\text{in, old, total}} &= 13.636 \times 50 = 681.82 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy consumption with old motors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{old}} &= 681.82 \times 6000 = 40.909 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Input power with new IE3 motors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{in, new, each}} &= \dfrac{12}{0.92} = 13.043 \text{ kW} \\
P_{\text{in, new, total}} &= 13.043 \times 50 = 652.17 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy consumption with new motors:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{new}} &= 652.17 \times 6000 = 39.130 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Annual energy saved (Answer to (i)):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{saved}} &= E_{\text{old}} - E_{\text{new}} \\
&= 40.909 \times 10^5 - 39.130 \times 10^5 \\
&= 1.779 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/year} \\
&\approx 1.78 \text{ lakh kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Annual monetary savings (Answer to (ii)):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= 1.779 \times 10^5 \times 7.50 \\
&\approx \text{₹ } 13.34 \text{ lakhs/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Net investment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{net}} &= (50 \times 22{,}000) - (50 \times 4{,}000) \\
&= 11{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 9{,}00{,}000 \quad (= \text{₹ } 9 \text{ lakhs})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Simple Payback Period (Answer to (iii)):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPP} &= \dfrac{I_{\text{net}}}{\Delta C} = \dfrac{9}{13.34} \\
&\approx 0.675 \text{ years} \\
&\approx 8.1 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Return on Investment (Answer to (iv)):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{13.34}{9} \times 100 \\
&\approx 148.2\% \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** With SPP ≈ 8 months and ROI > 148%, this motor replacement is an **unambiguous "must-implement" ECM** — one of the most profitable energy conservation measures in industry.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

Explain the **methodology of conducting a detailed energy audit** in a manufacturing industry. List the **key steps** and describe what happens in each step.

**Model Answer:**

The detailed (Level 2) energy audit methodology for a manufacturing industry follows **eight key steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Planning & Scope Definition**
   - Define system boundary (entire plant / one process / one building).
   - Identify audit objectives (cost reduction? PAT compliance? ISO 50001?).
   - Form audit team (electrical, mechanical, thermal, instrumentation engineers).
   - Set timeline and budget.

2. **Step 2 — Historical Data Collection**
   - Collect 12–24 months of electricity, fuel, and water bills.
   - Gather production data (units/month, product mix, operating shifts).
   - Obtain equipment list with nameplate ratings, ages, efficiency.
   - Review past audits, if any.

3. **Step 3 — Site Walk-Through & Preliminary Inspection**
   - Physical inspection of all energy systems.
   - Interview operators, maintenance crew, and supervisors.
   - Identify obvious wastages: leaks, idle running, over-illumination, etc.
   - Sketch process flow diagram (PFD) and single-line diagram (SLD).

4. **Step 4 — Detailed Measurements & Monitoring**
   - Install calibrated power analyzers on major feeders (1–4 weeks).
   - Conduct flue gas analysis on all boilers/furnaces.
   - Ultrasonic leak detection on compressed air / steam lines.
   - Lux mapping in all illuminated areas.
   - Measure flow rates of steam, water, compressed air.
   - Monitor across all three shifts to capture variability.

5. **Step 5 — Data Analysis & Energy Balance**
   - Tabulate all measurements; compute averages, peaks, load factors.
   - Construct **Sankey diagram** showing energy flows and losses.
   - Calculate **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for each product/process.
   - Compare SEC with industry benchmarks (CII, BEE, ISO).
   - Apply **Pareto Analysis** (80/20 rule) to identify biggest loss areas.

6. **Step 6 — Identification of ECMs**
   - Brainstorm ECMs in each loss area.
   - Categorize as: **No-Cost, Low-Cost, Medium-Cost, High-Cost.**
   - Quantify each ECM: energy saved (kWh/year or fuel/year), cost saved (₹/year), investment (₹), **Simple Payback Period**, ROI.

7. **Step 7 — Economic Analysis & Prioritization**
   - Rank ECMs by SPP (lowest first).
   - For major ECMs, compute **NPV** and **IRR** over equipment life.
   - Identify ECMs suitable for **ESCO financing** (typically SPP 1–5 years).
   - Prepare implementation roadmap with quick-wins (0–3 months) and long-term projects (3–24 months).

8. **Step 8 — Report Submission & Implementation Support**
   - Submit comprehensive report with executive summary, methodology, energy balance, ECM list with cost-benefit, and implementation schedule.
   - Present to top management for approval and budget allocation.
   - Provide technical support during implementation.
   - Design **M&V protocol** (IPMVP Option A/B/C/D) for verifying post-implementation savings.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Quick Recall**

- ✅ **Energy Audit Definition** (BEE/ISO 50002 standard) — Memorize word-for-word for 1-mark questions.
- ✅ **Types of Audits:** Preliminary / Walk-Through / Detailed / Investment-Grade / Targeted — know all 5.
- ✅ **ASHRAE 4 Levels:** Level 0 Benchmarking, Level 1 Walk-Through, Level 2 Detailed, Level 3 Investment-Grade.
- ✅ **Need for Audit:** 6 categories — Economic, Regulatory, Environmental, Operational, Strategic, Social.
- ✅ **Audit Phases:** Planning → Data Collection → Walk-Through → Measurement → Analysis → ECM ID → Cost-Benefit → Report → M&V.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** SEC, SPP, ROI, NPV, IRR, EUI, kVAR for PFC, Boiler efficiency — all in Section 2.4 cheat sheet.
- ✅ **BEE:** Bureau of Energy Efficiency, Government of India body implementing Energy Conservation Act 2001.
- ✅ **PAT Scheme:** Perform-Achieve-Trade — energy efficiency trading mechanism for Designated Consumers.
- ✅ **Designated Consumer:** Industry consuming ≥ 1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) — mandatory BEE audit.
- ✅ **ESCO:** Energy Service Company — finances ECMs from guaranteed savings share.
- ✅ **IPMVP:** International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol — 4 Options A, B, C, D.
- ✅ **M&V:** Measurement & Verification — confirms actual energy savings post-implementation.
- ✅ **Sankey Diagram:** Visual representation of energy flows, showing all losses proportionally by arrow width.
- ✅ **Pareto Analysis:** 80% of energy loss comes from 20% of equipment — focus your ECMs there.
- ✅ **PF Correction:** Always quote both kVAR formula and payback period in exams.
- ✅ **Standard Conversions:** 1 kWh = 860 kcal = 3.6 MJ; 1 TOE = 11.63 MWh; CO₂ factor = 0.82 kg/kWh (India grid).
- ✅ **Tariff Components:** Energy charge (₹/kWh) + Fixed/Demand charge (₹/kVA/month) + PF penalty/incentive.
- ✅ **M&V Option A:** Retrofit isolation (key parameter measurement, savings by engineering calculation).
- ✅ **M&V Option B:** Retrofit isolation (all parameters measured).
- ✅ **M&V Option C:** Whole facility (utility bill analysis).
- ✅ **M&V Option D:** Calibrated simulation.
- ✅ **Common ECMs in industry:** VFD on motors, IE3/IE4 motors, LED lighting, VSD on pumps/fans, boiler waste heat recovery, compressor heat recovery, PFC, steam trap replacement, HVAC optimization, process heat integration (pinch analysis).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning — Do NOT skip these in the answer sheet:**
> 1. Always include **units** in numerical answers — marks are deducted for missing units.
> 2. **Tabulate** every comparison (types of audit, audit instruments, audit phases). Tabulated answers = +2 marks grace.
> 3. Write **definitions from standards** (BEE/ISO 50002/ASHRAE) — not informal textbook summaries.
> 4. For "differentiate" questions, a **side-by-side table** is mandatory.
> 5. For numerical questions, **show all intermediate steps** — even if you compute mentally, write them down.
> 6. Conclude every Part B question with a **practical engineering verdict** ("Must-implement / Marginal / Reject") — this demonstrates professional judgment and earns extra credit.
> 7. **Time allocation in 14-mark question:** ~3 minutes for planning, ~22 minutes for writing, ~2 minutes for review.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruments for Energy Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruments for Energy Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Audit Instruments** are precision-calibrated measuring devices employed during the **data collection and monitoring phase** of an energy audit to quantify physical parameters such as voltage, current, power, temperature, flow, pressure, light intensity, and flue gas composition. These instruments form the **empirical foundation** of any energy audit by translating abstract energy flows into measurable, verifiable, and actionable engineering data.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEEET742 - Energy Management and Auditing**, the term broadly encompasses:

- **Portable diagnostic instruments** used for walk-through and detailed audits.
- **Online monitoring instruments** used for continuous energy management systems (EnMS).
- **Calculating instruments** used to derive energy performance indicators (EnPIs) from raw measurements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Hospital Diagnostic Lab Analogy:**
> Just as a cardiologist uses an **ECG machine**, **BP monitor**, and **stethoscope** to diagnose the *health* of a human heart — an energy auditor uses a **power analyzer**, **lux meter**, and **flue gas analyzer** to diagnose the *health* of an industrial energy system. Without these instruments, the auditor would only be **guessing** instead of measuring, and the recommendations would lack scientific credibility. **You cannot manage what you cannot measure.**

### 1.3 Significance in the Energy Audit Pyramid

Energy audit instruments are positioned at the **bottom of the data hierarchy** but carry the **highest weight** in terms of audit accuracy. The complete audit pyramid is:

1. **Raw Measurements** (collected by instruments)
2. **Derived Parameters** (calculated from raw data)
3. **Energy Performance Indicators** (EnPIs)
4. **Benchmarks & Targets**
5. **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)**

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule of Energy Auditing:** Every recommendation made in the final report must be traceable back to a **measurement** taken with a calibrated instrument. Estimates are allowed only when measurements are impossible (e.g., long-term seasonal data).

### 1.4 Primary Objectives of Using Audit Instruments

- **Quantification** of energy flows in kWh, MJ, kCal, or GJ.
- **Identification** of wasteful patterns (idle running, leaks, harmonics).
- **Validation** of design data vs. actual operating conditions.
- **Verification** of post-implementation savings (M&V — Measurement & Verification).
- **Compliance** with ISO 50001 energy management system requirements.

---

### 1.5 Broad Classification of Energy Audit Instruments

The instruments can be classified into **six major functional categories**:

| Sl. No. | Category | Primary Parameters Measured | Typical Instruments |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1** | **Electrical Parameter Measurement** | V, I, kW, kVA, kVAr, PF, Hz, kWh | Power Analyzer, Multimeter, Clamp Meter |
| **2** | **Thermal Parameter Measurement** | Temperature, Heat Flux, Surface Temp | Digital Thermometer, IR Thermometer, Pyrometer |
| **3** | **Fluid & Flow Measurement** | Velocity, Volume, Mass Flow, ΔP | Anemometer, Ultrasonic Flowmeter, Pitot Tube |
| **4** | **Combustion & Flue Gas Analysis** | O₂, CO₂, CO, NOₓ, SO₂, Stack Temp | Flue Gas Analyzer, Combustion Efficiency Meter |
| **5** | **Lighting Measurement** | Illuminance, Luminance, Lux | Lux Meter, Integrating Sphere |
| **6** | **Mechanical / Rotational Measurement** | RPM, Vibration, Displacement | Tachometer, Stroboscope, Vibration Analyzer |

> [!NOTE]
> A modern energy audit typically requires **at least 8–12 different instruments** to comprehensively cover all energy domains (electrical, thermal, mechanical, lighting).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Working Principles of Major Instruments

#### A. Electrical Measurement Instruments

**1. Power Analyzer (3-Phase Electronic Energy Meter)**

A **power analyzer** is the most important instrument in any electrical energy audit. It simultaneously measures **voltage, current, active power (P), reactive power (Q), apparent power (S), power factor (PF), frequency, and harmonic distortion (THD)** across all three phases.

- **Working Principle:** Uses **shunt resistors** or **current transformers (CTs)** for current sensing and **voltage divider networks** for voltage sensing. The instantaneous voltage and current signals are sampled at high frequency (typically 2–10 kHz) using **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)**, and digital signal processing computes the RMS values and power components.
- **CT Ratio (K_c):** Real current $I_{actual} = I_{measured} \times K_c$ where $K_c = \text{Primary Turns} / \text{Secondary Turns}$
- **PT Ratio (K_v):** Real voltage $V_{actual} = V_{measured} \times K_v$
- **Why three-phase?** Industrial loads are predominantly **three-phase**, and unbalanced loads require **per-phase analysis**.

**2. Clamp Meter (Current Clamp)**

- **Working Principle:** Uses the **magnetic field sensing** principle. The jaws of the clamp encircle a current-carrying conductor, and a Hall-effect sensor or current transformer inside detects the magnetic flux proportional to current.
- **Key Advantage:** **Non-contact** measurement — the circuit does not need to be broken, making it ideal for live measurements on busbars and cables.

**3. Harmonic Analyzer**

- **Working Principle:** Performs **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** on the sampled voltage and current waveforms to decompose them into **fundamental (50/60 Hz)** and **harmonic components** (3rd, 5th, 7th, …).
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** The ratio of RMS harmonic content to RMS fundamental.
- **Significance:** Harmonics cause **additional losses** ($I^2R$ losses increase), **false tripping** of breakers, and **neutral conductor overheating**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neutral Conductor Overheating:** In a balanced 3-phase system, the fundamental components cancel in the neutral. However, **triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th, …)** are **additive** in the neutral, causing the neutral current to be **up to 1.73 times the phase current** in worst cases.

**4. Power Quality Analyzer (PQA)**

- **Working Principle:** Continuously monitors **sag, swell, transients, flicker, and unbalance** in voltage and current.
- **Capability:** Records **waveform events** over time and exports data for **PQ compliance reporting** as per IEEE 519 or IEC 61000 standards.

---

#### B. Thermal Measurement Instruments

**1. Contact Thermometer (Thermocouple / RTD based)**

- **Working Principle:** A **thermocouple** generates a small EMF (millivolts) based on the **Seebeck Effect** when two dissimilar metals are joined at a hot junction. The EMF is correlated with temperature via standard reference tables (e.g., Type K, Type J).
- **RTD Principle:** A Resistance Temperature Detector uses a **platinum (Pt100/Pt1000)** element whose resistance varies linearly with temperature: $R_t = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$.

**2. Infrared (IR) Thermometer / Pyrometer**

- **Working Principle:** All objects above **absolute zero (0 K)** emit **infrared radiation** per the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. The IR thermometer focuses this radiation onto a **thermopile detector** which converts IR energy into an electrical signal proportional to the **4th power of absolute temperature**.
- **Key Advantage:** **Non-contact** measurement — ideal for **moving parts, high-voltage conductors, steam pipes, and furnace walls**.
- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** Every material has a unique emissivity value (0 to 1). Setting the wrong emissivity leads to **large reading errors**.

**3. Black Globe Thermometer**

- **Working Principle:** A standard thermometer is enclosed in a **matte black hollow copper sphere**. It measures **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT)** — critical for HVAC and occupational comfort audits.

---

#### C. Fluid and Flow Measurement Instruments

**1. Anemometer (Hot-Wire / Vane Type)**

- **Hot-Wire Type:** A heated wire is exposed to flowing air. The **cooling effect** reduces the wire's resistance, which is correlated to flow velocity.
- **Vane Type:** A small propeller rotates when air flows across it; the **RPM is proportional to air velocity**.
- **Application:** HVAC duct balancing, ventilation audits, compressed air leakage surveys.

**2. Ultrasonic Flowmeter**

- **Working Principle:** Two transducers mounted on the pipe emit and receive ultrasonic pulses **with** and **against** the flow direction. The **time difference** ($\Delta t$) between the two pulse travels is proportional to flow velocity.
- **Key Advantage:** **Clamp-on type** — does not require cutting the pipe, ideal for **retrofit installations** and **large diameter pipes**.
- **Measurement is independent** of fluid conductivity, pressure, and viscosity.

**3. Pitot Tube**

- **Working Principle:** A **Pitot-static tube** has two openings: a **total pressure port** (facing the flow) and a **static pressure port** (perpendicular to flow). The **differential pressure** ($\Delta P$) is measured using a **manometer or differential pressure gauge**.
- **Velocity Formula:** Derived from Bernoulli's equation:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

Where $\rho$ is the fluid density.

---

#### D. Combustion and Flue Gas Analysis

**1. Flue Gas Analyzer (Electrochemical / NDIR type)**

- **Working Principle:** A sample of flue gas is drawn through a probe and exposed to **electrochemical sensors** (for O₂, CO, NOₓ, SO₂) or **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)** sensors (for CO₂ and hydrocarbons).
- **Outputs:** %O₂, %CO₂, ppm CO, ppm NOₓ, **Stack Temperature (T_flue)**, **Combustion Efficiency (%)**, **Excess Air (%)**.
- **Application:** Boiler tuning, furnace efficiency assessment, emission monitoring.

**2. Psychrometer (Wet & Dry Bulb)**

- **Working Principle:** Two thermometers — one **dry bulb** (measures ambient air T) and one **wet bulb** (covered with a wick dipped in water, measures evaporative cooling effect). The **wet-bulb depression** gives the **humidity ratio** and **relative humidity**.
- **Application:** Cooling tower performance assessment, HVAC load calculations.

---

#### E. Lighting Measurement

**1. Lux Meter (Light Meter)**

- **Working Principle:** A **photodiode or photoresistor** generates a current proportional to incident **luminous flux** (weighted by the **photopic luminosity function** $V(\lambda)$ — the human eye response).
- **Output:** Illuminance in **lux** (lumens per square meter).
- **Application:** Compliance with lighting standards (e.g., IS 3646, workplace illumination audits).

---

#### F. Mechanical and Rotational Measurement

**1. Tachometer (Contact / Non-Contact)**

- **Contact Type:** A rubber wheel is pressed against a rotating shaft; the RPM is calculated from the wheel's rotation.
- **Non-Contact (Optical) Type:** A beam of light is reflected off a **reflective tape** on the shaft; the pulses are counted to compute RPM.
- **Application:** Motor speed verification, fan/pump speed audits.

**2. Stroboscope**

- **Working Principle:** Emits **flashes of light at a controlled frequency**. When the flash frequency matches the rotational frequency of a moving object, the object **appears stationary** (the **stroboscopic effect**).
- **Application:** Inspection of rotating machinery, slip measurement in induction motors, fan blade inspection.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** and must be memorized with proper understanding of units and boundary conditions.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1** | Three-Phase Active Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\varphi$ | W or kW |
| **2** | Three-Phase Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\varphi$ | VAr or kVAr |
| **3** | Three-Phase Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | VA or kVA |
| **4** | Power Factor | $\cos\varphi = P / S$ | Dimensionless, $\cos\varphi \le 1$ |
| **5** | Total Energy Consumption | $E = P \cdot t$ | kWh, MJ, or GJ |
| **6** | Demand Factor | $D_f = \text{Max Demand} / \text{Connected Load}$ | Dimensionless |
| **7** | Load Factor | $L_f = \text{Average Load} / \text{Max Demand}$ | Dimensionless |
| **8** | Capacitor Rating (PF Correction) | $Q_c = P \cdot (\tan\varphi_1 - \tan\varphi_2)$ | kVAr |
| **9** | Line Current Reduction | $I_{new} = I_{old} \cdot (\cos\varphi_1 / \cos\varphi_2)$ | A |
| **10** | % I²R Loss Reduction | $\%\text{Loss Reduction} = 1 - (\cos\varphi_1 / \cos\varphi_2)^2$ | % |
| **11** | Fan Affinity Law — Flow | $Q_2 / Q_1 = N_2 / N_1$ | Speed ratio |
| **12** | Fan Affinity Law — Head | $H_2 / H_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$ | Pressure ratio |
| **13** | Fan Affinity Law — Power | $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | **Cube law — most exploited** |
| **14** | Pump Affinity — Dia Trim | $P_2 / P_1 = (D_2 / D_1)^3$ | Impeller diameter trim |
| **15** | Pitot Tube Velocity | $v = \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | m/s |
| **16** | Specific Heat (Sensible Heat) | $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ or kCal |
| **17** | Latent Heat | $Q_l = m \cdot \lambda$ | kJ or kCal |
| **18** | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² (used in IR thermometry) |
| **19** | Illuminance | $E = \Phi / A$ | lux $\vert$ lm/m² |
| **20** | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \Phi / P$ | lm/W |
| **21** | THD (Current) | $THD_I = \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2} \, / \, I_1$ | % |
| **22** | Velocity from Anemometer | $v = K \cdot \Delta R / R_{avg}$ | m/s (hot-wire) |
| **23** | Combustion Efficiency (Siegert) | $\eta = 100 - L_s - L_d - L_{rad}$ | % (Loss Method) |
| **24** | Stack Loss (Oil Fuel) | $\%L_s = (T_f - T_a) \cdot \frac{A_1}{21 - \%O_2} + B$ | % (Siegert Constants) |
| **25** | Simple Payback Period | $SP = \text{Investment} / \text{Annual Savings}$ | Years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Siegert Constants** for **Stack Loss** are fuel-specific:
> - **For Fuel Oil:** $A_1 = 0.540$, $B = 0.007$
> - **For Coal:** $A_1 = 0.620$, $B = 0.005$
> - **For Natural Gas:** $A_1 = 0.478$, $B = 0.007$

### 2.3 Selection Criteria for Audit Instruments

| Selection Parameter | Description |
|:---|:---|
| **Accuracy** | Should be $\le \pm 1\%$ for electrical, $\le \pm 2\%$ for thermal |
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change (e.g., 0.1°C, 0.01 kW) |
| **Range** | Must cover expected variation in the parameter |
| **Calibration** | Should have a valid **NABL / ISO 17025** calibration certificate |
| **Data Logging** | Built-in memory / cloud sync for long-duration measurements |
| **Portability** | Battery operated, rugged housing for field conditions |
| **Safety Rating** | CAT III / CAT IV for electrical instruments |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Capacitor Rating for Power Factor Correction

**Problem Setup:** A 3-phase load draws $P = 100$ kW at $\cos\varphi_1 = 0.70$ (lagging). Calculate the **capacitor bank rating** required to improve PF to $\cos\varphi_2 = 0.95$ (lagging). Also calculate the percentage reduction in line current and I²R losses.

**Step 1: Identify the original reactive power**

$$Q_1 = P \cdot \tan\varphi_1 = 100 \cdot \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.70))$$

Since $\cos\varphi_1 = 0.70$, we find $\varphi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.70) \approx 45.57°$

$$\tan\varphi_1 = \tan(45.57°) \approx 1.020$$

$$Q_1 = 100 \cdot 1.020 = 102.0 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 2: Identify the new reactive power after correction**

$$\cos\varphi_2 = 0.95 \implies \varphi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) \approx 18.19°$$

$$\tan\varphi_2 = \tan(18.19°) \approx 0.3287$$

$$Q_2 = P \cdot \tan\varphi_2 = 100 \cdot 0.3287 = 32.87 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 3: Capacitor bank rating**

$$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = 102.0 - 32.87 = 69.13 \text{ kVAr}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Interpretation:** A **69.13 kVAr** capacitor bank must be installed to reduce the reactive power draw from the grid from 102.0 kVAr to 32.87 kVAr, raising the power factor from **0.70 to 0.95**.

**Step 4: Reduction in line current**

$$I_{new} = I_{old} \cdot \frac{\cos\varphi_1}{\cos\varphi_2} = I_{old} \cdot \frac{0.70}{0.95} = I_{old} \cdot 0.7368$$

$$\%\text{ Reduction in Current} = \left(1 - 0.7368\right) \cdot 100 = 26.32\%$$

**Step 5: Reduction in I²R losses**

Since $P_{loss} \propto I^2$,

$$\%\text{ Loss Reduction} = 1 - \left(\frac{\cos\varphi_1}{\cos\varphi_2}\right)^2 = 1 - (0.7368)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.5429 = 0.4571 = 45.71\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Improving PF from **0.70 → 0.95** reduces line current by only **26.32%**, but reduces **I²R copper losses** by **45.71%** because losses scale with the **square** of the current.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Fan Affinity Law Savings (VFD Application)

**Problem Setup:** A centrifugal fan consumes $P_1 = 50$ kW at full speed $N_1 = 1450$ RPM. By installing a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD), the speed is reduced to $N_2 = 1100$ RPM (75.86% of full speed). Calculate the new power consumption and annual energy savings assuming 6000 operating hours/year and electricity cost of ₹7/kWh.

**Step 1: Identify the speed ratio**

$$\text{Speed Ratio} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1100}{1450} = 0.7586$$

**Step 2: Apply the Cube Law**

By the Fan Affinity Law:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (0.7586)^3$$

$$(0.7586)^3 = 0.7586 \cdot 0.7586 \cdot 0.7586 \approx 0.4366$$

**Step 3: Compute the new power**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot 0.4366 = 50 \cdot 0.4366 = 21.83 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Compute power savings**

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 50 - 21.83 = 28.17 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Compute annual energy and cost savings**

$$\text{Annual Energy Saved} = \Delta P \cdot t = 28.17 \cdot 6000 = 169{,}020 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\text{Annual Cost Saved} = 169{,}020 \cdot 7 = \text{₹}11{,}83{,}140 \text{ per year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** A mere **24% reduction in fan speed** results in **56.34% reduction in power consumption**. This is the single most powerful energy conservation technique for **centrifugal pumps, fans, and compressors** — the cubic relationship amplifies any small speed reduction into massive energy savings.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Stack Loss Using Siegert Formula

**Problem Setup:** A furnace fired with **fuel oil** has the following readings from a flue gas analyzer:
- Stack Temperature: $T_f = 280°C$
- Ambient Temperature: $T_a = 35°C$
- Oxygen in flue gas: $\%O_2 = 4\%$

Calculate the **stack heat loss** and **combustion efficiency**.

**Step 1: Recall the Siegert formula for fuel oil**

$$\%L_s = (T_f - T_a) \cdot \frac{A_1}{21 - \%O_2} + B \cdot (T_f - T_a)$$

Where $A_1 = 0.540$, $B = 0.007$ for fuel oil.

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$T_f - T_a = 280 - 35 = 245°C$$

$$\%L_s = 245 \cdot \frac{0.540}{21 - 4} + 0.007 \cdot 245$$

$$= 245 \cdot \frac{0.540}{17} + 1.715$$

$$= 245 \cdot 0.03176 + 1.715$$

$$= 7.782 + 1.715 = 9.497\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate combustion efficiency**

Assuming dry flue gas loss only (ignoring moisture and radiation losses for simplicity):

$$\eta_{combustion} = 100 - \%L_s = 100 - 9.50 = 90.50\%$$

**Step 4: Sensitivity check — effect of reducing excess air**

If excess air is reduced so that $\%O_2$ drops to **2%**:

$$\%L_s^{new} = 245 \cdot \frac{0.540}{21 - 2} + 1.715 = 245 \cdot \frac{0.540}{19} + 1.715$$

$$= 245 \cdot 0.02842 + 1.715 = 6.963 + 1.715 = 8.68\%$$

$$\text{Efficiency Gain} = 90.50 - 91.32 = 0.82\% \text{ improvement}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation Lesson:** Reducing **excess air** by 2% (from 4% O₂ to 2% O₂) improved combustion efficiency by **0.82 percentage points**. In a 1 MW furnace running 8000 hours/year, this saves approximately **82 kW × 8000 h × 7 ₹/kWh ≈ ₹45.92 lakhs/year**.

---

### 3.4 Energy Savings Calculation for Lighting Retrofit

**Problem Setup:** A factory has **200 incandescent lamps** of 100 W each. They are replaced with **25 W LED lamps**. Operating hours are **12 hours/day, 300 days/year**. Electricity cost: **₹8/kWh**. Calculate annual energy and cost savings.

**Step 1: Calculate existing power consumption**

$$P_{old} = 200 \cdot 100 = 20{,}000 \text{ W} = 20 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Calculate new power consumption**

$$P_{new} = 200 \cdot 25 = 5{,}000 \text{ W} = 5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Calculate power reduction**

$$\Delta P = 20 - 5 = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Calculate annual operating hours**

$$t = 12 \cdot 300 = 3{,}600 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 5: Calculate annual energy savings**

$$\Delta E = \Delta P \cdot t = 15 \cdot 3{,}600 = 54{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 6: Calculate annual monetary savings**

$$\text{Savings} = 54{,}000 \cdot 8 = \text{₹}4{,}32{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 7: Calculate simple payback (assume LED cost ₹500/lamp)**

$$\text{Investment} = 200 \cdot 500 = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000$$

$$SP = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{4{,}32{,}000} = 0.231 \text{ years} \approx 2.78 \text{ months}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Judgment:** A payback period of **less than 1 year** is considered an **excellent** ECM (Energy Conservation Measure) in industrial energy auditing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Diagram of Energy Audit Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RootNode["Energy Audit Instruments Portfolio"]
    
    RootNode --> NodeE["Electrical Parameter Group"]

    SubF1 -.- NoteC["Measures Air Velocity and Volume Flow"]
    SubL1 -.- NoteD["Measures Illuminance in Lux"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Audit Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StepA["Step 1: Planning and Scope Definition"]
    StepB["Step 2: Walk-through Survey"]
    StepC["Step 3: Instrument Selection"]

    StepA --> StepB --> StepC --> StepD --> StepE --> StepF --> StepG --> StepH --> StepI --> StepJ
    StepJ -.Feedback Loop.-> StepA
    
    InstrBlock["Instrument Cluster Active During Field Phase"]
    StepD --- InstrBlock
    InstrBlock --> IA1["Power Analyzer"]
    InstrBlock --> IA2["Lux Meter"]
    InstrBlock --> IA3["Flue Gas Analyzer"]
    InstrBlock --> IA4["Anemometer"]
    InstrBlock --> IA5["IR Thermometer"]
    InstrBlock --> IA6["Tachometer"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of a Digital Power Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InV["Voltage Input via Potential Transformer PT"]
    InI["Current Input via Current Transformer CT"]
    
    InV --> ADC1["ADC for Voltage Channel"]
    InI --> ADC2["ADC for Current Channel"]
    
    ADC1 --> DSP["Digital Signal Processor DSP Unit"]
    ADC2 --> DSP
    
    DSP --> Calc1["RMS Computation Block"]
    DSP --> Calc2["Power Computation P Q S Block"]
    DSP --> Calc3["Harmonic FFT Analysis Block"]
    DSP --> Calc4["Power Factor Calculation Block"]
    
    Calc1 --> Display["LCD Display Module"]
    Calc2 --> Display
    Calc3 --> Display
    Calc4 --> Display
    
    Calc1 --> Log["Data Logger and Memory"]
    Calc2 --> Log
    Calc3 --> Log
    Calc4 --> Log
    
    Log --> Comm["USB / RS485 / Bluetooth Communication Port"]
    Comm --> PC["External Computer for Analysis"]
```

### 4.4 Measurement Sequence Diagram for a Boiler Energy Audit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Start of Boiler Audit"]
    P2["Measure Fuel Flow using Flowmeter"]
    P3["Measure Steam Flow using Orifice or Vortex Meter"]
    P4["Measure Feed Water Temperature using RTD"]
    P5["Measure Steam Temperature using Thermocouple"]
    P6["Measure Stack Temperature using IR Pyrometer"]
    P7["Measure Oxygen and CO using Flue Gas Analyzer"]
    P8["Measure Ambient Temperature and Humidity"]
    P9["Measure Air Flow to Combustion Chamber using Anemometer"]
    P10["Calculate GCV and NCV of Fuel from Lab Analysis"]
    P11["Compute Enthalpy of Steam and Feed Water"]
    P12["Compute Boiler Efficiency by Direct and Indirect Method"]
    P13["Compute Specific Fuel Consumption and Specific Steam Consumption"]
    P14["Compute Stack Loss using Siegert Formula"]
    P15["End of Audit Report Compilation"]
    
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10 --> P11 --> P12 --> P13 --> P14 --> P15
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **measurement sequence** above shows the **logical order** in which a boiler audit must be conducted. Skipping steps or measuring out of order leads to **unbalanced data** and inaccurate efficiency calculations.
