<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six instruments used in an energy audit and state the parameter measured by each.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> 1. **Power Analyzer** — measures voltage, current, kW, kVA, kVAr, power factor, frequency.
> 2. **Lux Meter** — measures **illuminance** in lux (lm/m²).
> 3. **Flue Gas Analyzer** — measures **%O₂, %CO₂, ppm CO, ppm NOₓ**, stack temperature.
> 4. **Infrared Thermometer** — measures **non-contact surface temperature**.
> 5. **Anemometer** — measures **air velocity** and **volume flow rate**.
> 6. **Ultrasonic Flowmeter** — measures **fluid flow rate** in pipes without cutting.
> 7. **Tachometer** — measures **rotational speed** (RPM) of motors and shafts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the working principle of an infrared pyrometer. Why is emissivity setting important?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The IR pyrometer works on the principle that every object above absolute zero emits infrared radiation. The radiated power per unit area is given by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, where $\varepsilon$ is emissivity, $\sigma$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature. The IR pyrometer focuses this radiation onto a **thermopile detector** and computes the temperature.
>
> **Importance of Emissivity:** Emissivity $\varepsilon$ ranges from 0 (perfect reflector) to 1 (blackbody). If the wrong $\varepsilon$ is set:
> - $\varepsilon$ set too low → instrument **reads higher** than actual temperature.
> - $\varepsilon$ set too high → instrument **reads lower** than actual temperature.
> For polished metals, $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$–0.2; for painted or oxidized surfaces, $\varepsilon \approx 0.85$–0.95. **Always apply emissivity tape** to shiny surfaces for accurate readings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the working principle of a **3-phase digital power analyzer**. List any **six parameters** measured by it. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 3-phase industrial load draws a power of **75 kW at a power factor of 0.65 (lagging)**. Calculate:
- (i) The original reactive power.
- (ii) The **capacitor bank rating** required to improve the PF to **0.95 (lagging)**.
- (iii) The percentage **reduction in line current** and **I²R losses**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Working of 3-Phase Digital Power Analyzer **[7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram Description:**

1. **Voltage Sensing Stage:** Three-phase voltage inputs are stepped down via **Potential Transformers (PTs)** to a safe level (e.g., 0–110 V). The signals are filtered to remove high-frequency noise.
2. **Current Sensing Stage:** Three-phase currents are sensed using **Current Transformers (CTs)** with proper polarity. For very large currents, **Rogowski coils** are also used.
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Both voltage and current signals are **amplified, filtered, and offset-adjusted** to match the input range of the ADCs.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Both signals are sampled simultaneously at a high rate (**2 kHz to 10 kHz**) to capture waveform shape and harmonics.
5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** Computes:
   - **RMS values** of V and I.
   - **Active power** $P = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$ for each phase.
   - **Reactive power** via the **Hilbert transform** or **90° phase-shift method**.
   - **Apparent power** $S = V_{RMS} \cdot I_{RMS}$.
   - **Power factor** $\cos\varphi = P / S$.
   - **THD** via **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**.
6. **Display Module:** All computed values are displayed on an **LCD/LED screen** in real-time.
7. **Data Logger:** Internal memory stores data for download via **USB, RS485, Ethernet, or Bluetooth**.

**Six Parameters Measured:**
1. RMS Voltage (V)
2. RMS Current (A)
3. Active Power (kW)
4. Apparent Power (kVA)
5. Reactive Power (kVAr)
6. Power Factor (cos φ)
7. (Bonus) Frequency (Hz) and THD (%)

---

#### Part (b) — Power Factor Correction Calculation **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $P = 75$ kW
- $\cos\varphi_1 = 0.65$ (lagging)
- $\cos\varphi_2 = 0.95$ (lagging)

**Step 1: Original reactive power**

$$\varphi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.65) \approx 49.46°$$

$$\tan\varphi_1 = \tan(49.46°) \approx 1.169$$

$$Q_1 = P \cdot \tan\varphi_1 = 75 \cdot 1.169 = 87.69 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 2: New reactive power after correction**

$$\varphi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) \approx 18.19°$$

$$\tan\varphi_2 = \tan(18.19°) \approx 0.3287$$

$$Q_2 = P \cdot \tan\varphi_2 = 75 \cdot 0.3287 = 24.65 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 3: Capacitor rating**

$$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = 87.69 - 24.65 = 63.04 \text{ kVAr}$$

**Step 4: Percentage reduction in line current**

$$\%\text{ Current Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{\cos\varphi_1}{\cos\varphi_2}\right) \cdot 100 = \left(1 - \frac{0.65}{0.95}\right) \cdot 100$$

$$= (1 - 0.6842) \cdot 100 = 31.58\%$$

**Step 5: Percentage reduction in I²R losses**

$$\%\text{ Loss Reduction} = 1 - \left(\frac{\cos\varphi_1}{\cos\varphi_2}\right)^2 = 1 - (0.6842)^2$$

$$= 1 - 0.4681 = 0.5319 = 53.19\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students incorrectly use the linear formula for **loss reduction**. Since copper losses are **proportional to I²**, the **square of the current ratio** must be used. Loss reduction is **always greater than** current reduction in PF correction problems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an **ultrasonic flowmeter** and a **Pitot tube**. State two advantages of each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A factory has installed **300 incandescent lamps of 60 W** each, operating **10 hours/day for 300 days/year**. The proposal is to replace them with **18 W LED lamps** costing **₹400 each**.
- (i) Calculate the **annual energy and cost savings** at **₹7/kWh**.
- (ii) Calculate the **simple payback period**.
- (iii) Comment on the **viability** of the proposal. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Ultrasonic Flowmeter and Pitot Tube **[7 Marks]**

**Ultrasonic Flowmeter:**

**Working Principle:** Two ultrasonic transducers (T₁ and T₂) are mounted on the outer surface of a pipe at a known angle. Each transducer alternately transmits and receives ultrasonic pulses:

- **Downstream travel** (T₁ → T₂): The pulses travel **with** the flow and take time $t_d$.
- **Upstream travel** (T₂ → T₁): The pulses travel **against** the flow and take time $t_u$.

Since $t_d < t_u$, the **time difference** $\Delta t = t_u - t_d$ is proportional to the **average flow velocity** in the pipe:

$$v = \frac{L}{2 \cos\theta} \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{t_d \cdot t_u}$$

Where $L$ is the path length and $\theta$ is the angle of the transducer with respect to the pipe axis.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Non-invasive:** Clamp-on installation; **no pipe cutting**, no pressure loss, no contamination risk.
2. **Versatile:** Works on **any fluid** (water, oil, chemicals) regardless of conductivity, viscosity, or temperature.

**Pitot Tube:**

**Working Principle:** A Pitot-static tube has two concentric tubes:
- The **inner tube** has an opening facing the flow, sensing the **stagnation (total) pressure** $P_t$.
- The **outer tube** has side openings perpendicular to the flow, sensing the **static pressure** $P_s$.

The **differential pressure** $\Delta P = P_t - P_s$ is measured using a manometer or pressure transducer. By Bernoulli's equation, the **flow velocity** is:

$$v = C \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

Where $C$ is the discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.95$–0.99) and $\rho$ is fluid density.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Low cost** and simple to install.
2. **High accuracy** for clean, single-phase fluids.

---

#### Part (b) — Lighting Retrofit Economics **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $N = 300$ lamps
- $P_{old} = 60$ W, $P_{new} = 18$ W
- $t = 10 \cdot 300 = 3{,}000$ hours/year
- Cost per LED = ₹400
- Electricity cost = ₹7/kWh

**Step 1: Existing power consumption**

$$P_{old}^{total} = 300 \cdot 60 = 18{,}000 \text{ W} = 18 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: New power consumption**

$$P_{new}^{total} = 300 \cdot 18 = 5{,}400 \text{ W} = 5.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Power reduction**

$$\Delta P = 18 - 5.4 = 12.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Annual energy savings**

$$\Delta E = 12.6 \cdot 3{,}000 = 37{,}800 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 5: Annual cost savings**

$$\text{Savings} = 37{,}800 \cdot 7 = \text{₹}2{,}64{,}600 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6: Total investment**

$$\text{Investment} = 300 \cdot 400 = \text{₹}1{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 7: Simple Payback Period**

$$SP = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{2{,}64{,}600} = 0.4535 \text{ years}$$

Converting to months: $0.4535 \cdot 12 = 5.44 \text{ months}$

**Step 8: Viability Comment**

> **Viability:** The proposal is **highly viable** because the payback period of **~5.4 months** is well below the **2-year threshold** commonly used in industrial ECM evaluation. Additionally, LED lamps have a longer life (~50,000 hrs vs ~1,000 hrs), reducing maintenance costs further.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often forget to **multiply the number of lamps by hours per day and days per year** when computing total operating hours. Always write: $t_{annual} = N_{hours/day} \times N_{days/year}$. Also, convert **Watts to Kilowatts** before multiplying with hours to get kWh.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Power Analyzer** is the **single most important instrument** for electrical energy audits — it measures V, I, kW, kVA, kVAr, PF, Hz, THD.
- ✅ **CT Ratio and PT Ratio** are **always** mentioned in audit readings — remember to multiply measured values by the ratio to get the **actual primary-side values**.
- ✅ **IR Pyrometer** depends on **emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** — wrong $\varepsilon$ leads to wrong temperature; **always use emissivity tape** on shiny/polished surfaces.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic flowmeters** are **clamp-on** and **non-invasive** — ideal for retrofit applications.
- ✅ **Pitot tube** measures flow velocity using **Bernoulli's equation**: $v = \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$.
- ✅ **Fan Affinity Laws** — the **cube law** ($P \propto N^3$) is the **most exploited** energy conservation law for centrifugal equipment.
- ✅ **Capacitor rating** for PF correction: $Q_c = P(\tan\varphi_1 - \tan\varphi_2)$.
- ✅ **Loss reduction** in PF correction is **proportional to the SQUARE of the current ratio** — use $(\cos\varphi_1 / \cos\varphi_2)^2$, not the linear ratio.
- ✅ **Siegert formula** for stack loss uses **fuel-specific constants** — memorize $A_1$ for fuel oil (0.540), coal (0.620), gas (0.478).
- ✅ **Lux meter** measures illuminance; **1 lux = 1 lm/m²**.
- ✅ **Flue gas analyzer** measures %O₂, %CO₂, CO, NOₓ, SO₂ — the **%O₂ reading** is used to compute **excess air** and **stack loss**.
- ✅ **Tachometer** (contact or non-contact) measures RPM; **stroboscope** can freeze the motion of rotating parts.
- ✅ **Simple Payback** = Investment / Annual Savings — payback **less than 2 years** is generally considered **excellent** for ECMs.
- ✅ **Clamp meter** allows **non-contact current measurement** using **magnetic field sensing** — no need to break the circuit.
- ✅ **Harmonics** — **triplen harmonics** (3rd, 9th, 15th, …) add up in the **neutral conductor**, causing overheating.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit Report
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit Report

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Energy Audit Report (EAR)** is a comprehensive, structured, and systematically documented document that records, quantifies, analyzes, and presents the findings, observations, measurements, and recommendations arising from a formal energy audit conducted at an industrial, commercial, or institutional facility. It serves as the official communication medium between the **Energy Auditor/Energy Audit Firm** and the **Plant Management/Client Organization**, translating raw field data into actionable engineering, operational, and managerial recommendations aimed at improving energy efficiency, reducing energy costs, and ensuring regulatory compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight**
> As per the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** guidelines adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus, an Energy Audit Report is *not merely a data dump*; it is an **engineered advisory document** that must contain baseline data, energy performance indices (EnPIs), quantified savings potential, and an implementation roadmap.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of an **Energy Audit Report** as the *"complete medical health-checkup report"* you receive from a hospital after undergoing a full body check-up.

| Medical Check-up Analogy | Energy Audit Report Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Doctor examining the patient | Energy Auditor surveying the plant |
| Blood test, ECG, X-ray reports | Electrical load survey, combustion analysis, thermal imaging |
| Diagnosis of diseases | Identification of energy waste areas |
| Prescribed medicines & lifestyle changes | Recommended energy-saving measures (REMs) |
| Follow-up appointment schedule | Implementation schedule & monitoring plan |
| Patient (you) | Plant Management / Client |

Just as a medical report ends with a clear **"what to do next"** section, an Energy Audit Report ends with a **prioritized list of energy conservation measures (ECMs)** with payback periods.

### 1.3 Why is the Energy Audit Report Important?

> [!NOTE]
> The **ENERGY CONSERVATION ACT, 2001** (India) mandates that designated energy consumers (as notified by BEE) **must** conduct periodic energy audits and submit audit reports in a **standardized format**. Therefore, the report is a *legal document* in India, not just an academic exercise.

**Key Importance Points:**
1. **Legal Compliance** — Required by statutory bodies (BEE, State Designated Agencies).
2. **Decision-Making Tool** — Management uses it to approve capital expenditure (CAPEX).
3. **Benchmarking** — Establishes the **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** baseline.
4. **Investment Justification** — Provides financial metrics like **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** and **Return on Investment (ROI)**.
5. **Knowledge Transfer** — Documents best practices for future plant personnel.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Used in an Audit Report

- **Baseline Data**: The reference energy consumption pattern before any conservation measure.
- **Energy Performance Index (EnPI)**: KPI used to compare energy performance over time.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**: Energy used per unit of production (e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/kg).
- **Recommendation of Energy Measures (REMs)**: Actionable energy-saving opportunities.
- **Implementation Schedule**: Timeline for executing the recommended measures.
- **Energy Audit Report (EAR)**: The final formal document submitted to the client.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standardized Structure of an Energy Audit Report

The **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** has prescribed a standardized format for energy audit reports. KTU examiners expect students to know this structure. The major components are listed in the structured format below.

### 2.1.1 Cover Page & Executive Summary
- **Cover Page**: Title, plant name, address, audit firm logo, report number, date, signatures of Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) and Accredited Energy Auditor (AEA).
- **Executive Summary**: A 1–2 page concise overview for top management containing:
  - Plant description (1 paragraph)
  - Total energy consumption (annual, in **Tera Joules (TJ)** or **Million Units/MU**)
  - Total energy cost (₹ per annum)
  - Number of energy conservation opportunities identified
  - Total annual savings potential (₹/year and Energy units)
  - Total investment required (₹)
  - Average payback period (years)

### 2.1.2 Introduction & Plant Description
- Plant name, location, products manufactured, production capacity.
- Process flow diagram (PFD) of major manufacturing operations.
- List of major energy-consuming equipment with rated capacities.
- Operating hours per day, days per year, shift pattern.

### 2.1.3 Energy Consumption Analysis
This is the **core analytical section** containing:
- **Energy Balance Diagram**: Sankey diagram showing energy input vs. useful output vs. losses.
- **Fuel-wise energy consumption** (Coal, Oil, Gas, Electricity).
- **Product-wise energy distribution**.
- **Utility-wise energy breakdown** (Boilers, Compressors, Pumps, HVAC, Lighting).

### 2.1.4 Energy Performance Assessment
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for each product/process.
- Comparison with **BEE Sectoral Benchmarks** (e.g., for Cement, Steel, Pulp & Paper).
- Identification of **Energy Inefficient Areas** and gaps.

### 2.1.5 Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) / Recommendations
Each recommendation must be documented in a **standard 4-column format**:
1. **Recommendation Description** (What to do?)
2. **Annual Energy Savings** (kWh / tonnes of oil equivalent / Nm³ of gas)
3. **Investment Required** (₹ in Lakhs)
4. **Simple Payback Period** (Years) + **Net Present Value (NPV)**

### 2.1.6 Implementation Schedule & Monitoring
- Phased implementation (Short term < 1 yr, Medium term 1–3 yrs, Long term > 3 yrs).
- **Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) system** design.
- Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to be tracked.

### 2.1.7 Appendices
- Detailed measurement data, calibration certificates, calculation sheets, instrument list, glossary, and references.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU University Exams. Memorize the units carefully.

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{Total\ Energy\ Consumed}{Total\ Production\ Output}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | $\dfrac{MJ}{kg},\ \dfrac{kWh}{tonne}$ |
| 2 | $EPI = \dfrac{Total\ Annual\ Energy\ (MJ)}{Total\ Annual\ Production\ (Tonnes)}$ | Energy Performance Index | $\dfrac{MJ}{tonne}$ |
| 3 | $Annual\ Savings = Energy\ Saved\ per\ unit \times Annual\ Production$ | Annual monetary savings | ₹/year |
| 4 | $SPP = \dfrac{Investment\ Required\ (₹)}{Annual\ Monetary\ Savings\ (₹/yr)}$ | Simple Payback Period | Years |
| 5 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - Initial\ Investment$ | Net Present Value | ₹ |
| 6 | $IRR: NPV = 0$ | Internal Rate of Return | % |
| 7 | $ROI = \dfrac{Annual\ Savings}{Investment} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | % |
| 8 | $Energy\ Cost\ Share\ \% = \dfrac{Energy\ Cost}{Total\ Production\ Cost} \times 100$ | Energy intensity in cost | % |
| 9 | $Efficiency\ (\eta) = \dfrac{Useful\ Energy\ Output}{Energy\ Input} \times 100$ | Equipment efficiency | % |
| 10 | $CO_2\ Reduction = Energy\ Saved\ (TOE) \times 4.16$ | CO₂ emission reduction (approx.) | Tonnes of CO₂ |
| 11 | $Power\ Factor = \cos\phi = \dfrac{kW}{kVA}$ | Power factor | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $TOE = Energy\ in\ TJ \times 23.88$ | Tonne of Oil Equivalent | TOE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Constants (must memorize):**
> - 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 41,868 MJ
> - 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ
> - 1 Calorie = 4.184 J
> - 1 BOE (Barrel of Oil Equivalent) ≈ 5.8 MMBTU

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Sector**: Helps plant managers identify wasteful practices and reduce manufacturing cost per unit.
- **Commercial Buildings (HVAC focus)**: Used for green building certification (e.g., **LEED, GRIHA, IGBC**).
- **Power Sector**: Generation companies use reports to assess plant heat rate improvements.
- **Regulatory**: BEE uses aggregated reports from designated consumers to formulate the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme benchmarks.
- **Financial Sector**: Banks/Financial Institutions may require the audit report before sanctioning **energy efficiency loans**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Measurement to Final Audit Report Metric

This section demonstrates **how each numerical value in the report is derived step-by-step** from raw field data. This is the **core calculation logic** examiners test.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

Consider a small industry producing **$P$ tonnes** of output annually and consuming electricity from two sources: grid (in kWh) and diesel (in litres). We need to convert all energy into a **common unit (MJ or TOE)** before computing SEC.

**Given Data:**
- Annual electricity from grid: $E_{grid}$ in kWh
- Annual diesel consumption: $D$ in litres
- Calorific value of diesel: $CV_{diesel} = 35.86\ MJ/L$ (approximate)
- Annual production: $P$ in tonnes

**Step 1: Convert electricity to MJ**

$$E_{grid}\ (MJ) = E_{grid}\ (kWh) \times 3.6$$

**Step 2: Convert diesel to MJ**

$$E_{diesel}\ (MJ) = D\ (L) \times CV_{diesel} = D \times 35.86$$

**Step 3: Total Energy in MJ**

$$E_{total} = E_{grid} + E_{diesel}$$

**Step 4: Calculate SEC**

$$SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{P}$$

**Step 5: Compare with Benchmark** — If the sectoral benchmark (BEE) is $SEC_{bench}$, then **Energy Performance Gap** is:

$$\Delta SEC = SEC_{actual} - SEC_{bench}$$

If $\Delta SEC > 0$, the plant is under-performing and ECMs are needed.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Simple Payback Period (SPP) and ROI

Consider an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) where:
- Investment = ₹ $I$
- Annual energy savings = $S$ kWh
- Electricity tariff = $T$ ₹/kWh

**Step 1: Annual Monetary Savings**

$$Annual\ Savings\ (\text{₹/yr}) = S \times T$$

**Step 2: Simple Payback Period (SPP)**

$$SPP = \frac{I}{S \times T}$$

The SPP is dimensionally consistent: [₹ / (₹/yr)] = Years.

**Step 3: Return on Investment (ROI)**

$$ROI\ (\%) = \frac{Annual\ Savings}{I} \times 100 = \frac{S \times T}{I} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Net Present Value (NPV) over $n$ years at discount rate $r$**

$$NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n}\frac{S \times T}{(1+r)^t}$$

A project is **financially viable** if $NPV > 0$.

### 3.1.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example

> **Question**: A motor replacement ECM requires ₹ 5,00,000 investment. The new motor saves 50,000 kWh annually. Electricity cost is ₹ 6/kWh. Calculate SPP, ROI over 5 years, and NPV at 12% discount rate.

**Step 1: Annual Monetary Savings**

$$Annual\ Savings = 50{,}000 \times 6 = \text{₹ } 3{,}00{,}000\ /\text{yr}$$

**Step 2: Simple Payback Period**

$$SPP = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 1.667\ \text{years}$$

**Step 3: ROI over 5 years**

$$Total\ Returns = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = \text{₹ } 15{,}00{,}000$$

$$ROI\ (\%) = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 200\ \%$$

**Step 4: NPV at 12% discount rate**

The discount factor for an annuity of 5 years at 12% is:

$$PVIFA = \frac{1-(1+0.12)^{-5}}{0.12}$$

$$PVIFA = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = 3.6048$$

Therefore:

$$NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6048) = -5{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}81{,}440$$

$$NPV = \text{₹ } 5{,}81{,}440$$

Since $NPV > 0$, the project is **financially viable**.

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix: Prioritization of ECMs

In an audit report, ECMs are ranked using the following weighted matrix:

| ECM | SPP (yr) | NPV (₹ Lakhs) | CO₂ Reduction (t/yr) | Technical Feasibility | Priority Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Replace inefficient motors | 1.5 | 8.2 | 45 | High | **1 (Highest)** |
| Install VFD on pumps | 2.1 | 6.5 | 32 | High | **2** |
| LED lighting retrofit | 0.8 | 3.0 | 18 | Very High | **3** |
| Waste heat recovery | 4.5 | 12.0 | 120 | Medium | **4** |
| Replace old chiller | 6.0 | -2.5 | 60 | Low | **5 (Lowest)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The priority score is calculated using a **weighted sum model** where lower SPP, higher NPV, higher CO₂ reduction, and higher technical feasibility push the ECM to the top of the recommendation list.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Audit Report

The following flowchart maps the **structural flow** of an Energy Audit Report — from the initial data collection phase to the final submission and monitoring phase. This is the kind of structured presentation KTU examiners reward.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Start: Audit Commissioned] --> A2[Phase 1: Pre-Audit Planning]
    A2 --> A3[Plant Walk-through Survey]
    A3 --> A4[Data Collection Forms Sent]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 2: Detailed Field Measurements]
    A5 --> A6[Load Survey with Power Analyzer]
    A5 --> A7[Combustion Analysis of Boilers]
    A5 --> A8[Thermal Imaging of Insulation]
    A5 --> A9[Compressed Air Leak Audit]
    A6 --> B1[Phase 3: Data Analysis and Energy Balance]
    A7 --> B1
    A8 --> B1
    A9 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[Compute SEC and EPI]
    B2 --> B3[Compare with BEE Benchmarks]
    B3 --> C1[Phase 4: Identification of Energy Conservation Measures]
    C1 --> C2[Short Term ECMs]
    C1 --> C3[Medium Term ECMs]
    C1 --> C4[Long Term ECMs]
    C2 --> D1[Phase 5: Economic Analysis of ECMs]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Calculate SPP NPV and ROI for each ECM]
    D2 --> E1[Phase 6: Drafting the Audit Report]
    E1 --> E2[Cover Page and Executive Summary]
    E1 --> E3[Plant Description and Process Flow]
    E1 --> E4[Energy Balance Diagram]
    E1 --> E5[Detailed ECM Recommendations Table]
    E1 --> E6[Implementation Schedule and M and T Plan]
    E2 --> F1[Phase 7: Report Submission and Review]
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    E6 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Final Report Submitted to Client]
    F2 --> F3[Management Approval and CAPEX Allocation]
    F3 --> F4[Implementation Phase Begins]
    F4 --> F5[Monitoring Verification and M and T]
    F5 --> G1[End: Continuous Energy Improvement Loop]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Structure of Report Sections

This diagram shows the **hierarchical table-of-contents structure** that must appear in the audit report.

```mermaid
graph TD
    H0[Energy Audit Report Document] --> H1[Section 1: Executive Summary]
    H0 --> H2[Section 2: Plant Description]
    H0 --> H3[Section 3: Energy Consumption Analysis]
    H0 --> H4[Section 4: Energy Performance Assessment]
    H0 --> H5[Section 5: Recommendations ECMs]
    H0 --> H6[Section 6: Implementation Plan]
    H0 --> H7[Section 7: Appendices]
    H1 --> H1A[Plant Snapshot]
    H1 --> H1B[Key Findings]
    H1 --> H1C[Total Savings Potential]
    H2 --> H2A[Process Flow Diagram]
    H2 --> H2B[Major Equipment List]
    H3 --> H3A[Fuel Wise Consumption]
    H3 --> H3B[Utility Wise Breakdown]
    H3 --> H3C[Energy Balance Sankey Diagram]
    H4 --> H4A[SEC Calculation]
    H4 --> H4B[EPI Calculation]
    H4 --> H4C[Benchmark Comparison]
    H5 --> H5A[Short Term ECMs less than 1 year]
    H5 --> H5B[Medium Term ECMs 1 to 3 years]
    H5 --> H5C[Long Term ECMs greater than 3 years]
    H6 --> H6A[Phased Schedule]
    H6 --> H6B[Monitoring and Targeting M and T]
    H7 --> H7A[Raw Measurement Data]
    H7 --> H7B[Instrument Calibration Certificates]
    H7 --> H7C[Glossary of Terms]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Report Review and Approval Workflow

This shows the **sign-off workflow** that ensures the report is reviewed at multiple levels before submission.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Energy Auditor Drafts Report] --> R2[Internal Technical Review]
    R2 --> R3{Quality Check Passed?}

```

> The above diagrams are **functional architecture schematics** that demonstrate the logical flow, structure, and governance of an Energy Audit Report. KTU examiners expect students to draw such structured flowcharts in exam answers for full marks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List any six essential components of a standard Energy Audit Report as per BEE guidelines.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each):**
1. Cover Page with signatures of Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) and Accredited Energy Auditor (AEA).
2. Executive Summary with total energy cost, savings potential, and investment.
3. Plant Description with Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and major equipment list.
4. Energy Consumption Analysis with fuel-wise and utility-wise breakdown.
5. Energy Performance Assessment with SEC, EPI, and benchmark comparison.
6. Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) with cost-benefit analysis (SPP, NPV).
7. Implementation Schedule and Monitoring & Targeting (M&T) plan.
8. Appendices with raw data, instrument calibration certificates, and glossary.

*(Any 6 of the above fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term 'Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)' and state its unit. Why is SEC important in an Energy Audit Report?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition**: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is the energy consumed per unit of production output. It is a key performance indicator (KPI) that quantifies the energy intensity of a manufacturing process.
- **Mathematical Expression**: $SEC = \dfrac{Total\ Energy\ Consumed\ (MJ\ or\ kWh)}{Total\ Production\ Output\ (tonne\ or\ kg)}$
- **Units**: $\dfrac{MJ}{kg}$, $\dfrac{kWh}{tonne}$, $\dfrac{TOE}{tonne}$
- **Importance**:
  1. Allows benchmarking across plants in the same sector.
  2. Tracks improvement in energy efficiency over time.
  3. Forms the basis of BEE's PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme targets.
  4. Helps management set realistic energy reduction goals.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: A fertilizer manufacturing plant conducted an energy audit. The audit identified the following Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). As a Certified Energy Auditor, draft a complete summary of the Energy Audit Report Executive Summary section for this plant.**

| S.No. | ECM Description | Investment (₹ Lakhs) | Annual Energy Savings | Annual Monetary Savings (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Replace old motor with IE3 premium efficiency motor | 8 | 1,20,000 kWh | 7.2 |
| 2 | Install VFD on feedwater pump | 12 | 90,000 kWh | 5.4 |
| 3 | Install waste heat recovery from ammonia reformer | 45 | 5,00,000 Nm³ of NG | 22.0 |
| 4 | LED retrofit across plant | 5 | 80,000 kWh | 4.8 |
| 5 | Improve boiler insulation | 6 | 50,000 kWh (heat loss reduction) | 3.0 |

**Additional Data:**
- Total annual energy cost of the plant: ₹ 850 Lakhs
- Total annual production: 50,000 tonnes of fertilizer
- Electricity tariff: ₹ 6/kWh
- Natural gas price: ₹ 44/Nm³
- Discount rate for NPV: 10%

**Part (a) [7 Marks]**: Calculate the **Total Investment**, **Total Annual Savings**, and **Average Simple Payback Period** for the above ECMs. Also calculate the **overall SEC** of the plant if its total annual energy consumption is 18,000 MWh of electricity and 20 million Nm³ of natural gas.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]**: Prepare a **sample Executive Summary** for the Energy Audit Report based on the above data. Also rank the ECMs by priority and justify the ranking.

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1: Total Investment Required**

$$I_{total} = 8 + 12 + 45 + 5 + 6 = \text{₹ } 76\ \text{Lakhs}$$

**Step 2: Total Annual Monetary Savings**

$$S_{total} = 7.2 + 5.4 + 22.0 + 4.8 + 3.0 = \text{₹ } 42.4\ \text{Lakhs/year}$$

**Step 3: Average Simple Payback Period (weighted average)**

$$SPP_{avg} = \frac{I_{total}}{S_{total}} = \frac{76}{42.4} = 1.79\ \text{years}$$

**Step 4: Convert all energy to a common unit (MJ) for SEC**

Electricity:

$$E_{elec} = 18{,}000\ MWh \times 1000\ kWh/MWh \times 3.6\ MJ/kWh = 64{,}800{,}000\ MJ$$

Natural Gas (assuming calorific value = 38 MJ/Nm³):

$$E_{gas} = 20 \times 10^6\ Nm^3 \times 38\ MJ/Nm^3 = 760{,}000{,}000\ MJ$$

Total Energy:

$$E_{total} = 64.8 \times 10^6 + 760 \times 10^6 = 824.8 \times 10^6\ MJ$$

**Step 5: SEC Calculation**

$$SEC = \frac{824.8 \times 10^6\ MJ}{50{,}000\ tonnes} = 16{,}496\ MJ/tonne \approx 16.5\ GJ/tonne$$

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Sample Executive Summary

**SAMPLE EXECUTIVE SUMMARY**

> **Plant**: XYZ Fertilizers Ltd.
> **Audit Period**: January 2024 – March 2024
> **Report Date**: 31 March 2024
> **Audited by**: ABC Energy Audit Firm (AEA Reg. No. XXXXX)
>
> **Plant Overview**: XYZ Fertilizers Ltd. is a 50,000 TPA ammonia-based fertilizer plant located in Kerala, with an annual energy expenditure of approximately ₹ 850 Lakhs.
>
> **Key Audit Findings**:
> - Total annual energy consumption: 824,800 GJ (≈ 19,694 TOE)
> - Specific Energy Consumption (SEC): 16.5 GJ/tonne
> - Sectoral BEE benchmark: 14.8 GJ/tonne
> - Performance gap: 1.7 GJ/tonne (plant is 11.5% above benchmark)
>
> **Identified Energy Conservation Opportunities**:
> - 5 nos. ECMs identified
> - Total Investment Required: **₹ 76 Lakhs**
> - Total Annual Monetary Savings: **₹ 42.4 Lakhs/year**
> - Average Payback Period: **1.79 years**
> - Estimated annual CO₂ reduction: **≈ 1,762 tonnes**
>
> **Recommendation**: All 5 ECMs are recommended for immediate implementation as they are technically feasible and financially viable (positive NPV at 10% discount rate).

---

**Priority Ranking of ECMs (Justified by Simple Payback Period):**

| Rank | ECM | SPP (years) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED retrofit (ECM 4) | 1.04 | Lowest investment, fastest payback |
| 2 | Motor replacement (ECM 1) | 1.11 | Quick wins, low technical risk |
| 3 | Boiler insulation (ECM 2) | 2.00 | Payback within 2 years |
| 4 | VFD on pump (ECM 3) | 2.22 | Process-critical, moderate risk |
| 5 | WHR from reformer (ECM 5) | 2.05 | Largest absolute savings, high impact |

> Note: Rank 5 (WHR) deserves strategic priority despite its higher payback because it provides the largest absolute energy savings (₹ 22 Lakhs/year) and the highest CO₂ reduction. Strategic priority ≠ Payback priority.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q: Discuss in detail the standardized format of an Energy Audit Report as prescribed by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE). Explain the key sections, their purpose, and the importance of the Executive Summary. List the financial metrics used to evaluate each Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) and derive the formula for Net Present Value (NPV).**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]**: Describe the **seven key sections** of a BEE-standardized Energy Audit Report with the purpose of each section.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]**: Explain any **four financial metrics** used for ECM evaluation. Derive the **NPV formula** and solve: An ECM requires ₹ 10 Lakhs investment and saves ₹ 3 Lakhs/year for 5 years. Discount rate is 12%. Is the project viable?

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Seven Sections of BEE-Standardized EAR

| Section No. | Section Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cover Page & Acknowledgement** | Identification of report, plant, audit firm, CEA/AEA signatures |
| 2 | **Executive Summary** | 1–2 page snapshot for top management — energy cost, savings, payback, key recommendations |
| 3 | **Plant Description & Process Flow** | Understanding of process, products, capacity, major equipment |
| 4 | **Energy Consumption Analysis** | Fuel-wise, utility-wise, product-wise breakdown; Sankey diagram of energy flow |
| 5 | **Energy Performance Assessment** | SEC/EPI calculation, BEE benchmark comparison, identification of inefficient areas |
| 6 | **Recommendations (ECMs)** | Detailed table with description, investment, savings, SPP, NPV, priority |
| 7 | **Implementation Schedule & M&T** | Phased plan (short/medium/long term), Monitoring & Targeting framework for verification |
| 8 (Optional) | **Appendices** | Raw data, instrument calibration, calculation sheets, glossary |

**Importance of Executive Summary**:
- It is the **only section read by top management** in most cases.
- Provides **at-a-glance financial viability** of the audit findings.
- Acts as a **decision-making trigger** for CAPEX approval.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Financial Metrics & NPV Derivation

**Four Key Financial Metrics:**

1. **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**: $SPP = \dfrac{Investment}{Annual\ Savings}$ — Measures how quickly the investment is recovered.

2. **Net Present Value (NPV)**: Considers time value of money — the difference between present value of cash inflows and outflows.

3. **Return on Investment (ROI)**: $ROI = \dfrac{Annual\ Savings}{Investment} \times 100\ (\%)$ — Measures percentage return.

4. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**: Discount rate at which NPV = 0. If IRR > hurdle rate, project is viable.

**Derivation of NPV:**

Consider an investment $I$ at time $t=0$ and annual cash flows $CF$ for $n$ years. The present value of future cash flow at time $t$ with discount rate $r$ is:

$$PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

Summing over $n$ years:

$$PV_{total} = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

Therefore, NPV is:

$$NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n}\frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Worked Example:**

- Investment $I$ = ₹ 10,00,000
- Annual saving $CF$ = ₹ 3,00,000
- $n = 5$ years, $r = 12\% = 0.12$

PVIFA calculation:

$$PVIFA = \frac{1 - (1+0.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = 3.6048$$

NPV:

$$NPV = -10{,}00{,}000 + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6048)$$

$$NPV = -10{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}81{,}440 = +\text{₹ } 81{,}440$$

Since $NPV > 0$, the project is **financially viable**. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not forget unit conversions** in SEC calculations. KTU examiners specifically test whether you converted kWh to MJ correctly (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ). Marks are deducted for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Do not write vague "energy is saved" statements** in the Executive Summary. Always quantify in ₹/year AND in physical units (kWh, Nm³, tonnes).
> 3. **Do not confuse SPP and NPV** — SPP does NOT consider time value of money; NPV does. A project can have short SPP but negative NPV if cash flows decline.
> 4. **Always state the discount rate** in NPV problems. Default 10–12% is acceptable if not specified, but explicit mention gets full marks.
> 5. **Do not omit the BEE benchmark comparison** in the SEC calculation. Without benchmark comparison, the audit report is incomplete.
> 6. **Remember to sign the report** as Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) in exam answers about report structure — BEE requires it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- **Definition**: Energy Audit Report = formal documented record of an energy audit's findings, recommendations, and financial analysis.
- **Legal basis**: **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** + **BEE guidelines** mandate standardized reports.
- **8 Sections**: Cover Page → Executive Summary → Plant Description → Energy Analysis → Performance Assessment → ECMs → Implementation → Appendices.
- **3 Core Metrics**: SEC (MJ/tonne), EPI (MJ/tonne), Energy Cost Share (%).
- **4 Financial Metrics**: SPP, NPV, ROI, IRR.
- **Priority Ranking**: Short SPP + High NPV + High CO₂ reduction + High technical feasibility = Top priority.
- **Constant Conversions**: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ; 1 Nm³ NG ≈ 38 MJ; 1 litre diesel ≈ 35.86 MJ.
- **CO₂ Emission Factor**: 1 TOE saved ≈ 4.16 tonnes CO₂ avoided.
- **Executive Summary length**: 1–2 pages, written for top management, contains all key financial numbers.
- **Sign-off Authority**: Report must be signed by **Certified Energy Auditor (CEA)** and counter-signed by **Accredited Energy Auditor (AEA)** for BEE compliance.
- **Monitoring & Targeting (M&T)**: Every audit report must include an M&T plan to verify post-implementation savings.
- **PAT Scheme Connection**: Reports of designated consumers feed into the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme of BEE.
- **Common Pitfall**: Never use SPP alone to judge viability — always cross-verify with NPV at appropriate discount rate (10–12%).

> **All the best for your KTU University Examination!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Quality Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Quality Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Quality (PQ)** refers to the conformance of electrical power to a set of predefined technical parameters — including voltage magnitude, frequency, waveform shape, symmetry, and continuity — that ensure the reliable and efficient operation of electrical and electronic equipment.

A **Power Quality Audit (PQA)** is a systematic, measurement-based evaluation of an electrical installation aimed at quantifying the quality of power delivered and consumed. It involves:

- **Identifying** power quality disturbances (harmonics, sags, swells, transients, unbalance, flicker).
- **Measuring** key electrical parameters using calibrated instruments.
- **Analyzing** the data against international benchmarks (IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, IEC 61000).
- **Reporting** findings and recommending corrective actions (filters, capacitor banks, UPS, voltage regulators).

Formally, in the framework of **Energy Management and Auditing (PEEET742)**, the Power Quality Audit is the **electrical-quality counterpart of the Energy Audit** — where the energy audit focuses on the *quantity* (kWh, kVAh, demand) of energy, the power quality audit focuses on the *quality* (distortion, deviation, purity) of that energy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Water Quality vs. Water Quantity"**
>
> Imagine water flowing into your home through pipes.
> - An **Energy Audit** is like checking *how much* water you use (cubic meters per month) and finding leaks.
> - A **Power Quality Audit** is like checking *how clean and steady* that water is — is it muddy (harmonics)? Does the pressure drop suddenly when your neighbor opens a tap (voltage sag)? Does it surge randomly (transient/spike)?
>
> Even if you have plenty of water (energy), dirty or pulsating water (poor power quality) can damage washing machines, boilers, and pumps. Similarly, plenty of electricity with poor quality damages motors, PLCs, drives, and computers.

So a Power Quality Audit is essentially a **"health check-up of electricity"** — using instruments like a doctor uses a stethoscope.

## 1.3 Why Power Quality Audit Matters — KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Poor power quality causes:
> - **Equipment failure** (capacitor burnout, motor overheating, drive tripping).
> - **Process interruption** (PLC resets, sensor misreads).
> - **Energy losses** (harmonics increase $I^2R$ losses, poor PF attracts penalties).
> - **Premature aging** of insulation due to harmonic heating.
>
> Hence PQ audit is a **non-negotiable component** of any comprehensive Energy Management Program.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

| Parameter | Standard Reference | Tolerance / Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage (LT India) | **230 V (single-phase) / 415 V (three-phase)** | ±10% as per Indian Electricity Rules |
| Nominal Frequency | **50 Hz** | ±1% (49.5 – 50.5 Hz) as per CBSE grid code |
| Power Frequency | **50 Hz** | ±5% momentary, ±1% sustained |
| THD (Voltage) | ≤ **5%** (IEEE 519 at PCC) | Individual harmonic limits apply |
| THD (Current) | Depends on $I_{SC}/I_L$ ratio | IEEE 519 Table |
| Power Factor | **Unity (1.0)** ideal | Discom penalty if < 0.85–0.90 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Power Quality Parameters — Structured Breakdown

A Power Quality Audit measures and analyses **seven fundamental parameters**. Each is explained below with the underlying *why* and *how*:

### 2.1.1 Voltage Magnitude (RMS)
- **Definition**: The root-mean-square value of the sinusoidal voltage waveform.
- **Why it matters**: Sustained over-voltage stresses insulation; sustained under-voltage (brownout) causes motors to draw excessive current and overheat.
- **KTU Recognition**: Voltage deviations classified as:
  - **Sag (dip)**: 0.1 pu to 0.9 pu, duration 0.5 cycle to 1 min.
  - **Swell**: 1.1 pu to 1.8 pu, similar duration.
  - **Interruption**: < 0.1 pu.

### 2.1.2 Frequency Deviation
- **Why it matters**: Synchronous motors and clocks depend on stable frequency. Frequency drift indicates generation–load imbalance.
- **KTU Recognition**: Indian grid maintains **49.9 – 50.05 Hz** typical; > 49.5 / < 50.5 Hz is an audit flag.

### 2.1.3 Harmonics
- **Definition**: Sinusoidal components at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency (50 Hz). Harmonics are caused by **non-linear loads** (VFDs, SMPS, LED drivers, arc furnaces, UPS, rectifiers).
- **Why it matters**: They distort the voltage waveform, increase neutral current, cause capacitor failures, and create torque pulsations in motors.

### 2.1.4 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)
- **Why it matters**: A single-number metric to quantify waveform distortion.

### 2.1.5 Voltage Unbalance (Negative Sequence)
- **Why it matters**: Three-phase induction motors are highly sensitive. A 2% voltage unbalance can cause a motor to overheat by ~12–15%.
- **KTU Recognition**: NEMA MG-1 limits unbalance to ≤ **1%** for long motor life.

### 2.1.6 Transients
- **Impulsive transients**: Lightning, capacitor switching (microseconds).
- **Oscillatory transients**: Switching events (kHz range).
- **Why it matters**: Can destroy semiconductor devices (thyristors, IGBTs).

### 2.1.7 Flicker
- **Definition**: Visual instability of lamps due to rapid voltage fluctuations (typically 0.5–25 Hz).
- **Why it matters**: Causes irritation, fatigue, and is a sign of large intermittent loads (arc furnaces, welding).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet for Exam Hall

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are EXAM-CRITICAL for Module 1 of PEEET742.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RMS Voltage | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$ | Volt (V) |
| 2 | RMS Current | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T i^2(t)\, dt}$ | Ampere (A) |
| 3 | THD (Voltage) | $\text{THD}_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | Per IEEE 519 |
| 4 | THD (Current) | $\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$ | Per IEEE 519 |
| 5 | True PF | $\text{PF} = \dfrac{P}{S} = \cos\phi_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}$ | Displacement × Distortion |
| 6 | Distortion PF | $\text{PF}_{dist} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}$ | Less than 1 always |
| 7 | Voltage Unbalance (NEMA) | $\%V_{unb} = \dfrac{\max \vert V_{ab}-V_{avg}\vert}{V_{avg}} \times 100\,\%$ | Use line voltages |
| 8 | Voltage Unbalance (IEEE) | $\dfrac{V_{-}}{V_{+}} \times 100\,\%$ | Negative/Positive seq. |
| 9 | Crest Factor | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | Pure sine = 1.414 |
| 10 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | Pure sine = 1.11 |
| 11 | K-Factor (Transformer) | $K = \dfrac{\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} I_h^2 \, h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} I_h^2}$ | For derating |
| 12 | Neutral Current (3φ, balanced) | $I_N = 3 \sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + \cdots}$ | Triplen harmonics add |
| 13 | Active Power | $P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Watt (W) |
| 14 | Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | VAR |
| 15 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L = \sqrt{P^2+Q^2}$ | VA |
| 16 | Voltage Sag Depth | $\text{Sag}\% = \dfrac{V_{nominal}-V_{actual}}{V_{nominal}}\times 100$ | At PCC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are used by PQ engineers to compute distortion levels, transformer derating, capacitor sizing for PF correction, harmonic filter design, and to assess compliance with IEEE 519 / Indian CEA standards.

## 2.3 Power Quality Audit — Step-by-Step Methodology

A formal PQ Audit follows a structured **5-phase methodology**:

1. **Pre-Audit Planning** — Identify PCCs (Points of Common Coupling), non-linear load inventory, single-line diagram review.
2. **Field Measurement** — Install Class-A PQ analyzers (Fluke 435, Hioki PW3198, Dranetz HDPQ) for minimum **one complete working week** (covers all shift patterns).
3. **Data Logging** — Capture waveforms, RMS trends, events with timestamps at sampling rate ≥ 128 samples/cycle.
4. **Analysis & Benchmarking** — Compare against IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, IEC 61000-2-2/4-30, and CEA (India) Technical Standards for Connectivity.
5. **Reporting & Recommendations** — Mitigation plan: passive/active harmonic filters, K-rated transformers, PF correction, isolation transformers, UPS.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of THD (Total Harmonic Distortion)

By definition, THD is the ratio of RMS of all harmonic components to the RMS of the fundamental.

**Step 1**: Total RMS of a distorted periodic signal containing harmonics.

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_h^2 + \cdots}
$$

**Step 2**: In any power-quality analysis, the *fundamental* $V_1$ is treated as the reference. Define the harmonic content RMS:

$$
V_H = \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}
$$

**Step 3**: Take the ratio. Multiply by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$
\text{THD}_V = \dfrac{V_H}{V_1} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4**: Substitute $V_H$ explicitly:

$$
\text{THD}_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5**: Note that IEEE 519 truncates the summation at $h = 50$ for practical measurement (since beyond the 50th harmonic, energy is negligible):

$$
\text{THD}_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{50} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *definition*. In the lab, instruments compute it using a **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** on sampled data.

## 3.2 Derivation of True Power Factor in Presence of Harmonics

**Step 1**: Start with general definition of PF.

$$
\text{PF} = \dfrac{P}{S}
$$

**Step 2**: Active power is the product of fundamental voltage, fundamental current, and the cosine of the angle between them (the displacement angle $\phi_1$):

$$
P = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1
$$

**Step 3**: Apparent power uses total RMS values (which include harmonics):

$$
S = V_{rms} \, I_{rms}
$$

**Step 4**: For a non-sinusoidal waveform, only the fundamental contributes to active power transfer. The harmonics cause additional RMS heating but no net active energy delivery per cycle. Thus:

$$
P = V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1
$$

**Step 5**: The current RMS expands to:

$$
I_{rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_2^2 + I_3^2 + \cdots} = I_1\sqrt{1 + \text{THD}_I^2}
$$

**Step 6**: Assuming the voltage waveform is relatively clean ($V_{rms} \approx V_1$), we get:

$$
S = V_1 \cdot I_1\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}
$$

**Step 7**: Form the PF:

$$
\text{PF} = \dfrac{V_1 I_1 \cos\phi_1}{V_1 I_1 \sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}
$$

**Step 8**: Simplify by cancelling $V_1 I_1$:

$$
\text{PF} = \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}
$$

**Step 9**: Identify the two multiplicative factors:

$$
\text{PF} = \underbrace{\cos\phi_1}_{\text{Displacement PF}} \times \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}}_{\text{Distortion PF}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight**: Even with a unity displacement PF (perfectly timed current w.r.t. voltage), the presence of harmonics drags the true PF below 1.0. This is why *capacitor-only* PF correction fails in modern harmonic-rich plants — **active or detuned filters** are needed.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Calculating THD and True PF

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example**: A three-phase industrial bus has the following measurements:
> - Fundamental line current: $I_1 = 100\,\text{A}$
> - 5th harmonic current: $I_5 = 30\,\text{A}$
> - 7th harmonic current: $I_7 = 15\,\text{A}$
> - 11th harmonic current: $I_{11} = 10\,\text{A}$
> - Displacement PF: $\cos\phi_1 = 0.92$ (lagging)
>
> **Find**: (a) THD of current, (b) True PF.

### Part (a) — Current THD

**Step 1**: Compute harmonic RMS content.

$$
I_H = \sqrt{I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_{11}^2}
$$

**Step 2**: Square each value.

$$
I_5^2 = 900,\quad I_7^2 = 225,\quad I_{11}^2 = 100
$$

**Step 3**: Sum them.

$$
I_H^2 = 900 + 225 + 100 = 1225
$$

**Step 4**: Take square root.

$$
I_H = \sqrt{1225} = 35.0 \,\text{A}
$$

**Step 5**: Apply THD formula.

$$
\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{I_H}{I_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{35.0}{100} \times 100 = 35.0\%
$$

### Part (b) — True PF

**Step 1**: Use the derived formula.

$$
\text{PF} = \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}
$$

**Step 2**: Convert THD to decimal.

$$
\text{THD}_I = 0.35
$$

**Step 3**: Square it.

$$
\text{THD}_I^2 = 0.1225
$$

**Step 4**: Add 1.

$$
1 + 0.1225 = 1.1225
$$

**Step 5**: Take square root.

$$
\sqrt{1.1225} = 1.0595
$$

**Step 6**: Divide displacement PF by the distortion factor.

$$
\text{PF} = \dfrac{0.92}{1.0595} = 0.868 \text{ (lagging)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: Despite a healthy displacement PF of 0.92, the actual operating PF is **0.868** — a significant drop caused entirely by current harmonics. This will attract utility penalty and increase line losses by $\left(\frac{1}{0.868}\right)^2 - 1 = 32.7\%$ extra.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common PQ Problems and Solutions

| PQ Issue | Symptom | Root Cause | Standard Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Sag | Motor stalling, PLC reset | Large load starting, grid fault | UPS, DVR, Sag ride-through |
| Harmonics (5th, 7th) | Capacitor burn, breaker trip | 6-pulse rectifiers, VFDs | Passive/Active Harmonic Filter |
| Triplen (3rd, 9th) | Neutral overheating | Single-phase non-linear loads | K-rated transformer, oversized neutral |
| Poor PF | Utility penalty | Inductive motors, lightly loaded | APFC panel, detuned filter |
| Voltage Unbalance | Motor overheat | Single-phase loads, loose connections | Load balancing, re-phasing |
| Transients | Component damage | Lightning, capacitor switching | Surge Protection Device (SPD) |
| Flicker | Lamp flicker, fatigue | Arc furnace, welding | SVC, STATCOM |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

## 4.2 Power Quality Disturbance Classification Flow (IEEE 1159)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Power Quality Disturbances"] --> P1["Voltage Magnitude"]
    P --> P2["Waveform Distortion"]
    P --> P3["Frequency Deviation"]
    P --> P4["Transients"]
    P1 --> P1a["Sag 0.1 to 0.9 pu"]
    P1 --> P1b["Swell 1.1 to 1.8 pu"]
    P1 --> P1c["Interruption below 0.1 pu"]
    P2 --> P2a["Harmonics DC to 9 kHz"]
    P2 --> P2b["Interharmonics Non Integer"]
    P2 --> P2c["Notching Commutation"]
    P2 --> P2d["Noise High Freq"]
    P3 --> P3a["Underfrequency below 49.5 Hz"]
    P3 --> P3b["Overfrequency above 50.5 Hz"]
    P4 --> P4a["Impulsive Lightning Microseconds"]
    P4 --> P4b["Oscillatory Capacitor Switch kHz"]
```

## 4.3 Harmonic Order and Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H["Harmonic Order h"] --> S1["Positive Sequence"]
    H --> S2["Negative Sequence"]
    H --> S3["Zero Sequence Triplen"]
    S1 --> P1["1st 4th 7th 10th 13th"]
    S2 --> P2["2nd 5th 8th 11th 14th"]
    S3 --> P3["3rd 6th 9th 12th 15th"]
    P3 --> P3N["Add in Neutral of 3 phi 4 wire"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide**: For example, the 5th harmonic (250 Hz) is **negative sequence** — it produces a counter-rotating field in three-phase motors, causing braking torque and overheating. The 3rd, 9th, 15th are **triplen** — they add in the neutral conductor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    M --> M2["Load Management"]
    M --> M3["Pathway Treatment"]

    M2 --> M2b["Isolation transformers"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define Power Quality Audit. List any four power quality parameters evaluated during an audit.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Power Quality Audit (PQA)** is a systematic measurement-based evaluation of an electrical installation to identify deviations in voltage, current, frequency, and waveform from the ideal sinusoidal supply, with the goal of ensuring equipment reliability, energy efficiency, and regulatory compliance.

**Four Power Quality Parameters:**

1. **Voltage magnitude (RMS)** — sag, swell, interruption.
2. **Frequency deviation** — drift from 50 Hz.
3. **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — voltage and current.
4. **Voltage unbalance** — magnitude asymmetry in three phases.

*(Other valid parameters: flicker, transients, crest factor, power factor, neutral current.)*

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks (4 × 0.5) for parameters.]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
> *What is Total Harmonic Distortion? State the IEEE 519 limit for voltage THD at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC).*

**Model Answer:**

**THD** is the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (from the 2nd harmonic upward) to the RMS value of the fundamental, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\text{THD}_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{50} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%
$$

**IEEE 519 Limit for Voltage THD at PCC**: For systems below 69 kV with $I_{SC}/I_L < 20$, the **individual harmonic limit is 3%** and the **total voltage THD limit is 5%**.

*(This limit is the global benchmark used by utilities and auditors to certify compliance.)*

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark IEEE 519 limit.]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme — ESE Pattern, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

### 🔷 Question Choice A — (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** *(Module 1 — PEA)*
> **(a)** Explain the different categories of power quality disturbances as classified by **IEEE 1159**, with a neat classification diagram. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A 415 V, 50 Hz, three-phase industrial feeder supplies a 6-pulse VFD cluster. The harmonic current spectrum measured at the PCC is as follows:

| Harmonic Order (h) | 1 | 5 | 7 | 11 | 13 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current (A) | 200 | 80 | 50 | 30 | 15 |

> The displacement power factor is 0.95 lagging. Calculate:
> (i) Total Current THD
> (ii) True Power Factor
> (iii) Total RMS line current **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Choice A

**(a) IEEE 1159 Classification (7 Marks):**

IEEE 1159 categorizes power quality phenomena into **seven major classes**:

1. **Transients**
   - *Impulsive* (µs): Lightning, ESD — unidirectional, high di/dt.
   - *Oscillatory* (ms–µs): Capacitor switching — bidirectional, divided into low, medium, high frequency sub-classes.

2. **Short-duration RMS variations**
   - *Sag (dip)*: 0.1–0.9 pu, 0.5 cycle – 1 min.
   - *Swell*: 1.1–1.8 pu, 0.5 cycle – 1 min.
   - *Interruption*: < 0.1 pu, up to 1 min.

3. **Long-duration RMS variations**
   - *Overvoltage*, *Undervoltage*, *Sustained Interruption* (> 1 min).

4. **Voltage Unbalance** — magnitude or phase asymmetry in 3-phase systems.

5. **Waveform Distortion**
   - *DC offset*, *Harmonics*, *Interharmonics*, *Notching*, *Noise*.

6. **Voltage Fluctuations** — repetitive or random RMS variations (< 25 Hz envelope).

7. **Power Frequency Variations** — drift from nominal 50 Hz due to generation–load imbalance.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for naming the 7 categories + 6 marks for 2-line description each. Diagram is bonus.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $I_1 = 200\,\text{A}$, $I_5 = 80\,\text{A}$, $I_7 = 50\,\text{A}$, $I_{11} = 30\,\text{A}$, $I_{13} = 15\,\text{A}$
- $\cos\phi_1 = 0.95$ (lagging)
- $V_L = 415\,\text{V}$, 3-phase

---

**(i) Total Current THD:**

**Step 1**: Identify the harmonic content RMS.

$$
I_H = \sqrt{I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_{11}^2 + I_{13}^2}
$$

**Step 2**: Square each value.

$$
I_5^2 = 6400, \quad I_7^2 = 2500, \quad I_{11}^2 = 900, \quad I_{13}^2 = 225
$$

**Step 3**: Sum them.

$$
I_H^2 = 6400 + 2500 + 900 + 225 = 10025
$$

**Step 4**: Take square root.

$$
I_H = \sqrt{10025} \approx 100.12\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 5**: Apply THD formula.

$$
\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{I_H}{I_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{100.12}{200} \times 100 = 50.06\%
$$

> *(2 marks for full derivation)*

---

**(ii) True Power Factor:**

**Step 1**: Express THD as decimal.

$$
\text{THD}_I = 0.5006
$$

**Step 2**: Square it.

$$
\text{THD}_I^2 = 0.2506
$$

**Step 3**: Add 1.

$$
1 + 0.2506 = 1.2506
$$

**Step 4**: Take square root.

$$
\sqrt{1.2506} \approx 1.1183
$$

**Step 5**: Divide displacement PF by the distortion factor.

$$
\text{PF} = \dfrac{0.95}{1.1183} \approx 0.8495 \text{ (lagging)}
$$

> *(2 marks)*

---

**(iii) Total RMS Line Current:**

**Step 1**: Use the relation:

$$
I_{rms} = I_1 \sqrt{1 + \text{THD}_I^2}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute values.

$$
I_{rms} = 200 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.2506}
$$

**Step 3**: Simplify.

$$
I_{rms} = 200 \times 1.1183 \approx 223.66\,\text{A}
$$

> *(2 marks)*

**Final Answers:**

- (i) $\text{THD}_I \approx 50.06\%$
- (ii) True PF $\approx 0.85$ lagging
- (iii) $I_{rms} \approx 223.66\,\text{A}$

> **(1 mark bonus for units & inference comment)**

---

### 🔷 Question Choice B — (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** *(Module 1 — PEA)*
> **(a)** What is **Power Quality Audit**? Explain its step-by-step methodology, including pre-audit planning, measurement, analysis, and reporting. State the relevant Indian and international standards. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A 230 V single-phase non-linear load draws a fundamental current of 10 A at unity displacement PF and a 3rd harmonic current of 6 A. Compute:
> (i) Current THD
> (ii) True PF
> (iii) Neutral current in a 3-phase, 4-wire system feeding three identical such loads **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Choice B

**(a) Power Quality Audit — Methodology and Standards (7 Marks):**

A **Power Quality Audit** is a structured investigation of the electrical supply's conformity to standards, focused on identifying disturbances that affect equipment performance and energy efficiency.

**Five-Phase Methodology:**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Planning (1 Mark):**
- Collect single-line diagram, load list, and billing data.
- Identify non-linear loads (VFDs, SMPS, UPS, LED drivers, rectifiers).
- Select Points of Common Coupling (PCC).
- Define measurement objectives and duration (≥ 7 days, covering all shift patterns).

**Phase 2 — Instrument Selection & Installation (1 Mark):**
- Use **Class-A PQ analyzers** (per IEC 61000-4-30) with sampling rate ≥ 128 samples/cycle.
- Examples: Fluke 435-II, Hioki PW3198, Dranetz HDPQ.
- Install CTs and PTs at each PCC.

**Phase 3 — Data Logging (1 Mark):**
- Capture waveforms, RMS trends, transients, harmonics (up to 50th).
- Record events with timestamps.

**Phase 4 — Analysis & Benchmarking (2 Marks):**
- Compute THD, TDD, unbalance, flicker, PF, K-factor.
- Compare against:
  - **IEEE 519** — harmonic limits.
  - **IEEE 1159** — PQ monitoring.
  - **IEC 61000-2-2 / 4-30** — compatibility levels.
  - **CEA (India) Technical Standards for Connectivity** of the Distributed Generation Resources.

**Phase 5 — Reporting & Mitigation (2 Marks):**
- Tabulate findings vs. limits.
- Recommend solutions: passive/active filters, K-rated transformers, APFC panels, UPS, SPDs.
- Cost–benefit analysis of mitigation.
- Re-verification audit after implementation.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $V = 230\,\text{V (single-phase)}$
- $I_1 = 10\,\text{A}$ at $\cos\phi_1 = 1.0$
- $I_3 = 6\,\text{A}$

---

**(i) Current THD (2 marks):**

**Step 1**: Harmonic content RMS — only 3rd harmonic is non-zero.

$$
I_H = \sqrt{I_3^2} = 6\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply formula.

$$
\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{I_H}{I_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{6}{10} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

---

**(ii) True PF (2 marks):**

**Step 1**: Convert THD to decimal.

$$
\text{THD}_I = 0.60
$$

**Step 2**: Square it.

$$
\text{THD}_I^2 = 0.36
$$

**Step 3**: Compute distortion factor.

$$
\sqrt{1 + 0.36} = \sqrt{1.36} = 1.1662
$$

**Step 4**: Divide.

$$
\text{PF} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.1662} \approx 0.8575 \text{ lagging (or leading, depending on $\phi_1$)}
$$

Since $\cos\phi_1 = 1$, the displacement PF is unity, so the drop is entirely due to distortion.

---

**(iii) Neutral Current in 3-phase, 4-wire System (3 marks):**

When three identical single-phase non-linear loads are connected across a 3-phase, 4-wire system (one between each phase and neutral), the **triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th, …) add arithmetically in the neutral** because they are zero-sequence.

**Step 1**: Each phase carries 3rd harmonic current of 6 A.

**Step 2**: All three 3rd harmonics are in phase (zero sequence), so they add directly in the neutral:

$$
I_N = 3 \times I_3 = 3 \times 6 = 18\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 3**: In general, for a 3-phase, 4-wire system with triplen harmonics, the neutral current is:

$$
I_N = 3 \sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + \cdots}
$$

Since only 3rd harmonic is present:

$$
I_N = 3 \times 6 = 18\,\text{A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Inference**: The **neutral current (18 A) is higher than the phase fundamental current (10 A)**. This is a classic signature of triplen harmonics and causes severe neutral overheating in commercial buildings with large LED/SMPS loads. **Mitigation**: Use K-rated transformers and oversized neutral conductors.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\text{THD}_I = 60\%$
- (ii) True PF $\approx 0.857$
- (iii) $I_N = 18\,\text{A}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks
>
> **Common pitfalls in Power Quality Audit questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PF types** — Writing just $\cos\phi$ when the question asks for *True PF*. Always check if THD data is provided; if yes, use the formula $\text{PF} = \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** — THD is conventionally expressed as a **percentage**, not a ratio.
> 3. **Mixing RMS and peak** — When computing $I_{rms}$ from harmonics, use **squared sum of squares**, not arithmetic sum. This is the single most common error.
> 4. **Ignoring units** — THD has no unit (% or pu). PF is dimensionless. Current is in Amperes (A). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Wrong formula for triplen neutral current** — Use **arithmetic sum (×3)** for identical loads, NOT $\sqrt{3}$. The $\sqrt{3}$ applies only to positive/negative sequence components.
> 6. **Missing the standards question** — Always mention at least **two standards** (e.g., IEEE 519 + IEEE 1159). Examiners look for standard names in PQ answers.
> 7. **Skipping the inference/comment line** — In numerical answers, always end with one sentence interpreting the result (e.g., "THD exceeds IEEE 519 limit of 5%; harmonic filter recommended").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Power Quality Audit** = "Health check-up of electricity" — measures voltage, current, frequency, harmonics, transients, flicker, unbalance.

> ✅ The **7 IEEE 1159 PQ categories**: Transients, Short-duration RMS variations, Long-duration RMS variations, Unbalance, Waveform distortion, Voltage fluctuations, Frequency variations.

> ✅ **IEEE 519 Voltage THD limit at PCC** = **5%** (individual harmonic ≤ 3%) for low-voltage systems.

> ✅ **THD Formula**: $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{50} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$

> ✅ **True PF Formula (with harmonics)**: $\text{PF} = \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\sqrt{1+\text{THD}_I^2}}$

> ✅ **Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th)** are zero sequence and **add in the neutral** of 3-phase, 4-wire systems.

> ✅ **5th harmonic** is **negative sequence** — causes motor overheating and counter-torque.

> ✅ **Class-A PQ Analyzers** (per IEC 61000-4-30) are mandatory for compliance-grade audits.

> ✅ **Mitigation Hierarchy**: Source → Load → Pathway. Passive filters, active filters, K-rated transformers, UPS, DVR, STATCOM.

> ✅ **Audit Duration**: Minimum **one full working week** (7 days, all shifts) to capture all operational scenarios.

> ✅ **Key Standards to Quote in Exams**: IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, IEEE 1453 (flicker), IEC 61000-2-2/4-30, CEA Technical Standards for Connectivity (India).

> ✅ **Two exam-readiness tips**:
> - For Part B 14-mark questions, **always draw a block diagram** (audit methodology OR mitigation hierarchy) — gets you 1–2 easy marks.
> - For 3-mark Part A questions, **one formula + one sentence of interpretation** is the safest full-mark template.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation in Buildings: ECBC Code (Basic Aspects)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy Conservation in Buildings: ECBC Code (Basic Aspects) # MODULE 1 — ENERGY CONSERVATION IN BUILDINGS: ECBC CODE (BASIC ASPECTS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** is a code developed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the **Ministry of Power, Government of India**, first published in **2007**. It prescribes the minimum energy performance standards for the **building envelope, HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning), lighting, and electrical systems** of commercial buildings having a connected load of **100 kW or 120 kVA** (or a contract demand of 250 kVA) and a built-up area exceeding **1,000 m²**. The ECBC is the **Indian counterpart** of international standards such as **ASHRAE 90.1 (USA)** and the **Building Regulations (UK)**. It is designed to achieve **50%–75% energy savings** over conventional (pre-ECBC) Indian buildings by setting prescriptive, performance, and trade-off compliance paths. ### 1.2 Scope of ECBC | Component | Description | | :--- | :--- | | **Building Envelope** | Opaque walls, roof, glazing (windows), shading, and insulation | | **HVAC Systems** | Heating, cooling, ventilation, fans, and water heating | | **Lighting Systems** | Interior and exterior illumination with control strategies | | **Electrical Systems** | Transformers, motors, lifts, pumps, and renewable integration | | **Solar Hot Water & Daylighting** | Passive architectural features | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** ECBC applies **only to commercial buildings** (offices, malls, hotels, hospitals). It does **NOT** apply to residential buildings — that is covered by **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Diet Plan" Imagine your building is a **human body**: - The **food** it eats every day = **electricity and fuel** (energy input). - The **work it does** = cooling, lighting, operating equipment (energy output). - A **building that is wasteful** (poor insulation, inefficient AC, leaky windows) is like a person **eating double the calories needed** and still feeling lethargic. The **ECBC** is essentially a **diet plan and gym membership** prescribed for the building: - **Insulation** → Its warm jacket (reduces energy loss). - **Efficient glazing** → UV-filtering sunglasses (blocks unwanted heat). - **Energy-efficient HVAC** → A treadmill that burns less energy while doing more work. - **Lighting controls** → Automatic switches that turn off lights when no one is around. If the building follows this "diet," it becomes **slim (low energy consumption), healthy (low carbon footprint), and productive (lower utility bills)**. ### 1.4 Key Stakeholders and Origin | Element | Detail | | :--- | :--- | | **Developing Agency** | Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India | | **Year of Launch** | 2007 (1st edition); Revised 2017 (ECBC 2017, with 25%–50% further savings) | | **Ministry** | Ministry of Power, Government of India | | **Foundation** | **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** (Section 14(p) and 14(w)) | | **Coverage** | All 5 climatic zones of India — Composite, Hot-Dry, Warm-Humid, Temperate, Cold | > [!NOTE] > **Climatic Zones Reference:** India has been classified into **five climatic zones** by ECBC based on monthly mean temperature and relative humidity. Building envelope requirements (wall U-factor, roof U-factor, SHGC) differ zone-wise because of varying solar insolation and ambient temperature conditions. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why ECBC Was Needed — The Energy Crisis Context India's commercial sector consumes **~30% of total electricity** in the country, and this share is growing at **8%–10% per year**. Buildings alone are projected to account for **~80% of new electricity demand by 2030** in urban areas. Conventional Indian commercial buildings are typically **2 to 3 times more energy-intensive** than global benchmarks of the same climate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Without ECBC compliance, a typical Indian commercial building consumes **180–250 kWh/m²/year**. With ECBC compliance, this drops to **90–120 kWh/m²/year** — a **~50% reduction** in Energy Performance Index (EPI). ## 2.2 ECBC Compliance Approaches — Three Pathways ECBC offers **three compliance options**, allowing flexibility while ensuring equivalent energy performance: ### Pathway 1: Prescriptive Method - Each component (wall, roof, window, AC, lighting) must meet **prescriptive minimum requirements** (e.g., wall U-value ≤ prescribed limit). - **Simplest** to apply; recommended for **small-to-medium buildings**. - Compliance is checked component-by-component against tables. ### Pathway 2: Trade-off Method - Designer can **trade performance between components** — e.g., a slightly worse roof insulation compensated by a better window SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient). - Allows design flexibility while keeping **overall building energy performance constant**. - Uses a **simulated energy budget** to verify. ### Pathway 3: Whole Building Performance Method - A **dynamic building energy simulation tool** (e.g., EnergyPlus, eQUEST, IES VE) is used to model the entire building. - The simulated annual energy consumption must be **≤ ECBC-compliant baseline building** (typically ≤ 120 kWh/m²/year for warm-humid zones). - **Most accurate** but requires specialized software and trained personnel. ## 2.3 Building Envelope — The "Shell" of the Building The envelope separates the **interior conditioned space** from the **outside environment**. Heat transfer through the envelope occurs via three mechanisms: | Mechanism | Description | Controlling Property | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Conduction** | Heat transfer through opaque materials (walls, roof) | **U-factor (W/m²·K)** | | **Radiation** | Solar heat gain through transparent surfaces (glass) | **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient)** | | **Infiltration** | Uncontrolled air leakage through cracks | **Air Leakage Rate (ACH₅₀)** | ### 2.3.1 Overall Thermal Transmittance (U-factor) The U-factor quantifies heat flow per unit area per unit temperature difference: $$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{R_{si} + R_{1} + R_{2} + \cdots + R_{n} + R_{se}}$$ Where: - $R_{si}$ = Inner surface resistance (typically **0.13 m²·K/W**) - $R_{se}$ = Outer surface resistance (typically **0.04 m²·K/W**) - $R_{i} = \dfrac{L_{i}}{k_{i}}$ = Thermal resistance of each layer - $L_{i}$ = Thickness of layer *i* (in meters) - $k_{i}$ = Thermal conductivity of material *i* (in W/m·K) **Units:** W/m²·K (Watts per square meter per Kelvin) **Lower U-factor** = **Better insulation** = **Less heat transfer**. ### 2.3.2 Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC) SHGC is the fraction of incident solar radiation that enters a building through a window: $$SHGC = \tau + \alpha \cdot \eta$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Transmittance of glass - $\alpha$ = Absorptance of glass - $\eta$ = Heat transfer factor inward (typically 0.5 for indoor conditions) **Range:** 0 (no solar gain) to 1 (full solar gain) **Lower SHGC** = **Less heat gain** = **Lower cooling load in hot climates**. ### 2.3.3 Visible Light Transmittance (VLT) The fraction of visible light (380–780 nm) passing through glass. There is an **inherent trade-off** between SHGC and VLT — to reduce heat gain, one must typically reduce visible light too. ECBC specifies minimum VLT for daylighting compliance. ## 2.4 HVAC — Heating, Ventilation & Air Conditioning ECBC specifies minimum efficiency standards for: - **Chillers** → Minimum **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** or **kW/Ton**. - **Air-Conditioning Units (Split/Window)** → Minimum **Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio (ISEER)**. - **Pumps and Fans** → Minimum motor efficiency and VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) usage. - **Ductwork and Piping** → Minimum **R-value (thermal insulation)**. **Key Relationships:** $$COP = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$$ $$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$ $$\eta_{HVAC} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}} \times 100\%$$ ## 2.5 Lighting Systems ECBC mandates: - **Maximum LPD (Lighting Power Density)**: 0.9 to 1.2 W/ft² (≈ 9.7 to 13 W/m²) depending on space type. - Use of **LED fixtures** with minimum efficacy of **≥ 80 lumens/W**. - Mandatory **occupancy sensors** in conference rooms, restrooms, and stairwells. - Mandatory **daylight dimming controls** for perimeter zones near windows. **Lighting Efficacy Equation:** $$\eta_{luminaire} = \frac{\Phi_{lumens}}{P_{watts}} \quad \text{(lumens/Watt)}$$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{conditioned}}$ | kWh/m²/year | Primary ECBC compliance metric | | 2 | **U-factor** | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$ | W/m²·K | Wall/Roof insulation | | 3 | **R-value (single layer)** | $R = \dfrac{L}{k}$ | m²·K/W | Insulation thickness design | | 4 | **R-value (composite)** | $R_{total} = R_{si} + \sum R_{i} + R_{se}$ | m²·K/W | Multi-layer wall | | 5 | **SHGC** | $SHGC = \tau + \alpha \cdot \eta$ | dimensionless | Window solar control | | 6 | **COP** | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | dimensionless | Chiller efficiency | | 7 | **ISEER** | $ISEER = \dfrac{TC}{EC \cdot 1000}$ | Wh/Wh | AC seasonal efficiency | | 8 | **Cooling Load** | $Q_{c} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T + SHGC \cdot I \cdot A$ | W or kW | HVAC sizing | | 9 | **LPD** | $LPD = \dfrac{P_{lighting}}{A_{lit}}$ | W/m² | Lighting design | | 10 | **Luminaire Efficacy** | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | lumens/W | Lamp efficiency | | 11 | **1 TR equivalent** | $1 TR \equiv 3.517 kW$ | — | Capacity conversion | | 12 | **ACH** | $ACH = \dfrac{Q_{air}}{V_{room}}$ | h⁻¹ | Ventilation rate | | 13 | **Heat Gain through Wall** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD$ | W | Envelope heat transfer | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions:** All ECBC compliance calculations assume **standard operating schedules**, **standard occupancy densities**, and **TMY (Typical Meteorological Year) weather data** for the city in which the building is located. ## 2.7 Energy Performance Index (EPI) — The Master Indicator EPI is the **single most important metric** in ECBC.
