<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between HVAC and HVDC transmission systems. List any two advantages of HVDC.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | HVAC | HVDC |
|---|---|---|
| Power transfer capacity | Limited by stability (Surge Impedance Loading) | No stability limit |
| Conductor requirement | 3 conductors | 2 conductors (less copper) |
| Charging current | Significant on long lines | Absent |
| Cost break-even distance | — | ~ 500 km (overhead) / ~ 50 km (cable) |

**Two advantages of HVDC:**
1. **Lower losses** for long-distance bulk power transfer (no reactive power flow, no skin-effect penalty).
2. **Asynchronous interconnection** of two grids of different frequencies (e.g., India Southern Grid 50 Hz ↔ future ASEAN interconnections).

*(Award 1 mark for the table, 1 mark each for the two advantages.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define "Voltage Regulation" of a transmission line. Why is it an important parameter in distribution system audits?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Voltage regulation of a transmission line is defined as the change in voltage at the receiving end when the full-load is removed, expressed as a percentage of the full-load receiving-end voltage, with the sending-end voltage held constant.

Mathematically:

$$
\%V.R. = \dfrac{\vert V_S \vert - \vert V_R,\,no\,load \vert}{\vert V_R,\,full\,load \vert} \times 100
$$

In the KTU practical convention, the no-load receiving voltage equals the sending voltage (since $I = 0 \Rightarrow$ drop = 0), so the simplified audit formula is:

$$
\%V.R. = \dfrac{\vert V_S \vert - \vert V_R \vert}{\vert V_R \vert} \times 100
$$

**Importance in audits (1 mark):** A high voltage regulation indicates that consumers at the far end of radial feeders receive under-voltage, leading to:
- Poor performance of induction motors (slip increases, torque falls).
- Higher current draw (worsening $I^2R$ losses — a *vicious cycle*).
- Non-compliance with CEA voltage standards (230 V ± 10% for LT consumers).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

A 3-phase, 50 Hz, short transmission line delivers a load of 2000 kW at 0.85 p.f. lagging at a receiving-end line voltage of 11 kV. The resistance and reactance of the line per phase are 4 $\Omega$ and 6 $\Omega$ respectively.

**(a)** Calculate the sending-end voltage (line value) and the voltage regulation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the transmission efficiency and the total line copper loss. **(7 marks)**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1:* Per-phase receiving-end voltage:

$$
V_{R(ph)} = \dfrac{11{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} = 6350.85\ \text{V}
$$

*Step 2:* Line current magnitude:

$$
I_L = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11{,}000 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
I_L = \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{16{,}194.4} = 123.5\ \text{A}
$$

*Step 3:* Impedance and power-factor angle:

$$
Z = 4 + j6\ \Omega \Rightarrow \vert Z \vert = 7.211\ \Omega,\ \theta_Z = 56.31^\circ
$$

$$
\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.85) = 31.79^\circ
$$

*Step 4:* Phasors (taking $V_R$ as reference):

$$
\vec{V_R} = 6350.85\angle 0^\circ\ \text{V},\quad \vec{I_R} = 123.5\angle -31.79^\circ\ \text{A}
$$

*Step 5:* Sending-end voltage:

$$
\vec{V_S} = \vec{V_R} + \vec{I_R} Z = 6350.85\angle 0^\circ + 123.5\angle -31.79^\circ \times 7.211\angle 56.31^\circ
$$

Voltage drop:

$$
\vec{I_R} Z = 123.5 \times 7.211\angle 24.52^\circ = 890.5\angle 24.52^\circ
$$

In rectangular form:

$$
\vec{I_R} Z = 890.5(\cos 24.52^\circ + j\sin 24.52^\circ) = 890.5(0.9096 + j0.4153)
$$

$$
\vec{I_R} Z = 810.0 + j369.8\ \text{V}
$$

Sending voltage:

$$
\vec{V_S} = (6350.85 + 810.0) + j369.8 = 7160.85 + j369.8
$$

$$
\vert \vec{V_S} \vert = \sqrt{7160.85^2 + 369.8^2} = \sqrt{51.28 \times 10^6 + 0.137 \times 10^6} = 7170.4\ \text{V (ph)}
$$

Line value:

$$
V_{S(L)} = 7170.4 \times \sqrt{3} = 12{,}419.4\ \text{V} \approx 12.42\ \text{kV}
$$

*Step 6:* Voltage regulation:

$$
\%V.R. = \dfrac{7170.4 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100 = 12.89\ \%
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1:* Three-phase copper loss:

$$
P_{loss} = 3 I^2 R = 3 \times (123.5)^2 \times 4 = 3 \times 15{,}252.25 \times 4 = 183{,}027\ \text{W}
$$

$$
P_{loss} \approx 183.03\ \text{kW}
$$

*Step 2:* Transmission efficiency:

$$
\eta_T = \dfrac{P_R}{P_R + P_{loss}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 183.03} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_T = \dfrac{2000}{2183.03} \times 100 = 91.62\ \%
$$

*Step 3:* Sending-end power factor (for completeness):

$$
P_S = P_R + P_{loss} = 2183.03\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
I_L = 123.5\ \text{A},\ V_{S(L)} = 12.42\ \text{kV}
$$

$$
\cos\phi_S = \dfrac{P_S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{S(L)} I_L} = \dfrac{2{,}183{,}030}{\sqrt{3} \times 12{,}419.4 \times 123.5} = 0.820\ \text{lag}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes:** Forgetting to convert 11 kV *line* to per-phase voltage costs 1 mark. Always state which quantity is line vs phase.
> 2. **Phasor arithmetic errors:** Treating $V_R$ and $I$ as scalars and writing $V_S = V_R + IR$ (real addition only) is *wrong*. Use the full phasor $V_S = V_R + IZ$ and only take the magnitude *at the end*.
> 3. **Efficiency definition:** Some students mistakenly use $\eta = P_R / P_S$ without the percentage, or use the wrong denominator. Show the formula symbolically *first*, then substitute.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

Explain the constructional features and operating principles of the following with neat block diagrams:
**(a)** EHV AC substation with single-line diagram representation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare the radial, ring-main, and interconnected distribution systems with their relative advantages and disadvantages in the context of an industrial energy audit. **(7 marks)**

---

**Complete Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Definition (1 mark):* An EHV AC substation is an assembly of apparatus that steps down the transmission voltage (e.g., 220 kV or 400 kV) to a lower sub-transmission level (e.g., 33 kV or 11 kV), with provision for switching, protection, metering, and control.

*Key components (5 marks):*
- **Bus bars:** The common electrical node to which all incoming/outgoing lines and transformers connect. Operate at the substation's rated voltage.
- **Power transformer:** Step-down, three-phase, oil-immersed, with conservator, breather, Buchholz relay.
- **Circuit breakers:** SF$_6$ / vacuum / oil type — for fault interruption.
- **Isolators (disconnectors):** For visible isolation of equipment during maintenance (no-load operation only).
- **Current Transformers (CTs):** Step down line current to a standard 1 A or 5 A for metering and protection.
- **Potential Transformers (PTs):** Step down voltage to 110 V / $\sqrt{3}$ for metering and protection.
- **Lightning arresters (LA):** ZnO-based, divert lightning/switching surges to earth.
- **Earthing system:** Earth electrodes, earth mat, equipotential bonding.
- **Control room:** Houses relays, SCADA, batteries, mimic bus.

*Single-line diagram (1 mark):* A single-line diagram (SLD) is a simplified symbolic representation where each three-phase set is shown as a *single line*. Standard symbols — square for transformer, circle for CT, rectangle with arrow for LA, cross-hatched bar for bus — must be used.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

| Feature | Radial | Ring-Main | Interconnected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Tree from one substation | Closed loop | Multiple sources, mesh |
| **Capital cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Reliability** | Lowest (single fault → outage) | Medium (fault isolated by ring switches) | Highest (alternate feeds) |
| **Voltage regulation** | Worst at far end | Better | Best |
| **$I^2R$ loss** | Highest | Lower | Lowest |
| **Protection complexity** | Simple | Medium | High (graded, directional) |
| **Application** | Rural, low-density loads | Urban feeders, industrial | Metro cities, critical loads |
| **Audit recommendation** | Convert to HVDS / ring-main | Add auto-capacitor banks | — |

*Energy audit context (2 marks):* In an industrial energy audit, the choice of distribution topology determines the *baseline* against which loss-reduction measures are measured. Radial systems offer the *greatest* loss-reduction headroom (often 8–15% of energy input is recoverable), while interconnected systems have a *lower* baseline but require *more sophisticated* audit instrumentation (e.g., power-quality analyzers at multiple buses).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Diagram omissions:** When asked for a "neat diagram," a freehand sketch is acceptable — but it *must* be labeled. An unlabeled SLD is awarded 0 marks for the diagram portion.
> 2. **Mere listing without comparison:** Part (b) is worth 7 marks *because* it demands comparative analysis. Writing three independent paragraphs (one for each system) is worth only 3–4 marks. The *tabular comparison* is non-negotiable.
> 3. **Forgetting the "energy audit" context:** Students often answer this in a generic Power Systems tone. The KTU 2024 examiner awards the *top band* only when the answer is explicitly framed around **audit-baseline loss quantification**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electricity Transmission and Distribution System**

- [ ] **Three stages of power system:** Generation (11 kV) → Transmission (EHV: 220/400/765 kV) → Distribution (Primary 11/22 kV, Secondary 415/230 V).
- [ ] **Why EHV?** High voltage ⇒ low current ⇒ $I^2R$ loss $\downarrow \downarrow$ ⇒ efficiency $\uparrow$. Doubling voltage quarters the loss.
- [ ] **Indian standard voltages to memorize:** 230 V, 415 V, 11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV.
- [ ] **Short line:** $< 80$ km, lumped R and L, *no* shunt C. ABCD = $\begin{pmatrix}1 & Z \\ 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}$.
- [ ] **Medium line:** 80 – 250 km, nominal-$\pi$ or nominal-T. ABCD for nominal-$\pi$: $A=D=1+YZ/2$, $B=Z$, $C=Y(1+YZ/4)$.
- [ ] **Long line:** $> 250$ km, distributed parameters, hyperbolic functions ($\gamma$, $Z_c$, $\sinh$, $\cosh$).
- [ ] **Voltage regulation formula:** $\%V.R. = \dfrac{\vert V_S \vert - \vert V_R \vert}{\vert V_R \vert} \times 100$. *Always on per-phase magnitudes.*
- [ ] **Efficiency formula:** $\eta_T = \dfrac{P_R}{P_R + 3I^2R} \times 100$.
- [ ] **$AD - BC = 1$** — the reciprocity identity is your *quick verification check* for ABCD constants.
- [ ] **Three distribution topologies:** Radial (low cost, low reliability, high loss), Ring-Main (medium), Interconnected (high cost, high reliability, low loss).
- [ ] **8 audit-level loss-reduction measures:** re-conductoring, capacitor banks, load balancing, DTR right-sizing, HVDS, BEE-rated transformers, ABC cables, theft prevention.
- [ ] **AT&C loss** (Aggregate Technical & Commercial) is the *single most cited metric* in Indian DISCOM performance reviews and is the primary KPI in any T&D energy audit.
- [ ] **HVAC vs HVDC:** HVDC is economical above ~ 500 km (overhead) or ~ 50 km (underwater cable); key advantages — no charging current, asynchronous tie, lower corona loss.
- [ ] **For any KTU numerical problem:** identify the line category → draw the per-phase equivalent → compute Z and (if medium) Y → choose the right formula → solve in **phasor form** → take magnitudes only at the end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** When writing T&D system answers in the KTU 2024 ESE, *always* explicitly mention the operating voltage class and line category (short / medium / long) in your *first line* of solution. Examiners use this as a **threshold marker** for awarding method marks even when the numerical answer contains a minor arithmetic slip.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascade Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cascade Efficiency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Cascade Efficiency** is defined as the overall (net) efficiency of a multi-stage energy conversion system, where the output of one stage becomes the input of the subsequent stage. It is mathematically expressed as the product of the individual stage efficiencies, and it represents the true thermodynamic and economic effectiveness of an energy chain used in power plants, cogeneration systems, refrigeration cascades, and process industries. $$\eta_{\text{cascade}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \dots \times \eta_n$$ Where each $\eta_i$ represents the efficiency of the $i^{th}$ stage in the energy conversion chain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cascade Efficiency is a **fundamental concept in Module 2** (Energy Conversion and Efficiency Analysis) of the Energy Management and Auditing course. It is heavily tested in Part B (14-mark) numerical problems and directly links to the syllabus outcomes of calculating overall plant efficiency, evaluating cogeneration potential, and assessing energy performance indices. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Explanation (Plain English):** Imagine you have a chain of buckets passing water downhill. Each bucket leaks a little water (losses), and only the water that remains in the final bucket is "useful." Cascade Efficiency is simply the fraction of original water that finally reaches the bottom — it's **not** the average of bucket efficiencies, but their **product** (multiplication), because each successive stage acts on a progressively smaller stream of energy. ### Real-World Relevance - **Cogeneration / Combined Heat and Power (CHP) Plants:** Evaluating combined electrical and thermal efficiency. - **Cascade Refrigeration Systems:** Computing overall coefficient of performance (COP). - **Thermal Power Plants:** Boiler → Turbine → Generator efficiency chain. - **Process Industries:** Furnace → Boiler → Turbine → Compressor chains. - **Energy Auditing:** Determining where the maximum losses occur in an energy cascade. ### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Electrical Equivalent of Heat:** **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** (used in converting electrical output to thermal input). - **Standard Reference Calorific Values:** GCV of coal ≈ **25 MJ/kg**, furnace oil ≈ **42 MJ/kg**, natural gas ≈ **38 MJ/m³**. - **Typical Cascade Efficiency Range (KTU Benchmark):** **30% – 45%** for conventional thermal power cascades; **70% – 85%** for modern combined-cycle cogeneration cascades. - **Boiler Efficiency Threshold:** **≥ 80%** (mandatory for energy audit compliance under PAT scheme). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Underlying Principle — The "Why" Behind Cascade Multiplication When energy flows from one subsystem to the next: 1. **Stage 1** receives the primary energy input $E_{\text{in}}$ and converts it to useful work $W_1$ with efficiency $\eta_1 = \dfrac{W_1}{E_{\text{in}}}$. 2. **Stage 2** receives $W_1$ (which is the output of stage 1) and converts it further to $W_2$ with efficiency $\eta_2 = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1}$. 3. This continues until the final useful output $W_n$ is obtained. Because **each stage operates on a smaller energy base than the original**, the efficiencies are **multiplicative**, not additive. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight (Why not averaging?):** If you average 50% and 50% you get 50%. But the cascade efficiency is $0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 = 25\%$. This is why KTU examiners emphasize that **stage efficiency losses compound dramatically** in real energy systems. ### Logical Step-by-Step Framework - **Step 1:** Identify all stages in the energy conversion chain (e.g., Boiler → Turbine → Generator). - **Step 2:** Determine individual stage efficiencies from design data, manufacturer plates, or energy audit measurements. - **Step 3:** Apply the cascade multiplication rule. - **Step 4:** Compare with benchmark values and identify the weakest (lowest-efficiency) stage. - **Step 5:** Recommend energy conservation measures (ECMs) targeting the weakest link for maximum payback. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **S. No.** | **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Units** | **Boundary / Limiting Condition** | |:----------:|:----------------------|:---------------------:|:---------:|:----------------------------------| | 1 | Cascade Efficiency (n-stage) | $\eta_{\text{cascade}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$ | Dimensionless (or %) | Each $0 \le \eta_i \le 1$ | | 2 | Two-stage cascade | $\eta_{\text{cas}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2$ | Dimensionless | $\eta_1, \eta_2 \in [0,1]$ | | 3 | Three-stage cascade | $\eta_{\text{cas}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3$ | Dimensionless | Used in Boiler–Turbine–Gen | | 4 | Energy loss at stage $i$ | $L_i = (1 - \eta_i) \times E_{i-1}$ | kW or MJ/s | Loss chain is **not** multiplicative | | 5 | Energy input equivalent of electrical output | $E_{\text{thermal,eq}} = E_{\text{electrical}} \times 3.6$ | MJ | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | | 6 | Cogeneration overall efficiency | $\eta_{\text{cogen}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{elec}} + Q_{\text{thermal}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Dimensionless | Must be > 45% for BEE star rating | | 7 | Equivalent electrical efficiency (cogen) | $\eta_{\text{eq,elec}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{elec}} + 0.75 \times Q_{\text{th}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Dimensionless | Factor 0.75 from BEE/RULE formula | | 8 | Plant load factor (PLF) | $\text{PLF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual energy generated}}{\text{Installed capacity} \times \text{hours}}$ | Dimensionless | $\le 1$ for grid-connected plants | | 9 | Specific fuel consumption (SFC) | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{fuel}}}{W_{\text{out}}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better | | 10 | Boiler efficiency (indirect) | $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 100 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \dots + L_6)$ | % | Sum of 6 standard losses | ### Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where Cascade Efficiency is used in industry:** > - **Thermal Power Plants:** Net plant efficiency = η_boiler × η_turbine × η_generator × η_transformer × η_transmission. > - **Cogeneration / Tri-generation:** Combined electrical and thermal output efficiency. > - **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Cascade refrigeration uses two vapor-compression cycles with different refrigerants for ultra-low temperatures (e.g., –40°C to –80°C). > - **Process Industry Audits:** Identifying the "weakest link" for ECM (Energy Conservation Measure) implementation. ### Limiting Cases - **If any single stage efficiency = 0:** $\eta_{\text{cascade}} = 0$ (total system failure). - **If all stages are ideal ($\eta_i = 1$):** $\eta_{\text{cascade}} = 1$ (100% — physically impossible per Second Law). - **Even with two 90% stages:** $\eta_{\text{cas}} = 0.81 = 81\%$ — losses still compound. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: General n-Stage Cascade Efficiency Let the input energy to stage 1 be $E_0$. The useful output of stage $i$ is $E_i$. By definition of stage efficiency: $$ \eta_i = \frac{E_i}{E_{i-1}} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n $$ Rearranging for the output of each stage: $$ E_1 = \eta_1 \cdot E_0 $$ $$ E_2 = \eta_2 \cdot E_1 = \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot E_0 $$ $$ E_3 = \eta_3 \cdot E_2 = \eta_3 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot E_0 $$ By induction, the final output after $n$ stages is: $$ E_n = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i \right) \cdot E_0 $$ Dividing both sides by the input $E_0$ gives the cascade (overall) efficiency: $$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{cascade}} = \frac{E_n}{E_0} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i} $$ **Interpretation:** The cascade efficiency is the **product** of all individual stage efficiencies, not their arithmetic or geometric mean. ### Derivation 2: Two-Stage Cascade with Numerical Verification Consider: - Stage 1 (Boiler): $\eta_1 = 0.85$ (85%) - Stage 2 (Turbine–Generator set): $\eta_2 = 0.40$ (40%) Energy input to boiler: $E_0 = 1000 \text{ MJ}$ (fuel energy). Energy output of stage 1 (steam thermal energy): $$ E_1 = \eta_1 \cdot E_0 = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ MJ} $$ Energy output of stage 2 (electrical energy): $$ E_2 = \eta_2 \cdot E_1 = 0.40 \times 850 = 340 \text{ MJ} $$ Overall cascade efficiency: $$ \eta_{\text{cascade}} = \frac{E_2}{E_0} = \frac{340}{1000} = 0.34 = 34\% $$ Verification using the product formula: $$ \eta_{\text{cascade}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 = 0.85 \times 0.40 = 0.34 = 34\% \quad \checkmark $$ Equivalent electrical energy: $$ W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{340 \text{ MJ}}{3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh}} = 94.44 \text{ kWh} $$ ### Derivation 3: Three-Stage Cogeneration Cascade (Boiler + Turbine + Heat Recovery) A combined heat and power (CHP) plant has: - Boiler efficiency: $\eta_B = 0.88$ - Turbine–Generator efficiency: $\eta_{TG} = 0.38$ - Heat Recovery Boiler (HRB) efficiency: $\eta_{HRB} = 0.75$ **Step 1 — Input fuel energy** (assume 1000 MJ): $E_0 = 1000 \text{ MJ}$ **Step 2 — Steam energy from boiler:** $$ E_1 = 0.88 \times 1000 = 880 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 3 — Electrical energy from turbine-generator:** $$ E_2 = 0.38 \times 880 = 334.4 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 4 — Recovered thermal energy from HRB (acts on exhaust steam = $E_1 - E_2$):** $$ E_3 = 0.75 \times (880 - 334.4) = 0.75 \times 545.6 = 409.2 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 5 — Total useful energy (cogeneration):** $$ E_{\text{useful}} = E_2 + E_3 = 334.4 + 409.2 = 743.6 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 6 — Cogeneration overall efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{cogen}} = \frac{743.6}{1000} = 0.7436 = 74.36\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** In cogeneration, the **electrical efficiency alone is only ~33%**, but with heat recovery, the **overall efficiency jumps to ~74%**. This is the central economic justification for CHP plants in industrial energy audits. ### Derivation 4: Equivalent Electrical Efficiency (BEE Formula) The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India, uses an "equivalent electrical efficiency" to rank cogeneration plants: $$ \eta_{\text{eq,elec}} = \frac{W_{\text{elec}} + 0.75 \times Q_{\text{th}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} $$ The factor **0.75** represents the conversion of thermal energy to equivalent electrical output (based on typical boiler + turbine efficiency of about 75%). Substituting from the previous derivation: $$ \eta_{\text{eq,elec}} = \frac{334.4 + 0.75 \times 409.2}{1000} = \frac{334.4 + 306.9}{1000} = 0.6413 = 64.13\% $$ ### Analytical Matrix: Stage-by-Stage Loss Allocation | **Stage** | **Efficiency $\eta_i$** | **Loss Fraction $(1-\eta_i)$** | **Input Energy (MJ)** | **Output Energy (MJ)** | **Loss Energy (MJ)** | **Cumulative Loss (MJ)** | |:---------:|:-----------------------:|:------------------------------:|:---------------------:|:----------------------:|:--------------------:|:------------------------:| | 1 — Boiler | 0.88 | 0.12 | 1000.0 | 880.0 | 120.0 | 120.0 | | 2 — Turbine + Gen | 0.38 | 0.62 | 880.0 | 334.4 | 545.6 | 665.6 | | 3 — HRB | 0.75 | 0.25 | 545.6 | 409.2 | 136.4 | 802.0 | | **Total** | — | — | **1000.0** | **743.6** | — | **256.4 recovered / 802 lost** | > [!NOTE] > **Audit Insight:** The turbine-generator stage is the weakest link (62% loss fraction). The recommended ECM (Energy Conservation Measure) is turbine overhauling/improved blade profile, which can raise $\eta_{TG}$ from 38% to ~45%, increasing cascade efficiency by ~5 percentage points. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cogeneration Cascade ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FUEL INPUT
Qfuel = 1000 MJ"] --> B["BOILER
etaB = 0.88"] B --> C["TURBINE + GENERATOR
etaTG = 0.38"] C --> D["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT
Welec = 334.4 MJ"] C --> E["WASTE HEAT EXHAUST
Qexh = 545.6 MJ"] E --> F["HEAT RECOVERY BOILER
etaHRB = 0.75"] F --> G["USEFUL THERMAL OUTPUT
Qth = 409.2 MJ"] B -.->|"LOSS 120 MJ"| L1["FLUE GAS + RADIATION"] C -.->|"LOSS 545.6 MJ"| L2["CONDENSER + MECHANICAL"] F -.->|"LOSS 136.4 MJ"| L3["STACK + BLOWDOWN"] G --> H["COGENERATION
eta_cogen = 74.36 percent"] D --> H ``` ### Diagram 2: Sequential Cascade Stage Representation ```mermaid flowchart TD IN["Energy Input E0"] --> S1["Stage 1
eta1"] S1 --> S2["Stage 2
eta2"] S2 --> S3["Stage 3
eta3"] S3 --> SN["Stage n
etan"] SN --> OUT["Energy Output En"] S1 -.->|"Loss 1 minus eta1"| W1["Wastage L1"] S2 -.->|"Loss 1 minus eta2"| W2["Wastage L2"] S3 -.->|"Loss 1 minus eta3"| W3["Wastage L3"] SN -.->|"Loss 1 minus etan"| WN["Wastage Ln"] ``` ### Diagram 3: Decision Tree for Energy Audit Improvement ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Compute eta_cascade"] --> B{"eta_cascade above 35 percent?"} B -- "Yes" --> C["Benchmark: Acceptable thermal plant"] B -- "No" --> D["Identify weakest stage
lowest eta_i"] D --> E["Is it Boiler?"] D --> F["Is it Turbine?"] D --> G["Is it Generator?"] E -- "Yes" --> E1["ECM: Combustion tuning,
air preheater, economiser"] F -- "Yes" --> F1["ECM: Turbine overhaul,
reheat, blade retrofit"] G -- "Yes" --> G1["ECM: AVR tuning,
rewinding, cooling upgrade"] E1 --> H["Re-evaluate eta_cascade"] F1 --> H G1 --> H H --> A ``` ### Diagram 4: Cascade Refrigeration Application (Two-Stage) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Low-Temp Load
minus 40 deg C"] --> B["LT Cycle
Refrigerant R-23
eta1 = 0.45"] B --> C["Cascade Heat Exchanger
Inter-stage coupling"] C --> D["HT Cycle
Refrigerant R-134a
eta2 = 0.55"] D --> E["Ambient Condenser
T outside = 35 deg C"] C --> F["eta_cascade = eta1 x eta2
= 0.45 x 0.55 = 0.2475"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define Cascade Efficiency. Why is it expressed as the **product** and not the **average** of individual stage efficiencies? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cascade Efficiency is the overall efficiency of a multi-stage energy conversion system, defined as the ratio of final useful output energy to the original energy input. Mathematically: $$ \eta_{\text{cascade}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \dots \times \eta_n $$ It is the **product** (not the average) because each successive stage acts on a progressively smaller energy base — the output of the previous stage becomes the input of the next. Thus, losses compound multiplicatively, not additively. For example, two 70% stages in cascade give $0.7 \times 0.7 = 0.49 = 49\%$, not $(0.7 + 0.7)/2 = 70\%$. #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State the formula for the overall (cascade) efficiency of a 3-stage system consisting of Boiler → Turbine → Generator. What is the typical range of this overall efficiency in a conventional thermal power plant? **Model Answer (3 marks):** For a 3-stage thermal plant: $$ \eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{boiler}} \times \eta_{\text{turbine}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}} $$ Typical values: $\eta_{\text{boiler}} \approx 0.85$–$0.92$, $\eta_{\text{turbine}} \approx 0.35$–$0.45$, $\eta_{\text{generator}} \approx 0.96$–$0.98$. The resulting overall cascade efficiency for a conventional subcritical thermal power plant is in the range of **30% to 40%**. Modern supercritical and ultra-supercritical plants achieve **40% to 45%**, and combined-cycle gas turbine (CCGT) plants reach **55% to 60%**. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** A coal-fired thermal power plant has the following stage efficiencies: - Boiler efficiency: **85%** - Turbine efficiency (isentropic + mechanical combined): **40%** - Generator efficiency: **97%** Calculate the **overall cascade efficiency** of the plant. If the plant consumes **2000 tonnes of coal per day** with a GCV of **25 MJ/kg**, determine: 1. The daily electrical energy output in **kWh**. 2. The daily fuel cost if electricity is valued at **₹6/kWh** (revenue). 3. The **percentage point improvement** in cascade efficiency if boiler efficiency is raised to 90% and turbine efficiency to 42% (generator unchanged). **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the concept of **cogeneration cascade efficiency**. A sugar mill cogeneration plant uses bagasse as fuel with GCV = **8 MJ/kg**. The boiler efficiency is **80%**, the turbine-generator efficiency is **32%**, and the heat recovery boiler recovers **60%** of the exhaust heat. Calculate the **overall cogeneration efficiency** and the **equivalent electrical efficiency** (BEE formula). Assume fuel consumption = **100 tonnes/day**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** ##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - $\eta_B = 0.85$, $\eta_T = 0.40$, $\eta_G = 0.97$ - Coal consumption = 2000 tonnes/day = $2 \times 10^6$ kg/day - GCV = 25 MJ/kg - Electricity value = ₹6/kWh **Step 1: Daily fuel energy input** $$ E_{\text{fuel}} = m \times \text{GCV} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ kg} \times 25 \text{ MJ/kg} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Step 2: Cascade efficiency (current)** $$ \eta_{\text{cas}} = 0.85 \times 0.40 \times 0.97 = 0.3298 = 32.98\% $$ **Step 3: Daily electrical energy output** $$ E_{\text{elec}} = \eta_{\text{cas}} \times E_{\text{fuel}} = 0.3298 \times 5 \times 10^7 = 1.649 \times 10^7 \text{ MJ/day} $$ Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $$ E_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1.649 \times 10^7}{3.6} = 4.581 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day} $$ **Step 4: Daily revenue** $$ \text{Revenue} = 4.581 \times 10^6 \times 6 = \text{₹ } 2.749 \times 10^7 \approx \text{₹ } 2.75 \text{ crore/day} $$ **Step 5: Improved cascade efficiency** $$ \eta_{\text{cas,new}} = 0.90 \times 0.42 \times 0.97 = 0.3667 = 36.67\% $$ **Step 6: Improvement in percentage points** $$ \Delta\eta = 36.67\% - 32.98\% = 3.69 \text{ percentage points} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** A modest 5% improvement in boiler efficiency and 2% in turbine efficiency yields ~3.69 percentage points gain in cascade efficiency, translating to a daily extra revenue of approximately ₹3.06 lakh — a strong economic argument for energy audits. --- ##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Concept (Block Diagram):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Bagasse Fuel
100 t/day"] --> B["BOILER
etaB = 0.80"] B --> C["STEAM TO TURBINE-GEN
etaTG = 0.32"] C --> D["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT
Welec"] C --> E["TURBINE EXHAUST HEAT
Qexh"] E --> F["HEAT RECOVERY BOILER
etaHRB = 0.60"] F --> G["PROCESS THERMAL OUTPUT
Qth"] D --> H["eta_cogen = Welec + Qth / Qfuel"] G --> H ``` **Given:** - Bagasse = 100 t/day = $10^5$ kg/day - GCV = 8 MJ/kg - $\eta_B = 0.80$, $\eta_{TG} = 0.32$, $\eta_{HRB} = 0.60$ **Step 1: Daily fuel energy** $$ E_{\text{fuel}} = 10^5 \times 8 = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Step 2: Steam energy from boiler** $$ E_{\text{steam}} = 0.80 \times 8 \times 10^5 = 6.4 \times 10^5 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Step 3: Electrical output** $$ W_{\text{elec}} = 0.32 \times 6.4 \times 10^5 = 2.048 \times 10^5 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Step 4: Exhaust heat (available for HRB)** $$ Q_{\text{exh}} = 6.4 \times 10^5 - 2.048 \times 10^5 = 4.352 \times 10^5 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Step 5: Recovered thermal output** $$ Q_{\text{th}} = 0.60 \times 4.352 \times 10^5 = 2.6112 \times 10^5 \text{ MJ/day} $$ **Step 6: Cogeneration overall efficiency** $$ \eta_{\text{cogen}} = \frac{W_{\text{elec}} + Q_{\text{th}}}{E_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{2.048 \times 10^5 + 2.6112 \times 10^5}{8 \times 10^5} $$ $$ \eta_{\text{cogen}} = \frac{4.6592 \times 10^5}{8 \times 10^5} = 0.5824 = 58.24\% $$ **Step 7: Equivalent electrical efficiency (BEE formula)** $$ \eta_{\text{eq,elec}} = \frac{W_{\text{elec}} + 0.75 \times Q_{\text{th}}}{E_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{2.048 \times 10^5 + 0.75 \times 2.6112 \times 10^5}{8 \times 10^5} $$ $$ \eta_{\text{eq,elec}} = \frac{2.048 \times 10^5 + 1.9584 \times 10^5}{8 \times 10^5} = \frac{4.0064 \times 10^5}{8 \times 10^5} = 0.5008 = 50.08\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Audit Insight:** The plant qualifies for **BEE PAT scheme** benefits since $\eta_{\text{cogen}} = 58.24\% > 45\%$ threshold for sugar mill cogeneration. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** A cascade refrigeration system has two stages. The low-temperature (LT) cycle operates with a COP of **1.8** and the high-temperature (HT) cycle has a COP of **3.2**. Compute: 1. The overall cascade COP assuming the cooling loads of both cycles are equal. 2. The power consumption per kW of refrigeration, and the equivalent cascade efficiency. 3. The annual energy saving (in kWh) if the plant runs **6000 hours/year** and is retrofitted to improve the LT cycle COP from 1.8 to 2.2. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An industrial furnace heats a metal charge from **25°C to 1100°C**. The furnace efficiency is **45%**, the boiler downstream operates at **80%** efficiency to recover flue gas heat, and the steam turbine-generator converts this steam to electricity at **35%** overall efficiency. Calculate: 1. The overall cascade efficiency from fuel input to electrical output. 2. If the furnace is supplied **500 kg/h of furnace oil** (GCV = **42 MJ/kg**), find the electrical power generated in **kW**. 3. Suggest two Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) to improve the cascade. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** ##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Given:** $\text{COP}_{LT} = 1.8$, $\text{COP}_{HT} = 3.2$ **Step 1: Overall cascade COP** For a two-stage cascade refrigeration with equal cooling loads: $$ \text{COP}_{\text{cascade}} = \text{COP}_{LT} \times \text{COP}_{HT} = 1.8 \times 3.2 = 5.76 $$ **Step 2: Power consumption per kW refrigeration** $$ \text{Specific Power} = \frac{1}{\text{COP}_{\text{cascade}}} = \frac{1}{5.76} = 0.1736 \text{ kW per kW cooling} $$ **Step 3: Equivalent cascade efficiency** (electrical to cooling, theoretical Carnot reference at 5°C evaporator, 40°C condenser): $$ \eta_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_{\text{evap}}}{T_{\text{cond}} - T_{\text{evap}}} = \frac{278}{313 - 278} = \frac{278}{35} = 7.94 $$ $$ \eta_{\text{rel,cascade}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{cascade}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{5.76}{7.94} = 0.7254 = 72.54\% $$ **Step 4: After retrofit — $\text{COP}_{LT,\text{new}} = 2.2$** $$ \text{COP}_{\text{cascade,new}} = 2.2 \times 3.2 = 7.04 $$ New specific power: $$ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{7.04} = 0.1420 \text{ kW per kW cooling} $$ **Step 5: Annual energy saving for 100 kW cooling load (assumed)** Power saved per kW cooling: $$ \Delta P = 0.1736 - 0.1420 = 0.0316 \text{ kW/kW} $$ For 100 kW cooling load: $$ \Delta P_{\text{total}} = 100 \times 0.0316 = 3.16 \text{ kW} $$ Annual energy saved: $$ E_{\text{saved}} = 3.16 \times 6000 = 18{,}960 \text{ kWh/year} $$ At ₹8/kWh → **annual monetary saving = ₹1.52 lakh**. --- ##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** $\eta_{\text{furnace}} = 0.45$, $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 0.80$, $\eta_{\text{turbine-gen}} = 0.35$ **Step 1: Overall cascade efficiency** $$ \eta_{\text{cas}} = 0.45 \times 0.80 \times 0.35 = 0.126 = 12.6\% $$ **Step 2: Electrical power from 500 kg/h furnace oil** Fuel energy per hour: $$ E_{\text{fuel}} = 500 \times 42 = 21{,}000 \text{ MJ/h} $$ Convert to kW (1 MJ/h = 0.2778 kW): $$ E_{\text{fuel}} = 21{,}000 \times 0.2778 = 5833.3 \text{ kW (thermal)} $$ Electrical output: $$ P_{\text{elec}} = 0.126 \times 5833.3 = 735.0 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3: Two ECMs** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recommended Energy Conservation Measures:** > 1. **Air-preheater on furnace flue gas** (recovers 8%–10% of stack loss) → improves $\eta_{\text{furnace}}$ from 45% to ~52% → cascade efficiency rises to ~14.5%. > 2. **Turbine reblading / steam superheat upgrade** → improves $\eta_{TG}$ from 35% to ~40% → cascade efficiency rises further to ~16.6%. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Most common mistake:** Adding individual stage efficiencies (incorrect). Always **multiply** them. > - **Second common mistake:** Forgetting to convert MJ to kWh using **3.6 MJ/kWh** when the question asks for energy in kWh. > - **Third common mistake:** Computing arithmetic mean of efficiencies (e.g., 30% and 50% → 40%) instead of geometric/cascade product (15%). This loses 3 marks. > - **Fourth common mistake:** Not specifying units in the final answer (write "%" or "dimensionless"). > - **Fifth common mistake:** Ignoring the BEE 0.75 factor in cogeneration equivalent efficiency — KTU examiners specifically test this. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Cascade Efficiency is the product of individual stage efficiencies in a multi-stage energy conversion chain. - **Formula:** $\eta_{\text{cas}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \dots \times \eta_n$. - **Why product and not average?** Because each stage operates on the output of the previous stage (energy base shrinks progressively). - **Typical Range:** Conventional thermal = **30%–40%**; Combined cycle = **55%–60%**; Cogeneration = **60%–85%**. - **Cogeneration Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{cogen}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{elec}} + Q_{\text{th}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}}$. - **BEE Equivalent Electrical Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{eq,elec}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{elec}} + 0.75 \, Q_{\text{th}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}}$. - **Conversion Factor:** **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** (must be remembered by heart for KTU numericals). - **Energy Loss at Stage $i$:** $L_i = (1 - \eta_i) \times E_{i-1}$ — losses are computed on the input to that stage, not the original input. - **Auditing Tip:** Always identify the **weakest stage** (lowest $\eta_i$) to target Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) for maximum impact. - **Common 14-mark Question Pattern:** Compute cascade efficiency → convert to kWh → compute revenue/savings → suggest 2 ECMs. - **Reference Benchmark:** BEE PAT scheme mandates $\eta_{\text{cogen}} \ge 45\%$ for qualifying industries.

topics.title: Lighting: Modern Energy Efficient Light Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lighting: Modern Energy Efficient Light Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Energy Efficient Light Sources** are illumination devices that convert electrical energy into visible light with a substantially higher **luminous efficacy** (lumens per watt) and longer operational life compared to conventional incandescent lamps, thereby delivering the same or better illuminance (lux) at a fraction of the input power.

The most widely accepted modern energy-efficient sources covered under the KTU syllabus include:

1. **Compact Fluorescent Lamps (CFL)** — folded-tube low-pressure mercury discharge lamps with integrated electronic ballast.
2. **Light Emitting Diodes (LED)** — solid-state semiconductor devices that emit photons through electron-hole recombination (electroluminescence).
3. **Induction Lamps** — electrodeless fluorescent lamps energised by high-frequency magnetic induction.
4. **High-Frequency Electronic Ballasts for T5/T8 Fluorescent Tubes** — upgrading existing linear fluorescent installations.
5. **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps** — Metal Halide (MH), High-Pressure Sodium Vapour (HPSV), and Low-Pressure Sodium Vapour (LPSV).
6. **Cold Cathode Lamps (CCFL)** and **Organic LEDs (OLED)** — emerging niche technologies.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fuel Efficiency of Light"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Miles per Gallon for a Car vs Lumens per Watt for a Lamp**

Think of light sources like cars:
- An **incandescent lamp** is a **gas-guzzling vintage car** — it converts almost 90% of its fuel (electricity) into *heat* (infra-red radiation) and only 10% into *light*.
- An **LED lamp** is a **modern electric vehicle** — it channels ~40–50% of input energy into useful visible light, with minimal heat loss.

Just as a car buyer asks *"How many miles per gallon?"*, a lighting auditor asks *"How many lumens per watt?"* — the higher this number, the more **energy-efficient** the light source.

### 1.3 Fundamental Photometric Quantities (KTU Standard Metrics)

| Symbol | Quantity | Definition | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Phi$ | Luminous Flux | Total visible light energy emitted per second | **lumen (lm)** |
| $I$ | Luminous Intensity | Luminous flux per unit solid angle | **candela (cd)** |
| $E$ | Illuminance | Luminous flux incident per unit area | **lux (lx = lm/m²)** |
| $L$ | Luminance | Luminous intensity per unit projected area | **cd/m²** |
| $\eta$ | Luminous Efficacy | Ratio of luminous flux to power input | **lm/W** |
| Ra | Colour Rendering Index | Ability to render true colours (max 100) | dimensionless |
| CCT | Correlated Colour Temperature | Warmth/coolness of white light | **Kelvin (K)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every numerical problem in Lighting Auditing (Module 2) starts from these seven terms. Memorising them with units is the single highest-yield activity for Part-A questions.

### 1.4 Why Modern Light Sources? — The Energy Context

- Lighting accounts for approximately **19–20% of global electricity consumption** and **15–20% of commercial building load** in India (BEE data).
- A 1:1 incandescent-to-LED retrofit in India can save **~80 TWh/year** — equivalent to the output of several large power stations.
- The Indian **BEE Star Label programme (2023 update)** mandates efficacy thresholds of **≥ 90 lm/W** for LED bulbs to qualify for the highest 5-star rating.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Identify the Light Generation Principle**

Modern lamps are classified by *how* they convert electrical energy into photons:

- **Incandescence** (heating a filament) → inefficient, but excellent CRI = 100.
- **Discharge (Gas Excitation)** → passing current through ionised gas/vapour produces UV, which a phosphor coating converts to visible light.
- **Electroluminescence** (solid-state) → semiconductor p-n junction emits photons directly when forward-biased.

**Step 2 — Group by Technology Family**

$$
\text{Modern Light Sources} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Fluorescent Family} \rightarrow \text{CFL, T5/T8 Linear, Induction} \\
\text{HID Family} \rightarrow \text{MH, HPSV, LPSV} \\
\text{Solid-State Family} \rightarrow \text{LED, OLED, COB-LED}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Performance on the Five KPIs**

1. Luminous Efficacy (lm/W)
2. Colour Rendering Index (Ra)
3. Lamp Life (hours)
4. Correlated Colour Temperature (K)
5. Cosine of Solid Angle Distribution (beam spread)

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Why" matters:** Examiners often frame Part-A questions as *"Why is LED preferred over HPSV for indoor commercial lighting?"* — the answer must link **Ra + instant restrike + dimmability** to *indoor* applications, not just efficacy.

### 2.2 Technology-by-Technology Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Compact Fluorescent Lamp (CFL)
- **Operating Principle:** Mercury vapour discharge excites UV photons → phosphor coating (tri-phosphor) down-converts UV to visible.
- **Components:** Folded glass tube, electronic ballast, Edison/G24 base.
- **Efficacy:** **45–75 lm/W**
- **CCT options:** 2700 K (warm white) to 6500 K (daylight)
- **Lamp life:** 6 000 – 15 000 hours
- **Limitations:** Contains 3–5 mg mercury (hazardous); slow warm-up; not compatible with dimmers below 10% of rated power.

#### 2.2.2 Light Emitting Diode (LED)
- **Operating Principle:** Electroluminescence at a p-n junction. Photon energy $E = h\nu$ corresponds to band-gap energy $E_g$ of the semiconductor (GaAs, InGaN, AlGaInP).
- **Key architectural variants:** SMD (Surface Mount Device), COB (Chip-on-Board), Filament-LED (retrofit look).
- **Efficacy:** **80 – 220 lm/W** (laboratory records exceed 300 lm/W)
- **CRI:** 80 – 98 (tunable)
- **CCT:** 2200 K – 6500 K
- **Lamp life:** 25 000 – 50 000 hours (L70 B50 rating)
- **Driver:** Constant-current SMPS; requires thermal management (heat sink).
- **Why the "Why":** LEDs are **directional, dimmable, mercury-free, instant-restrike, and have the lowest lumen depreciation curve** — these four properties make them the global default for new installations.

#### 2.2.3 Induction Lamp
- **Operating Principle:** Electrodeless. High-frequency (2.65 MHz) current in an external coil induces eddy currents in mercury vapour inside the bulb → UV → phosphor → visible.
- **Efficacy:** **70 – 90 lm/W**
- **Lamp life:** **60 000 – 100 000 hours** (longest among discharge lamps — no filament or electrode erosion).
- **Applications:** Hard-to-reach fixtures (tall industrial bays, tunnels, street-lighting).

#### 2.2.4 High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps

| Sub-type | Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI (Ra) | Life (h) | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LPSV (Low-Pressure Sodium) | **100–200** | < 0 (mono-chromatic yellow) | 18 000 | Motorways, security, fog-piercing |
| HPSV (High-Pressure Sodium) | **80–140** | 20–25 | 20 000–24 000 | Street lighting, warehouses |
| Metal Halide (MH) | **75–110** | 65–90 | 10 000–15 000 | Sports stadiums, retail, horticulture |

- **Mechanism:** Arc discharge between tungsten electrodes inside a ceramic or quartz arc tube filled with metal halide salts (NaI, TlI, InI, DyI₃, HoI₃).
- **Why MH over HPSV for retail:** Higher Ra means goods appear in their *true* colour, increasing customer perception of quality.

#### 2.2.5 Linear Fluorescent — T5 vs T8
- T8 (26 mm dia, G13 base): ~50–80 lm/W; legacy technology.
- T5 (16 mm dia, G5 base) with **high-frequency electronic ballast (≥ 20 kHz)**: **up to 100 lm/W** — eliminates flicker and the 50 Hz stroboscopic effect that causes headaches and eyestrain.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All these formulas are testable. Memorise the units, not just the symbols.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Luminous efficacy | lm/W |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Illuminance on a surface | lx |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega}$ | Luminous intensity | cd |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | Inverse-square law with cosine | lx |
| 5 | $L = \dfrac{I}{A_{\text{proj}}}$ | Luminance | cd/m² |
| 6 | $\text{Utilization Factor} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{utilised}}}{ \Phi_{\text{lamp}}}$ | UF (always < 1) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Maintenance Factor} = \text{LLF} \times \text{LDD} \times \text{RSDD} \times \text{BF}$ | MF (depreciation factor) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Phi_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}$ | Lumen method design | lm |
| 9 | $\text{Annual Energy} = P \times N \times h \times \text{days}$ | kWh consumed | kWh |
| 10 | $\text{Annual Saving} = (P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new}}) \times h \times \text{Rs/kWh}$ | Money saved | ₹ |
| 11 | $\text{Simple Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Incremental Cost}}{\text{Annual Saving}}$ | Years to recover | years |
| 12 | $\text{kW reduction} = \dfrac{P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new}}}{1000}$ | Demand saving | kW |
| 13 | $\text{Reduction in connected load (kVA)} = \dfrac{kW}{\text{Power Factor}}$ | Apparent-power saving | kVA |
| 14 | $\text{L70 Life} = \text{hours until output} \le 70\%$ of initial | Lamp life metric | h |
| 15 | $\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{\text{Annual kWh}}{\text{Lux} \times \text{m}^{2}}$ | kWh/lux·m² | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note on $|x|$:** When expressing the inverse-square cosine formula, KTU values **derivation steps**, not just the final substitution. Always start from $d\Omega = dA \cos\theta \div d^{2}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **BEE-ECA scheme auditors** use these formulas to certify energy-savings retrofits.
- **ISO 50001 (EnMS)** energy reviews for hospitals, IT parks, and manufacturing plants depend on **Formula 8** (Lumen method).
- **Smart-city street-lighting PPPs** are financially viable only if **Formulas 10 and 11** yield payback under 5 years.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Illuminance from a Point Source (Inverse-Square Law with Cosine)

**Starting Premise:** A point source $S$ emits luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions (isotropic). We want the illuminance $E$ on a small area $dA$ located at distance $d$ from the source, where the normal to $dA$ makes angle $\theta$ with the line joining the source to $dA$.

**Step 1 — Define solid angle subtended by $dA$ at the source:**

The solid angle of a small patch of area $dA$ viewed from a distance $d$ is reduced by the cosine projection factor:

$$
d\Omega = \frac{dA \cos\theta}{d^{2}}
$$

*Explanation:* The factor $\cos\theta$ accounts for the fact that the patch is tilted relative to the incoming flux; the $1/d^{2}$ accounts for the spherical spreading of flux over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$.

**Step 2 — Relate flux to solid angle via luminous intensity:**

By definition, luminous intensity $I$ is flux per unit solid angle, so:

$$
d\Phi = I \, d\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $d\Omega$ into $d\Phi$:**

$$
d\Phi = I \cdot \frac{dA \cos\theta}{d^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that illuminance $E = d\Phi / dA$:**

$$
E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}
$$

*This is the famous photometric inverse-square cosine law.*

**Step 5 — Convert to flux form using $I = \Phi / (4\pi)$ for an isotropic source:**

$$
E = \frac{\Phi \cos\theta}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marker Expectation:** A 7-mark derivation question typically gives **3 marks for the conceptual link between flux, solid angle, and intensity**, **2 marks for the substitution**, and **2 marks for the final boxed equation with units stated**. Skipping units costs a mark.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Annual Energy & Cost Saving of an LED Retrofit

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU-style):**

> A factory floor has **100 fittings** of **400 W HPSV lamps** operating **24 hours/day, 300 days/year**. They are proposed to be replaced by **150 W LED high-bay fixtures** delivering the same illuminance. Electricity tariff = **₹ 8.50 /kWh**. Cost per LED fixture = **₹ 6 500**; salvage value of removed HPSV = negligible.
>
> Calculate:
> (a) The annual energy saving in kWh.
> (b) The annual monetary saving in ₹.
> (c) The simple payback period in years.

---

#### (a) Annual Energy Saving

**Step 1 — Compute connected power of existing system:**

$$
P_{\text{old,total}} = 100 \times 400 = 40\,000~\text{W} = 40~\text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute connected power of proposed system:**

$$
P_{\text{new,total}} = 100 \times 150 = 15\,000~\text{W} = 15~\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Power reduction:**

$$
\Delta P = 40 - 15 = 25~\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual operating hours:**

$$
h_{\text{annual}} = 24 \times 300 = 7\,200~\text{hours/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy saving:**

$$
\Delta E = \Delta P \times h_{\text{annual}} = 25 \times 7\,200
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta E = 1\,80\,000~\text{kWh/year}}
$$

#### (b) Annual Monetary Saving

$$
\text{Saving} = \Delta E \times \text{Tariff} = 1\,80\,000 \times 8.50
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Annual Saving} = \text{₹ }15\,30\,000 / \text{year}}
$$

#### (c) Simple Payback Period

**Step 1 — Total retrofit cost:**

$$
\text{Cost} = 100 \times 6\,500 = \text{₹ }6\,50\,000
$$

**Step 2 — Payback formula (Formula 11 of cheat sheet):**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{\text{Incremental Cost}}{\text{Annual Saving}} = \frac{6\,50\,000}{15\,30\,000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Payback} = 0.4248 \approx 5.1 \text{ months}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Trick:** A 5-month payback is extraordinarily short. The student must explicitly state the **assumption of zero maintenance cost differential**; otherwise, deduct **0.5 marks** in a typical 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Lumen Method (Verification of Required Fittings)

**Problem Statement:**

> An office room measures **10 m × 8 m × 3 m** (W × L × H). Required illuminance $E = 500$ lux. Utilisation Factor (UF) = 0.6, Maintenance Factor (MF) = 0.8. A 36 W T5 fluorescent tube delivers **3 350 lumens** (initial, before depreciation). How many tubes are required?

**Step 1 — Apply Formula 8 (Lumen method):**

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = \frac{500 \times (10 \times 8)}{0.6 \times 0.8} = \frac{500 \times 80}{0.48} = \frac{40\,000}{0.48}
$$

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = 83\,333.33~\text{lumens}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of tubes:**

$$
N = \frac{\Phi_{\text{required}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp}}} = \frac{83\,333.33}{3\,350} = 24.88
$$

**Step 4 — Round up to the next whole number (a fractional tube is impossible):**

$$
\boxed{N = 25 \text{ tubes}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to round *up*. Twenty-four tubes would give only 80 400 lm, falling short of 83 333 lm — a **5% illuminance shortfall**, which violates IS 3646 minimum-lux requirements.

### 3.4 Comparative Performance Matrix (Analytical Summary)

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is the single most reproduce-able answer in Part-B. Drawing a 3×3 table in the exam script earns full marks.

| Parameter | Incandescent | CFL | T5 FL | HPSV | Metal Halide | LED |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Efficacy (lm/W) | 10–15 | 45–75 | 70–100 | 80–140 | 75–110 | 80–200 |
| CRI (Ra) | 100 | 80 | 80–90 | 20–25 | 65–90 | 80–98 |
| Lamp Life (kh) | 1 | 8–15 | 15–25 | 20–24 | 10–15 | 25–50 |
| Warm-up Time | 0 | 30 s | Instant | 3–5 min | 3–5 min | Instant |
| Restrike | Instant | 30 s | Instant | 1–2 min | 5–15 min | Instant |
| Dimmable | Yes | Limited | Yes | Limited | Limited | Yes (0–100%) |
| Mercury | No | 3–5 mg | 5–10 mg | 10–30 mg | 10–30 mg | No |
| Cost (₹/klm) | 1.0× (base) | 2× | 2.5× | 3× | 4× | 5–8× |

### 3.5 Energy-Saving Roadmap Derivation

Starting from the universal energy equation for a lighting system:

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{lamp}} \times N \times h_{\text{annual}} \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{[kWh]}
$$

**Percent saving** between old and new system:

$$
\% \text{Saving} = \frac{P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100
$$

> **Plugging our 400 W HPSV → 150 W LED case:**

$$
\% \text{Saving} = \frac{400 - 150}{400} \times 100 = \frac{250}{400} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{Saving} = 62.5\%}
$$

This derivation explains *why* governments worldwide are pushing LED mandates — the **62.5% reduction in connected load directly translates to a 62.5% reduction in per-luminaire energy** in a demand-charge metered industrial tariff.

### 4.2 LED Construction & Working — Block-Level Architecture

A physically accurate drawing is not feasible in Mermaid; the following **block diagram** conveys the same engineering information as a cross-section.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[AC Mains Input 230V 50Hz] --> S[SMPS Driver Constant Current]
    S --> J[LED Chip p-n Junction]
    J --> H[Heat Sink Aluminium]
    J --> O[Optical Lens PMMA]
    O --> E[Illuminated Area Lux]
    H --> A[Ambient Air Convection]
    S -.-> T[Thermal Feedback]
    T --> S
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- $P \rightarrow S$: Mains rectified & chopped to low-voltage DC.
- $S \rightarrow J$: Constant-current (typically 350 mA or 700 mA) drives the LED chip.
- $J \rightarrow H$: Junction heat dissipated through metal-core PCB to aluminium heat sink.
- $J \rightarrow O$: Lambertian or narrow-beam lens shapes the photometric distribution.
- $H \rightarrow A$: Convective + radiative heat rejection is the *primary failure mechanism* — every 10 °C rise above 25 °C junction temp halves LED life.
- $S \dashrightarrow T$: Optional NTC thermistor feedback throttles current if heat sink overheats.

### 4.3 Energy-Saving Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Audit Existing Installation] --> Q1{Are Lamps 10 Years Old?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Replace with LED]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is Illuminance Adequate?}
    Q3 -- No --> Q4[Add Fixtures or Replace]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5{Operating Hours 12h per Day?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Replace with LED High Efficiency]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Group Relamping Retrofit]
    Q2 --> S[Verify Lux Compliance IS 3646]
    Q4 --> S
    Q6 --> S
    Q7 --> S
    S --> Out[Monitor kWh for 3 Months Verify Saving]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This decision tree is the structure examiners expect in a 7-mark *"Discuss the lighting energy-saving opportunities in a commercial building"* question. Use it as a mental template.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define luminous efficacy. Why is it the single most important figure-of-merit for an energy auditor selecting a light source?**

**Model Answer:**

Luminous efficacy $\eta$ is the ratio of the *luminous flux* $\Phi$ (in lumens) emitted by a lamp to the *electrical power* $P$ (in watts) consumed:

$$
\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P} \quad \text{[lm/W]}
$$

It is the photometric equivalent of "miles per gallon" and quantifies how efficiently a lamp converts electrical input into *visible* light (weighted by the human eye's spectral sensitivity V(λ)).

For an energy auditor, $\eta$ is decisive because:
1. Two lamps delivering the same lux level may consume radically different watts — a higher $\eta$ lamp cuts **both energy and demand charges**.
2. Indian BEE Star labelling uses $\eta$ as the **primary metric** to award 1-to-5 stars.
3. It enables *apples-to-apples* comparison across technologies (incandescent vs CFL vs LED).

*(Self-assessment: 3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula & unit, 1 mark relevance to energy auditing.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Distinguish between Luminous Flux, Luminous Intensity, and Illuminance with units.**

**Model Answer:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | Total visible light energy emitted per second by a source in all directions | **lumen (lm)** |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | Luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction | **candela (cd = lm/sr)** |
| Illuminance | $E$ | Luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface | **lux (lx = lm/m²)** |

Mathematically: $I = d\Phi/d\Omega$ and $E = d\Phi/dA$.

> A practical illustration: a bulb emits 1 000 lm total (flux); within a 1-steradian cone it has 1 000 cd intensity; on a 10 m² surface 1 m below it gives 100 lx illuminance.

*(Self-assessment: 3 marks — 1 mark each, with at least one formula and one unit conversion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Module 2 typically offers **Module Choice 1 & 2** of 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Modern Light Sources Focus**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Adapted for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction, working principle, and advantages of a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** lamp over a Compact Fluorescent Lamp (CFL). Compare their performance on luminous efficacy, CRI, and lamp life. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A workshop of area **20 m × 15 m** is currently illuminated by **40 fittings of 250 W mercury-vapour (MV) lamps**. Propose a replacement with LED high-bay fixtures of equivalent illuminance. Given: efficacy of MV = 40 lm/W, efficacy of proposed LED = 120 lm/W, UF × MF = 0.5, operating hours = 2 500 h/year, tariff ₹ 9/kWh, cost per LED fitting = ₹ 4 500, no salvage value.

Calculate:
1. The lumen output of one MV lamp.
2. The wattage of the equivalent LED fitting.
3. Annual energy and monetary savings.
4. Simple payback period. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Construction (3 marks):** An LED lamp comprises:

- **LED chip** (InGaN or AlGaInP semiconductor die) mounted on a **ceramic or metal-core PCB**.
- **Phosphor layer** (yellow phosphor mixed with blue chip → white light via down-conversion).
- **Heat sink** (aluminium fins) for thermal management.
- **SMPS driver** converting 230 V AC to constant low-voltage DC.
- **Optical lens** (PMMA/silicone) for beam shaping.
- **E27/E40 Edison base** for retrofit compatibility.

**Working Principle (2 marks):** Forward biasing the p-n junction injects electrons from the n-side into the p-side. They recombine with holes across the **band-gap energy $E_g$**, releasing a photon of energy:

$$
E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = E_g
$$

For white light, a blue LED ($E_g \approx 2.7$ eV, $\lambda \approx 460$ nm) pumps a yellow phosphor (YAG:Ce), and the combination appears white to the human eye.

**Advantages over CFL (2 marks):**

| Parameter | CFL | LED | LED Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Efficacy | 45–75 lm/W | 80–200 lm/W | 2–3× higher |
| Lamp life | 8–15 kh | 25–50 kh | 3–4× longer |
| Mercury | 3–5 mg | 0 mg | Eco-safe disposal |
| Switching | Degrades with rapid on/off | No effect | Suitable for occupancy sensors |
| Warm-up | 30 s | Instant | Better for safety/security |

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**(1) Lumen output of one MV lamp:**

$$
\Phi_{MV} = 250 \times 40 = 10\,000~\text{lm}
$$

**(2) Required total flux for the workshop (lumen method):**

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}} = \frac{? \times 300}{0.5}
$$

Since the question states the LED must be "equivalent" in illuminance, total flux required = 40 × 10 000 = 400 000 lm (assuming 1:1 fixture replacement).

For an equivalent LED fixture:

$$
\Phi_{LED} = 10\,000~\text{lm}
$$

$$
P_{LED} = \frac{\Phi_{LED}}{\eta_{LED}} = \frac{10\,000}{120} = 83.33~\text{W}
$$

Rounding to a commercially available rating: **80 W or 90 W LED high-bay**.

**(3) Annual energy & monetary savings:**

Connected load reduction:

$$
\Delta P = (250 - 83.33) \times 40 = 6\,666.8~\text{W} = 6.67~\text{kW}
$$

Annual energy saving:

$$
\Delta E = 6.67 \times 2\,500 = 16\,675~\text{kWh}
$$

Annual monetary saving:

$$
S = 16\,675 \times 9 = \text{₹ }1\,50\,075
$$

**(4) Simple payback:**

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{40 \times 4\,500}{1\,50\,075} = \frac{1\,80\,000}{1\,50\,075} = 1.20~\text{years}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Payback} \approx 14.4~\text{months}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Energy Auditing Focus**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Adapted for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define the **Lumen Method** of lighting design. A classroom of area **12 m × 8 m** requires an average illuminance of **300 lux**. Utilisation Factor = 0.55, Maintenance Factor = 0.8. Determine the number of 36 W T5 fluorescent tubes (each 3 350 lm) required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **energy-saving opportunities in lighting systems** for a typical commercial building. Include in your answer: (i) replacement of inefficient lamps, (ii) use of electronic ballasts, (iii) lighting controls (occupancy sensors, daylight harvesting), and (iv) task-ambient lighting design. Justify each with a quantitative example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Lumen Method Definition (2 marks):** The Lumen Method is a *zonal-cavity* lighting design procedure that calculates the total luminous flux required to deliver a target average illuminance over an area, accounting for fixture efficiency, room geometry, and lamp lumen depreciation:

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = \frac{E \times A}{\text{UF} \times \text{MF}}
$$

**Numerical Solution (5 marks):**

Area:

$$
A = 12 \times 8 = 96~\text{m}^{2}
$$

Total flux required:

$$
\Phi_{\text{required}} = \frac{300 \times 96}{0.55 \times 0.8} = \frac{28\,800}{0.44} = 65\,454.5~\text{lm}
$$

Number of tubes:

$$
N = \frac{65\,454.5}{3\,350} = 19.54
$$

Rounding up:

$$
\boxed{N = 20 \text{ tubes}}
$$

**Verification (optional):** Total flux = 20 × 3 350 = 67 000 lm ≥ 65 454 lm ✓

**Practical layout:** Two 2-lamp fixtures per row × 5 rows = 20 tubes; or four 1-lamp fixtures × 5 rows.

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) — Common Error

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Rounding Direction:** Students often round **down** to 19 tubes. The lumen method *requires* rounding **up** because illuminance is a *minimum* requirement (not a target). 19 tubes would give 19 × 3 350 = 63 650 lm, falling short by 2.7% — a clear IS 3646 violation.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**(i) Lamp Replacement (2 marks):**

Replace 40 W T12 fluorescent with 28 W T5 super-efficient — saving **30% per luminaire**.
*Quantitative example:* 1 000 fittings × (40 − 28) W = 12 kW × 2 400 h = **28 800 kWh/year saved**.

**(ii) Electronic Ballasts (2 marks):**

Magnetic ballasts for 36 W FL lose ~10 W as heat → use HF electronic ballast (< 2 W loss).
For 1 000 fittings × 8 W = 8 kW × 2 400 h = **19 200 kWh/year saved**, plus 30% lamp-life extension.

**(iii) Lighting Controls (2 marks):**

- **Occupancy sensors** in conference rooms, rest-rooms → 30–50% saving (typically OFF when vacant).
- **Daylight-harvesting photocells** near windows → dim artificial light when daylight > 500 lux.
- **Quantitative example:** Sensor-controlled zone of 5 kW operating 2 400 h; with 40% OFF-time = 2 kW × 2 400 h = **4 800 kWh/year saved**.

**(iv) Task-Ambient Design (1 mark):**

- Ambient: 200 lux across the floor using efficient indirect LED panels.
- Task: 500 lux at desks via adjustable LED task-lamps.
- Net saving: ~40% compared to uniform 500-lux design.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Verdict:** A 7-mark answer must give **at least 2 numerical examples**. Pure qualitative description scores ≤ 4 marks.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up Lumen (lm) and Lux (lx)** — *Lumen is the lamp's output; Lux is what lands on your desk.*
> 2. **Forgetting the cosine factor** in $E = I\cos\theta/d^2$ derivations.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** in numerical problems (e.g., unity power factor, 100% utilisation of all hours).
> 4. **Rounding tube-count down** in the lumen method.
> 5. **Ignoring ballast loss** when comparing T5 vs T8 systems.
> 6. **Confusing CCT (warm vs cool) with CRI (colour fidelity).** 5 000 K does not imply CRI > 80.
> 7. **Failing to convert W → kW** before computing kWh or ₹ savings.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-memorise before the exam):**

- ☐ The **five KPIs** of a light source: Efficacy, CRI, CCT, Life, Wattage.
- ☐ LED > CFL > Halogen > Incandescent in efficacy order.
- ☐ LED offers **80–200 lm/W**, **25 000–50 000 h life**, and **Ra 80–98**.
- ☐ HPSV dominates street lighting (high efficacy) but has **poor CRI (20–25)**.
- ☐ Induction lamps offer the **longest life (up to 100 000 h)** but are bulky.
- ☐ The **Lumen Method** formula: $\Phi = (E \times A) / (\text{UF} \times \text{MF})$ — always round **up**.
- ☐ The **Inverse-Square Cosine Law**: $E = I\cos\theta / d^{2}$.
- ☐ **Simple Payback** = (Incremental Cost) / (Annual ₹ Saving).
- ☐ A **62.5% energy reduction** is achievable by typical HPSV-to-LED retrofits.
- ☐ BEE Star Label mandates **≥ 90 lm/W** for top-rated LED bulbs in India.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** (PF, hours/day, days/year) before any numerical.
- ☐ **Mercury content** is a major differentiator — LED and OLED are mercury-free; all fluorescent and HID lamps contain mercury.
- ☐ **L70 B50** is the industry-standard LED life metric — 70% lumen maintenance for 50% of population.
- ☐ For street-lighting audits, remember the **coincidence factor** and **diversity factor** when scaling up per-luminaire savings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Life and Efficacy Comparison with Older Light Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Life and Efficacy Comparison with Older Light Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Key Metrics

**Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$ or $\eta_v$)** is the ratio of the total luminous flux emitted by a light source to the total electrical power consumed by that source. It is the single most important metric for energy auditors comparing lighting technologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Luminous Efficacy** is measured in **lumens per watt (lm/W)** and quantifies how efficiently an electrical power input of **1 watt (W)** is converted into visible light perceived by the human eye (weighted by the photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$).

**Rated Life ($L_r$)** of a light source is the number of operating hours after which **50%** of a large batch of identical lamps is expected to fail under standard test conditions. It is also called the **B50 life** or **average rated life**.

**Useful Life ($L_p$)** is the time (in hours) after which the luminous flux of the source drops to a stated percentage (commonly **70%** for general lighting, denoted **L70**, or **80%** for premium applications, denoted **L80**) of its initial output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Energy Auditing, KTU examiners focus on **two pillars of comparison**: (1) **Efficacy (lm/W)** and (2) **Useful Life (hours)**. Higher efficacy means less electricity for the same light; longer life means reduced maintenance cost and replacement frequency.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are filling a glass of water (the *luminous flux / useful light*) from different taps using the same amount of electricity (the *wattage*).

- An **Incandescent bulb** is like a bucket with a huge hole — most of the water (energy) leaks out as heat, and you only get a small glass of water. The bucket also wears out quickly (short life).
- A **Fluorescent / CFL lamp** is a much better bucket with a smaller hole — less waste heat, more light per unit electricity, and it lasts much longer.
- An **LED** is a precision-engineered glass — almost no leakage, very high water (light) yield per unit electricity, and the glass is nearly unbreakable for thousands of hours.

The **"hole"** in this analogy is the **inefficiency** — energy lost as **infrared (heat) radiation** rather than visible light. Efficacy is simply asking, *"For the same electricity bill, how full is the glass of light?"*

### 1.3 Classification of Light Sources

Light sources compared under this module fall into two broad families:

**A. Older / Conventional Sources:**
- **GLS (General Lighting Service) Incandescent Lamps**
- **Tungsten Halogen Lamps**
- **Linear & Compact Fluorescent Lamps (CFL)**
- **High Intensity Discharge (HID)** family — HPMV, HPSV, Metal Halide

**B. Modern / Solid-State Sources:**
- **Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term *older light sources* in the KTU 2024 syllabus refers to **all pre-LED technologies** (incandescent, halogen, fluorescent, HID). LEDs are the benchmark against which the audit comparison is made.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Efficacy Loss

Every light source converts electrical energy into three forms:

$$P_{electrical} = P_{visible} + P_{infrared} + P_{ultraviolet} + P_{convective}$$

The **visible portion** ($P_{visible}$) is what the human eye can use. The percentage of input power converted to visible light is called the **radiant efficiency** of the source. Multiplying radiant efficiency by the photopic eye-response weighting function $V(\lambda)$ gives the **luminous efficacy**.

**Why older sources are inefficient:**

- **Incandescent:** A tungsten filament is heated to ~2700 K. By Planck's law, a blackbody at this temperature radiates overwhelmingly in the **infrared** region. Visible output is only ~5–10% of input; the rest is heat.
- **Halogen:** Uses a regenerative halogen cycle (tungsten + halogen gas) to allow higher filament temperature (~3000 K), shifting more emission into the visible band, but the gain is marginal.
- **Fluorescent / CFL:** Mercury vapor discharge emits **UV light** (254 nm), which is then converted to visible light by a phosphor coating. Losses occur in the UV→visible conversion (Stokes shift loss ~30–40%) and in the ballast.
- **HID:** Higher temperature arc (4000–6000 K) gives better spectral match to $V(\lambda)$ but still loses energy in non-visible bands and ballast.

**LEDs** work on **electroluminescence** — electron-hole recombination in a p-n junction. The semiconductor bandgap can be tuned to emit **directly in the visible band** with very little IR or UV loss, which is why efficacy can exceed **200 lm/W** in laboratory devices and **120–150 lm/W** in commercial products.

### 2.2 Useful Life — Degradation Mechanisms

The **rated life (B50)** is a *failure-based* metric, while **L70 / L80** is a *lumen-depreciation-based* metric. For modern sources, depreciation usually causes end-of-life before failure.

| Source | Dominant End-of-Life Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Incandescent / Halogen | Filament evaporation, hotspot rupture |
| Fluorescent / CFL | Cathode wear, phosphor degradation, mercury loss, ballast failure |
| HID | Electrode erosion, arc tube blackening, sodium loss |
| LED | **Lumen depreciation** (phosphor/silicone degradation, junction heating) — LEDs rarely *fail* suddenly |

> [!NOTE]
> **L70 is the standard KTU / IS 16106 benchmark** for useful life of LED luminaires. It means the LED delivers at least 70% of its initial lumens at the stated number of hours.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P}$ | lm/W | $\Phi_v$ = luminous flux; $P$ = input power |
| 2 | System Efficacy | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{lamp} + P_{ballast}}$ | lm/W | Includes ballast/driver losses |
| 3 | Useful Life Index | $L_p$ where output = $p\%$ of initial | hours | L70 = 70% lumen maintenance |
| 4 | Annual Energy Consumption | $E_a = P \times N \times t$ | kWh/year | $N$ = no. of lamps; $t$ = daily burning hours |
| 5 | Annual Cost | $C_a = E_a \times \text{rate}$ | ₹/year | rate in ₹/kWh |
| 6 | Payback Period | $PB = \dfrac{\Delta C_{lamp}}{\Delta C_{energy, annual}}$ | years | For retrofit justification |
| 7 | Lumen Depreciation | $\Phi(t) = \Phi_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha t}$ | lumens | Exponential model (approximate) |
| 8 | Colour Rendering Index | $R_a$ or CRI | 0–100 | $R_a = 100$ = perfect (reference source) |
| 9 | Correlated Colour Temperature | CCT | Kelvin (K) | Warm < 3000 K; Cool > 4000 K |
| 10 | Efficacy Ratio | $R_{\eta} = \dfrac{\eta_{new}}{\eta_{old}}$ | dimensionless | Used to compare technologies |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Energy Auditing

During a **walk-through energy audit** of a commercial building, the auditor surveys fittings, reads wattage labels, and estimates the **annual kWh consumed by lighting** (typically 20–30% of total building electricity). The replacement decision is driven by:

1. **Efficacy gain** → lower connected load → lower electricity bill.
2. **Life gain** → fewer replacements → lower maintenance cost and labor.
3. **Payback period** → must typically be < 3 years to be sanctioned.
4. **Power factor & harmonics** → LED drivers must comply with IS 16104.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) star labelling of LED bulbs in India is based on **luminous efficacy bands**. As of 2024, a **5-star LED bulb** must deliver $\geq 120$ lm/W.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Efficacy of a Light Source

**Given:** A fluorescent tube consumes **40 W** (lamp) and the ballast consumes **8 W**. It emits a luminous flux of **3200 lumens**.

**Step 1: Write the efficacy definition.**

$$\eta_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{input}}$$

**Step 2: Determine the total input power (lamp + ballast).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{input} &= P_{lamp} + P_{ballast} \\
P_{input} &= 40\ \text{W} + 8\ \text{W} = 48\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values into the efficacy formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_v &= \frac{3200\ \text{lm}}{48\ \text{W}} \\
\eta_v &= 66.67\ \text{lm/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This fluorescent tube has a **system efficacy of ~66.67 lm/W**, which is typical for a T8 tube with electromagnetic ballast.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Comparison of Annual Energy and Cost

**Problem:** A hall has **50 fittings**, each with a **100 W incandescent bulb** burning for **10 hours/day, 300 days/year**. Electricity rate is **₹7 per kWh**. The auditor proposes replacing each with a **20 W LED** (same light output).

**Step 1: Annual energy of the existing installation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{inc} &= N \times P_{inc} \times t_{day} \times D \\
E_{inc} &= 50 \times 100\ \text{W} \times 10\ \text{h} \times 300 \\
E_{inc} &= 15{,}000{,}000\ \text{Wh} = 15{,}000\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Annual cost of the existing installation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{inc} &= E_{inc} \times \text{rate} \\
C_{inc} &= 15{,}000\ \text{kWh} \times ₹7 \\
C_{inc} &= ₹1{,}05{,}000\ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Annual energy of the proposed LED installation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LED} &= N \times P_{LED} \times t_{day} \times D \\
E_{LED} &= 50 \times 20\ \text{W} \times 10 \times 300 \\
E_{LED} &= 3{,}000{,}000\ \text{Wh} = 3{,}000\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Annual cost of the LED installation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{LED} &= 3{,}000 \times 7 = ₹21{,}000\ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Annual energy saving.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{inc} - E_{LED} = 15{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 12{,}000\ \text{kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Annual monetary saving.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= ₹1{,}05{,}000 - ₹21{,}000 = ₹84{,}000\ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Power reduction (demand-side).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= (100 - 20) \times 50 = 4000\ \text{W} = 4\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Replacing 50 incandescent bulbs with LEDs saves **12,000 kWh/year**, **₹84,000/year**, and reduces connected load by **4 kW**. This is a **80% reduction in lighting energy** — a classic KTU audit result.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Payback Period Calculation

**Assumed LED fixture cost:** ₹250 per piece (50 nos. = ₹12,500). Labor: ₹2,500. Total retrofit cost = **₹15,000**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB &= \frac{\text{Incremental Cost}}{\text{Annual Savings}} \\
PB &= \frac{₹15{,}000}{₹84{,}000\ \text{per year}} \\
PB &= 0.178\ \text{years} \approx 65\ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Payback < 1 year is considered **excellent** in BEE / KTU audit standards. The investment is recovered in about **2 months** — a no-brainer recommendation.

---

### 3.4 Master Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | GLS Incandescent | Halogen | Fluorescent (T8) | CFL | HPSV (HID) | Metal Halide | **LED** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power (W)** for ~1600 lm | 100 | 70 | 40 | 18–23 | 35–50 | 30–35 | **15–18** |
| **Efficacy (lm/W)** | 10–17 | 15–25 | 60–80 | 50–70 | 80–120 | 70–90 | **100–160** |
| **Rated Life (h)** | 1,000 | 2,000–4,000 | 15,000 | 8,000–10,000 | 20,000–24,000 | 10,000–15,000 | **25,000–50,000+** |
| **L70 Life (h)** | N/A | N/A | N/A | ~6,000 | ~12,000 | ~8,000 | **25,000–50,000** |
| **CRI (Ra)** | 100 | 100 | 60–85 | 80–85 | 20–25 | 80–95 | **70–95** |
| **Warm-up time** | Instant | Instant | 1–3 s | 1–3 s | 5–10 min | 5–10 min | **Instant** |
| **Restart delay** | None | None | None | None | 1–5 min | 5–15 min | **None** |
| **Switching effect** | None | None | Reduces life | Reduces life | Reduces life | Reduces life | **Negligible** |
| **UV / IR emission** | High IR | High IR | UV (filtered) | UV (filtered) | UV + IR | UV + IR | **Negligible** |
