| **Mercury content** | None | None | 5–15 mg | 3–5 mg | 20–50 mg | 20–50 mg | **None** |
| **Dimmable** | Yes | Yes | With ballast | With CFL dimmer | Limited | Limited | **Yes (0–10 V / PWM)** |
| **Cost per kIm (₹)** | ~50 | ~70 | ~40 | ~60 | ~50 | ~80 | **~30–50** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key audit insight:** A **100 W incandescent** delivering ~1600 lm is replaced by an **18 W LED** delivering the same lumens. Power saving = **82 W per lamp**. Across 1,000 fittings, this is a demand reduction of **82 kW** and annual energy savings exceeding **2,00,000 kWh**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Comparison of Light Source Families

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode([Electrical Light Sources])
    startNode --> familyA[Conventional Sources]
    startNode --> familyB[Modern Solid State Sources]
    familyA --> incNode[GLS Incandescent]
    familyA --> haloNode[Tungsten Halogen]
    familyA --> fluorNode[Linear Fluorescent T8 T5]
    familyA --> cflNode[Compact Fluorescent CFL]
    familyA --> hidNode[HID Family]
    hidNode --> hpmvNode[HPMV]
    hidNode --> hpsvNode[HPSV]
    hidNode --> mhNode[Metal Halide]
    familyB --> ledNode[Light Emitting Diode LED]
    ledNode --> smdNode[SMD LED]
    ledNode --> cobNode[COB LED]
    ledNode --> filNode[Filament LED]
    incNode -->|10 to 17 lm per W| rating1[Lowest Efficacy]
    ledNode -->|100 to 160 lm per W| rating2[Highest Efficacy]
    cflNode -->|8000 to 10000 h| life1[Short to Medium Life]
    ledNode -->|25000 to 50000 h| life2[Longest Life]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Audit Decision Flow for Lighting Retrofit

```mermaid
graph TD
    auditStart([Walk Through Lighting Audit])
    auditStart --> surveyNode[Step 1 Inventory of Fittings]
    surveyNode --> loadNode[Step 2 Measure Connected Load kW]
    loadNode --> hoursNode[Step 3 Record Burning Hours per day]
    hoursNode --> kwhNode[Step 4 Compute Annual kWh]
    kwhNode --> compareNode[Step 5 Compare Efficacy Old vs LED]
    compareNode --> savingNode[Step 6 Calculate Annual Energy Saving]
    savingNode --> payNode[Step 7 Compute Payback Period]
    payNode --> decisionNode{Payback under 3 years}
    decisionNode -->|Yes| recommendNode[Recommend LED Retrofit]
    decisionNode -->|No| checkNode[Check Subsidies or Re evaluate Operating Hours]
    checkNode --> payNode
    recommendNode --> reportNode[Include in Audit Report with BEE Compliance]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Lumen Depreciation Curve Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    t0[T equals 0 hours] --> p100[Output equals 100 percent]
    p100 -->|Lamp operates| phase1[Phase 1 Rapid Initial Drop First 1000 h]
    phase1 --> phase2[Phase 2 Steady Linear Decline]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3 End of Useful Life L70 or L80]
    phase3 --> eol[End of Life Replacement Trigger]
    incCurve[GLS Incandescent reaches 70 percent in approx 750 h] -.-> phase3
    ledCurve[LED reaches L70 only after 25000 to 50000 h] -.-> phase3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The depreciation curve above is the conceptual basis of the **TM-21 / LM-80 test** used to project LED life. KTU 2024 syllabus students should remember: **rated life ≠ useful life**. Always use **L70 or L80** for LED retrofit decisions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1. Define the term "luminous efficacy" of a light source. State its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**

Luminous efficacy of a light source is defined as the ratio of the total luminous flux (in lumens) emitted by the source to the total electrical power (in watts) consumed by it. It measures how efficiently electrical energy is converted into visible light as perceived by the human eye.

$$\eta_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{P}$$

**SI unit:** **lumen per watt (lm/W)**.

A higher value of $\eta_v$ indicates a more energy-efficient source. For example, a typical GLS incandescent lamp has $\eta_v \approx 10$–$17$ lm/W, while a modern LED has $\eta_v \approx 100$–$160$ lm/W.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2. What is meant by the "useful life" (L70) of an LED luminaire? Why is it preferred over the rated life for energy audit purposes?**

**Model Answer:**

The **useful life (L70)** of an LED luminaire is the number of operating hours after which the luminous flux of the LED drops to **70%** of its initial (rated) output. It is denoted **L70** and is also called the **70% lumen maintenance life**.

It is preferred over the *rated life* (B50) because:

- LEDs **rarely fail catastrophically** like incandescent filaments. They *gradually dim* as the phosphor and silicone encapsulant degrade.
- A *rated life* of 50,000 h would falsely suggest 50,000 h of full output.
- L70 reflects **actual useful photometric performance**, which is what the auditor/occupant experiences.
- International standards **IES LM-80 and TM-21** (and Indian Standard **IS 16106**) define LED life on the L70 basis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **rated life (B50)** with **L70**. Rated life = 50% failures. L70 = 70% lumen maintenance. **Mixing them up will cost you 1–2 marks.**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the efficacy and useful life of the following light sources in a tabular form: GLS Incandescent, Halogen, Fluorescent (T8), HPSV, Metal Halide, and LED. Identify which source is the most energy-efficient and justify your answer with a typical numerical example.

**Model Answer:**

The comparison is presented in the table below (developed from the Master Comparison Matrix in Section 3.4):

| Source | Efficacy (lm/W) | Useful Life (h) | CRI (Ra) |
|---|---|---|---|
| GLS Incandescent | 10–17 | 1,000 | 100 |
| Halogen | 15–25 | 2,000–4,000 | 100 |
| Fluorescent (T8) | 60–80 | 10,000–15,000 | 60–85 |
| HPSV | 80–120 | 20,000–24,000 | 20–25 |
| Metal Halide | 70–90 | 10,000–15,000 | 80–95 |
| **LED** | **100–160** | **25,000–50,000** | **70–95** |

**Justification with numerical example:**

To deliver a target luminous flux of **2,000 lumens**:

- A GLS incandescent would need $P = 2000/13 \approx 154$ W (using mid-range efficacy 13 lm/W).
- An LED would need $P = 2000/130 \approx 15.4$ W (using mid-range efficacy 130 lm/W).

Power saved per lamp = $154 - 15.4 = 138.6$ W, i.e., a **~90% reduction** in input power. The LED is therefore the most energy-efficient source, with the added benefit of **25–50 times** longer life, drastically reducing maintenance.

**Conclusion:** **LED is the most efficient light source** in the comparison group, followed by HPSV (for high-bay applications where CRI is not critical). HPSV outperforms LED only in very few niche applications (e.g., street lighting of high-pressure sodium type), but LED is rapidly replacing even those.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A factory has 200 metal-halide lamps, each rated 250 W. These operate 12 hours/day, 300 days/year. As energy auditor, propose a retrofit to LED equivalents (each 80 W) and calculate the annual energy saved, cost saved (electricity @ ₹8/kWh), and simple payback period if the LED retrofit costs ₹3,000 per fitting (total ₹6,00,000).

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Annual energy of the existing MH installation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{MH} &= N \times P_{MH} \times t_{day} \times D \\
E_{MH} &= 200 \times 250 \times 12 \times 300 \\
E_{MH} &= 1.8 \times 10^8\ \text{Wh} = 1{,}80{,}000\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Annual energy of the LED installation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LED} &= 200 \times 80 \times 12 \times 300 \\
E_{LED} &= 5.76 \times 10^7\ \text{Wh} = 57{,}600\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Annual energy saving.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 1{,}80{,}000 - 57{,}600 = 1{,}22{,}400\ \text{kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Annual monetary saving.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= 1{,}22{,}400 \times 8 = ₹9{,}79{,}200\ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Demand (kW) reduction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 200 \times (250 - 80) = 200 \times 170 = 34{,}000\ \text{W} = 34\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Simple Payback Period.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PB &= \frac{₹6{,}00{,}000}{₹9{,}79{,}200} \\
PB &= 0.613\ \text{years} \approx 7.35\ \text{months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The retrofit is **strongly recommended**. It pays for itself in **less than 8 months**, saves **₹9.79 lakh per year**, and reduces the connected load by **34 kW** — qualifying for **BEE / DISCOM incentive** schemes in Kerala (e.g., KSEB DSM programs).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concept of **lumen maintenance** in LED lighting. How is the L70 life of an LED determined experimentally? Mention the relevant IS / IES standard.

**Model Answer:**

**Lumen Maintenance Concept:**

Lumen maintenance refers to the ability of a light source to retain its initial luminous output over its operational life. Unlike incandescent lamps, which fail abruptly by filament rupture, LEDs experience **gradual lumen depreciation** due to:

1. **Phosphor degradation** (thermal + photonic stress).
2. **Encapsulant yellowing** (silicone/epoxy exposure to heat and UV).
3. **Junction temperature rise** (poor thermal management accelerates decay).
4. **Solder bond fatigue** (thermal cycling).

**Experimental Determination of L70:**

The L70 life is determined using the following standardized procedure:

- **IES LM-80-15** (Approved Method for Lumen Maintenance of LED Light Sources): LEDs are operated at **three different case temperatures** (typically 55°C, 85°C, and a third, e.g., 105°C) for a minimum of **6,000 hours (recommended 10,000 h)**, with periodic measurements of luminous flux.
- **IES TM-21-11** (Projecting Long Term Luminous Flux Maintenance of LED Lamps and Luminaires): The LM-80 data is fitted with an **exponential decay model**:

$$
\Phi(t) = \Phi_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha t}
$$

The fitted $\alpha$ is used to **extrapolate** the time at which $\Phi(t) / \Phi_0 = 0.70$ — this is the **projected L70 life**.

**Relevant Standard:** **IS 16106:2012 (Luminaires — LED — Performance Requirements)** and **IS 16103 (LED Modules)**. The **BEE Star Labelling** program in India also follows the same L70 framework for 5-star rating.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** An office has 100 tube-light fittings (each 40 W fluorescent + 10 W conventional ballast). A proposal is made to replace them with 18 W LED tube lights (no external ballast). Compute (i) efficacy of the existing and proposed systems, (ii) annual energy saved for 8 hours/day, 250 days/year, and (iii) payback if LED tube costs ₹350 each and labour is ₹50/fitting (electricity ₹9/kWh).

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Efficacy of the existing fluorescent system.**

Total input per fitting = $40 + 10 = 50$ W. A typical 4-ft T8 tube delivers ~3000 lm.

$$
\eta_{fluor} = \frac{3000}{50} = 60\ \text{lm/W}
$$

**Step 2: Efficacy of the proposed LED system.**

$$
\eta_{LED} = \frac{3000}{18} \approx 166.7\ \text{lm/W}
$$

**Step 3: Annual energy of the existing system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{fluor} &= 100 \times 50 \times 8 \times 250 \\
E_{fluor} &= 1.0 \times 10^7\ \text{Wh} = 10{,}000\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Annual energy of the LED system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LED} &= 100 \times 18 \times 8 \times 250 \\
E_{LED} &= 3.6 \times 10^6\ \text{Wh} = 3{,}600\ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Annual energy saved.**

$$
\Delta E = 10{,}000 - 3{,}600 = 6{,}400\ \text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 6: Annual cost saved.**

$$
\Delta C = 6{,}400 \times 9 = ₹57{,}600\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 7: Retrofit cost.**

$$
C_{retrofit} = 100 \times (350 + 50) = 100 \times 400 = ₹40{,}000
$$

**Step 8: Payback period.**

$$
PB = \frac{40{,}000}{57{,}600} = 0.694\ \text{years} \approx 8.3\ \text{months}
$$

**Conclusion:** Replacing 100 fluorescent tube-lights with LED tubes yields an **efficacy gain from 60 → 166.7 lm/W** (a **2.78× improvement**), saves **6,400 kWh/year** and **₹57,600/year**, with a payback of just **8.3 months** — an **excellent** energy audit recommendation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing efficacy (lm/W) with efficiency (%).** Efficacy is a *photometric* quantity weighted by the eye response $V(\lambda)$; efficiency is a pure *radiometric* ratio. Do not interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting ballast loss.** Always quote *system efficacy* (lamp + ballast) for fluorescent / HID, not just lamp efficacy. KTU specifically tests this in Part B numericals.
> 3. **Mixing L70 with rated life.** L70 = 70% lumen maintenance (used for LEDs). Rated life (B50) = 50% failures (used for conventional lamps).
> 4. **Not mentioning Lumen Depreciation Curve (TM-21)** when explaining LED life — KTU 2024 specifically emphasizes this in Module 2.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always write kWh, ₹, lm/W, and hours explicitly.
> 6. **Wrong power factor / ballast calculation:** For electromagnetic ballasts, add 8–10 W per 40 W tube; for electronic ballasts, add 2–3 W only.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Luminous efficacy** $\eta_v = \Phi_v / P$ in **lm/W** is the primary energy-audit metric for lighting.
- **Useful life (L70/L80)** is used for LEDs; **rated life (B50)** is used for conventional lamps.
- **Order of efficacy (low → high):** Incandescent (10–17) < Halogen (15–25) < CFL (50–70) < Fluorescent T8 (60–80) < Metal Halide (70–90) < HPSV (80–120) < **LED (100–160)**.
- **Order of life (short → long):** Incandescent (1,000 h) < Halogen (2,000–4,000 h) < CFL (8,000–10,000 h) < Fluorescent (15,000 h) < Metal Halide (10,000–15,000 h) < HPSV (20,000–24,000 h) < **LED (25,000–50,000 h)**.
- **CRI is highest (100)** for incandescent and halogen — they are still preferred where color accuracy is critical (e.g., jewelry, art galleries) despite poor efficacy.
- **L70 life of LED** is determined by **IES LM-80 + TM-21** test (or **IS 16106** equivalent in India).
- **Annual energy of lighting** $E_a = N \times P \times t_{day} \times D$ is the foundation of every KTU lighting audit numerical.
- **Payback period** must be < 3 years (preferably < 1 year) for any BEE-approved lighting retrofit.
- **BEE 5-star LED standard (2024):** $\geq 120$ lm/W.
- **LED advantage pillars:** (1) High efficacy, (2) Long life, (3) Low heat, (4) No UV/IR, (5) No mercury, (6) Robust to switching, (7) Directional light reduces wastage.
- **HPSV is preferred over LED** in some specific outdoor / street-lighting retrofits (older HID systems) but LED is rapidly superseding it.
- **CFL contains mercury (3–5 mg)** — must be disposed as hazardous waste; LED has **no mercury**.
- **Lumen Depreciation Curve** has 3 phases: (i) initial rapid drop (burn-in), (ii) steady linear decline, (iii) end of life (L70/L80).
- For **KTU 2024 numericals**, always compute **(i) existing energy, (ii) proposed energy, (iii) saving in kWh, (iv) saving in ₹, (v) payback in years** — this is the standard 5-step audit format.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation in Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Conservation in Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Lighting Energy Conservation** is a systematic engineering discipline within the broader domain of *Energy Management & Auditing* that focuses on optimizing the conversion, distribution, and utilization of electrical energy into visible light. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET742 – Module 2)**, it is defined as the practice of providing the required illumination level (measured in **lux**) for a given visual task while minimizing the electrical input (in **watts**) by improving **luminous efficacy (η, lm/W)**, employing efficient luminaires, deploying intelligent controls, and adhering to the *National Building Code (NBC) of India* and *Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS – IS 3646)* recommended illuminance levels.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Water in a Tank"**
> Think of electricity as water flowing into a tank, and useful light as the water that actually reaches a bucket placed below. A poorly designed lighting system is like a tank with **cracks and holes** — most of the water (electricity) is wasted as **heat** (infrared radiation) or leaks (light going in the wrong direction). Energy conservation in lighting is essentially the process of *patching the cracks* (using LED instead of incandescent) and *redirecting the flow* (using proper reflectors) so that **maximum water reaches the bucket** (the work-plane) with **minimum wastage**. In technical terms: we want to *maximize lumens delivered per watt consumed*.

### 1.3 Fundamental Photometric Quantities

Lighting calculations differ from electrical calculations because the human eye does not respond equally to all wavelengths. The eye's response is **photopically weighted** by the **CIE Standard Photopic Luminosity Function V(λ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Photometric Quantities (MUST MEMORIZE for KTU ESE):**
> - **Luminous Flux (Φ)** — Total light energy emitted per second, weighted by V(λ). Unit: **lumen (lm)**.
> - **Luminous Intensity (I)** — Flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction. Unit: **candela (cd)**.
> - **Illuminance (E)** — Flux incident on a unit area of a surface. Unit: **lux (lx)** = **lm/m²**.
> - **Luminance (L)** — Intensity per unit projected area of a luminous/reflecting surface. Unit: **cd/m²**.
> - **Luminous Efficacy (η)** — Flux emitted per unit electrical power. Unit: **lm/W**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Use in numericals):**
> - **1 candela (cd)** = **1 lumen per steradian (lm/sr)**.
> - **1 lux (lx)** = **1 lumen per square metre (lm/m²)**.
> - **Maximum theoretical luminous efficacy of white light** ≈ **683 lm/W** (at 555 nm, green-yellow).
> - **Standard maintained illuminance values** (as per **IS 3646 / NBC 2016**):
>   - Office general work: **300–500 lux**
>   - Classrooms: **300 lux**
>   - Corridors: **100 lux**
>   - Detailed engineering drawing: **750 lux**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Lighting Energy Conservation

Approximately **20–25% of total electrical energy** consumed in commercial buildings is attributed to lighting. India has committed to reduce its energy intensity under the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), making lighting conservation a **high-priority industrial topic** and hence a **guaranteed KTU ESE question**.

The hierarchy of energy conservation in lighting follows the **Energy Management Hierarchy (4 Es)**: **Eliminate → Economize → Efficient Equipment → Employ Renewables**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Photometric Calculations

To size any lighting installation, the KTU examiner expects the student to follow this 4-step logic:

- **Step 1 – Establish Visual Task Requirements:** Determine the required maintained illuminance ($E$) in lux from IS 3646.
- **Step 2 – Calculate Total Flux Required:** Compute the total luminous flux needed on the work-plane.
- **Step 3 – Account for System Losses:** Apply **Utilization Factor (UF)** and **Maintenance Factor (MF)** to convert "flux on the desk" to "flux out of the lamp".
- **Step 4 – Convert to Electrical Load:** Divide by the **luminous efficacy (η)** of the chosen lamp to get the electrical wattage.

### 2.3 Lamp Technologies: Comparative Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Lamp Comparison (Very High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Incandescent (GLS):** Efficacy ≈ **10–15 lm/W**, life ≈ **1000 hrs**, 90% of energy becomes heat. *Obsolete for energy audit purposes.*
> - **Fluorescent Tube (T8/T5):** Efficacy ≈ **50–80 lm/W**, life ≈ **15,000 hrs**, requires **electromagnetic or electronic ballast**.
> - **CFL (Compact Fluorescent):** Efficacy ≈ **50–70 lm/W**, life ≈ **8,000 hrs**, direct incandescent replacement.
> - **HID (Sodium/Metal Halide):** Efficacy ≈ **80–120 lm/W**, used for street and high-bay lighting.
> - **LED (Light Emitting Diode):** Efficacy ≈ **100–200 lm/W** (premium LEDs), life ≈ **50,000–100,000 hrs**, mercury-free, instant on.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Symbol Legend | Unit |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Luminous Intensity | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\Omega}$ | Φ = flux, Ω = solid angle | **cd** |
| 2 | Solid Angle | $\Omega = \dfrac{A}{r^{2}}$ | A = spherical area, r = radius | **sr (steradian)** |
| 3 | Inverse Square Law | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | I = intensity, d = distance | **lux** |
| 4 | Cosine Law (Lambert) | $E_{h} = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | θ = angle of incidence | **lux** |
| 5 | Luminance | $L = \dfrac{I}{A_{p}}$ where $A_{p} = A\cos\theta$ | Aₚ = projected area | **cd/m²** |
| 6 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | P = electrical power | **lm/W** |
| 7 | Number of Lamps (Lumen Method) | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi_{L} \times UF \times MF}$ | A = area, Φ_L = flux per lamp | dimensionless |
| 8 | Total Electrical Load | $P_{total} = N \times P_{lamp}$ | P_lamp = wattage per lamp | **W (watts)** |
| 9 | Annual Energy Consumption | $E_{annual} = P_{total} \times \text{Hours} \times \text{Days}$ | operational hours | **kWh/year** |
| 10 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Incremental Cost}}{\text{Annual Saving}}$ | cost and savings in ₹ | **years** |
| 11 | Energy Cost Saving | $\text{Saving} = (P_{old} - P_{new}) \times t \times \text{Rate}$ | t = time in hrs, Rate in ₹/kWh | **₹/year** |
| 12 | Reduction in Demand | $\Delta D = (P_{old} - P_{new})$ | connected load reduction | **W or kW** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Callout (Frequently asked as 2-mark question):**
> - **Utilization Factor (UF)** = $\dfrac{\text{Flux reaching the working plane}}{\text{Total flux emitted by the lamps}}$ (typically **0.4 to 0.8**).
> - **Maintenance Factor (MF)** = $\dfrac{\text{Illuminance after a period of use}}{\text{Illuminance at the start}}$ (typical values: **0.7 for clean, 0.5 for industrial, 0.6 for commercial**).
> - **Depreciation Factor (DF)** = $\dfrac{1}{MF}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Audits:** Replacing **400 W HPSV (High Pressure Sodium Vapour)** street lights with **100 W LED** yields **75% reduction** in load — directly lowers **Maximum Demand (kVA) charges** and **ToU (Time-of-Use) tariffs**.
- **Green Building Certification:** IGBC and GRIHA ratings award credits for maintaining **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** below ASHRAE 90.1 / ECBC limits.
- **Smart Cities Mission:** All Indian municipal lighting under the **Smart Cities program** mandates LED conversion.
- **PAT Scheme Compliance:** Designated Consumers (DCs) under BEE's **PAT cycle** can earn **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** quantified in **tonnes of oil equivalent (toe)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law

**Statement:** The illuminance at a point on a surface illuminated by a point source is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source.

**Logical Derivation:**

Consider a point source *S* emitting luminous flux Φ uniformly in all directions. Surround it with an imaginary sphere of radius *r*. By definition, the **luminous intensity** *I* is the flux per unit solid angle:

$$
I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi} \quad \text{(since total solid angle of a sphere = 4π sr)}
$$

The flux intercepted by a small patch of area *dA* on the sphere subtends a solid angle:

$$
d\Omega = \frac{dA}{r^{2}}
$$

The flux falling on *dA* is therefore:

$$
d\Phi = I \cdot d\Omega = I \cdot \frac{dA}{r^{2}}
$$

By definition, **illuminance** *E* is flux per unit area:

$$
E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{I}{r^{2}}
$$

Thus, we obtain the **Inverse Square Law**:

$$
\boxed{E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why a lamp mounted at 4 m height produces *1/16th* the illuminance compared to the same lamp at 1 m — distance is the most critical factor in lighting design.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cosine Law (Lambert's Law)

**Statement:** The illuminance on a surface varies as the cosine of the angle of incidence between the light ray and the surface normal.

**Derivation:**

For a point source *S* at distance *d* from a horizontal work-plane, let the ray strike the surface at an angle *θ* to the normal.

The flux *dΦ* falling on an inclined area *dA* is the same as the flux falling on a **projected horizontal area** $dA_{h} = dA \cos\theta$.

Applying the inverse square law to the projected area:

$$
E_{h} = \frac{I}{d^{2}}
$$

But because the actual surface *dA* is larger than the projected area, the illuminance on *dA* is:

$$
\boxed{E_{h} = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambert's Cosine Law** is the foundation of indoor lighting design — lamps tilted at 45° to the work-plane deliver only **70.7% (cos 45°)** of the flux they would deliver if mounted directly above.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Number of Lamps Formula (Lumen Method)

**Step 1:** Let the work-plane area be *A* m² and required illuminance be *E* lux. The total flux needed on the work-plane:

$$
\Phi_{required} = E \times A
$$

**Step 2:** Only a fraction (UF) of the flux emitted by the lamps reaches the work-plane:

$$
\Phi_{emitted} = \frac{\Phi_{required}}{UF} = \frac{E \times A}{UF}
$$

**Step 3:** As the lamp ages, dust accumulates and lumen depreciation occurs. So fresh lamp flux must be higher by factor 1/MF:

$$
\Phi_{fresh} = \frac{E \times A}{UF \times MF}
$$

**Step 4:** If each lamp produces flux $\Phi_{L}$, the number of lamps required is:

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_{L} \times UF \times MF}}
$$

**Step 5:** Total electrical load:

$$
P_{total} = N \times P_{lamp}
$$

### 3.4 Worked-Out Analytical Example (KTU ESE Style)

**Problem:** A factory floor of dimensions **40 m × 20 m** requires an illuminance of **300 lux**. Lamps of **400 W metal halide** (each producing **36,000 lm**) with **UF = 0.6** and **MF = 0.75** are proposed. Calculate:
*(a)* Number of lamps required.
*(b)* Total connected load.
*(c)* Annual energy consumption at **12 hours/day × 300 days/year**.
*(d)* Annual electricity cost at **₹8.50/kWh**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Area $A = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ m}^2$

$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_{L} \times UF \times MF} = \frac{300 \times 800}{36000 \times 0.6 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
N = \frac{240000}{16200} = 14.81
$$

Always round **up** to ensure adequate illuminance: $N = 15$ lamps.

**Part (b):** Total connected load

$$
P_{total} = 15 \times 400 = 6000 \text{ W} = 6.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Part (c):** Annual energy

$$
E_{annual} = 6.0 \times 12 \times 300 = 21600 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Part (d):** Annual cost

$$
\text{Cost} = 21600 \times 8.50 = \text{₹ 1,83,600/year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative (LED Retrofit) Check for Audit Comparison:**
> If 150 W LED (flux ≈ 22,500 lm) replaces 400 W metal halide:
> - New $N = \frac{240000}{22500 \times 0.6 \times 0.75} = 23.7 \approx 24$ lamps.
> - New $P_{total} = 24 \times 150 = 3600 \text{ W} = 3.6 \text{ kW}$.
> - **Savings = 6.0 – 3.6 = 2.4 kW** (40% reduction in demand) and **8640 kWh/year** energy saved → **₹73,440/year** financial saving.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Conservation Hierarchy in Lighting

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of lighting energy conservation measures, organized in the priority order recommended by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Identify Visual Task] --> B[Step 2 Set Illuminance Target as per IS 3646]
    B --> C[Step 3 Choose High Efficacy Lamps]
    C --> C1[LED 100 to 200 lm per W]
    C --> C2[T5 Fluorescent 80 lm per W]
    C --> C3[Metal Halide HID 90 lm per W]
    C1 --> D[Step 4 Optimize Luminaire Design]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Use Specular Reflectors]
    D --> D2[Use Electronic Ballasts]
    D --> D3[Avoid Glare via Proper Diffusers]
    D1 --> E[Step 5 Deploy Lighting Controls]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Occupancy Sensors]
    E --> E2[Daylight Harvesting via Photocells]
    E --> E3[Dimming Drivers 0 to 10 V]
    E --> E4[Time Schedulers and BMS]
    E1 --> F[Step 6 Calculate Energy Savings]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Step 7 Verify via Light Meter Lux Audit]
    G --> H[Final Outcome Reduced kWh and Reduced kVA]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Photometric Quantity Relationships

The following block diagram maps the **functional flow** between radiant power, photometric quantities, and the human eye response curve:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Radiant Power W] -->|Multiplied by V lambda| Phi[Luminous Flux lm]
    Phi -->|Divided by Solid Angle| I[Luminous Intensity cd]
    I -->|Divided by Distance Squared| E[Illuminance lux]
    Phi -->|Divided by Area| E
    I -->|Divided by Projected Area| L[Luminance cd per m2]
    L -->|Perceived by Human Eye| Eye[Visual Sensation]
    Phi -->|Divided by Electrical Power P| Eff[Luminous Efficacy lm per W]
    P --> Eff
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Audit Lighting Retrofit Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Audit] --> I1[Inventory Existing Lamps and Wattage]
    I1 --> I2[Measure Actual Lux using Light Meter]
    I2 --> Q1{Are Lux Levels Excessively Higher than IS 3646 Target?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| M1[Delamping Strategy Reduce Lamp Count]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is Efficacy Less than 50 lm per W?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| M2[Replace with LED or T5 Retrofit]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Are Lights ON in Unoccupied Areas?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| M3[Install Occupancy Sensors]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Are Daylit Areas Over Lit?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| M4[Install Daylight Sensors with Dimming]
    Q4 -->|No| R[No Major Retrofit Recommended]
    M1 --> P[Compute Payback Period]
    M2 --> P
    M3 --> P
    M4 --> P
    R --> End[Submit Audit Report]
    P --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms with units: *(i)* Luminous flux, *(ii)* Luminous intensity, *(iii)* Illuminance.

**Model Answer:**
*(i)* **Luminous flux (Φ)** is the total quantity of light energy emitted per second from a source, weighted according to the CIE photopic luminosity curve V(λ). **Unit: lumen (lm).**
*(ii)* **Luminous intensity (I)** is the luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction. **Unit: candela (cd) = lm/sr.**
*(iii)* **Illuminance (E)** is the luminous flux incident on a unit area of a surface. **Unit: lux (lx) = lm/m².**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is **luminous efficacy**? Compare the efficacy of an incandescent lamp, a fluorescent tube, and an LED lamp.

**Model Answer:**
**Luminous efficacy (η)** is the ratio of luminous flux emitted by a lamp to the electrical power consumed by it. **Unit: lm/W.**

Comparison:

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) |
|------------|-----------------|
| Incandescent (GLS) | 10 – 15 |
| Fluorescent Tube (T5) | 80 – 100 |
| LED | 100 – 200 |

Higher efficacy means less electrical energy is consumed for the same light output, making LEDs the most efficient option.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by *forgetting to round the number of lamps UP* in the Lumen Method. If $N = 14.81$, write $N = 15$ — never 14.
> - Do **not confuse Utilization Factor (UF)** with **Maintenance Factor (MF)**. UF is a *design-time geometry factor*; MF is a *time-dependent depreciation factor*.
> - Always quote **units in every step**. KTU examiners *specifically* deduct marks for unitless final answers.
> - In retrofit problems, **state all assumptions** (hours/day, days/year, tariff) clearly at the start to avoid step-marks loss.
> - Inverse Square Law applies **only to point sources**; for fluorescent tubes, use the *polar curve method* instead.

---

**Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, adapted]

A conference hall of size **25 m × 15 m × 6 m (height)** is to be illuminated to a level of **250 lux** on the working plane. Using **5 ft × 40 W LED panels** (each emitting **4000 lm**), determine:
*(a) [7 Marks]* the number of LED panels required, given **UF = 0.65** and **MF = 0.8**.
*(b) [7 Marks]* the total connected load and the annual energy cost if the hall operates **10 hours/day for 300 days/year** at a tariff of **₹9.00/kWh**. Comment on the role of the **maintenance factor** in long-term energy consumption.

**Model Solution (A):**

**Part (a):**
Area of the hall:

$$
A = 25 \times 15 = 375 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Total flux required on the work-plane:

$$
\Phi_{required} = E \times A = 250 \times 375 = 93750 \text{ lm}
$$

Applying UF and MF:

$$
N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_{L} \times UF \times MF} = \frac{93750}{4000 \times 0.65 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
N = \frac{93750}{2080} = 45.07
$$

Rounding up: $\boxed{N = 46 \text{ LED panels}}$.

**Part (b):**
Total connected load:

$$
P_{total} = 46 \times 40 = 1840 \text{ W} = 1.84 \text{ kW}
$$

Annual energy consumption:

$$
E_{annual} = 1.84 \times 10 \times 300 = 5520 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

Annual electricity cost:

$$
\text{Cost} = 5520 \times 9.00 = \text{₹ 49,680/year}
$$

**Role of Maintenance Factor (Commentary):**
The Maintenance Factor accounts for the *depreciation of luminous output* over time due to lamp ageing, dust accumulation on luminaires, and surface dirt on walls/ceiling. A lower MF (e.g., 0.6 in dusty industrial environments) implies that the initial illuminance must be *higher* to ensure the **maintained illuminance** never falls below the IS 3646 recommended value. This directly impacts the *number of lamps* and hence the *connected load*. Periodic luminaire cleaning and group relamping can improve the effective MF, thereby **reducing long-term energy costs** by allowing fewer lamps to be installed.

---

**Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, adapted]

A street lighting system consists of **80 High Pressure Sodium Vapour (HPSV) lamps of 250 W each**, operating **12 hours/day for 365 days/year**. The system is to be retrofitted with **120 W LED street lights** (replacement flux ≥ original flux per pole). Calculate:
*(a) [7 Marks]* the annual energy savings in kWh and the reduction in maximum demand in kW.
*(b) [7 Marks]* the simple payback period if the LED replacement cost is **₹9,000 per luminaire** and the electricity tariff is **₹7.50/kWh**.

**Model Solution (B):**

**Part (a):**
Old system power:

$$
P_{old} = 80 \times 250 = 20000 \text{ W} = 20 \text{ kW}
$$

New system power:

$$
P_{new} = 80 \times 120 = 9600 \text{ W} = 9.6 \text{ kW}
$$

Reduction in maximum demand:

$$
\Delta D = 20 - 9.6 = 10.4 \text{ kW}
$$

Annual energy savings:

$$
\Delta E = \Delta D \times t = 10.4 \times 12 \times 365 = 45552 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Part (b):**
Annual monetary savings:

$$
\text{Saving} = 45552 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ 3,41,640/year}
$$

Total retrofit investment:

$$
\text{Cost} = 80 \times 9000 = \text{₹ 7,20,000}
$$

Simple Payback Period:

$$
SPP = \frac{720000}{341640} = 2.11 \text{ years}
$$

This is well within the typical **3-year payback** benchmark used by BEE for approving PAT scheme investments, making the project highly viable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Luminous Efficacy (η) = Φ / P** is the single most important parameter for energy-efficient lamp selection; **LED > HID > Fluorescent > CFL > Incandescent**.
- The **Lumen Method Formula** $N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi_{L} \times UF \times MF}$ is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU ESE — memorize it verbatim.
- **Inverse Square Law** $E = I/d^2$ applies strictly to point sources.
- **Lambert's Cosine Law** $E_h = I \cos\theta / d^2$ accounts for the angle of incidence.
- **UF** is geometry; **MF** is time; **DF = 1/MF**.
- **Always round UP** the number of lamps to the next integer.
- **Major lighting conservation measures (in order of priority):**
  1. **Delamping** where over-illumination exists.
  2. **Replacing magnetic ballasts with electronic ballasts** (saves 8–12% ballast losses).
  3. **LED retrofit** (saves 50–75% energy).
  4. **Occupancy / Daylight sensors** (saves 20–40% additional).
  5. **Task lighting** instead of uniform ambient lighting.
- **Reference Standards (cite in answers for full marks):** IS 3646 (Illuminance levels), ECBC 2017 (Building code), NBC 2016, BEE PAT Scheme.
- **Unit conversions:** **1 klm = 1000 lm**, **1 fc (foot-candle) ≈ 10.76 lux**, **1 sphere = 4π sr ≈ 12.57 sr**.
- **Annual cost formula:** Cost = kW × hours × days × tariff (₹/kWh).
- **Maximum Demand Charge reduction** is often more valuable than energy savings for industrial consumers.
- **Payback Period < 3 years** is the typical BEE benchmark for lighting retrofits.
- **Cosine of incidence** (Lambert) reduces effective illuminance on tilted surfaces — important in street lighting and stadium design.
- **Efficacy ceiling:** The maximum theoretical efficacy of white light is **683 lm/W**; commercial LEDs are approaching **200 lm/W** (≈ 30% of theoretical max).
- **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** in W/m² is the metric used by **ECBC** to benchmark buildings (e.g., offices: ≤ **9.0 W/m²** for ECBC+ compliance).
- **CRI (Colour Rendering Index):** LEDs typically have CRI > 80; preferred for indoor work; HPSV has low CRI (~25) — not suitable for colour-critical tasks.
- **Stroboscopic effect** in fluorescent lamps is eliminated by **electronic ballasts (HF, > 20 kHz)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motors: Development of Energy Efficient Motors and the Present Status
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motors: Development of Energy Efficient Motors and the Present Status

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Energy Efficient Motor (EEM)** is a specially designed alternating current (AC) induction motor — predominantly the three-phase squirrel cage type — engineered with superior materials, tighter manufacturing tolerances, and optimized electromagnetic design to deliver the same mechanical shaft output as a standard (conventional) motor while consuming substantially less electrical energy across its operational life.

As per **IS 12615:2018 (Indian Standard)** and the harmonized **IEC 60034-30-1:2014** international standard, EEMs are classified under the **IE3 (Premium Efficiency)** class as the mandatory minimum efficiency benchmark, replacing the older IE1 and IE2 classifications in most regulatory jurisdictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEET742 / Module 2):**
> Induction motors consume approximately **65% to 70%** of all electrical energy used in Indian industry. A mere **1% improvement** in the efficiency of the global installed motor base can save over **50 TWh of electricity annually**, equivalent to several thousand MW of avoided generation capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tagging — Course Outcomes Met:**
> - **CO2:** Evaluate energy conservation opportunities in electrical systems.
> - **CO3:** Conduct detailed energy audits on motor-driven systems.
> - **CO4:** Recommend energy-efficient technologies with techno-economic justification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two delivery trucks of identical cargo capacity:

- **Truck A (Standard Motor):** Has an old, less-tuned engine. It does the job, but burns more diesel, requires more frequent servicing, and has higher exhaust (losses in the form of heat, noise, and vibration).
- **Truck B (Energy Efficient Motor):** Has a modern, finely calibrated engine, low-friction tyres, aerodynamic body, and a precision gearbox. It carries the same load but uses **significantly less fuel** (electricity) and runs cooler and quieter.

The cargo (shaft output) is identical, but Truck B's running cost (electricity bill) is dramatically lower. Over thousands of hours of operation, the fuel savings (energy saved) far outweigh the slightly higher upfront purchase price. This is the entire philosophy behind energy efficient motors.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Energy & Performance Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Conversion constant:** **1 hp = 0.746 kW** and **1 kW = 1.341 hp**
> - **Standard reference efficiency benchmarks:** **IE1, IE2, IE3, IE4, IE5** (per IEC 60034-30-1)
> - **Indian efficiency standard:** **IS 12615:2018** (aligned with IE3)
> - **Typical industrial motor load factor:** **0.6 to 0.8** of rated capacity
> - **Operating hours benchmark (Indian industry):** **4000 to 6000 hours/year**
> - **Average industrial electricity tariff (India):** **₹6 to ₹9 per kWh**

## 1.4 Why Energy Efficient Motors Matter in the Indian Context

| Parameter | Standard Motor (IE1) | Energy Efficient Motor (IE3) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Average efficiency (15 kW, 4-pole) | ~ 87.0% | ~ 91.5% |
| Total losses | 13.0% of input | 8.5% of input |
| Loss reduction | Baseline | **~ 35% lower losses** |
| Stator winding temperature rise | ~ 80 °C | ~ 60 to 70 °C |
| Insulation class | Class B / F | Class F (used at B rating) |
| Bearing life | ~ 40,000 hrs | ~ 60,000 hrs (greaseable) |
| Typical payback | — | 6 to 24 months |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of Losses in an Induction Motor

To understand *how* EEMs save energy, one must first classify where conventional motors *waste* it. The total losses in a three-phase induction motor are decomposed into **five distinct categories**:

### 2.1.1 Stator Copper Losses (P_sc or P_cu1)
- **Definition:** Resistive (I²R) heating in the stator (stationary) winding.
- **Why it occurs:** Current flowing through the stator conductors generates heat proportional to the square of the current and the DC resistance of the winding.
- **Magnitude:** Largest single loss category, typically **30% to 40%** of total motor losses at full load.
- **EEM Reduction Strategy:**
  - Use **larger cross-section copper conductors** (more copper, lower resistance).
  - **End-windings are tightened** to reduce effective conductor length.
  - Higher slot fill factor (typically 75% to 80%) via precision winding machines.

### 2.1.2 Rotor Copper Losses (P_rc or P_cu2)
- **Definition:** Resistive heating in the rotor cage (squirrel cage) bars and end rings.
- **Magnitude:** Typically **15% to 25%** of total losses.
- **EEM Reduction Strategy:**
  - Use **die-cast copper rotor cages** (instead of aluminum) in premium EEMs.
  - Tightly controlled rotor bar and end-ring geometry to minimize resistance.
  - Precision slot skewing to reduce harmonic circulating currents.

### 2.1.3 Core (Iron) Losses (P_core)
- **Definition:** Hysteresis and eddy current losses in the stator and rotor laminations.
- **Sub-components:**
  - **Hysteresis loss (P_h):** Proportional to frequency and the area of the B-H loop of the lamination steel.
  - **Eddy current loss (P_e):** Proportional to frequency squared, flux density squared, and the square of lamination thickness.
- **EEM Reduction Strategy:**
  - Use **thin-gauge, low-loss, cold-rolled grain-oriented (CRGO) silicon steel laminations** (e.g., 0.35 mm thickness, or even 0.20 mm in premium designs).
  - Lower operating flux density through longer core stacks.
  - Superior inter-laminar insulation coatings (C-5 or inorganic coating).

### 2.1.4 Friction and Windage Losses (P_fw)
- **Definition:** Mechanical losses due to bearing friction, shaft seals, and aerodynamic drag of the rotor plus internal fan.
- **EEM Reduction Strategy:**
  - **Low-friction, precision-grade bearings** (sealed-for-life or regreaseable types).
  - **Aerodynamically optimized, low-noise, bi-directional cooling fans** with reduced fan diameter and unique blade profiles.
  - Smaller fan hub and lower fan tip speed.

### 2.1.5 Stray Load Losses (P_stray)
- **Definition:** Miscellaneous losses due to leakage flux harmonics, circulating currents, non-uniform current distribution, and minor geometric imperfections.
- **Magnitude:** Often the most underestimated loss; typically assigned a standard value of **0.5% to 1.5%** of input power per IEEE 112 and IEC 60034-2-1.
- **EEM Reduction Strategy:**
  - Precision machining of stator and rotor slots.
  - Optimal slot combinations (e.g., using skewed rotor bars).
  - Symmetrical winding distribution and tighter air-gap tolerance.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 9 equations — they appear in nearly every numerical question on this topic.**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + \sum P_{losses}} = 1 - \frac{\sum P_{losses}}{P_{in}}
$$

$$
\sum P_{losses} = P_{sc} + P_{rc} + P_{core} + P_{fw} + P_{stray}
$$

$$
\text{Annual Energy Savings (kWh)} = P_{rated} \times \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{std}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{EEM}} \right) \times \text{Operating Hours}
$$

$$
\text{Annual Monetary Savings} = \text{Energy Saved} \times \text{Energy Cost per kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Simple Payback Period (years)} = \frac{\text{Extra Cost of EEM}}{\text{Annual Monetary Savings}}
$$

$$
\text{Life Cycle Cost} = C_{capital} + C_{energy} + C_{maintenance} + C_{disposal}
$$

$$
C_{energy} = \frac{P_{rated} \times \text{Hours} \times \text{Years} \times \text{Cost/kWh}}{\eta}
$$

$$
\text{Return on Investment (\%)} = \frac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Extra Cost}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Stator Copper Loss at Fractional Load } x = P_{sc,fl} \times x^{2}
$$

$$
\text{Core Loss is approximately constant} = P_{core,fl} \times 1.0
$$

## 2.3 Consolidated Formula Reference Table

| Formula Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency | % (dimensionless ratio $\times 100$) | Valid for $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| $P_{out}$ | Shaft (mechanical) output power | kW or W | Always less than $P_{in}$ |
| $P_{in}$ | Electrical input power | kW or W | $P_{in} = P_{out} + \sum P_{losses}$ |
| $P_{sc}$ | Stator copper loss | W | $\propto I^{2} R_{1}$ |
| $P_{rc}$ | Rotor copper loss | W | $\propto s^{2} \cdot$ rotor resistance |
| $P_{core}$ | Iron (hysteresis + eddy) loss | W | Nearly independent of load |
| $P_{fw}$ | Friction and windage loss | W | Nearly independent of load |
| $P_{stray}$ | Stray load loss | W | $\propto I^{2}$ (varies with load squared) |
| $x$ | Fractional load ratio | dimensionless | $0 \le x \le 1.5$ (typical operating range) |
| LCC | Life cycle cost | ₹ or \$ | Includes NPV-discounted future costs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These formulas drive the design of **Motor Selection Software** (e.g., ABB's MotoSize, Siemens' SIMOTICS Selector) used by energy auditors worldwide to recommend motor replacements during ISO 50001 audits. Mastery of this formula sheet is mandatory for **CMEA (Certified Measurement & Verification Professional)** and **BEE Certified Energy Auditor** examinations in India.

## 2.4 Development Timeline of Energy Efficient Motors (Global & India)

| Era | Global Development | Indian Development |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1970s | Energy crisis prompts first efficiency standards (USA — EPAct 1992, EU — CEMEP 1998) | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) initiates standardization work |
| 1980s | High-efficiency motors commercialized in Europe and Japan | Limited availability; mostly imported EEMs |
| 1990s | NEMA Premium (USA) and EFF1/EFF2 (EU) classes introduced | IS 12615 first published; initial voluntary labeling |
| 2000s | IEC 60034-30 harmonizes global classes (IE1/IE2/IE3) | BEE Star Labeling Program for motors launched (2009 onwards) |
| 2010s | IE4 (Super Premium) motors commercialized; permanent magnet motors emerge | IS 12615 revised (2011, 2018); PAT scheme includes motors as Designated Consumers |
| 2020s | IE5 (Ultra Premium) standard published (IEC 60034-30-2:2016) | Government mandates IE3 minimum for new sales under EC Act |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why Energy Efficient Motors Save Money

### Step 1 — Start with the basic energy balance of any motor

Every motor obeys the First Law of Thermodynamics — electrical energy in equals mechanical energy out plus all losses:

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}
$$

This is the **non-negotiable starting identity** for all subsequent derivations.

### Step 2 — Substitute into the efficiency definition

By definition, efficiency is the ratio of useful output to total input:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}
$$

Substituting $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$ into the denominator:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}
$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for input power

To find the *electrical input* required to deliver a given *mechanical output*:

$$
P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta}
$$

This is the single most important equation for energy auditors — it tells you that **a less efficient motor demands more electrical input** for the same shaft output.

### Step 4 — Compute annual energy consumption

Assuming the motor operates for $H$ hours per year at a constant load:

$$
E_{annual} = P_{in} \times H = \frac{P_{out} \times H}{\eta}
$$

The unit is **kWh/year**.

### Step 5 — Compute annual energy difference between two motors

For the same shaft output $P_{out}$ and same operating hours $H$:

$$
\Delta E = \frac{P_{out} \times H}{\eta_{std}} - \frac{P_{out} \times H}{\eta_{EEM}}
$$

Factoring out the common term $P_{out} \times H$:

$$
\Delta E = P_{out} \times H \times \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{std}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{EEM}} \right)
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to monetary annual savings

Multiply the saved energy by the unit cost of electricity $C_e$ (in ₹ per kWh):

$$
S_{annual} = \Delta E \times C_e = P_{out} \times H \times C_e \times \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{std}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{EEM}} \right)
$$

### Step 7 — Compute simple payback period

If the EEM costs $C_{extra}$ more than the standard motor, the payback period $T_{pb}$ is:

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{C_{extra}}{S_{annual}} = \frac{C_{extra}}{P_{out} \times H \times C_e \times \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{std}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{EEM}} \right)}
$$

### Step 8 — Life Cycle Cost (LCC) Justification

Over a motor's life of $N$ years, the LCC of the EEM minus the LCC of the standard motor is:

$$
\Delta LCC = (C_{EEM} - C_{std}) + \sum_{y=1}^{N} \frac{S_{annual}}{(1+d)^{y}} - (M_{EEM} - M_{std})
$$

where $d$ is the discount rate and $M$ is the maintenance cost. **If $\Delta LCC < 0$, the EEM is the economically superior choice** even though its initial cost is higher.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Because electricity cost grows with time (and discount rate is partially offset by tariff inflation), LCC analysis almost always favours the EEM for motors operating more than ~2000 hours/year. This is the **technical justification for EEM retrofits** in continuous-process industries.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Loss Reduction Percentages in a Typical 15 kW EEM

| Loss Category | Standard Motor (W) | Reduction Technique | EEM Value (W) | % Reduction |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Stator copper $P_{sc}$ | 850 | More copper, tighter end-windings | 595 | 30% |
| Rotor copper $P_{rc}$ | 620 | Die-cast copper cage | 496 | 20% |
| Core (iron) $P_{core}$ | 480 | CRGO lamination, longer core | 360 | 25% |
| Friction & windage $P_{fw}$ | 320 | Optimized fan, low-friction bearings | 176 | 45% |
| Stray load $P_{stray}$ | 280 | Precision slot geometry, tighter air gap | 196 | 30% |
| **Total Losses** | **2550** | — | **1823** | **28.5%** |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Payback Period

> **Problem:** A 15 kW, 3-phase standard induction motor has an efficiency of **87.0%**. An equivalent IE3 energy efficient motor has an efficiency of **91.5%**. The motor runs **4500 hours/year** and electricity costs **₹7.50/kWh**. The EEM costs **₹12,000 more** than the standard motor. Calculate (a) the annual energy savings in kWh, (b) the annual monetary savings, and (c) the simple payback period.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Annual Energy Savings:**

Apply the derived formula from Section 3.1, Step 5:

$$
\Delta E = P_{out} \times H \times \left( \frac{1}{\eta_{std}} - \frac{1}{\eta_{EEM}} \right)
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\Delta E = 15 \times 4500 \times \left( \frac{1}{0.870} - \frac{1}{0.915} \right)
$$

Compute the inverse efficiencies:

$$
\frac{1}{0.870} = 1.14943 \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{0.915} = 1.09290
$$

Subtract the two inverse efficiencies:

$$
1.14943 - 1.09290 = 0.05653
$$

Multiply by the constant front factor:

$$
\Delta E = 15 \times 4500 \times 0.05653
$$

$$
\Delta E = 67500 \times 0.05653
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta E \approx 3815.8 \text{ kWh/year}}
$$

**Step (b) — Annual Monetary Savings:**

$$
S_{annual} = \Delta E \times C_e = 3815.8 \times 7.50
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{annual} = \text{₹} 28{,}618.5 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Step (c) — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{C_{extra}}{S_{annual}} = \frac{12000}{28618.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{pb} \approx 0.42 \text{ years} \approx 5.0 \text{ months}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 5-month payback on a 15 kW motor replacement is exceptional. Energy auditors use a rule of thumb: **Payback < 1 year = "must-do" retrofit**, **1 to 2 years = "should-do"**, **> 3 years = "review carefully"**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Efficiency Improvement with Loss Reduction

> **Problem:** A standard motor has total losses of **2550 W** at full load and delivers **15 kW** shaft output. The EEM version reduces stator copper by 30%, rotor copper by 20%, core loss by 25%, friction/windage by 45%, and stray loss by 30%. Calculate the new efficiency and the percentage improvement.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute original efficiency (baseline check):**

$$
P_{in,std} = 15000 + 2550 = 17550 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta_{std} = \frac{15000}{17550} \times 100 = 85.47\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each reduced loss individually:**

$$
P_{sc,EEM} = 850 \times (1 - 0.30) = 595 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{rc,EEM} = 620 \times (1 - 0.20) = 496 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{core,EEM} = 480 \times (1 - 0.25) = 360 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{fw,EEM} = 320 \times (1 - 0.45) = 176 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{stray,EEM} = 280 \times (1 - 0.30) = 196 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the new total losses:**

$$
\sum P_{losses,EEM} = 595 + 496 + 360 + 176 + 196 = 1823 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute new input power and efficiency:**

$$
P_{in,EEM} = 15000 + 1823 = 16823 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta_{EEM} = \frac{15000}{16823} \times 100 = 89.16\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the percentage improvement in efficiency (absolute point gain):**

$$
\Delta \eta = 89.16\% - 85.47\% = 3.69 \text{ percentage points}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the percentage reduction in losses (relative metric):**

$$
\% \text{ Loss Reduction} = \frac{2550 - 1823}{2550} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Loss Reduction} = \frac{727}{2550} \times 100 = 28.51\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{EEM} = 89.16\% \quad ; \quad \text{Loss Reduction} = 28.51\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Note that a **~28.5% reduction in total losses** translates to only a **~3.7 percentage point gain in efficiency**. This non-linear relationship is a favourite KTU examiner question. Always state: "Efficiency improvements appear small in percentage terms but yield large absolute energy savings over a motor's operating life."

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Life Cycle Cost Comparison

> **Problem:** A 22 kW standard motor costs **₹65,000** with 88% efficiency. The EEM costs **₹85,000** with 92% efficiency. Both motors run **5000 hours/year** for **12 years**. Electricity cost = **₹8/kWh**, discount rate = **8%**, annual maintenance = **2% of capital cost** for standard and **1%** for EEM. Compare LCC.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute annual energy cost for each motor (undiscounted):**

$$
E_{std} = \frac{22 \times 5000}{0.88} = 125000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
E_{EEM} = \frac{22 \times 5000}{0.92} = 119565.2 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

Annual energy cost:

$$
C_{energy,std} = 125000 \times 8 = \text{₹} 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{energy,EEM} = 119565.2 \times 8 = \text{₹} 9{,}56{,}522
$$

**Step 2 — Compute annual maintenance cost:**

$$
M_{std} = 0.02 \times 65000 = \text{₹} 1300/\text{year}
$$

$$
M_{EEM} = 0.01 \times 85000 = \text{₹} 850/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the discounted LCC over 12 years using annuity factor:**

The present value annuity factor (PVAF) for $d = 8\%$ and $n = 12$ years is:

$$
PVAF = \frac{1 - (1 + 0.08)^{-12}}{0.08} = \frac{1 - 0.3971}{0.08} = 7.536
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total LCC for the standard motor:**

$$
LCC_{std} = 65000 + (1000000 + 1300) \times 7.536
$$

$$
LCC_{std} = 65000 + 1001300 \times 7.536 = 65000 + 7545798
$$

$$
LCC_{std} \approx \text{₹} 76.11 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total LCC for the EEM:**

$$
LCC_{EEM} = 85000 + (956522 + 850) \times 7.536
$$

$$
LCC_{EEM} = 85000 + 957372 \times 7.536 = 85000 + 7214786
$$

$$
LCC_{EEM} \approx \text{₹} 73.00 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare and conclude:**

$$
\Delta LCC = 76.11 - 73.00 = \text{₹} 3.11 \text{ lakhs saved by EEM}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{The EEM saves approximately ₹ 3.11 lakhs over 12 years despite its higher initial cost.}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Categorization of Induction Motor Losses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Motor Input Power P_in"]:::root
    B["Useful Shaft Output P_out"]:::good
    C["Total Electrical and Mechanical Losses"]:::loss
    A --> B
    A --> C
    C --> D1["Stator Copper Loss P_sc"]:::loss
    C --> D2["Rotor Copper Loss P_rc"]:::loss
    C --> D3["Core Iron Loss P_core"]:::loss
    C --> D4["Friction and Windage Loss P_fw"]:::loss
    C --> D5["Stray Load Loss P_stray"]:::loss
    D1 --> E1["More copper, lower R1, tighter end-windings"]:::fix
    D2 --> E2["Die-cast copper cage, precision bar geometry"]:::fix
    D3 --> E3["CRGO lamination, thinner gauge, lower flux density"]:::fix
    D4 --> E4["Optimized fan, low friction bearings"]:::fix
    D5 --> E5["Precision slot geometry, tight air gap, skewing"]:::fix
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef good fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef loss fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fix fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — IE Efficiency Class Hierarchy (IEC 60034-30-1)

```mermaid

    A2["IE2 High Efficiency"]:::lvl2

    A5["IE5 Ultra Premium Efficiency"]:::lvl5

    A2 -->|"Upgrades"| A3
    A3 -->|"Upgrades"| A4
    A4 -->|"Upgrades"| A5
    A3 -->|"IS 12615 2018 Mandatory Minimum in India"| A6["BEE Star Rated Motors 1 to 5 Star"]:::bee
    classDef lvl1 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Efficient Motor Selection & Audit Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Identify Motor in Field Audit"]:::step
    S2["Step 2: Read Nameplate Rating and Age"]:::step
    S3["Step 3: Measure Loading with Clamp Meter or Power Analyzer"]:::step
    S4["Step 4: Compute Operating Hours per Year"]:::step
    S5["Step 5: Apply Loss Reduction Matrix and Estimate EEM Efficiency"]:::step
    S6["Step 6: Compute Annual Energy Savings in kWh and Rupees"]:::step
    S7["Step 7: Compute Payback Period in Months"]:::step
    S8["Step 8: Decision - Replace or Retain"]:::decision
    S9["Payback under 1 Year - Immediate Replacement"]:::good
    S10["Payback 1 to 3 Years - Schedule Replacement"]:::warn
    S11["Payback above 3 Years - Re-evaluate with VSD Retrofit"]:::bad
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    S8 -->|Yes| S9
    S8 -->|Wait| S10
    S8 -->|No| S11
    classDef step fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#a16207,stroke:#a16207,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef good fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warn fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bad fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Comparison between Standard Motor and EEM

```mermaid

        SM1["Winding: Aluminum or small copper"]:::stdpart
        SM2["Lamination: Standard hot-rolled silicon steel"]:::stdpart

        EM3["Bearings: Low-friction sealed or shielded"]:::eempart
```
