# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define an energy efficient motor. List any **four design features** that distinguish it from a standard induction motor.

**Model Answer:**

An **Energy Efficient Motor (EEM)** is an AC induction motor designed and manufactured to deliver a specified mechanical output with reduced electrical losses — meeting the **IE3 (Premium Efficiency)** or higher class as defined by **IEC 60034-30-1** and the Indian standard **IS 12615:2018**.

**Four distinguishing design features:**

1. **Larger copper cross-section** in the stator winding (lower I²R loss).
2. **Low-loss CRGO silicon steel laminations** of thinner gauge (reduced core loss).
3. **Aerodynamically optimized cooling fan** with reduced diameter and refined blade profile (lower windage loss).
4. **Precision bearings and tight rotor air-gap tolerance** (lower friction and stray load losses).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for standard reference, 1 for design features.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** What is the significance of the **BEE Star Labeling Program** for motors in India? State the efficiency class currently mandated under IS 12615.

**Model Answer:**

The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) Star Labeling Program** for motors — operational since 2009 — is a voluntary (and later mandatory) rating scheme that classifies motors from **1 star (lowest)** to **5 stars (highest)** based on their measured full-load efficiency. It empowers industrial consumers to make informed purchase decisions and provides a transparent benchmark for energy savings.

Under the latest revision of **IS 12615:2018** and the Energy Conservation Act amendments, the **IE3 (Premium Efficiency)** class is the **mandatory minimum** efficiency level for three-phase squirrel cage induction motors sold in India in the range 0.12 kW to 1000 kW.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for BEE program explanation, 1.5 for IS 12615 mandate.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five categories of losses** in a three-phase induction motor. For each loss, state the **specific design improvement** used in an energy efficient motor to reduce it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **22 kW**, 4-pole, three-phase standard induction motor has an efficiency of **88.5%** at full load. An equivalent IE3 energy efficient motor has an efficiency of **92.0%**. The motor operates **5000 hours/year**, the electricity tariff is **₹8.50/kWh**, and the EEM costs **₹18,000 more** than the standard motor. Calculate:
- (i) The annual energy savings in kWh
- (ii) The annual monetary savings
- (iii) The simple payback period in months
**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Loss Categories and EEM Improvements:**

| Loss Type | Cause | EEM Design Improvement | Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stator copper | I²R heating in stator winding | Larger copper, higher slot fill, tighter end-windings | 1.5 |
| Rotor copper | I²R heating in cage bars | Die-cast copper cage, precision bar geometry | 1.5 |
| Core (iron) | Hysteresis and eddy currents | CRGO thin-gauge lamination, lower flux density | 1.5 |
| Friction & windage | Bearing drag, fan air drag | Low-friction bearings, optimized fan blade design | 1.0 |
| Stray load | Harmonics, leakage flux | Precision slot geometry, tight air-gap, skewing | 1.5 |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Annual Energy Savings:**

$$
\Delta E = 22 \times 5000 \times \left( \frac{1}{0.885} - \frac{1}{0.920} \right)
$$

Compute inverse efficiencies:

$$
\frac{1}{0.885} = 1.12994 \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{0.920} = 1.08696
$$

Difference:

$$
1.12994 - 1.08696 = 0.04298
$$

Final computation:

$$
\Delta E = 22 \times 5000 \times 0.04298 = 110000 \times 0.04298
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta E \approx 4728 \text{ kWh/year}}
$$

**(ii) Annual Monetary Savings:**

$$
S_{annual} = 4728 \times 8.50 = \text{₹} 40{,}188/\text{year}
$$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period:**

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{18000}{40188} = 0.448 \text{ years} = 0.448 \times 12
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{pb} \approx 5.37 \text{ months}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Students often write $\eta_{EEM} - \eta_{std}$ (4.5% absolute) and report it as "energy saved" — this is **WRONG**. Always compute $(1/\eta_{std} - 1/\eta_{EEM})$, never $\eta_{EEM} - \eta_{std}$.
> - **Common Error 2:** Forgetting to convert payback from years to months. The examiner wants months. (**-1 mark**)
> - **Common Error 3:** Reporting efficiency as a decimal (0.885) in monetary calculations. Always multiply by kWh cost after computing energy in kWh. (**-1 mark**)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **IE efficiency classification of induction motors** as per **IEC 60034-30-1**. Describe the present status of energy efficient motors in India, including BEE standards, IS 12615, and the PAT scheme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The losses in a **standard 11 kW, 3-phase motor** at full load are: stator copper = **620 W**, rotor copper = **450 W**, core = **360 W**, friction and windage = **240 W**, stray = **210 W**. The energy efficient version reduces these by **25%, 20%, 30%, 50%, and 30%** respectively. Calculate:
- (i) The full-load efficiency of the standard motor
- (ii) The full-load efficiency of the EEM
- (iii) The percentage reduction in total losses
**(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — IE Classification and Indian Status:**

The **IE classification** under IEC 60034-30-1 defines five efficiency levels for single-speed, three-phase, squirrel-cage induction motors:

1. **IE1 — Standard Efficiency:** The baseline level, equivalent to older conventional motors.
2. **IE2 — High Efficiency:** A step above IE1 with typical loss reduction of 10–20%.
3. **IE3 — Premium Efficiency:** The current global benchmark; mandatory minimum in many regions.
4. **IE4 — Super Premium Efficiency:** Achieved through copper rotors and advanced lamination; ~15% lower losses than IE3.
5. **IE5 — Ultra Premium Efficiency:** Defined for permanent magnet and synchronous reluctance motors.

**Present Status in India:**

- **IS 12615:2018** mandates **IE3** as the minimum for motors from 0.12 kW to 1000 kW.
- The **BEE Star Label Program** rates motors 1 to 5 stars based on efficiency.
- The **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme** treats motor-driven industries as **Designated Consumers** with energy reduction targets.
- The **Standards & Labelling (S&L) notification** under the Energy Conservation Act makes IE3 mandatory.
- The **National Motor Replacement Programme (NMRP)** and **UJALA**-style financing schemes encourage retrofits.

*(7 marks — 3 for IE classes, 4 for Indian regulatory landscape.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Standard motor efficiency:**

Total losses:

$$
\sum P_{losses,std} = 620 + 450 + 360 + 240 + 210 = 1880 \text{ W}
$$

Input power:

$$
P_{in,std} = 11000 + 1880 = 12880 \text{ W}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{std} = \frac{11000}{12880} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{std} = 85.40\%}
$$

**(ii) EEM losses (with reductions applied):**

$$
P_{sc,EEM} = 620 \times (1 - 0.25) = 465 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{rc,EEM} = 450 \times (1 - 0.20) = 360 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{core,EEM} = 360 \times (1 - 0.30) = 252 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{fw,EEM} = 240 \times (1 - 0.50) = 120 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{stray,EEM} = 210 \times (1 - 0.30) = 147 \text{ W}
$$

Total EEM losses:

$$
\sum P_{losses,EEM} = 465 + 360 + 252 + 120 + 147 = 1344 \text{ W}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{EEM} = \frac{11000}{11000 + 1344} \times 100 = \frac{11000}{12344} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{EEM} = 89.11\%}
$$

**(iii) Percentage loss reduction:**

$$
\% \text{ Loss Reduction} = \frac{1880 - 1344}{1880} \times 100 = \frac{536}{1880} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Loss Reduction} = 28.51\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Error 4:** For Part (b)(i), students often mistakenly divide output by total losses instead of input. Always remember: **Efficiency = Output / (Output + Losses)**. (**-2 marks**)
> - **Common Error 5:** Reporting the **percentage points** gain (89.11 − 85.40 = 3.71) as the "percentage improvement". The question asks for the **% loss reduction**, which is a relative measure on losses, not on efficiency points. (**-1 mark**)
> - **Common Error 6:** In Part (a), omitting the **PAT scheme** or the **NMRP** (National Motor Replacement Programme). KTU specifically tests awareness of Indian regulatory bodies. Always mention BEE + IS 12615 + PAT + EC Act.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Review Before Every Test**

- [ ] **Energy Efficient Motor (EEM):** Same shaft output, lower electrical input, IE3 minimum per IS 12615.
- [ ] **Five motor losses:** Stator Cu, Rotor Cu, Core, Friction & Windage, Stray Load.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $\eta = P_{out} / (P_{out} + \sum P_{losses})$.
- [ ] **Annual savings formula:** $\Delta E = P \times H \times (1/\eta_{std} - 1/\eta_{EEM})$.
- [ ] **Payback formula:** $T_{pb} = C_{extra} / S_{annual}$.
- [ ] **Always use inverse efficiency difference**, never direct efficiency difference.
- [ ] **IE classes:** IE1 (Std) → IE2 (High) → IE3 (Premium) → IE4 (Super) → IE5 (Ultra).
- [ ] **India's minimum standard:** **IE3** (since 2018).
- [ ] **BEE Star Label:** 1 star = least efficient, 5 stars = most efficient.
- [ ] **PAT scheme** = Perform, Achieve, Trade — covers Designated Consumers.
- [ ] **EEM design changes:** More copper, CRGO lamination, optimized fan, low-friction bearings, tight air gap.
- [ ] **Rule of thumb:** Payback < 1 year = immediate retrofit; 1–3 years = scheduled; > 3 years = re-evaluate with VSD option.
- [ ] **Typical efficiency gains:** IE1 to IE3 ≈ **3 to 4 percentage points**; ~**25 to 30%** loss reduction.
- [ ] **Conversion:** 1 hp = **0.746 kW**; 1 kWh = 1000 Wh = 3.6 MJ.
- [ ] **LCC analysis** is the gold standard for justifying EEM retrofits over the motor's 12–20 year life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Takeaway for KTU Viva / Interview:**
> "An energy efficient motor recovers its incremental cost within 1 to 2 years through reduced I²R and core losses, achieves IE3 efficiency per IS 12615, and is now a **mandatory minimum** standard for three-phase motors sold in India — making it the most cost-effective energy conservation measure available to any industrial energy auditor."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Improving Energy Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Improving Energy Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of useful energy output (or service delivered) to the energy input consumed by a system, expressed as a dimensionless fraction or percentage. A **Technique for Improving Energy Efficiency** is any engineered, operational, or managerial intervention that increases this ratio without compromising the functional output, quality, or productivity of the process.

Mathematically, the fundamental energy efficiency relation used throughout the KTU syllabus is:

$$\eta = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100\,\%$$

Where:
- $\eta$ is the **Energy Efficiency (%)**
- $E_{useful}$ is the useful energy output (kWh, MJ, or kJ)
- $E_{input}$ is the total energy input (kWh, MJ, or kJ)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Context:** In Module 2, the B.Tech examiner expects students to know *both* the engineering techniques (hardware) and the management techniques (operational/behavioral). The Energy Conservation Act 2001 (India) and the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** star labelling program are part of the regulatory framework implicitly linked to this topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with a leaking pipe** that fills a glass at the other end.

- The water reaching the glass is your **useful output**.
- The water leaking out along the pipe is **wasted energy**.
- The total water pumped is your **energy input**.

**Techniques for Improving Energy Efficiency are exactly like fixing those leaks:**
1. **Replace the rusty old pipe with a smooth new one** (Energy Efficient Motors, VFDs).
2. **Install a pressure regulator at the tap** (Power Factor Correction).
3. **Use a smaller pipe when full flow isn't needed** (Load Matching, Right-Sizing).
4. **Fix dripping joints** (Insulation, maintenance).

The result: **Same glass filled, but with far less water pumped** — that is, the same useful work, but with dramatically lower energy input.

## 1.3 Universal Performance Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

The KTU board examiner rewards correct use of standardized metrics. Always express the following in **bold** in your answer scripts:

- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = Energy Used (kWh) ÷ Production Output (units)  
- **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** = A normalized quantity (kWh/ton, MJ/kg, kWh/m³) that benchmarks performance
- **kVAh** and **kVARh** = Apparent and reactive energy consumed (used in PF analysis)
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** = Financial justification metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** If a question asks "list techniques," always group them under three headers — **(i) Electrical, (ii) Mechanical/Thermal, (iii) Process & Behavioral.** This structured format consistently fetches full marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Efficiency Techniques

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus broadly classifies techniques into the following seven engineering families. The bullet logic below mirrors the operation of each system.

### 2.1.1 Energy Efficient Motors (EEM)
- **Why they exist:** Standard induction motors are designed for **standard**, general-purpose duty cycles, and operate at fixed efficiency points (often 85–90%) regardless of load.
- **How they work:** EEMs use **low-loss silicon steel laminations**, **thinner air gaps**, **larger conductor cross-sections** of high-purity copper, and **lower bearing friction** to push efficiencies to **IE3 (Premium, ~91–93%)** and **IE4 (Super-Premium, ~94–96%)** classes as per **IEC 60034-30-1**.
- **Real-world application:** HVAC fans, pumps, compressors, conveyor drives in cement, paper, textile, and chemical plants.

### 2.1.2 Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) / Variable Speed Drives (VSD)
- **Why they exist:** Centrifugal loads (pumps, fans, blowers) have a **cubic relationship** between speed and power. Reducing speed by 20% cuts power by ~49%.
- **How they work:** A VFD rectifies AC → DC (rectifier), filters DC (DC bus), then inverts DC → variable-frequency AC (inverter, using **IGBT switches with PWM**). This controls motor speed precisely.
- **Real-world application:** AHU fans in commercial buildings, boiler FD/ID fans, chilled water pumps, hydraulic elevators.

### 2.1.3 Power Factor Correction (PFC)
- **Why it exists:** Industrial inductive loads (motors, transformers, induction heaters) draw **reactive power (kVAR)** that does no useful work but causes **kVA demand charges**, **line losses ($I^2R$)**, and **voltage drop**.
- **How it works:** **Shunt capacitors** supply leading kVAR locally, offsetting the lagging kVAR of the load. This raises PF from ~0.7 to a target of **0.95–0.98 lagging**.

### 2.1.4 Lighting System Optimization
- Replace **incandescent (15 lm/W)** and **halogen** lamps with **LEDs (100–150 lm/W)**.
- Use **electronic ballasts** instead of electromagnetic ballasts in fluorescent fixtures.
- Install **occupancy sensors**, **daylight harvesting controls**, and **task lighting**.

### 2.1.5 Energy Efficient Transformers
- Use **CRGO (Cold Rolled Grain Oriented)** silicon steel core with **step-lap joints**.
- **Amorphous metal core transformers (AMDT)** reduce no-load losses by **~70–80%** compared to conventional units.

### 2.1.6 Compressed Air System Optimization
- Fix leaks (a typical factory loses **20–30%** of compressed air to leaks).
- Reduce operating pressure by even 1 bar saves **~7%** energy.
- Use **VFD on compressor**, **heat recovery** from exhaust, and **proper sizing of receiver tanks**.

### 2.1.7 Process & Thermal Techniques
- **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)**, **Cogeneration/Combined Heat and Power (CHP)**, **Pinch Analysis** for heat exchanger network synthesis, **insulation upgrades**, and **boiler/steam system optimization**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strictly avoid the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside table rows** (used for absolute value) — instead, use **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** as shown below. KTU evaluators prefer LaTeX-rendered math in your script's table.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | When to Use in Exam |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Motor, boiler, transformer calculations |
| 2 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{input}}{Production}$ | kWh/unit, MJ/kg | Comparing process lines |
| 3 | Power Factor (3-phase) | $PF = \cos\varphi = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L}$ | dimensionless | Always in AC system problems |
| 4 | Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L\,\sin\varphi$ | kVAR | PFC sizing |
| 5 | Required PFC Capacitor | $Q_c = P\,(\tan\varphi_1 - \tan\varphi_2)$ | kVAR | Capacitor bank sizing |
| 6 | Affinity Law — Flow | $Q_2/Q_1 = N_2/N_1$ | ratio | Pump/fan with VFD |
| 7 | Affinity Law — Pressure | $H_2/H_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$ | ratio | Pump/fan with VFD |
| 8 | Affinity Law — Power | $P_2/P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3$ | ratio | Pump/fan with VFD |
| 9 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Savings}}$ | years | Financial justification |
| 10 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Project ranking |
| 11 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | ₹/currency | Multi-year LCC analysis |
| 12 | Line Loss in Conductor | $P_{loss} = 3\,I^2\,R$ | W | Cables, busbars |
| 13 | Line Loss Reduction | $\Delta P_{\%} = 1 - (PF_1/PF_2)^2$ | % | PFC benefit calculation |
| 14 | Transformer Loading | $X = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{S_{rated}}$ | p.u. | Efficiency at partial load |
| 15 | Optimum Loading (transformer) | $X_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}}$ | p.u. | Maximum efficiency point |

## 2.3 Engineering Field Relevance

| Industry Sector | Most-Used Technique | Energy Saving Range |
|-----------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Cement | WHR + VFD on kilns | 15–25% |
| Steel | Coke oven gas recovery + CHP | 10–30% |
| Textile | EEM + steam trap maintenance | 12–20% |
| Commercial Buildings | LED + VFD-HVAC + BMS | 25–40% |
| Pulp & Paper | Pinch Analysis + black liquor recovery | 20–35% |
| Data Centres | Free cooling + UPS efficiency + hot/cold aisle | 30–50% |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU board examiners award **methodology marks** for writing each transition step with a justification. Never skip algebraic manipulations; show the substitution explicitly. Every equation below is followed by a plain-English logic row.

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Factor Correction Capacitor Sizing

**Given:** A 3-phase industrial load draws $P$ kW at an initial lagging power factor $\cos\varphi_1$. The target power factor is $\cos\varphi_2$. Find the capacitor kVAR required.

**Step 1 — Start with the power triangle geometry.** In a single-phase representation, the apparent power triangle has:
- Real power component = $P$ (kW)
- Reactive power component = $P\tan\varphi$ (kVAR)
- Apparent power = $P/\cos\varphi$ (kVA)

**Step 2 — Write initial reactive power demand.**

$$Q_1 = P \tan\varphi_1$$

**Step 3 — Logic:** The real power $P$ does not change after correction (the load is unchanged). Only the reactive power $Q$ is reduced by the leading kVAR supplied by the capacitor.

$$Q_2 = P \tan\varphi_2$$

**Step 4 — Capacitor must supply the difference.**

$$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = P \tan\varphi_1 - P \tan\varphi_2$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed expression.**

$$\boxed{Q_c = P\,(\tan\varphi_1 - \tan\varphi_2)}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example.** A factory draws $P = 500$ kW at $\cos\varphi_1 = 0.75$ lagging. Target PF $\cos\varphi_2 = 0.95$ lagging.

- $\varphi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41°$, so $\tan\varphi_1 = 0.8819$
- $\varphi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$, so $\tan\varphi_2 = 0.3287$

$$Q_c = 500 \times (0.8819 - 0.3287) = 500 \times 0.5532 = 276.6\ \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 7 — Standard commercial rating.** The nearest standard capacitor bank rating is **300 kVAR** (3 × 100 kVAR unit), which would slightly over-correct — acceptable as a practical design choice.

## 3.2 Derivation: VFD Energy Savings Using the Cubic Affinity Law

**Given:** A centrifugal pump runs continuously at full speed, delivering 100% flow. The actual process requires only 80% flow. Compare energy consumption at full speed vs. 80% speed using a VFD.

**Step 1 — State the affinity laws.**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply to flow ratio of 0.8.**

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 0.8$$

**Step 3 — Compute power ratio.**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (0.8)^3 = 0.512$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

$$\boxed{\text{Power reduction} = 1 - 0.512 = 0.488 = 48.8\%}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.** Pump input power at full speed = 50 kW. Annual operating hours = 6000 h. Energy cost = ₹8/kWh.

- Full-speed annual energy = $50 \times 6000 = 300{,}000$ kWh
- Cost at full speed = $300{,}000 \times 8 = ₹24{,}00{,}000$

- 80% speed annual energy = $50 \times 0.512 \times 6000 = 153{,}600$ kWh
- Cost at 80% speed = $153{,}600 \times 8 = ₹12{,}28{,}800$

- **Annual savings** = $24{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}28{,}800 = ₹11{,}71{,}200$

## 3.3 Derivation: Line Loss Reduction Due to PFC

**Step 1 — Original line loss in a feeder carrying current $I_1$ at PF$_1$.**

$$P_{loss,1} = 3\,I_1^2\,R$$

**Step 2 — After correction, real power $P$ is unchanged, so $I\cos\varphi = $ constant. Therefore:**

$$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \frac{\cos\varphi_1}{\cos\varphi_2} = \frac{PF_1}{PF_2}$$

**Step 3 — New line loss.**

$$P_{loss,2} = 3\,I_2^2\,R = 3\,R\,I_1^2\,\left(\frac{PF_1}{PF_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Fractional reduction in loss.**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_{loss,1}} = 1 - \left(\frac{PF_1}{PF_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example.** PF improves from 0.75 to 0.95.

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_{loss,1}} = 1 - \left(\frac{0.75}{0.95}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.6225 = 0.3775 = 37.75\%$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:** A modest PF improvement from 0.75 to 0.95 reduces distribution losses by nearly **38%** — a major incentive for utilities and HT consumers.

## 3.4 Derivation: Simple Payback Period for an EEM Replacement

**Step 1 — Identify cost components.** Cost of EEM = $C_{EEM}$. Cost of standard motor = $C_{std}$. Annual energy saved = $E_{saved}$ kWh. Energy tariff = $R$ ₹/kWh.

**Step 2 — Incremental investment.**

$$I_0 = C_{EEM} - C_{std}$$

**Step 3 — Annual monetary savings.**

$$S = E_{saved} \times R$$

**Step 4 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$\boxed{SPP = \frac{I_0}{S} = \frac{C_{EEM} - C_{std}}{E_{saved} \times R}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.**
- Standard motor: 75 kW, efficiency 88%, cost ₹60,000
- EEM (IE3): efficiency 93%, cost ₹85,000
- Operating hours: 5000 h/year
- Tariff: ₹7/kWh

- Input power standard: $75/0.88 = 85.23$ kW  
- Input power EEM: $75/0.93 = 80.65$ kW  
- Energy saved per year: $(85.23 - 80.65) \times 5000 = 22{,}900$ kWh  
- Monetary savings: $22{,}900 \times 7 = ₹1{,}60{,}300$/year  
- Incremental investment: $85{,}000 - 60{,}000 = ₹25{,}000$  
- $SPP = 25{,}000 / 1{,}60{,}300 = 0.156$ years $\approx 1.87$ months  

**Step 6 — Engineering conclusion:** Such a fast payback is typical for EEM retrofits. KTU valuation gives full credit when the student explicitly shows **all four steps** above.

## 3.5 Derivation: Optimum Loading of a Transformer

**Step 1 — Define losses.** Iron loss (core loss) $P_i$ is constant. Copper loss at full load is $P_{cu,FL}$, and at fractional load $x$ is $x^2 P_{cu,FL}$.

**Step 2 — Total loss.**

$$P_{total} = P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL}$$

**Step 3 — Output power at load fraction $x$.**

$$P_{out} = x \cdot S_{rated} \cdot \cos\varphi$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency expression.**

$$\eta = \frac{x S_{rated}\cos\varphi}{x S_{rated}\cos\varphi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}$$

**Step 5 — Maximize $\eta$ by differentiating with respect to $x$ and setting to zero.**

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = 0 \implies \boxed{x_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}}}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.** Maximum efficiency occurs when **variable copper loss equals the constant iron loss** — a classic result that is a KTU 2024 favourite.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define energy efficiency. List any four techniques used for improving energy efficiency in electrical systems.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *Energy efficiency* is the ratio of useful energy output to total energy input of a system, expressed as a percentage. It indicates how effectively energy is converted into the desired output service.
>
> $$\eta = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100\,\%$$
>
> Four techniques for improving energy efficiency in electrical systems are:
> 1. Use of **Energy Efficient Motors (EEMs)** of IE3 or IE4 class.
> 2. Installation of **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for variable-torque loads.
> 3. **Power Factor Correction (PFC)** using shunt capacitor banks.
> 4. Replacement of conventional lighting with **LED luminaires** and electronic ballasts.
>
> *(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for correctly listing four techniques)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. State the BEE star labelling programme. What is the role of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency in India?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **BEE Star Labelling Programme** is a voluntary energy-efficiency rating scheme administered by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**, Government of India, established under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**. Appliances are rated on a scale of **1 to 5 stars**, where more stars indicate higher energy efficiency and lower annual operating cost. Currently covered appliances include refrigerators, air conditioners, fans, LED lamps, and industrial motors.
>
> The BEE's role is to:
> 1. Develop minimum energy performance standards (MEPS) for notified equipment.
> 2. Promote energy-efficient practices through labelling and certification.
> 3. Coordinate with state designated agencies (SDAs) to enforce the EC Act.
>
> *(1 mark for BEE definition + 1 mark for star rating explanation + 1 mark for roles)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper Style]**
> **Q(A). (a)** Explain the construction and operating principle of an **Energy Efficient Motor (EEM)**. Compare its efficiency with a standard induction motor. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A factory operates a **75 kW, 3-phase, 415 V** induction motor at **0.78 power factor lagging** for **5500 hours/year**. The utility tariff is **₹7.50/kWh**, and a **demand charge of ₹350/kVA/month** applies. **(i)** Calculate the annual energy consumption. **(ii)** Calculate the annual kVA demand charge. **(iii)** Find the capacitor rating (kVAR) needed to improve PF to **0.95 lagging** and the new annual savings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Principle of EEM (7 marks):**

- **Core:** Built with **low-loss, high-grade CRNO silicon steel laminations** of thickness 0.35 mm with high silicon content (~3%). Laminations are coated to reduce eddy-current loss. The air gap between rotor and stator is **precisely minimized** to reduce magnetizing current.
- **Stator winding:** Uses **larger cross-section, high-purity copper conductors** to reduce $I^2R$ (copper) losses. Winding is tightly packed with high-quality insulation (Class F or H).
- **Rotor:** Aluminium die-cast or copper-bar cage rotor with optimised bar and end-ring geometry. **Rotor surface is smooth** to reduce harmonic and stray load losses.
- **Bearings & Cooling:** Low-friction bearings and optimised fan design reduce windage and friction losses.
- **Efficiency comparison:**

| Parameter | Standard Motor (IE1/IE2) | EEM (IE3/IE4) |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------|
| Full-load efficiency | 88–91% | 93–96% |
| No-load losses | High | 30–40% lower |
| Insulation class | B/F | F/H |
| Operating cost | High | Low |
| Payback | — | 6–24 months typical |

- **Standards reference:** Efficiency classes defined by **IEC 60034-30-1**: IE1 (Standard), IE2 (High), IE3 (Premium), IE4 (Super-Premium). In India, **IS 12615:2018** harmonises with IEC.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Annual Energy Consumption:**

$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta \times PF} \quad \text{but here, P = 75 kW is input rating.}$$

$$E = 75 \times 5500 = 4{,}12{,}500\ \text{kWh/year}$$

**(ii) Annual kVA Demand Charge:**

$$S = \frac{P}{PF} = \frac{75}{0.78} = 96.15\ \text{kVA}$$

$$\text{Annual demand charge} = 96.15 \times 350 \times 12 = ₹4{,}03{,}846$$

**(iii) Capacitor kVAR & Savings:**

- $\varphi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.78) = 38.74°$, $\tan\varphi_1 = 0.8023$
- $\varphi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$, $\tan\varphi_2 = 0.3287$

$$Q_c = 75 \times (0.8023 - 0.3287) = 75 \times 0.4736 = 35.52\ \text{kVAR}$$

- Round to standard **40 kVAR** capacitor bank.
- New apparent power: $S_{new} = 75/0.95 = 78.95$ kVA.
- New annual demand charge: $78.95 \times 350 \times 12 = ₹3{,}31{,}590$
- **Annual demand charge saving:** $4{,}03{,}846 - 3{,}31{,}590 = ₹72{,}256$ 
- Energy bill remains the same (real kW unchanged), so this is the dominant saving plus PF incentive from utility (typically ₹0.05–0.10/unit on full energy bill, not considered here unless specified).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> Students commonly confuse $P_{out}$ with $P_{in}$ in the energy formula. If the question states the **motor rating as 75 kW input**, use it directly in $E = P \times t$. If it is **output (shaft) power**, divide by efficiency first. **Always re-read the problem statement.**

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**
> **Q(B). (a)** With the help of the **affinity laws**, explain how energy is saved in a centrifugal pump driven by a VFD. A 30 kW pump runs at 2900 rpm and delivers 100% flow. If speed is reduced to 75% using a VFD, calculate the percentage reduction in power consumption. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the principle of **Pinch Analysis** for heat exchanger network synthesis. What is the significance of the **minimum approach temperature ($\Delta T_{min}$)**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Affinity Laws & VFD Pump Savings (7 marks):**

**Affinity laws state:**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

- **Flow** is proportional to speed.
- **Head (pressure)** is proportional to the square of speed.
- **Power** is proportional to the **cube** of speed.

For $N_2/N_1 = 0.75$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (0.75)^3 = 0.4219$$

**Power reduction percentage:**

$$\Delta P\,\% = (1 - 0.4219) \times 100 = 57.81\%$$

**Numerical savings (if motor is 30 kW, 5500 h/year, ₹7/kWh):**

- Original annual energy: $30 \times 5500 = 1{,}65{,}000$ kWh → cost = ₹11,55,000
- New annual energy: $30 \times 0.4219 \times 5500 = 69{,}614$ kWh → cost = ₹4,87,300
- **Annual savings:** ₹6,67,700 (≈ 57.8%)

**Logic statement:** Centrifugal pumps and fans are *variable torque* loads; even a modest speed reduction yields dramatic cubic-power savings, making VFDs the most cost-effective efficiency technique in process industries.

**(b) Pinch Analysis (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Pinch Analysis is a systematic **thermodynamic methodology** for optimizing heat exchanger networks (HENs) by matching hot and cold process streams to minimize external heating and cooling utilities.

**Procedure:**
1. Extract all hot streams (cooling required) and cold streams (heating required) from the process flowsheet.
2. Define stream data: supply/target temperatures, heat capacity flow rates (CP, kW/K).
3. Construct **Composite Curves** of the hot and cold streams on a T-H diagram.
4. The point of closest approach between the two curves is the **PINCH** — characterized by the **minimum approach temperature $\Delta T_{min}$**.

**Significance of $\Delta T_{min}$:**
- Smaller $\Delta T_{min}$ → larger heat recovery area (higher capital cost of HEN) but lower utility cost.
- Larger $\Delta T_{min}$ → smaller heat exchanger area, but more external heating/cooling needed.
- The **optimum $\Delta T_{min}$** is chosen by **total cost minimization** (capital + operating).

**Key insights:**
- **Above the pinch:** only hot utility (e.g., steam) is needed; do not cool with cold utility.
- **Below the pinch:** only cold utility (e.g., cooling water) is needed; do not heat with hot utility.
- **Across the pinch:** no heat transfer — violates the "+3% rule" of Linnhoff and triggers utility penalty.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> Many students incorrectly state "Pin = hot + cold" or omit the units of CP (kW/°C). A 14-mark answer must include: (1) composite curve sketch, (2) clear mention of the *Plus-Three-Percent Rule*, and (3) the trade-off between $\Delta T_{min}$, area, and utilities. Sketches earn 2–3 marks by themselves.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Energy Efficiency vs. Energy Conservation:** *Efficiency* = doing the same task with less energy; *Conservation* = doing less of the task.
- **IE3 / IE4 classes** are mandatory references for EEM in India (IS 12615:2018).
- **Affinity laws** apply only to centrifugal (variable-torque) loads — NOT to constant-torque loads (compressors, conveyors).
- **PFC** never reduces the energy (kWh) consumed by the load; it only reduces kVA demand and line $I^2R$ losses.
- **VFDs** add 2–5% own losses; for *constant-load* applications, they may not be economical.
- **Pinch Analysis** tools: **SUPERTARGET**, **HEATINT**, **ASPEN PINCH**.
- **BEE** operates under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**; PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme applies to **Designated Consumers (DCs)** in energy-intensive industries.
- **Mandatory energy audit** (under EC Act 2001) applies to DCs consuming ≥ **1 MTOE** annually.
- **Compressed air leaks:** 1 mm hole at 7 bar leaks ~6 SCFM → ₹30,000/year wasted per hole.
- **LED advantage:** 1 LED 9 W ≈ 1 incandescent 60 W with 50× longer life.
- **Optimum transformer loading** ($X_{opt} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,FL}}$) is a frequently asked 7-mark derivation in ESE.
- **SPP (years)** = Incremental Investment / Annual Savings — keep currency and units consistent.
- **Three-phase capacitor bank** = $Q_c = P(\tan\varphi_1 - \tan\varphi_2)$ — a 7-mark KTU favourite.
- Always mention **rebound effect** in management-type answers (efficiency gains may be offset by increased use).
- For **HT consumers**, PF below **0.90 lagging** attracts a penalty; maintaining >0.95 is preferred.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "EMV-LPS-CW":**  
> **E**EM → **M**echanical losses cut  
> **V**FD → cubic savings via **L**oad matching  
> **P**FC → reactive **kVAR** cancellation  
> **L**ighting → LEDs + **S**ensors  
> **C**ompressed air → leak **W**itch-hunt  
> Use this in your answer introduction to instantly show the examiner you have a structured mind — guaranteed to earn goodwill marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Necessity for Load Matching and Selection of Motors for Constant and Variable Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Necessity for Load Matching and Selection of Motors for Constant and Variable Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Load Matching?

**Load Matching** is the engineering process of selecting an electric motor whose output characteristics (power, torque, speed, efficiency curve) align precisely with the actual mechanical demand profile of the driven equipment. In the context of **Energy Management and Auditing (EMA)**, it is one of the most profitable energy conservation measures (ECMs), because electric motors consume roughly **60–65%** of all industrial electricity globally, and a poorly matched motor can waste 20–40% of the input energy as heat, noise, and stray losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Load matching* is the procedure of right-sizing a motor such that its rated output corresponds to the highest sustained load demand of the driven machine, while its efficiency and power factor remain near-optimum across the expected operating range.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Right Shoe Size" 👟

Imagine buying a shoe that is two sizes too large. You will walk inefficiently, your feet will slip, blisters form, and the shoe eventually tears. A motor that is oversized behaves identically — it operates at low load fraction, has poor power factor, low efficiency, and pays a "capacity charge" for power it never fully uses. **Load matching = choosing the right shoe size so every step delivers maximum output with minimum energy waste.**

## 1.2 Why is Load Matching *Necessary*?

In real industrial plants, the audit team frequently finds motors running at **30–50% loading**, while the motor nameplate says 100%. This mismatch occurs because:

1. **Original over-design margin:** Engineers historically added a 30–50% safety margin on top of calculated load.
2. **Process modifications:** Driven equipment was later downsized, but the motor was never replaced.
3. **De-rated operation:** Valves, dampers, or throttles were used to artificially limit output.
4. **Duty cycle changes:** A continuous-rated motor may now run intermittently at low load.

The consequences of this mismatch are severe:

| Parameter | Undersized Motor | Oversized Motor (Most Common) |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | Falls at overload | **Falls sharply below 50% load** |
| Power Factor | Slightly poor | **Drops to 0.5–0.6 (penalty in tariff)** |
| Temperature rise | Damages insulation | Cool, but wasteful |
| Service life | Shortens (burnout) | Long, but energy-costly |
| Starting current | High (heating) | Lower, but sustained losses |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For an IE2 standard motor, efficiency at 25% load may be only **78–82%**, whereas at 75–100% load it reaches **89–92%**. A 5-percentage-point efficiency loss on a 30 kW motor running 6000 hrs/year costs approximately **₹1.4 lakh/year** at ₹8/kWh.

## 1.3 What are "Constant Loads" and "Variable Loads"?

These two load categories dictate *entirely different* motor selection strategies.

- **Constant Load (Continuous Duty, S1):** The torque demand remains nearly constant over time. Examples — conveyors running at fixed speed, centrifugal pumps delivering fixed head, fans with fixed dampers, compressors at steady state. *Selection criterion:* Match rated power to demand; prioritize high-efficiency standard motor.

- **Variable Load (Fluctuating / Intermittent Duty, S2–S6):** The torque demand changes with time. Examples — elevators, cranes, presses, machine tool spindles, mixers, reciprocating compressors, conveyors with variable feed. *Selection criterion:* Match average AND peak demand; use speed-control or multi-speed motors to avoid throttling losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always identify the **load curve** (kW vs time) before selecting the motor. The selection algorithm differs for S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, and S6 duty cycles defined in IS 325 / IEC 60034-1.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Standards

| Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **IS 325** | Three-phase induction motors — performance and dimensions |
| **IEC 60034-30-1** | IE1 / IE2 / IE3 / IE4 efficiency classes |
| **IS 12615** | Energy-efficient induction motors (India) |
| **NEMA MG-1** | Motor construction and performance (USA) |

> [!NOTE]
> Always state the applicable standard in the KTU exam answer — it demonstrates awareness and earns easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Load Matching — A Structured Logic Flow

The necessity for load matching is rooted in **three physical realities** of induction motors:

### Logic Step 1 — Efficiency is a function of load fraction

Every induction motor has a fixed component of losses (iron/core loss, friction & windage, stray load loss) that is **independent of load**, and a variable component (stator copper loss $I^2R_s$, rotor copper loss $I^2R_r$) that scales with the **square of the load current**.

- At low load, fixed losses dominate → overall efficiency plummets.
- The "sweet spot" of an induction motor is generally **75–100% of rated load**.

### Logic Step 2 — Power factor worsens at low load

The magnetizing current required to establish the air-gap flux is **constant** (set by voltage and frequency). At low load, the real (in-phase) component of current is small, but the magnetizing (quadrature) component remains the same. Therefore, the displacement power factor $\cos\phi$ collapses at light loads.

### Logic Step 3 — Motors operate in the largest block of plant load

Since motors are responsible for the dominant share of industrial kWh, even a **2% efficiency improvement** at the motor level yields savings that exceed the savings from lighting + HVAC combined.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Replacing a 75 kW standard motor (η = 90%) with a 75 kW IE4 motor (η = 94.5%) running 5000 h/yr saves:
> $$ \Delta E = 75 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.90} - \frac{1}{0.945}\right) \times 5000 \approx 20{,}634 \text{ kWh/yr} $$

## 2.2 Selection Procedure for Constant Loads (S1 Duty)

For a constant load, the procedure is straightforward:

1. **Determine the load power demand** $P_{load}$ in kW from mechanical calculations.
2. **Apply a service factor** of typically **1.10–1.15** (10–15% margin) to account for transient overloads.
3. **Select a standard motor** from the catalogue whose rated output $P_{rated} \geq 1.15 \times P_{load}$.
4. **Verify efficiency** of the candidate motor at the *actual operating point* (not the rated point).
5. **Verify power factor** at the operating point ≥ 0.85 (to avoid utility penalty).
6. **Check ambient temperature**, altitude derating (above 1000 m, derate by 1% per 100 m).
7. **Select enclosure type** (TEFC, ODP, TENV) based on environment.

**Recommended motor types for constant loads:**
- **Squirrel-cage induction motor (SCIM)** — most common, robust, cheap.
- **Premium-efficiency IE3/IE4 motor** — when running hours > 4000/yr.
- **Synchronous motor (PFC duty)** — when power factor correction is also desired.

## 2.3 Selection Procedure for Variable Loads (S2–S6 Duty)

Variable loads require a more nuanced approach because a single-speed motor running a variable load wastes energy in throttling, bypass, or on-off cycling. The selection strategy is dictated by the **load profile and duty cycle**.

### Strategy A — Multi-Speed Motors
- **Pole-changing motors** (e.g., 4/6 pole, 1500/1000 rpm) using two separate windings or Dahlander connection.
- Ideal for **discrete two-step** loads (e.g., fans in winter/summer mode).

### Strategy B — Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)
- **PWM-based VFD** controlling a standard squirrel-cage motor.
- Most flexible: continuous speed range 0–200% of base speed.
- **Affinity Laws apply** — for a fan/pump:
$$ \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 $$
- Even a 20% speed reduction yields ~**49% power reduction** in cubic-law loads.

### Strategy C — Slip-Ring Induction Motor (SRIM) with Rotor Resistance Control
- Used for **high starting torque, low starting current** applications.
- External rotor resistance allows speed variation of 10–20%.
- Higher maintenance due to brushes and slip rings.

### Strategy D — DC Motor or Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor
- Smooth, wide speed range.
- Used in machine tools, EVs, elevators.
- Higher capital cost; justified for high running hours and precise control.

### Strategy E — Soft Starter / Star-Delta
- Does **not** save energy in steady state, but reduces starting current surge and mechanical stress.
- Useful when on-off cycling is frequent.

**Recommended motor types summary:**

| Load Pattern | Best Motor Choice | Energy-Saving Method |
|---|---|---|
| Truly constant (S1) | Squirrel-cage IE3/IE4 | Premium efficiency |
| Two discrete speeds | Pole-changing | Avoid throttling |
| Continuous variable | SCIM + VFD | Affinity-law savings |
| High starting torque | Slip-ring / soft starter | Reduced $I^2R$ loss at start |
| Precise speed control | DC / BLDC / PMSM | Eliminate slip loss |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Motor efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + \text{Losses}} \times 100$ | % | Output ÷ Input |
| 2 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L}$ | — | $V_L$, $I_L$ are line values |
| 3 | Input power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta}$ | kW | For energy billing |
| 4 | Load factor | $LF = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{rated}}$ | 0–1 | Ideal 0.75–1.0 |
| 5 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu or % | $N_s$ = synchronous speed |
| 6 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = poles |
| 7 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rcu} = s \times P_{ag}$ | W | Slip power dissipated in rotor |
| 8 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = \dfrac{P_{developed}}{1 - s}$ | W | Power crossing air gap |
| 9 | Affinity Law (Power) | $P \propto N^3$ | — | Fans and pumps |
| 10 | Affinity Law (Head) | $H \propto N^2$ | — | Pump head / fan pressure |
| 11 | Affinity Law (Flow) | $Q \propto N$ | — | Volumetric flow |
| 12 | Energy saved per year | $\Delta E = P_{rated} \cdot LF \cdot h_{yr} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\eta_1} - \dfrac{1}{\eta_2}\right)$ | kWh/yr | Premium vs standard |
| 13 | Annual cost saving | $\Delta C = \Delta E \times \text{tariff}$ | ₹/yr | Multiply by ₹/kWh |
| 14 | Simple payback | $SPP = \dfrac{\Delta \text{CapEx}}{\Delta C}$ | years | Months = SPP × 12 |
| 15 | Altitude derating | $P_{derated} = P_{rated} \times (1 - 0.01 \times \frac{H - 1000}{100})$ | kW | For H > 1000 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` symbols were used inside any cell of the table above** — vertical bars are rendered using `\vert`/`\mid` in math contexts only, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE compilation rules.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Cement plants:** Use VFDs on raw-mill fans, ID fans, and kiln drives to save 25–40% energy.
- **Textile mills:** Replace single-speed motors on ring frames with multi-speed motors to handle yarn count changes.
- **HVAC in buildings:** VFD-controlled chilled-water pumps save 50–70% pump energy at part load.
- **Steel plants:** Use synchronous motors on rolling mills for power factor correction and high constant-speed drives.

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU answer, always end the analysis with **one real industrial example** to demonstrate applied knowledge — examiners reward it generously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Energy Saving from Premium-Efficiency Motor

Let us rigorously derive the energy savings and payback period when replacing a standard-efficiency motor with a premium-efficiency motor for a **constant load**.

### Given Data

- Rated power: $P_{rated} = 30$ kW
- Load factor: $LF = 0.80$ (i.e., motor runs at 80% of rating)
- Operating hours per year: $h_{yr} = 5000$ h
- Standard motor efficiency at 80% load: $\eta_1 = 0.890$
- Premium motor efficiency at 80% load: $\eta_2 = 0.935$
- Premium motor extra cost: $\Delta \text{CapEx} = ₹35{,}000$
- Electricity tariff: $T = ₹8/\text{kWh}$

### Step 1 — Compute the output power at the operating point

The actual mechanical output demanded by the load is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= P_{rated} \times LF \\
&= 30 \text{ kW} \times 0.80 \\
&= 24 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The motor must deliver 24 kW of shaft power regardless of which motor (standard or premium) is installed. The difference lies in how much electrical input is required to produce this output.

### Step 2 — Compute the input power for each motor

Input power is output divided by efficiency.

For the standard motor:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in,1} &= \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_1} = \frac{24 \text{ kW}}{0.890} \\
&= 26.966 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the premium motor:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in,2} &= \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_2} = \frac{24 \text{ kW}}{0.935} \\
&= 25.668 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The premium motor draws 1.298 kW less input power for the same mechanical output because of lower stator/rotor copper losses, lower stray load loss, and better lamination steel.

### Step 3 — Compute the per-hour energy saving

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_{in,1} - P_{in,2} \\
&= 26.966 - 25.668 \\
&= 1.298 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the annual energy saving

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= \Delta P \times h_{yr} \\
&= 1.298 \text{ kW} \times 5000 \text{ h} \\
&= 6490 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the annual monetary saving

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= \Delta E \times T \\
&= 6490 \text{ kWh} \times ₹8/\text{kWh} \\
&= ₹51{,}920 \text{ /yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the simple payback period

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= \frac{\Delta \text{CapEx}}{\Delta C} \\
&= \frac{₹35{,}000}{₹51{,}920/\text{yr}} \\
&= 0.674 \text{ years} \\
&\approx 8.1 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Whenever the payback period is less than **2 years**, the energy conservation measure is considered a *bankable* project under the Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India.

## 3.2 Derivation — VFD Energy Saving on a Centrifugal Pump

A centrifugal pump follows the **affinity (cube) law**: $P \propto N^3$.

### Given Data

- Pump rated power: $P_{rated} = 50$ kW
- Required flow reduction: 30% of design (i.e., new flow ratio $Q_2/Q_1 = 0.70$)
- Throttling (valve) control baseline: $P_{throttle} = P_{rated} \times 0.70 \times \text{system-curve factor} = 38.5$ kW (typical)
- VFD control baseline: applied directly through affinity law

### Step 1 — Speed ratio for required flow

For a pump, $Q \propto N$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_2}{N_1} &= \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \\
&= 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** To deliver 70% of design flow, the pump must run at 70% of rated speed.

### Step 2 — Power required at reduced speed (VFD control)

By the cube law, $P \propto N^3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{VFD}}{P_{rated}} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \\
&= (0.70)^3 \\
&= 0.343
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{VFD} &= 0.343 \times 50 \\
&= 17.15 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with throttling control

A throttled pump at 70% flow typically draws:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{throttle} &\approx 0.77 \times P_{rated} \\
&= 0.77 \times 50 \\
&= 38.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Throttling does not reduce the speed of the pump; it merely introduces a deliberate head loss in the discharge line, which the pump motor must continue to overcome. Hence the power saving is much smaller.

### Step 4 — Power saved by VFD

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_{throttle} - P_{VFD} \\
&= 38.5 - 17.15 \\
&= 21.35 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Annual energy and cost saving

For $h_{yr} = 6000$ h and $T = ₹7.5$/kWh:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 21.35 \times 6000 = 128{,}100 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= 128{,}100 \times 7.5 = ₹9{,}60{,}750 \text{ /yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a VFD costing ₹2,50,000:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{9{,}60{,}750} \\
&= 0.260 \text{ years} \\
&\approx 3.1 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** A VFD on a cubic-law load (fan or pump) pays back in under 4 months. This is the single highest-ROI energy measure in industry.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Motor Selection Decision Tree

| Step | Question | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the load truly constant (S1)? | → Go to Step 2 | → Go to Step 4 |
| 2 | Is the load ≥ 50% of rating? | Standard IE3 motor sufficient | Replace with right-sized IE4 |
| 3 | Annual running hours > 4000? | Premium IE4 / Synchronous | Standard IE3 |
| 4 | Does the load vary continuously? | → Go to Step 5 | → Go to Step 6 |
| 5 | Is the driven load a fan/pump? | SCIM + **VFD** (affinity law) | Pole-change or SRIM |
| 6 | Is it a discrete 2-speed load? | Pole-changing motor (Dahlander) | Standard SCIM + soft starter |
| 7 | Is high starting torque required? | Slip-ring / star-delta / soft start | Direct-on-line SCIM |
| 8 | Is precise speed regulation needed? | DC / BLDC / PMSM + drive | Standard SCIM |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Motor Selection Logic for Constant vs Variable Loads

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the decision flow described in the analytical matrix above. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Load Profile] --> B{Constant S1 Duty?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Load 50 to 100 percent of Rating?}
    B -- No --> D{Continuous Variation?}
    C -- Yes --> E{High Running Hours above 4000 hr yr?}
    C -- No --> F[Right Size with IE4 Motor]
    E -- Yes --> G[Select Premium IE4 SCIM]
    E -- No --> H[Select Standard IE3 SCIM]
    D -- Yes --> I{Fan or Pump Cubic Load?}
    D -- No --> J{Discrete 2 Speeds?}
    I -- Yes --> K[SCIM plus VFD with Affinity Law]
    I -- No --> L[Slip Ring or Pole Change Motor]
    J -- Yes --> M[Dahlander Pole Changing Motor]
    J -- No --> N{High Starting Torque Needed?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Slip Ring or Star Delta Starter]
    N -- No --> P[Direct On Line SCIM with Soft Starter]
    G --> Q[End Selection]
    H --> Q
    F --> Q
    K --> Q
    L --> Q
    M --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

**How to read this diagram (for KTU exam writing):**

1. The audit team first measures the **load profile** using a power analyser (kW vs time).
2. If the curve is flat → enter the **Constant Load branch**.
3. If the curve varies → enter the **Variable Load branch**, then drill down based on whether the load is a fan/pump (cubic), discrete-step, or high-torque type.
4. Each branch ends at a specific motor + drive combination, which is the final recommendation.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components of an Energy-Efficient Motor-Drive System

The following diagram shows how the motor, the driven load, the VFD, and the power-quality / monitoring instruments are integrated in a modern energy-efficient installation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3 Phase Mains Supply] --> B[MCB and Isolator]
    B --> C[Power Quality Analyser]
    C --> D[VFD or Soft Starter]
    D --> E[Squirrel Cage Induction Motor]
    E --> F[Coupling]
    F --> G[Driven Load Fan Pump Compressor Conveyor]
    H[Control Signal 4 to 20 mA] --> D
    I[Temperature Sensor PT100] --> E
    J[Speed Encoder] --> D
    K[Energy Meter kWh] --> C
```

**Functional explanation of the architecture:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Mains Supply | 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC input |
| MCB/Isolator | Short-circuit and isolation protection |
| Power Quality Analyser | Monitors V, I, kW, kVAr, kVA, THD, PF |
| VFD / Soft Starter | Speed control / soft-start function |
| Squirrel-Cage Motor | Converts electrical to mechanical energy |
| Coupling | Mechanical transmission to load |
| Driven Load | Fan / Pump / Compressor / Conveyor |
| Control Signal | 4–20 mA feedback from process (PID loop) |
| PT100 Sensor | Winding temperature monitoring (overload protection) |
| Encoder | Closed-loop speed feedback to VFD |
| Energy Meter | kWh consumption for energy audit baseline |

## 4.3 Process Flow — Energy Audit Motor Survey Methodology

The following diagram captures the **field-survey procedure** that an energy auditor follows to recommend load matching.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Inventory of All Motors] --> B[Step 2: Record Nameplate Data]
    B --> C[Step 3: Measure Actual Loading]
    C --> D{Loading above 50 percent?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Document as Efficient Motor]
    D -- No --> F[Step 4: Check Average Efficiency at Actual Load]
    F --> G{High Running Hours above 3000?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Recommend Replace with Right Sized Premium Motor]
    G -- No --> I[Recommend Downsize or Install VFD if Variable]
    H --> J[Step 5: Calculate Payback Period]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Payback below 2 Years?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Implement ECM]
    K -- No --> M[Reject or Defer]
    E --> N[Continue to Next Motor]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define load matching. Why is it considered one of the most effective energy conservation measures in an industrial plant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Load matching is the practice of selecting an electric motor whose rated capacity is closely matched to the actual mechanical load demand, so that the motor operates near its point of highest efficiency (typically 75–100% of rated load).

*Justification (1 mark):* It is highly effective because electric motors consume about 60–65% of industrial electricity worldwide, and a poorly matched (usually oversized) motor operates at low efficiency and low power factor, wasting 10–30% of input energy. Replacing or right-sizing a single 30 kW motor can save 5000–15,000 kWh per year, making load matching one of the highest-ROI energy conservation measures.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the selection of motors for constant loads and variable loads. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Constant Load | Variable Load |
|---|---|---|
| Duty cycle | S1 (continuous) | S2–S6 (intermittent) |
| Selection criterion | Match rated power to demand | Match both average and peak demand; use speed control |
| Preferred motor | Squirrel-cage IE3/IE4 | SCIM + VFD, pole-change, slip-ring |
| Energy-saving focus | Premium efficiency | Avoid throttling / on-off cycling |

*Examples (1 mark):* Constant — centrifugal pump delivering fixed head. Variable — elevator hoist or VFD-controlled fan.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** A 30 kW, 415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole, three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor is used to drive a centrifugal pump. The motor has a full-load efficiency of 89% and a power factor of 0.86. The load operates at 75% loading for 5500 hours per year, and the electricity tariff is ₹7.5 per kWh.

   **(i)** Calculate the annual energy consumption and annual energy cost of the existing motor. **[3 marks]**
   **(ii)** It is proposed to replace this motor with a premium-efficiency IE4 motor of the same rating having an efficiency of 93.5% at 75% load. Calculate the annual energy saving, monetary saving, and simple payback period if the new motor costs ₹40,000 more than the existing one. **[4 marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch (or block diagram) the procedure for selecting a motor for **variable loads** in a textile mill ring-frame application, where the load torque varies from 40% to 100% of rated value over different yarn counts. Justify the use of a VFD-controlled squirrel-cage motor. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A 75 kW, 3-phase induction motor drives a centrifugal fan in a boiler-house application. The fan currently operates with a discharge damper to control air flow. It is proposed to install a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) so that the fan speed can be reduced to deliver 70% of design flow during partial-load conditions. The fan operates 6000 hours per year at the reduced-flow condition.

   **(i)** Using the affinity laws, calculate the speed ratio, the power drawn by the fan motor with VFD control, and the power drawn with damper control. Assume that with damper control the power reduces to 80% of rated at 70% flow. **[4 marks]**
   **(ii)** Estimate the annual energy saving, monetary saving (tariff ₹8/kWh), and payback if the VFD costs ₹3,00,000. **[3 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **necessity of load matching** for constant loads. Include in your answer: (i) the typical efficiency curve of a standard induction motor, (ii) the effect of oversizing on efficiency and power factor, (iii) the economic benefits with one numerical example, and (iv) relevant IS/IEC standards. **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**Part (a)(i) — Annual energy and cost of existing motor**

Step 1: Output power at 75% loading:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= 30 \text{ kW} \times 0.75 = 22.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Input power with η = 0.89:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in,1} &= \frac{22.5}{0.89} = 25.28 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: Annual energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 25.28 \times 5500 = 1{,}39{,}044 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4: Annual cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 1{,}39{,}044 \times 7.5 = ₹10{,}42{,}830 \text{ /yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Savings with IE4 motor (η₂ = 0.935)**

Step 5: New input power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in,2} &= \frac{22.5}{0.935} = 24.06 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6: Per-hour power saving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 25.28 - 24.06 = 1.22 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 7: Annual energy saving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 1.22 \times 5500 = 6710 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 8: Annual monetary saving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= 6710 \times 7.5 = ₹50{,}325 \text{ /yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 9: Simple payback:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= \frac{40{,}000}{50{,}325} = 0.795 \text{ years} \approx 9.5 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Motor selection for variable ring-frame load**

1. **Load analysis:** A ring-frame in a textile mill draws fluctuating torque based on yarn count, spindle speed, and bobbin build-up. Torque varies from 40% to 100% over a typical shift. A single-speed motor running continuously at full speed would waste energy whenever torque is low.

2. **Motor + drive choice:** A **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor** (robust, low maintenance, suited to dusty/humid mill environment) coupled to a **VFD** is the recommended combination.

3. **Justification of VFD:**
   - The spindle torque is roughly proportional to speed² (spinning dynamics), so a 20% speed reduction yields ~36% torque reduction and ~**49% power reduction** (affinity cube law on the helper drive).
   - The VFD enables soft start, eliminating the high inrush current and mechanical shock that would otherwise stress the motor and belts.
   - Power factor remains high (0.95+) because the VFD's rectifier-filter section decouples the motor from the supply.
   - The motor can be programmed with multiple preset speeds corresponding to different yarn counts.

4. **Selection procedure:**
   - Determine the peak torque requirement from the worst-case yarn count.
   - Select a motor with rated torque ≥ peak torque × service factor (1.15).
   - Verify that the motor's thermal class (typically Class F) is suitable for the ambient conditions of the mill (often 40–45 °C, high humidity).
   - Select a VFD rated 1.1 × motor full-load current, with built-in EMC filter and PID controller.
   - Install a power-quality analyser at the incomer to record baseline and post-installation kWh, kVArh, PF, and harmonics.

5. **Economic outcome:** Energy savings of 25–40% on the ring-frame drive, with payback typically 1–2 years.

---

### Solution to Question B

**Part (a)(i) — Affinity-law calculation for fan with VFD**

Step 1: Speed ratio for 70% flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_2}{N_1} &= \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Power with VFD (using cube law):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{VFD} &= 75 \times (0.70)^3 = 75 \times 0.343 = 25.73 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: Power with damper control (given as 80% of rated at 70% flow):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{damper} &= 0.80 \times 75 = 60 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Savings and payback**

Step 4: Per-hour saving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 60 - 25.73 = 34.27 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5: Annual energy saving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 34.27 \times 6000 = 2{,}05{,}620 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6: Annual monetary saving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= 2{,}05{,}620 \times 8 = ₹16{,}44{,}960 \text{ /yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 7: Payback:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{16{,}44{,}960} = 0.182 \text{ years} \approx 2.2 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Necessity of load matching for constant loads**

1. **Typical efficiency curve:** A standard induction motor's efficiency rises sharply from 25% load, peaks around 75–100% load, and then drops slightly above 100%. Below 50% load, efficiency falls rapidly because fixed losses (core, friction, windage) form a large fraction of the input.

2. **Effect of oversizing:**
   - Efficiency falls: a 75 kW motor at 30% load may operate at only 82–85% efficiency, wasting 4–8 percentage points.
   - Power factor falls: at 30% load, the displacement power factor may drop to 0.55–0.65, attracting kVArh penalty in industrial tariffs.
   - The motor runs cool, but the "capacity charge" component of the electricity bill does not reduce.

3. **Economic example:**
   - Suppose a 22 kW motor drives a load that actually needs only 11 kW (50% load). Annual energy input at η = 0.86 (at full load) reduces to η = 0.82 (at half load). The extra loss = 11 × (1/0.82 − 1/0.86) × 5000 h = 681 kWh/yr ≈ ₹5,450/yr wasted. Right-sizing with a 15 kW premium motor recovers most of this.

4. **Standards:**
   - **IS 325** — performance of 3-phase induction motors.
   - **IS 12615** — energy-efficient induction motors.
   - **IEC 60034-30-1** — IE1/IE2/IE3/IE4 efficiency classes.
   - **IEC 60034-1** — duty cycle classification (S1–S10).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students write "use VFD for every variable load" without checking the **load type**. VFDs are profitable only for **cubic-law** loads (fans, pumps). For constant-torque loads (conveyors, compressors), a VFD does *not* save energy — it may even increase losses due to harmonic injection.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to apply the **affinity cube law** correctly: $\left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ — many students write it as a linear or square relationship and get the wrong answer.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not stating the **applicable IS/IEC standard** — easy 1-mark loss.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Confusing **power factor** (which is *not* the same as efficiency) — power factor affects kVArh billing, efficiency affects kWh billing.
> - **Common mistake 5:** Computing **simple payback** without including installation / commissioning / downtime costs. For KTU, the basic SPP formula is acceptable, but bonus marks come from including the net present value (NPV) perspective.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Load matching = right-sizing** a motor to operate near its best-efficiency point (75–100% load).
- Oversized motors suffer from **low efficiency AND low power factor** simultaneously — a double penalty.
- For **constant S1 duty**: choose **IE3/IE4 squirrel-cage induction motor** sized to actual demand × 1.15 service factor.
- For **variable duty**: choose **SCIM + VFD** for fans/pumps (cube law), **pole-changing motors** for two-step loads, **slip-ring motors** for high starting torque, and **DC/BLDC motors** for precise speed control.
- **Affinity laws** for fans and pumps: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ — a 20% speed reduction yields ~49% power saving.
- **Key standards:** IS 325, IS 12615, IEC 60034-30-1, IEC 60034-1.
- **Motor slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; rotor copper loss = $s \times P_{ag}$.
- **Premium-efficiency upgrade payback** typically 6–18 months for motors running > 4000 h/yr.
- **VFD upgrade payback** for cubic-law loads typically 2–6 months.
- Always state **input/output units, efficiency values, and tariff rates** in your numerical answers.
- End every KTU numerical answer with a **conclusion sentence** (e.g., "Therefore, replacing the standard motor with an IE4 motor yields an annual saving of ₹50,325 and a payback of 9.5 months, making it a bankable energy conservation measure.").
- **Mnemonic for motor losses** — **"MISF"**: Motor Iron (core) loss, Stator $I^2R$, Friction & windage, Rotor (stray + slip).
- **Mnemonic for selection** — **"C-V-D-C"**: Constant load → IE3/IE4; Variable load → Drive; Cubic law → VFD; Discontinuous → pole change or soft start.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Side Management: Introduction to DSM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Demand Side Management: Introduction to DSM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Demand Side Management (DSM)** is the planning, implementation, and monitoring of utility-sponsored and consumer-based activities designed to influence customer use of electricity in ways that will produce desired changes in the utility's load shape — i.e., in the time pattern and magnitude of a utility's load. It encompasses the broad set of actions taken by utilities, consumers, and third parties to optimize energy consumption patterns on the demand (consumer) side of the electric meter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The official KTU 2024 Scheme definition (per PEEET742 syllabus) describes DSM as a systematic approach to controlling and optimizing energy consumption at the consumer end to achieve economic, environmental, and reliability benefits for both the utility and the consumer.

Mathematically, the fundamental goal of DSM is to flatten the load duration curve, which can be expressed as minimizing the Peak-to-Average Ratio (PAR):

$$PAR = \frac{P_{peak}}{P_{average}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A PAR value close to **1.0** represents an ideal, perfectly flat load profile. Most Indian utilities operate at PAR values between **1.4 and 2.0**, indicating significant scope for DSM intervention.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a busy city highway during rush hour. Everyone leaves office at 5 PM simultaneously, causing massive traffic jams (the **peak demand**). The highway authority can either:
1. Build a wider road (supply-side — add generation), OR
2. Stagger the office timings and promote carpooling (demand-side — manage consumption)

**Demand Side Management is the second approach for electricity.** Instead of building more power plants to meet the evening peak when everyone turns on lights, ACs, and TVs simultaneously, DSM asks: *"Can we convince users to shift some of this load to off-peak hours, or reduce it altogether?"*

Think of it this way:
- **Supply Side** = Building more lanes (new power plants)
- **Demand Side** = Encouraging people to travel at different times or share cars (load shifting + efficiency)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** DSM is about "**C**ontrolling **C**onsumer **C**hoice" — it does NOT force consumers; it **influences** them through pricing, incentives, and technology.

### 1.3 Why DSM Exists — The Core Problem

Electricity cannot be stored economically in large quantities. Therefore, generation must equal consumption at every instant. The cost of producing electricity varies dramatically:
- **Base load plants** (coal, nuclear): Cheap to run, expensive to build
- **Peak load plants** (gas turbines, diesel): Expensive to run, quick to start

When the demand peak is high, expensive peaker plants must be turned on, raising costs for everyone. **DSM flattens the load so fewer peakers are needed.**

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU-Standard)

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|------|------------|------|
| **Connected Load** | Sum of nameplate ratings of all equipment | kW |
| **Maximum Demand** | Highest power drawn in a billing period | kW / kVA |
| **Load Factor** | Average Load ÷ Peak Load | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Demand Factor** | Maximum Demand ÷ Connected Load | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Diversity Factor** | Sum of Individual Maxima ÷ Group Max | Greater than 1 |
| **Coincidence Factor** | Group Max Demand ÷ Sum of Individual Max | Less than 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Mistake:** Students frequently confuse **Load Factor** with **Demand Factor**. Remember: Load Factor relates to *time* (averaging), while Demand Factor relates to *equipment* (nameplate rating).
