The relationship between P, Q, and S is universally represented by the **Power Triangle**, which is the single most important visual tool for KTU problems.

```
              /|
             / |
            /  |  Q (Reactive Power, kVAr)
           /   |
          / φ  |
         /_____| 
            P (Real Power, kW)
            
   Hypotenuse = S (Apparent Power, kVA)
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Vector Orientation Rule (KTU Valuation Tip)**
> - **Inductive Load (Lagging PF):** Q vector points **UPWARD** (positive imaginary axis)
> - **Capacitive Load (Leading PF):** Q vector points **DOWNWARD** (negative imaginary axis)
> - When capacitors are added to an inductive load, the **upward Q vector shrinks**, rotating S closer to P, thereby increasing cos φ.

---

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used in KTU Solutions

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:----:|
| $P$ | Real (Active) Power | **kW** |
| $S$ | Apparent Power | **kVA** |
| $Q$ | Reactive Power | **kVAr** |
| $\cos\phi_1$ | Initial (Lower) Power Factor | — |
| $\cos\phi_2$ | Improved (Target) Power Factor | — |
| $Q_c$ | Reactive power supplied by capacitor | **kVAr** |
| $I_L$ | Line current (before/after) | **A** |
| $V_L$ | Line voltage | **V** |
| $f$ | Supply frequency | **Hz (50 Hz in India)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — The "Why" and "How"

## 2.1 Causes of Low Power Factor

Understanding **why** PF drops is critical for an energy auditor. The major causes are:

1. **Induction Motors** — The single largest contributor. At no-load or partial load, an induction motor draws a high magnetizing current with a large reactive component.
2. **Transformers** — Continuously draw magnetizing reactive current even at no-load (typically 5–10% of rated kVA).
3. **Arc Lamps (Arc Furnaces, Welding Sets)** — Highly inductive, very low lagging PF (often 0.3–0.5).
4. **Fluorescent Tubes and Electronic Ballasts** (older magnetic types) — Draw significant harmonic-rich current.
5. **Harmonic Distortion** — Non-linear loads (VFDs, SMPS, UPS) create harmonics that effectively lower the displacement PF and the true PF.
6. **Over-rated Motors Running Under-Loaded** — Common in industries with oversized machinery; efficiency drops and PF worsens.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> A frequent conceptual question asks: *"Why does an induction motor operate at low PF at no-load?"* The answer lies in the fact that at no-load, the **rotor is nearly short-circuited magnetically**, and almost all current drawn is **magnetizing current**, which is purely reactive (90° lagging). Real power at no-load is only to cover iron losses and friction.

---

## 2.2 Disadvantages / Effects of Low Power Factor

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** topic. The consequences cascade through the entire power system:

### ⚙️ Technical Disadvantages

1. **Higher Line Current** for the same real power delivered
$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$
   As $\cos\phi$ decreases, $I_L$ increases proportionally.

2. **Increased Copper (I²R) Losses** in cables, transformers, and busbars
$$P_{\text{loss}} = I_L^2 \cdot R$$
   This translates directly into wasted **kWh units** and thermal aging of insulation.

3. **Larger Conductor Size Required** — Cross-sectional area $A \propto I^2$, so wiring cost increases.

4. **Reduced Voltage Regulation** — Higher current causes larger voltage drops ($V_d = IZ$), causing poor voltage at load centers.

5. **Reduced Equipment Capacity** — Transformers and alternators rated in kVA deliver less useful kW.

### 💰 Economic Disadvantages (Energy Audit Perspective)

1. **kVA Demand Charges** — Utilities bill on kVA, not kW. Lower PF = higher kVA = higher fixed demand charges.
2. **Penalty Surcharges** — KSEB and other DISCOMs levy 1% to 1.5% extra for every 0.01 PF below 0.90.
3. **Poor Utilization of Capital Investment** — You pay for a 1000 kVA transformer but only extract 700 kW of useful work at 0.7 PF.

---

## 2.3 Methods of Power Factor Improvement (PFI Techniques)

There are two broad categories. KTU expects students to know the **principle, applicability, and trade-offs** of each.

### 🔌 A. Static Capacitor Installation (Most Common, KTU Favourite)

| Feature | Detail |
|:--------|:-------|
| **Principle** | Capacitors supply leading reactive power to neutralize the lagging reactive power of inductive loads |
| **Switching** | Manual contactors or Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) relays with multiple stages |
| **Placement** | Individual (motor terminals) / Group (MCC) / Central (HT bus) |
| **Advantages** | No moving parts, low losses (0.5 W/kVAr), modular, scalable |
| **Limitations** | Susceptible to harmonics (can cause resonance); fixed-step nature of switching |

### ⚙️ B. Other Methods (Brief — for KTU 2-mark questions)

- **Synchronous Condenser** — Over-excited synchronous motor running without load; supplies controllable leading kVAr. Expensive, used in large substations.
- **Phase Advancers** — An AC exciter mounted on the motor shaft supplying exciting ampere-turns to the rotor circuit of an induction motor.
- **Use of High PF Motors** — Premium-efficiency IE3/IE4 motors inherently run at higher PF.
- **Avoiding Over-rated Motors** — Right-sizing motors to load.

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield — Memorize All)

> [!IMPORTANT] **This formula table covers 95% of KTU PFI numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:----:|
| 1 | $\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L}$ | Definition of PF | — |
| 2 | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | Three-phase real power | **W** |
| 3 | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi = P \tan\phi$ | Reactive power | **VAr** |
| 4 | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | Apparent power | **VA** |
| 5 | $Q_c = P \cdot (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | **Required kVAr of capacitor bank** | **VAr** |
| 6 | $C = \frac{Q_c}{2\pi f V^2}$ | Capacitance per phase (Star) | **Farad** |
| 7 | $C_{\Delta} = \frac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_L^2 \cdot 3}$ | Capacitance per phase (Delta) | **Farad** |
| 8 | $\text{Saving in kVA} = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | Reduction in apparent power | **VA** |
| 9 | $\% \text{ Loss Reduction} = 1 - \left(\frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2$ | I²R loss improvement | **%** |
| 10 | $\text{New Line Current} = I_{L1} \cdot \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}$ | Current reduction ratio | **A** |
| 11 | $\text{PF Penalty} = \tan\phi \times \text{Energy Charges}$ | KSEB penalty formula basis | **₹** |
| 12 | $\text{Annual Saving} = (\text{kVA demand charge} \times \Delta S) + (\text{Energy charge} \times \Delta \text{Units})$ | Economic payback | **₹/year** |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Pro Tip:** Formula #5 is the **most important equation** in this entire module. The quantity $P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ is known as the **"kVAr Triangle"** and is the workhorse of every capacitor-sizing problem.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where this Knowledge is Applied

- **Energy Auditors (BEE Certified)** — Mandatory tool for any industrial energy audit report.
- **Design Engineers** — Sizing capacitor banks for HT/LT switchboards (IS 2834, IEEE 18 standards).
- **Power Quality Consultants** — Diagnosing harmonic resonance and PF issues.
- **Utility Engineers (KSEB, BESCOM, TANGEDCO)** — Tariff design and reactive power management.
- **Renewable Integration** — Solar inverters and wind farms are now required to operate at near-unity PF or provide reactive support.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE] **KTU Convention:** All derivations below follow the standard **three-phase balanced system** unless otherwise stated. The voltage $V$ refers to **line-to-line voltage** unless mentioned as phase voltage.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Capacitor kVAr Formula $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$

This is the **central derivation** that KTU expects you to reproduce on the answer script.

### Starting Point — Before PFI

Let an industrial load draw Real Power $P$ at lagging power factor $\cos\phi_1$.

The corresponding reactive power drawn from the grid is:

$$Q_1 = P \cdot \tan\phi_1$$

This is purely inductive reactive power. The grid must supply this $Q_1$ along with $P$.

### After Installing a Capacitor Bank

A capacitor bank of rating $Q_c$ kVAr is connected across the load. The capacitor supplies **leading reactive power**, which cancels an equal amount of the **lagging reactive power** from the inductive load.

The new (net) reactive power that the grid must now supply becomes:

$$Q_2 = Q_1 - Q_c$$

### The Target Power Factor Condition

We want the system to operate at a new, higher power factor $\cos\phi_2$ with the **same real power $P$** (the useful work doesn't change).

At this new state, the required reactive power from the grid is:

$$Q_2 = P \cdot \tan\phi_2$$

### Equating the Two Expressions for $Q_2$

$$Q_1 - Q_c = P \cdot \tan\phi_2$$

Substituting $Q_1 = P \tan\phi_1$:

$$P \tan\phi_1 - Q_c = P \tan\phi_2$$

Rearranging for $Q_c$:

$$Q_c = P \tan\phi_1 - P \tan\phi_2$$

Factoring out $P$:

$$\boxed{Q_c = P \cdot (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Physical Interpretation:**
> The term in parentheses represents the **vertical drop in the reactive power vector** when the power triangle is "rotated" to a steeper angle. Since the new angle $\phi_2 < \phi_1$, $\tan\phi_2 < \tan\phi_1$, making the difference positive — confirming that we need a **positive (capacitive) kVAr injection**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capacitance Required Per Phase

For a **three-phase system**, the total reactive power $Q_c$ (in VAr) supplied by a capacitor bank is shared equally across three phases.

### Star-Connected Capacitor Bank

In a star connection, the **phase voltage** is:

$$V_{\text{ph}} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

The reactive power of one phase capacitor is:

$$Q_{\text{ph}} = \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2}{X_c} = V_{\text{ph}}^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$

For three phases:

$$Q_c = 3 \cdot V_{\text{ph}}^2 \cdot 2\pi f C = 3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$

Simplifying the square:

$$Q_c = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L^2}{3} \cdot 2\pi f C = V_L^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$

Solving for $C$ (per phase, in farads):

$$\boxed{C_{\text{star}} = \frac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_L^2}}$$

### Delta-Connected Capacitor Bank

In a delta connection, the phase voltage equals the line voltage: $V_{\text{ph}} = V_L$.

$$Q_c = 3 \cdot V_L^2 \cdot 2\pi f C_{\Delta}$$

Solving for $C$:

$$\boxed{C_{\Delta} = \frac{Q_c}{3 \cdot 2\pi f V_L^2} = \frac{Q_c}{6\pi f V_L^2}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Important Comparison:**
> Since $C_{\Delta} = \frac{1}{3} C_{\text{star}}$, **delta-connected capacitor banks require only one-third the capacitance per phase**, but must be rated for the full **line voltage** (which is $\sqrt{3}$ times higher). This is why **delta is preferred for LV systems** and **star is preferred for HV systems**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Percentage Reduction in Line Losses

Before PFI:
$$P_{\text{loss},1} = I_{L1}^2 \cdot R$$

Where $I_{L1} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_1}$. So:

$$P_{\text{loss},1} = \left(\frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_1}\right)^2 \cdot R = \frac{P^2 \cdot R}{3 V_L^2 \cos^2\phi_1}$$

After PFI (same $P$ and $V_L$):
$$P_{\text{loss},2} = \frac{P^2 \cdot R}{3 V_L^2 \cos^2\phi_2}$$

The **percentage reduction** is:

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{P_{\text{loss},1} - P_{\text{loss},2}}{P_{\text{loss},1}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \1 - \frac{\cos^2\phi_1}{\cos^2\phi_2}\} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{Loss Reduction} = \left[1 - \left(\frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2\right] \times 100}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 1 — Classic KTU Model Problem (10 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEEET742]**
> A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz industrial workshop draws a line current of **100 A** at a power factor of **0.7 lagging**. An energy audit recommends improving the power factor to **0.95 lagging** by installing a delta-connected capacitor bank.
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The rating of the capacitor bank in kVAr
> (b) The new line current after PFI
> (c) The capacitance required per phase
> (d) The percentage reduction in line losses

### Given Data

- $V_L = 400$ V, $f = 50$ Hz
- $I_{L1} = 100$ A, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.7$ lag
- Target: $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$ lag
- Connection: **Delta**

### Solution — Part (a): Capacitor kVAr Rating

First, find the real power $P$:

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{L1} \cdot \cos\phi_1$$

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.7$$

$$P = 1.732 \cdot 400 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.7 = 48{,}496 \text{ W} \approx 48.5 \text{ kW}$$

Now compute the phase angles:

From $\cos\phi_1 = 0.7$:
$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.7) = 45.57°$$
$$\tan\phi_1 = \tan(45.57°) = 1.020$$

From $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$:
$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$$
$$\tan\phi_2 = \tan(18.19°) = 0.328$$

Apply the kVAr formula:

$$Q_c = P \cdot (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$$

$$Q_c = 48.5 \cdot (1.020 - 0.328)$$

$$Q_c = 48.5 \cdot 0.692$$

$$\boxed{Q_c \approx 33.56 \text{ kVAr}}$$

### Solution — Part (b): New Line Current

The new current (real power remains the same):

$$I_{L2} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi_2}$$

$$I_{L2} = \frac{48{,}496}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.95}$$

$$I_{L2} = \frac{48{,}496}{658.18}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L2} \approx 73.68 \text{ A}}$$

**Verification using the ratio formula:**
$$I_{L2} = I_{L1} \cdot \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2} = 100 \cdot \frac{0.7}{0.95} = 73.68 \text{ A} \checkmark$$

### Solution — Part (c): Capacitance Per Phase (Delta)

$$C_{\Delta} = \frac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_L^2 \cdot 3}$$

Convert $Q_c$ to VAr: $Q_c = 33{,}560$ VAr

$$C_{\Delta} = \frac{33{,}560}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot (400)^2 \cdot 3}$$

Denominator calculation:
$$2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 160000 \cdot 3 = 100\pi \cdot 480000 = 1.508 \times 10^8$$

$$C_{\Delta} = \frac{33{,}560}{1.508 \times 10^8}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\Delta} \approx 222.5 \text{ μF per phase}}$$

### Solution — Part (d): % Reduction in Line Losses

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \left[1 - \left(\frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2\right] \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \left[1 - \left(\frac{0.7}{0.95}\right)^2\right] \times 100$$

$$\left(\frac{0.7}{0.95}\right)^2 = (0.7368)^2 = 0.5429$$

$$\%\text{Reduction} = (1 - 0.5429) \times 100 = 45.71\%$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{Reduction} \approx 45.7\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight:** Improving the PF from 0.7 to 0.95 reduces the line losses by nearly **46%** — a dramatic energy saving that translates directly into lower electricity bills. This is the central message of every energy audit involving PFI.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 2 — Economic Payback Analysis (Bonus)

> A factory operates at 0.75 PF lag with a monthly electricity bill showing **₹ 4,50,000** in energy charges and a **demand charge of ₹ 350 per kVA**. After PFI to 0.95, the connected load is **800 kW**.

### Solution

Initial kVA demand:
$$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \frac{800}{0.75} = 1066.67 \text{ kVA}$$

Improved kVA demand:
$$S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_2} = \frac{800}{0.95} = 842.11 \text{ kVA}$$

Monthly saving in demand charges:
$$\Delta S = 1066.67 - 842.11 = 224.56 \text{ kVA}$$

$$\text{Monthly Demand Saving} = 224.56 \times 350 = \text{₹ } 78{,}596$$

If the capacitor bank costs ₹ 1,50,000 (one-time), the **payback period** is:
$$\text{Payback} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{78{,}596 \times 12} = 0.16 \text{ years} \approx 2 \text{ months}$$

> [!NOTE] **Real-world note:** In most industrial setups, payback periods of **6 to 18 months** are typical, making PFI one of the **highest ROI energy efficiency measures** in any audit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Power Factor Correction System Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow** of a typical **Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC)** system used in industrial switchboards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three Phase AC Mains Supply] --> B[Main Circuit Breaker MCCB]
    B --> C[Main Bus Bar]
    C --> D[CT and PT Measurement Unit]
    D --> E[APFC Relay Controller]
    E -->|Microprocessor Logic| F{Compare PF with Setpoint}
    F -->|PF Less Than 0.95| G[Trigger Capacitor Stage 1]
    F -->|PF Still Low| H[Trigger Capacitor Stage 2]
    F -->|PF Still Low| I[Trigger Capacitor Stage N]
    F -->|PF Above 0.95| J[All Stages OFF Hold]
    G --> K[Contactor Bank]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Delta Connected Capacitor Bank]
    L --> M[Inductive Load Motors and Transformers]
    M --> N[Improved Power Factor Output]
    N --> D
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Energy Audit Decision Tree for PFI

This diagram maps the **decision logic an energy auditor follows** when identifying whether PFI is recommended.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Collect Load Data] --> S2[Measure kW kVA and PF]
    S2 --> S3{Is PF Below 0.90}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Identify Major Inductive Loads]
    S3 -->|No| S5[PF Acceptable No Action]
    S4 --> S6{Check Monthly Energy Bill}
    S6 -->|KSEB Penalty Present| S7[Calculate Required kVAr]
    S6 -->|No Penalty| S8[Evaluate Technical Losses Only]
    S7 --> S9[Size Capacitor Bank]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Estimate Cost and Payback]
    S10 --> S11{Payback Less Than 2 Years}
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Recommend PFI Installation]
    S11 -->|No| S13[Recommend Alternative Measures]
    S12 --> S14[End: Prepare Audit Report]
    S13 --> S14
    S5 --> S14
```

---

## 4.3 Power Triangle — Phasor Visualization

The following Mermaid diagram renders a stylized **Power Triangle** showing the relationship between P, Q, and S before and after PFI.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph before[BEFORE PFI]
        direction LR
        P1[Real Power P Constant]:::base
        Q1[Reactive Q1 High]:::inductive
        S1A[Apparent S1 Large]:::inductive
    end
    subgraph after[AFTER PFI]
        direction LR
        P2[Real Power P Same]:::base
        Q2[Net Reactive Q2 Reduced]:::capacitive
        S2A[Apparent S2 Smaller]:::capacitive
    end
    QC[Capacitor kVAr Qc]:::capacitive
    QC -.->|Subtracts from| Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    classDef base fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
    classDef inductive fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef capacitive fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram:** The real power $P$ remains **constant** (the actual useful work doesn't change). The capacitor bank supplies $Q_c$ which **cancels** an equal portion of the inductive Q. The new apparent power $S_2$ is shorter, meaning **lower kVA billing** and **lower current** in the cables.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Locations for Capacitor Installation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Utility Grid HT Supply 11kV] --> B[Step Down Transformer]
    B --> C[Main LT Bus 415V]
    C --> D1[Location 1: Individual Motor Terminals]
    C --> D2[Location 2: MCC Sub Panels]
    C --> D3[Location 3: Central LT Capacitor Bank]
    C --> D4[Location 4: HT Side Compensation]
    D1 --> E1[Best for Large Motors 50 HP and Above]
    D2 --> E2[Good for Group of Similar Loads]
    D3 --> E3[Most Common Industrial Practice]
    D4 --> E4[Used by Utility and Large Plants]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Selection Guide for KTU:**
> - **Individual compensation** → Best for large, continuously running motors; maximum loss reduction.
> - **Central compensation** → Cheapest; most common in MSMEs and small industries.
> - **HT compensation** → Used when the LT bus has harmonic issues; capacitors rated for 11 kV/22 kV/33 kV.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define power factor. Why is power factor improvement necessary in industrial installations?**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Power factor is the cosine of the angle between the supply voltage and current in an AC circuit, defined as the ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA). It indicates how effectively the supplied electrical power is being used for useful work.

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}}$$

**Necessity (1.5 marks):**
Power factor improvement is necessary because low PF causes:
- Increased line current → higher I²R losses in cables
- Higher kVA demand → increased demand charges and utility penalties (KSEB penalizes PF below 0.90)
- Reduced capacity utilization of transformers and alternators
- Poor voltage regulation at the load end

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **List any three methods of power factor improvement. Explain briefly the most commonly used method.**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Three Methods (1.5 marks):**
1. Installation of static capacitor banks
2. Use of synchronous condensers (over-excited synchronous motors)
3. Use of phase advancers with induction motors

**Most Common — Static Capacitors (1.5 marks):**
Capacitors are connected in parallel with the inductive load. They supply leading reactive power equal to a portion of the lagging reactive power demand, thereby neutralizing it. As a result, the net reactive power drawn from the grid is reduced, raising the overall power factor toward unity. Capacitors have low losses, no moving parts, and can be switched in stages using APFC relays.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule: Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

### 📌 Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

> A 3-phase, **415 V, 50 Hz** induction motor load draws a line current of **75 A** at a power factor of **0.65 lagging**. An energy audit recommends improving the power factor to **0.92 lagging**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank required in kVAr. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** If the capacitor bank is connected in **star**, determine the capacitance per phase. Also find the new line current after power factor improvement and the percentage reduction in line losses. (7 Marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Real Power $P$**

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi_1$$

$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 75 \cdot 0.65$$

$$P = 1.732 \cdot 415 \cdot 75 \cdot 0.65 = 35{,}040 \text{ W} \approx 35.04 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Find Phase Angles**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.65) = 49.46° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_1 = 1.169$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.92) = 23.07° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.426$$

**Step 3: Apply kVAr Formula**

$$Q_c = P \cdot (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$$

$$Q_c = 35.04 \cdot (1.169 - 0.426)$$

$$Q_c = 35.04 \cdot 0.743$$

$$\boxed{Q_c \approx 26.03 \text{ kVAr}}$$

> **Final Answer (a):** Capacitor bank rating = **26.03 kVAr** (commonly rounded to next standard size 27.5 kVAr or 30 kVAr)

### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Capacitance Per Phase (Star Connection)**

In star, the formula is:

$$C_{\text{star}} = \frac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_L^2}$$

Note: In star connection, $Q_c = V_L^2 \cdot 2\pi f \cdot C$ is the total three-phase power. Substituting values with $Q_c = 26{,}030$ VAr:

$$C_{\text{star}} = \frac{26{,}030}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot (415)^2}$$

Denominator:
$$2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 172225 = 314.16 \cdot 172225 = 54{,}109{,}706$$

$$C_{\text{star}} = \frac{26{,}030}{54{,}109{,}706} = 4.81 \times 10^{-4} \text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{star}} \approx 481 \text{ μF per phase}}$$

**Step 2: New Line Current**

$$I_{L2} = I_{L1} \cdot \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2} = 75 \cdot \frac{0.65}{0.92} = 52.99 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L2} \approx 53 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 3: Percentage Reduction in Line Losses**

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \left[1 - \left(\frac{0.65}{0.92}\right)^2\right] \times 100$$

$$\left(\frac{0.65}{0.92}\right)^2 = (0.7065)^2 = 0.4992$$

$$\%\text{Reduction} = (1 - 0.4992) \times 100 = 50.08\%$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{Loss Reduction} \approx 50.08\%}$$

> **Final Answer (b):** $C = 481$ μF/phase, $I_{L2} = 53$ A, **Loss reduction ≈ 50.08%**

---

### 📌 Question B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> A small-scale industry has a **3-phase, 400 V** connected load of **150 kW** operating at an average power factor of **0.72 lagging**. The industry pays ₹ **500 per kVA** as demand charge and ₹ **8 per kWh** for energy. The line losses in the feeder amount to **5% of the energy consumed**. The industry operates for **3000 hours per year**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the annual saving in demand charges if the power factor is improved to **0.95 lagging** using a capacitor bank. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Estimate the kVAr rating of the capacitor bank, the new line current, and the annual saving in energy charges due to reduced line losses. (7 Marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Initial kVA Demand**

$$S_1 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \frac{150}{0.72} = 208.33 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2: Improved kVA Demand**

$$S_2 = \frac{P}{\cos\phi_2} = \frac{150}{0.95} = 157.89 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 3: Reduction in kVA**

$$\Delta S = 208.33 - 157.89 = 50.44 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 4: Annual Saving in Demand Charges**

$$\text{Saving} = \Delta S \times \text{Rate} = 50.44 \times 500 = \text{₹ } 25{,}220 \text{ per month}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Demand Saving} = 25{,}220 \times 12 = \text{₹ } 3{,}02{,}640}$$

> **Final Answer (a):** **₹ 3,02,640 per year** saved in demand charges alone.

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Capacitor kVAr Rating**

Compute the tangents:
$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.72) = 43.95° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_1 = 0.963$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.328$$

$$Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 150 \cdot (0.963 - 0.328)$$

$$Q_c = 150 \cdot 0.635 = 95.25 \text{ kVAr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_c \approx 95.25 \text{ kVAr}}$$

**Step 2: New Line Current**

Initial current:
$$I_{L1} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi_1} = \frac{150{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.72} = 300.7 \text{ A}$$

New current:
$$I_{L2} = I_{L1} \cdot \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2} = 300.7 \cdot \frac{0.72}{0.95} = 227.9 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{L2} \approx 228 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 3: Annual Energy Saving from Loss Reduction**

% Loss reduction:
$$\%\text{Reduction} = \left[1 - \left(\frac{0.72}{0.95}\right)^2\right] \times 100 = [1 - 0.574]\times 100 = 42.6\%$$

**Energy consumed annually:**
$$E = 150 \text{ kW} \times 3000 \text{ h} = 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Line losses annually (5% of E):**
$$E_{\text{loss}} = 0.05 \times 4{,}50{,}000 = 22{,}500 \text{ kWh}$$

**Annual energy saving from PFI:**
$$\Delta E = 0.426 \times 22{,}500 = 9585 \text{ kWh}$$

**Annual monetary saving:**
$$\text{Saving} = 9585 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 76{,}680$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Energy Saving} \approx \text{₹ } 76{,}680}$$

**Total Annual Savings (a + b):**
$$\boxed{3{,}02{,}640 + 76{,}680 = \text{₹ } 3{,}79{,}320 \text{ per year}}$$

---

> [!WARNING] **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute P first** — Many students directly multiply kVAr using $P$ in watts by mistake. Always express $P$ in **kW** when using the kVAr formula, or in **W** when using capacitance formulas.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_L$ and $V_{\text{ph}}$ in capacitor formulas** — The star and delta formulas differ. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Using degrees vs. radians** — The $2\pi f$ term requires $f$ in Hz (no issue), but ensure your calculator is in **degree mode** when computing $\cos^{-1}$ for the tan values.
> 4. **Ignoring the line current reduction step** — KTU often awards a separate 2 marks for computing $I_{L2}$ explicitly.
> 5. **Not drawing the power triangle** — In 14-mark questions, **always draw the phasor diagram** (before and after PFI) on the left margin of the answer script. It is worth 2 easy marks and demonstrates conceptual clarity.
> 6. **Confusing % loss reduction with absolute loss reduction** — The percentage formula gives the % of original losses saved, not the % of total energy.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute Revision — Read Before Exam**

- ✅ **Power Factor (PF)** = $\cos\phi$ = ratio of real power to apparent power (kW/kVA).
- ✅ A **lagging PF** indicates **inductive** load (motors, transformers).
- ✅ A **leading PF** indicates **capacitive** load (capacitor banks).
- ✅ **Low PF causes:** high line current, high I²R losses, high kVA demand, utility penalties.
- ✅ **Most common PFI method** = **static capacitor bank** connected in parallel.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ — *memorize by heart.*
- ✅ **Star-connected C:** $C = \dfrac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_L^2}$ — note: total 3-phase power used.
- ✅ **Delta-connected C:** $C_{\Delta} = \dfrac{Q_c}{6\pi f V_L^2}$ — capacitance is **3× smaller** than star.
- ✅ **New line current:** $I_{L2} = I_{L1} \cdot \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}$.
- ✅ **% Loss reduction:** $\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2\right] \times 100$.
- ✅ **PF penalty threshold** in Kerala (KSEB LT/HT tariffs): typically **0.90 lag** below which surcharges apply.
- ✅ **Other PFI methods:** synchronous condenser, phase advancer, high-PF motors — know at least 2 in definition form.
- ✅ **Payback period** of PFI in most industries: **6 to 18 months** — useful in viva answers.
- ✅ **Draw the power triangle** in every numerical — KTU rewards neat phasor diagrams.
- ✅ **Capacitor location trade-off:** Individual = max savings, Group = balance, Central = cheapest.
- ✅ **Watch for harmonics:** Capacitors can resonate with transformer inductance; derated reactors may be needed.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Real power P stays fixed. Reactive power Q shrinks after PFI. Apparent power S shrinks. Current drops. Losses drop. Bill drops. Draw the triangle!"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Numerical Examples" in Energy Management & Auditing?

In the context of **KTU PEEET742 – Energy Management and Auditing (Module 2)**, *Numerical Examples* refer to the structured mathematical and financial problem-solving exercises used to quantify **energy savings, cost reduction, investment returns, and efficiency improvements** identified during an energy audit.

These problems translate the theoretical concepts of **energy economics, financial appraisal methods, and energy efficiency calculations** into verifiable, numerical outcomes that justify investment decisions in energy conservation projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Context):**
> Numerical examples in Module 2 of PEEET742 are the computational exercises that apply financial evaluation techniques (Simple Payback, Return on Investment, Net Present Value, Internal Rate of Return) and energy performance calculations (power factor correction, energy savings, load factor, demand analysis) to real-world energy audit data.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are deciding whether to spend **₹2,00,000** to install solar panels on your house. The panels will save you **₹40,000** per year on electricity bills. Is it worth it? In **5 years**, you recover the cost — but what about inflation, interest rates, and the time value of money?

**Numerical examples in energy auditing are exactly this — but applied to industries.**

They help an Energy Manager answer practical questions like:

> - *"If I replace an old motor with an energy-efficient one, how many months will it take to recover the cost?"*
> - *"What is the true financial benefit of installing capacitors to improve power factor?"*
> - *"How much money is wasted every month due to poor power factor penalties from the electricity board?"*

**The Intuition:** An energy audit identifies problems. *Numerical examples* convert those problems into **rupees saved**, then compare that saving against the **investment cost** to decide whether the project is financially viable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Module 2 Numerical Examples:**
> - **Time value of money discount rate (i):** Typically **8% – 15%** (bank lending / hurdle rate)
> - **Working hours per year:** **8760 hours** (24 × 365), or operational hours per shift
> - **Energy unit price (₹/kWh):** Varies by state and tariff slab (e.g., **₹6 – ₹10/kWh**)
> - **Demand charge:** Charged in **₹/kVA** based on maximum demand
> - **Power factor penalty threshold:** Usually **0.90 lag** (below this, utilities charge penalty)
> - **Motor standard ratings:** Standard efficiency classes as per **IE 2, IE 3, IE 4** (IS 12615)

---

## 1.3 Why Numerical Examples are Critical for KTU Examinations

In the KTU 2024 Scheme examination pattern, Module 2 numerical problems form a **high-weightage, high-scoring** segment because:

1. They test **application-level** and **analysis-level** cognitive skills (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels 3 & 4).
2. They are **CO (Course Outcome) aligned** — direct mapping to **CO2**: *"Perform energy audit and evaluate energy conservation opportunities using financial appraisal methods."*
3. They appear routinely as **14-mark Part B questions** with internal choice.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Exam Tip:** Always show **formula substitution with units** in numerical answers. Examiners award step marks for every correctly written equation — never skip intermediate steps.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categorization of Numerical Examples in Module 2

The numerical problems in Module 2 of PEEET742 can be broadly classified into **four families**:

### **Family A: Simple Financial Appraisal (Direct Methods)**
These are the most frequently asked numericals in KTU exams. They answer: *"How fast will my money come back?"*

### **Family B: Time Value of Money Methods (Discounted Cash Flow)**
These answer: *"Is the project worth it when we consider the time value of money?"*

### **Family C: Electrical Energy Calculations**
These involve kWh, kVA, kVAR, power factor corrections, and motor efficiency.

### **Family D: Combined / Integrated Energy Audit Problems**
These integrate all families — a typical **14-mark full-question scenario** in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Why and How — Logical Breakdown of Each Method

### **A. Simple Payback Period (SPP)**
- **Why?** A factory owner wants to know the "breakeven time" in years or months.
- **How?** Divide the initial capital investment by the **annual net cash inflow** (savings).

### **B. Return on Investment (ROI)**
- **Why?** Investors compare this percentage against bank interest rates and alternative investment opportunities.
- **How?** Average annual return expressed as a percentage of the initial investment.

### **C. Net Present Value (NPV)**
- **Why?** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow (inflation, opportunity cost). NPV discounts future savings back to "present rupees."
- **How?** All future cash flows are discounted at a chosen rate (hurdle rate) and summed. A **positive NPV = project is financially viable.**

### **D. Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**
- **Why?** It finds the "true" interest rate at which the project breaks even (NPV = 0). It is the most rigorous profitability indicator.
- **How?** Solve the NPV equation for the discount rate that makes NPV = 0.

### **E. Power Factor Correction (PFC)**
- **Why?** Electricity boards charge penalties when PF < 0.90 lag. Improving PF reduces kVA demand and avoids penalties.
- **How?** Calculate required kVAR from capacitor bank, then compute annual ₹ savings.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** required for Module 2 numerical problems in KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize these before attempting any numerical.

| **S.No** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description of Variables** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{C}{S}$ | $C$ = Capital Investment; $S$ = Annual Net Savings | years |
| 2 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{S}{C} \times 100$ | $S$ = Annual Savings; $C$ = Capital Cost | % |
| 3 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_0$ | $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$; $i$ = Discount rate; $C_0$ = Initial investment; $n$ = Project life | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 4 | Present Worth Factor (Single Payment) | $PWF = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ | $i$ = Discount rate; $n$ = year number | dimensionless |
| 5 | Present Worth Factor (Uniform Series) | $USPWF = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | For equal annual savings | dimensionless |
| 6 | IRR Condition | $NPV = 0$ at $i = IRR$ | Solve iteratively or by interpolation | % |
| 7 | IRR by Interpolation | $IRR = i_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H}(i_H - i_L)$ | $L$ = lower rate; $H$ = higher rate | % |
| 8 | Active Power | $P = V \times I \times \cos\phi$ | $V$ = Voltage; $I$ = Current; $\cos\phi$ = Power Factor | W or kW |
| 9 | Apparent Power | $S = V \times I = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | $S$ = kVA; $P$ = kW; $Q$ = kVAR | kVA |
| 10 | Reactive Power | $Q = V \times I \times \sin\phi = P \tan\phi$ | $Q$ = kVAR; $\tan\phi$ = reactive factor | kVAR |
| 11 | Required kVAR for PF Correction | $Q_c = P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | $\phi_1$ = initial angle; $\phi_2$ = target angle | kVAR |
| 12 | Power Factor (Definition) | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{kW}{kVA}$ | Ratio of real to apparent power | dimensionless |
| 13 | Annual Energy Consumption | $E = P \times t$ | $P$ = Power; $t$ = time | kWh |
| 14 | Annual Energy Cost | $Cost = E \times \text{Rate}$ | $E$ = Energy in kWh; Rate = ₹/kWh | ₹ per year |
| 15 | Demand Charge Saving | $Savings = (kVA_1 - kVA_2) \times D$ | $D$ = Demand charge in ₹/kVA/month | ₹/month |
| 16 | Energy Saving (Efficiency Upgrade) | $\Delta E = P_{old} - P_{new}$ | Difference in input power between old and new equipment | kW |
| 17 | Annual Cost Saving | $S = \Delta E \times t \times \text{Rate}$ | $t$ = operating hours/year; Rate = ₹/kWh | ₹/year |
| 18 | Motor Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Output power / Input power | % |
| 19 | Load Factor | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Maximum Demand}}$ | Ratio of average to peak load | dimensionless |
| 20 | Transformer Loading | $Loading\,\% = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{Rated Capacity}} \times 100$ | Percentage of rated capacity used | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Table Pipe Symbol Error:** The formulas above deliberately use `\phi` (Greek phi) instead of vertical pipes to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your answer sheet, write all formulas using proper mathematical notation (e.g., $\cos\phi$, not $\cos|phi|$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These numerical methods are not just academic. They are used daily by:

- **Certified Energy Auditors (BEE empanelled)** in industries for project sanctioning.
- **Banks and financial institutions** for funding energy efficiency projects under PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme.
- **Electricity distribution companies (KSEB, TNEB, etc.)** for penalty/rebate calculations on power factor.
- **Industrial managers** to prioritize among competing energy conservation measures (ECMs).
- **Energy Service Companies (ESCOs)** for shared-savings project structuring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents **five complete, exhaustively solved numerical problems** covering the entire spectrum of Module 2 formulae. Every algebraic transition is shown step-by-step with explanatory text.

---

## 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 — Simple Payback Period & ROI

### **Problem Statement:**

> A textile mill in Kerala is considering replacing its existing 75 kW induction motor with a high-efficiency IE4 motor. The capital cost of the new motor including installation is **₹3,50,000**. The old motor operates **5000 hours per year** and has an efficiency of **88%**. The new IE4 motor has an efficiency of **94%**. The cost of electricity is **₹7.50 per kWh**. Calculate:
> (a) Annual energy savings in kWh
> (b) Annual cost savings in ₹
> (c) Simple Payback Period in years and months
> (d) Return on Investment (ROI)

### **Given Data:**
- Output Power Required, $P_{out} = 75$ kW
- Old motor efficiency, $\eta_{old} = 88\% = 0.88$
- New motor efficiency, $\eta_{new} = 94\% = 0.94$
- Operating hours, $t = 5000$ hours/year
- Electricity cost, $R = ₹7.50$/kWh
- Capital Investment, $C = ₹3,50,000$

### **Part (a) — Annual Energy Savings (kWh):**

**Step 1:** Calculate input power of old motor.

$$P_{in,old} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{old}} = \dfrac{75}{0.88}$$

$$P_{in,old} = 85.227 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate input power of new motor.

$$P_{in,new} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{new}} = \dfrac{75}{0.94}$$

$$P_{in,new} = 79.787 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate reduction in input power (power saved).

$$\Delta P = P_{in,old} - P_{in,new} = 85.227 - 79.787$$

$$\Delta P = 5.440 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by annual operating hours to get annual energy savings.

$$\Delta E = \Delta P \times t = 5.440 \times 5000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E = 27,200 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

### **Part (b) — Annual Cost Savings (₹):**

**Step 5:** Multiply energy saved by electricity rate.

$$S = \Delta E \times R = 27,200 \times 7.50$$

$$\boxed{S = ₹2,04,000 \text{ per year}}$$

### **Part (c) — Simple Payback Period:**

**Step 6:** Divide capital cost by annual savings.

$$SPP = \dfrac{C}{S} = \dfrac{3,50,000}{2,04,000}$$

$$SPP = 1.7157 \text{ years}$$

**Step 7:** Convert decimal years to months.

$$0.7157 \times 12 = 8.59 \text{ months} \approx 8.6 \text{ months}$$

$$\boxed{SPP \approx 1 \text{ year } 8.6 \text{ months}}$$

### **Part (d) — Return on Investment:**

**Step 8:** Apply ROI formula.

$$ROI = \dfrac{S}{C} \times 100 = \dfrac{2,04,000}{3,50,000} \times 100$$

$$ROI = 58.29\%$$

$$\boxed{ROI = 58.29\% \text{ per year}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A payback of less than 3 years and an ROI of nearly 60% is excellent. The motor replacement is **strongly recommended**.

---

## 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 — Net Present Value (NPV) with Time Value of Money

### **Problem Statement:**

> An industry is evaluating a proposed energy conservation project. The capital investment is **₹10,00,000**. The project is expected to generate uniform annual savings of **₹2,50,000** for **5 years**. The salvage value at the end of year 5 is **₹1,00,000**. The discount rate (hurdle rate) is **10% per annum**. Determine whether the project is financially viable by calculating the NPV.

### **Given Data:**
- Initial Investment, $C_0 = ₹10,00,000$
- Annual Savings, $S = ₹2,50,000$ (uniform for 5 years)
- Project Life, $n = 5$ years
- Salvage Value, $SV = ₹1,00,000$
- Discount Rate, $i = 10\% = 0.10$

### **Solution Strategy:**
We need to compute the present value of all future cash inflows (savings + salvage) and subtract the initial investment.

### **Step 1 — Compute the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF):**

The formula for USPWF is:

$$USPWF = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

**Substitute $i = 0.10$ and $n = 5$:**

$$USPWF = \dfrac{(1+0.10)^5 - 1}{0.10 \times (1+0.10)^5}$$

**Compute $(1.10)^5$ step-by-step:**

$$(1.10)^2 = 1.21$$

$$(1.10)^3 = 1.21 \times 1.10 = 1.331$$

$$(1.10)^4 = 1.331 \times 1.10 = 1.4641$$

$$(1.10)^5 = 1.4641 \times 1.10 = 1.61051$$

**Substitute back:**

$$USPWF = \dfrac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.61051} = \dfrac{0.61051}{0.161051}$$

$$USPWF = 3.7908$$

### **Step 2 — Present Value of Annual Savings:**

$$PV_{savings} = S \times USPWF = 2,50,000 \times 3.7908$$

$$PV_{savings} = ₹9,52,700$$

### **Step 3 — Present Value of Salvage Value:**

The salvage value is received at the end of year 5, so discount it once using the Single Payment Present Worth Factor (PWF).

$$PWF = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n} = \dfrac{1}{1.61051} = 0.6209$$

$$PV_{salvage} = SV \times PWF = 1,00,000 \times 0.6209$$

$$PV_{salvage} = ₹62,090$$

### **Step 4 — Total Present Value of Inflows:**

$$PV_{total} = PV_{savings} + PV_{salvage} = 9,52,700 + 62,090$$

$$PV_{total} = ₹10,14,790$$

### **Step 5 — Compute NPV:**

$$NPV = PV_{total} - C_0 = 10,14,790 - 10,00,000$$

$$\boxed{NPV = ₹14,790}$$

### **Decision Rule:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - If **NPV > 0** → Project is **ACCEPTED** (financially viable).
> - If **NPV < 0** → Project is **REJECTED**.
> - If **NPV = 0** → Indifferent; the project earns exactly the hurdle rate.

Since **NPV = ₹14,790 > 0**, the project is **financially acceptable**, though the margin is small.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is NPV positive only by a small amount?** Even though the simple annual saving looks good (₹2,50,000 × 5 = ₹12,50,000, which is more than ₹10,00,000), the time value of money reduces the actual present value. A larger NPV margin would be obtained with longer project life or lower discount rate.

---

## 3.3 EXAMPLE 3 — Power Factor Correction Numerical

### **Problem Statement:**

> A manufacturing plant has a **connected load of 500 kW** operating at a **lagging power factor of 0.75**. The electricity board tariff is:
> - Energy charge: **₹6.00 per kWh**
> - Demand charge: **₹300 per kVA per month**
> - Operating hours: **3000 hours/year**
>
> The plant installs a capacitor bank to improve the power factor to **0.95 lagging**.
> Calculate:
> (a) The kVAR rating of the capacitor bank required.
> (b) The reduction in kVA demand.
> (c) The annual savings in demand charge.
> (d) The simple payback period if the capacitor bank costs **₹4,00,000**.

### **Given Data:**
- Real Power, $P = 500$ kW
- Initial Power Factor, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.75$
- Target Power Factor, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$
- Energy charge, $R = ₹6.00$/kWh
- Demand charge, $D = ₹300$/kVA/month
- Operating hours, $t = 3000$ h/year
- Capacitor cost, $C = ₹4,00,000$

### **Part (a) — kVAR Required:**

**Step 1:** Find $\tan\phi_1$ for the initial power factor.

$$\cos\phi_1 = 0.75 \implies \phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41°$$

$$\tan\phi_1 = \tan(41.41°) = 0.8819$$

**Step 2:** Find $\tan\phi_2$ for the target power factor.

$$\cos\phi_2 = 0.95 \implies \phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$$

$$\tan\phi_2 = \tan(18.19°) = 0.3287$$

**Step 3:** Apply the kVAR formula.

$$Q_c = P (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$$

$$Q_c = 500 \times (0.8819 - 0.3287)$$

$$Q_c = 500 \times 0.5532$$

$$\boxed{Q_c = 276.6 \text{ kVAR}}$$

### **Part (b) — Reduction in kVA Demand:**

**Step 4:** Compute initial kVA demand.

$$S_1 = \dfrac{P}{\cos\phi_1} = \dfrac{500}{0.75} = 666.67 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 5:** Compute kVA demand after correction.

$$S_2 = \dfrac{P}{\cos\phi_2} = \dfrac{500}{0.95} = 526.32 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the reduction.

$$\Delta S = S_1 - S_2 = 666.67 - 526.32$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = 140.35 \text{ kVA}}$$

### **Part (c) — Annual Demand Charge Savings:**

**Step 7:** Calculate monthly savings.

$$\text{Monthly Savings} = \Delta S \times D = 140.35 \times 300 = ₹42,105 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 8:** Calculate annual savings.

$$\text{Annual Savings} = 42,105 \times 12 = ₹5,05,260 \text{ per year}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Demand Savings} = ₹5,05,260}$$

*(Note: Additional savings on cable and transformer losses also occur but are ignored here unless stated.)*

### **Part (d) — Simple Payback Period:**

$$SPP = \dfrac{C}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \dfrac{4,00,000}{5,05,260}$$

$$SPP = 0.7918 \text{ years}$$

$$0.7918 \times 12 = 9.5 \text{ months}$$

$$\boxed{SPP \approx 9.5 \text{ months}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Improving power factor from 0.75 to 0.95 not only avoids utility penalties but also **frees up transformer and cable capacity** (140.35 kVA) for additional load — a major operational benefit.

---

## 3.4 EXAMPLE 4 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR) by Interpolation

### **Problem Statement:**

> A company invests **₹5,00,000** in an energy conservation project. The project generates the following cash inflows (savings) over 5 years:

| **Year** | **Cash Inflow (₹)** |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1,50,000 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 |
| 4 | 2,20,000 |
| 5 | 2,50,000 |

> Calculate the IRR using the interpolation method.

### **Solution Approach:**

We need to find two discount rates — a **lower rate ($i_L$)** at which NPV is **positive**, and a **higher rate ($i_H$)** at which NPV is **negative**. Then interpolate.

### **Step 1 — Try a lower discount rate $i_L = 10\%$ and compute NPV:**

$$NPV_L = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1.10)^t}$$

**Compute each year's present value:**

| **Year ($t$)** | **CF ($₹$)** | $(1.10)^t$ | **PV = CF / $(1.10)^t$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 1.1000 | 1,36,363.64 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 1.2100 | 1,48,760.33 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 1.3310 | 1,50,262.96 |
| 4 | 2,20,000 | 1.4641 | 1,50,262.00 |
| 5 | 2,50,000 | 1.6105 | 1,55,231.30 |

**Sum of PVs:**

$$PV_{total} = 1,36,363.64 + 1,48,760.33 + 1,50,262.96 + 1,50,262.00 + 1,55,231.30$$

$$PV_{total} = 7,40,880.23$$

**NPV at 10%:**

$$NPV_L = 7,40,880.23 - 5,00,000 = +₹2,40,880.23$$

NPV is **positive** at 10%, so 10% is below IRR. We need a higher rate.

### **Step 2 — Try a higher discount rate $i_H = 25\%$:**

| **Year ($t$)** | **CF ($₹$)** | $(1.25)^t$ | **PV = CF / $(1.25)^t$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 1.2500 | 1,20,000.00 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 1.5625 | 1,15,200.00 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 1.9531 | 1,02,400.00 |
| 4 | 2,20,000 | 2.4414 | 90,112.00 |
| 5 | 2,50,000 | 3.0518 | 81,920.00 |

**Sum of PVs:**

$$PV_{total} = 1,20,000 + 1,15,200 + 1,02,400 + 90,112 + 81,920$$

$$PV_{total} = 5,09,632.00$$

**NPV at 25%:**

$$NPV_H = 5,09,632.00 - 5,00,000 = +₹9,632.00$$

Still positive, but very small. Try 26%.

### **Step 3 — Try $i_H = 26\%$:**

| **Year ($t$)** | **CF ($₹$)** | $(1.26)^t$ | **PV** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 1.2600 | 1,19,047.62 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 1.5876 | 1,13,378.91 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 2.0004 | 99,962.40 |
| 4 | 2,20,000 | 2.5205 | 87,286.39 |
| 5 | 2,50,000 | 3.1758 | 78,720.95 |

**Sum of PVs:**

$$PV_{total} = 4,98,396.27$$

**NPV at 26%:**

$$NPV_{26} = 4,98,396.27 - 5,00,000 = -₹1,603.73$$

NPV is **negative** at 26%. So the IRR lies between **25% and 26%**.

### **Step 4 — Interpolate between 25% (NPV = +9,632) and 26% (NPV = −1,604):**

$$IRR = i_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H}(i_H - i_L)$$

$$IRR = 25 + \dfrac{9632}{9632 - (-1603)} \times (26 - 25)$$

$$IRR = 25 + \dfrac{9632}{11235} \times 1$$

$$IRR = 25 + 0.8574$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 25.86\%}$$

### **Decision Rule:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - If **IRR > MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** → **ACCEPT** project.
> - If **IRR < MARR** → **REJECT** project.
>
> Assuming MARR = 15%, this project is **highly acceptable** since 25.86% > 15%.

---

## 3.5 EXAMPLE 5 — Comprehensive Integrated Energy Audit Problem

### **Problem Statement:**

> An industry operates for **6000 hours per year** on a **400 V, 3-phase** supply. The average load draws a line current of **250 A** at a power factor of **0.80 lagging**. The electricity tariff is:
> - Energy charge: **₹5.50 per kWh**
> - Power factor penalty: **2% of energy bill for every 0.01 fall below 0.90 PF**
>
> A proposal is made to install a capacitor bank of **120 kVAR** at a cost of **₹2,40,000** (life = 10 years, salvage = nil). The bank will improve PF to 0.95. Discount rate = 12%. Determine:
> (a) Initial kW, kVA, kVAR.
> (b) New kW, kVA, kVAR after correction.
> (c) Reduction in kVA demand.
> (d) Annual energy cost savings (assume kW remains same).
> (e) Annual PF penalty saved.
> (f) Total annual savings.
> (g) NPV of the project.

### **Given Data:**
- $V_L = 400$ V, $I_L = 250$ A, 3-phase
- $\cos\phi_1 = 0.80$, target $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$
- $t = 6000$ h/year, $R = ₹5.50$/kWh
- $C_0 = ₹2,40,000$, $n = 10$ years, $i = 12\%$, $SV = 0$

### **Part (a) — Initial Power Quantities:**

**Step 1:** Apparent power (3-phase formula).

$$S_1 = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 250$$

$$S_1 = 1.732 \times 400 \times 250 = 1,73,200 \text{ VA}$$

$$S_1 = 173.2 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2:** Real power.

$$P_1 = S_1 \times \cos\phi_1 = 173.2 \times 0.80$$

$$P_1 = 138.56 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3:** Reactive power.

$$Q_1 = S_1 \times \sin\phi_1 = 173.2 \times 0.60$$

$$Q_1 = 103.92 \text{ kVAR}$$

*(Note: $\sin\phi_1 = \sqrt{1 - 0.80^2} = 0.60$)*

### **Part (b) — New Power Quantities After Correction:**

**Step 4:** Real power is unchanged (capacitors do not consume real power).

$$P_2 = P_1 = 138.56 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** New reactive power.

$$Q_2 = Q_1 - Q_c = 103.92 - 120 = -16.08 \text{ kVAR}$$

*(Negative sign indicates net capacitive — slight leading PF, which is fine; kVAR rating can be slightly adjusted for exact 0.95.)*

**Step 6:** New apparent power.

$$S_2 = \sqrt{P_2^2 + Q_2^2} = \sqrt{138.56^2 + (-16.08)^2}$$

$$S_2 = \sqrt{19199 + 258.6} = \sqrt{19457.6}$$

$$S_2 = 139.49 \text{ kVA}$$

### **Part (c) — Reduction in kVA:**

$$\Delta S = S_1 - S_2 = 173.2 - 139.49 = 33.71 \text{ kVA}$$

### **Part (d) — Annual Energy Cost Savings (from reduced kWh, but real power unchanged):**

Since $P$ is unchanged, the **kWh energy consumption does not change**. Savings come from **demand charge reduction** and **penalty avoidance**.

> [!NOTE]
> In this problem, the energy bill (in kWh) is the **same** before and after correction. The savings are in the **kVA-based demand charge** and **PF penalty**. We will compute these in parts (e) and (f) below.

### **Part (e) — Annual PF Penalty Saved:**

**Step 7:** Initial PF = 0.80; Target PF = 0.90 (penalty threshold).

$$\text{PF drop} = 0.90 - 0.80 = 0.10$$

**Step 8:** Penalty units = 0.10 / 0.01 = **10 units**.

**Step 9:** Penalty % = 10 × 2% = **20% of energy bill**.

**Step 10:** Annual energy bill (initial).

$$\text{Energy Bill} = P_1 \times t \times R = 138.56 \times 6000 \times 5.50$$

$$\text{Energy Bill} = 45,72,480 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 11:** Penalty saved per year (if PF improved to ≥ 0.90).

$$\text{Penalty Saved} = 0.20 \times 45,72,480 = ₹9,14,496 \text{ per year}$$

After correction, the plant PF is 0.95, well above the 0.90 threshold, so **no penalty is charged**.

### **Part (f) — Total Annual Savings:**

$$\boxed{S_{annual} = ₹9,14,496 \text{ per year}}$$

*(Plus any demand charge savings if applicable; not specified here, so we restrict to penalty saving.)*

### **Part (g) — NPV of the Project:**

**Step 12:** Compute USPWF for $n = 10$, $i = 12\%$.

$$USPWF = \dfrac{(1.12)^{10} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{10}}$$

**Compute $(1.12)^{10}$:**

$$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$$

$$(1.12)^4 = 1.2544^2 = 1.5735$$

$$(1.12)^5 = 1.5735 \times 1.12 = 1.7623$$

$$(1.12)^{10} = 1.7623^2 = 3.1058$$

**Substitute:**

$$USPWF = \dfrac{3.1058 - 1}{0.12 \times 3.1058} = \dfrac{2.1058}{0.3727} = 5.6502$$

**Step 13:** PV of annual savings.

$$PV_{savings} = 9,14,496 \times 5.6502 = ₹51,67,200 \text{ (approx.)}$$

**Step 14:** NPV.

$$NPV = PV_{savings} - C_0 = 51,67,200 - 2,40,000$$

$$\boxed{NPV = ₹49,27,200}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The project yields a very high positive NPV, indicating **excellent financial viability**. The simple payback (ignoring discount) would be ₹2,40,000 / ₹9,14,496 ≈ **0.26 years (3.1 months)**.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Summary of Examples)

| **Example** | **Method Used** | **Key Result** | **Decision** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | SPP & ROI | SPP = 1.71 years; ROI = 58.29% | Accept |
| 2 | NPV | NPV = +₹14,790 (marginal) | Accept (borderline) |
| 3 | PF Correction | SPP = 9.5 months; kVAR = 276.6 | Strongly Accept |
| 4 | IRR | IRR = 25.86% (MARR = 15%) | Strongly Accept |
| 5 | Integrated NPV | NPV = +₹49,27,200 | Strongly Accept |

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Financial Appraisal Method

This Mermaid diagram shows how an energy auditor selects the appropriate financial evaluation technique based on project characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: Energy Audit ECM Identified] --> node2{Is the Time Value of Money Significant?}
    node2 -- No / Very Short Project --> node3[Use Simple Methods]
    node2 -- Yes / Multi-Year Project --> node4[Use Discounted Cash Flow Methods]
    node3 --> node5{Goal: Quick Breakeven Check?}
    node5 -- Yes --> node6[Simple Payback Period SPP]
    node5 -- No --> node7[Return on Investment ROI]
    node4 --> node8{Goal: Absolute Profitability?}
    node8 -- Yes --> node9[Net Present Value NPV]
    node8 -- No, Need Rate of Return --> node10[Internal Rate of Return IRR]
    node6 --> node11{SPP less than 3 years?}
    node7 --> node12{ROI greater than MARR?}
    node9 --> node13{NPV greater than 0?}
    node10 --> node14{IRR greater than MARR?}
    node11 -- Yes --> node15[Accept ECM]
    node11 -- No --> node16[Reject or Re-evaluate]
    node12 -- Yes --> node15
    node12 -- No --> node16
    node13 -- Yes --> node15
    node13 -- No --> node16
    node14 -- Yes --> node15
    node14 -- No --> node16
    node15 --> node17[Implement Project]
    node16 --> node18[Reject Project]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Power Factor Correction System

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[AC Source 400V 3-phase] --> nodeB[Industrial Load Motors and Heaters]
    nodeB -- Current I at PF 0.80 --> nodeC[Apparent Power S1 kVA]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Capacitor Bank Qc kVAR]
    nodeD -- Net Reactive Reduced --> nodeE[Improved Apparent Power S2 kVA]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Electricity Board Metering]
    nodeF -- If PF 0.90 --> nodeG[No Penalty]
    nodeF -- If PF less than 0.90 --> nodeH[PF Penalty Charged]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Reduced Energy Bill]

```

## 4.3 Process Diagram — NPV Calculation Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Identify Initial Investment C0] --> stepB[Step 2: List Annual Cash Inflows CF1 to CFn]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Choose Discount Rate i percent]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Calculate Present Worth Factor for each year]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Multiply CF by PWF for PV of each year]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Sum all PVs]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Subtract Initial Investment]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Obtain NPV]
    stepH --> stepI{NPV greater than 0?}
    stepI -- Yes --> stepJ[Project ACCEPTED]
    stepI -- No --> stepK[Project REJECTED]
```

## 4.4 Power Triangle Diagram (ASCII Schematic for Concept Visualization)

```
                          S (kVA)  ← Hypotenuse
                         /|
                        / |
                       /  | Q (kVAR)  ← Vertical
                      /   |
                     /    |
                    /_____|
                   P (kW)  ← Horizontal (Base)

    cos φ = P / S    tan φ = Q / P    sin φ = Q / S
```

When a capacitor is added, **Q decreases**, hence **S decreases** (for the same P), and the power factor **cos φ improves**.
