#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "AT&C loss" in the context of a distribution utility. Write the formula used to compute it.**

**Model Answer:**

AT&C (Aggregate Technical and Commercial) loss is a comprehensive metric used by Indian power distribution utilities (DISCOMs) to capture the total loss in the distribution system, combining both engineering losses and commercial inefficiencies. It is expressed as a percentage of total energy input.

The formula is:

$$\% \text{AT\&C Loss} = \left(1 - \frac{E_{\text{billed}} \times \text{Billing Realisation}}{E_{\text{input}}}\right) \times 100$$

where $E_{\text{billed}}$ is the total energy billed (kWh), Billing Realisation is the fraction of billed amount actually collected (in per unit or %), and $E_{\text{input}}$ is the total energy injected into the distribution system (kWh).

A reduction in AT&C loss directly improves the financial health of the utility and is the primary Key Performance Indicator (KPI) tracked by regulatory commissions such as KSERC, CERC, and SERCs.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Technical Losses and Commercial Losses in a distribution system. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Technical Losses | Commercial Losses |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Energy lost due to physical phenomena in electrical equipment and conductors | Energy lost due to administrative, managerial, and theft-related reasons |
| **Cause** | Heat dissipation, magnetic effects, dielectric stress | Theft, faulty meters, billing errors, unmetered supply |
| **Quantifiable** | Yes, through engineering calculations ($I^2R$, etc.) | Indirectly, by difference method |
| **Examples** | (i) $I^2R$ losses in LT feeders; (ii) Transformer core (no-load) losses | (i) Illegal tapping; (ii) Slow/erroneous meters |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks with a typical internal choice. Each sub-part is generally 7 marks. We provide TWO alternative 14-mark questions below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various components of technical losses in a distribution system. Discuss the role of power factor correction in reducing these losses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 11 kV, 8 km long distribution feeder has a resistance of 0.25 Ω/km per phase. The feeder supplies a balanced load of 1500 kW at 0.75 power factor (lagging). Calculate:
   (i) The total $I^2R$ loss in the feeder.
   (ii) The loss if the power factor is improved to 0.95 lagging.
   (iii) The annual energy saved (in kWh) and monetary savings (in ₹) at ₹7.50 per kWh, assuming 8000 operating hours per year. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The technical losses in a distribution system comprise the following components:

1. **Feeder $I^2R$ Losses:** Heat generated when current flows through conductor resistance. Proportional to $I^2$. Major contributor — typically 50–60% of total technical loss. Worst affected: long LT feeders with low voltage (415 V).
2. **Transformer Losses:**
   - *No-Load (Core) Losses:* Hysteresis + Eddy current losses; occur 24×7; depend on voltage, not load.
   - *Load (Copper) Losses:* $I^2R$ in windings; vary with load squared.
3. **Dielectric Losses:** In cable insulation; proportional to $V^2$ and frequency.
4. **Corona Losses:** Ionization around overhead conductors at high voltage; worse in rain/humidity.
5. **Junction and Switchgear Losses:** Loose/corroded contacts causing hot spots.
6. **Neutral Losses:** Unbalanced loads cause neutral current, adding to total loss.

**Role of Power Factor Correction:**

When the power factor is improved from $\cos\phi_1$ to $\cos\phi_2$ using shunt capacitors, the reactive component of current is reduced, lowering the total line current. Since losses are $I^2R$, even a small reduction in current gives significant loss reduction.

$$I_{\text{new}} = I_{\text{old}} \times \frac{\cos\phi_{\text{old}}}{\cos\phi_{\text{new}}}$$

**Numerical example:** Improving PF from 0.7 to 0.95 reduces current to 73.7%, reducing $I^2R$ loss to **54.3%** of original — a **45.7% reduction in copper loss.** This also releases transformer and feeder capacity, defers capital investment, and improves voltage regulation.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $V_L = 11 \text{ kV}$, Length = 8 km, $R = 0.25 \times 8 = 2.0 \, \Omega$ per phase, $P = 1500 \text{ kW}$, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.75$, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$.

**(i) Original Loss:**

$$I_1 = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000 \times 0.75} = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{14{,}290.2} \approx 104.97 \, \text{A}$$

$$P_{L1} = 3 \times (104.97)^2 \times 2.0 = 3 \times 11{,}019.0 \times 2.0 \approx 66{,}114 \, \text{W} \approx 66.11 \, \text{kW}$$

**(ii) Loss After PF Correction:**

$$I_2 = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000 \times 0.95} = \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{18{,}101.6} \approx 82.86 \, \text{A}$$

$$P_{L2} = 3 \times (82.86)^2 \times 2.0 = 3 \times 6{,}865.8 \times 2.0 \approx 41{,}195 \, \text{W} \approx 41.20 \, \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Annual Savings:**

$$\Delta P_L = 66.11 - 41.20 = 24.91 \, \text{kW}$$

$$\text{Energy saved} = 24.91 \times 8000 = 199{,}280 \, \text{kWh/year}$$

$$\text{Monetary saving} = 199{,}280 \times 7.50 = ₹14{,}94{,}600 \text{ per year} \approx ₹14.95 \, \text{lakh/year}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various categories of distribution losses. Discuss in detail the methods to reduce non-technical (commercial) losses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 415 V LT feeder of 1.2 km length supplies a balanced load of 100 A per phase. The conductor resistance is 0.05 Ω per phase per km.
   (i) Calculate the $I^2R$ loss in the feeder.
   (ii) If the same load is supplied through an 11 kV HT feeder (stepped down at the consumer end) with total HT line resistance of 0.5 Ω per phase, calculate the new loss and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Block Diagram of Distribution Loss Categories:**

(Refer to Section 4.1 above for the hierarchical flow diagram.)

**Categories:**
- **Technical Losses** — $I^2R$ in feeders, transformer losses, dielectric, corona, switchgear.
- **Non-Technical Losses** — Theft, meter tampering, billing errors, unmetered supply, collection inefficiency.

**Methods to Reduce Non-Technical Losses:**

1. **Energy Audit and Consumer Indexing:** Identify high-loss areas, tag and survey every consumer.
2. **Meter Replacement and Tamper-Proof Meters:** Replace old electromechanical meters with tamper-proof electronic/static meters; deploy prepaid meters.
3. **100% Metering:** Even government connections, agricultural pumps, and streetlights must be metered and billed.
4. **Aerial Bunched Cables (ABC):** Prevent direct tapping from bare conductors.
5. **Strict Enforcement and Legal Action:** Disconnection drives, special courts (e.g., under Electricity Act 2003, Section 135 for theft).
6. **Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI):** Real-time monitoring, tamper alerts, remote disconnect.
7. **High Voltage Distribution System (HVDS):** Reduces LT exposure, thereby reducing theft opportunities.
8. **Public Awareness Campaigns:** Encourage whistle-blowers via rewards.
9. **GIS Mapping:** Overlay consumer database on network maps to detect unauthorized loads.
10. **Computerized Billing with Audits:** Detect and correct anomalies in meter reading, billing, and collection.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** LT case: $V = 415 \text{ V}$, Length = 1.2 km, $R_{\text{cond}} = 0.05 \times 1.2 = 0.06 \, \Omega$/phase, $I = 100 \, \text{A}$.

**(i) Loss in LT Feeder:**

$$P_{L,\text{LT}} = 3 I^2 R = 3 \times (100)^2 \times 0.06 = 3 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.06 = 1800 \, \text{W} = 1.8 \, \text{kW}$$

**(ii) Loss When Supplied via HT Feeder (11 kV):**

For the same active power, current in HT line is:

$$I_{\text{HT}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 100}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000} = \frac{415 \times 100}{11000} \approx 3.77 \, \text{A}$$

$$P_{L,\text{HT}} = 3 \times (3.77)^2 \times 0.5 = 3 \times 14.21 \times 0.5 \approx 21.32 \, \text{W} = 0.0213 \, \text{kW}$$

**Comment:**

The HT feeder loss is roughly **85 times smaller** than the LT feeder loss for the same power delivered. This dramatic reduction mathematically demonstrates the KTU 2024 syllabus principle: **higher distribution voltage → smaller current → drastically lower $I^2R$ losses**. This is the engineering basis for KSEB's and other Indian utilities' ongoing shift from LT (415 V) to HT (11 kV) distribution at the consumer end, especially in rural and agricultural feeder networks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Distribution Loss Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3:** In a balanced 3-phase system, line loss = $3 I^2 R$, NOT $I^2 R$. Students often write $I^2R$ and lose **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Using phase voltage instead of line voltage:** $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{\text{ph}}$. Substituting 415 V (line) where phase voltage is required (or vice versa) is a frequent error.
> 3. **Mixing up % loss and absolute loss:** The question may ask for both; the % is with respect to load (kW), not to input.
> 4. **Neglecting units in final answer:** Always state kW, kWh, %, or ₹. Missing units can cost **0.5 mark per sub-part**.
> 5. **No block diagram in 7-mark theory answers:** KTU examiners expect a **neat labeled diagram** for full marks in any "explain" or "describe" question. Omitting it costs **1 to 2 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing AT&C Loss with Technical Loss:** AT&C includes both technical and commercial — students often define AT&C as if it were only technical loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Distribution Loss** = Energy Input − Energy Billed (or Collected, for AT&C).
- Two main classes: **Technical (engineering)** and **Non-Technical (commercial)**.
- **$I^2R$ loss** is the dominant technical loss; proportional to $I^2$ and $R$.
- **3-phase line loss formula:** $P_L = 3 I^2 R = \frac{(P^2 + Q^2) R}{V_L^2}$
- **Power factor improvement** is the most cost-effective loss reduction technique — improving PF from 0.7 to 0.95 reduces $I^2R$ loss by **~45%**.
- **HT vs LT loss ratio:** For the same power, a 11 kV feeder loses about **1/700th** of the loss of a 415 V LT feeder.
- **Transformer no-load losses** occur 24×7; **load losses** are load-dependent.
- **AT&C Loss** = the utility's most-watched KPI; combines technical + commercial + collection efficiency.
- **Common technical loss reduction methods:** Reconductoring, capacitor banks, HVDS, ABC cables, load balancing, transformer right-sizing.
- **Common commercial loss reduction methods:** Tamper-proof meters, AMI, energy audits, strict enforcement, 100% metering, public awareness.
- **Voltage regulation** improves (line drop decreases) when losses are reduced.
- **Energy Conservation Act 2001** mandates energy audits for large consumers and loss analysis for DISCOMs.
- **Kerala-specific context:** KSEB Ltd. has been progressively reducing AT&C loss from >30% (early 2000s) to ~12–13% (2024) through HVDS, ABC, and smart metering programs — a real-world case study relevant to KTU projects.
- **Formula to memorize for Part A:** $P_L = 3 I^2 R$ and the AT&C formula.
- **Diagram to memorize for Part B:** Block diagram of a distribution feeder with all loss points marked (Section 4.2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Trapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steam Trapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Steam Trap (per KTU / IS 13932:1994)**
> A **steam trap** is an automatic, self-actuating valve that distinguishes between **steam** and **condensate** (and/or non-condensable gases), and discharges the condensate plus associated non-condensables while preventing the live, useful steam from escaping. It operates without any external source of power, relying solely on thermodynamic and/or mechanical properties of the fluid (density, temperature, velocity, phase change).

In an Energy Management & Auditing (EM&A) context, a steam trap is a **critical control device** sitting on the periphery of every steam-consuming equipment (heat exchangers, reboilers, tracing lines, jacketed vessels, dryers, autoclaves). Its correct functioning is the single largest factor determining the **steam system efficiency** in industries such as paper, textiles, food processing, refineries, and chemical plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Energy Auditor is expected to identify steam leaks (including defective traps), quantify losses in **kg of steam/hour** and **Rs/year**, and recommend replacement / maintenance. The steam trap is therefore a **"silent energy waster"** — a failed trap can waste **30–50 %** of the steam passing through a header.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (First-Time Student Friendly)

Imagine a **staircase between two floors** in a building:

- The **upper floor** is a hot steam main (high-pressure, high-temperature).
- The **lower floor** is a return condensate line (lower pressure, lower temperature).
- People coming **down** (condensate) should walk down freely.
- People trying to go **up** (live steam) should be **stopped** at the staircase entrance.

A steam trap is exactly that "bouncer" at the staircase entrance. It only allows **condensate (cool, dense water)** to pass downward to the boiler/feed line, while holding back **steam (light, energetic vapour)** so that all of its **latent heat of vaporization** (≈ **2257 kJ/kg** at atmospheric pressure) can be utilized in the heat exchanger above it.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "DENSITY / TEMPERATURE / VELOCITY"** — the three physical properties that all steam traps exploit:
>
> 1. **Density** (steam ≪ water) → Mechanical traps (float, bucket, float-thermostatic)
> 2. **Temperature** (steam > saturated T at that P) → Thermostatic traps (bellows, bimetallic, expansion)
> 3. **Velocity / Momentum** (high-velocity steam vs. low-velocity condensate) → Thermodynamic traps (disc, impulse)

---

## 1.3 Why Steam Traps Matter in Energy Auditing — Quick Numbers

| Failed Trap Symptom | Typical Loss Rate | Annual Cost Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Trap stuck **OPEN** (blowing) | 5–80 kg/h steam | **₹ 2–15 lakh/year** per trap |
| Trap stuck **CLOSED** (choked) | 0 kg/h steam, but **waterlogging**, hammering, process loss | Equipment damage + batch rejection |
| Trap **leaking internally** | 1–10 kg/h | 0.5–5 lakh/year per trap |
| Trap **cycling fast** | 2–5 kg/h | 1–3 lakh/year per trap |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** An auditor must be able to differentiate **"blowing steam"** (visible white plume — trap failure) from **"flash steam"** (invisible until it contacts cold air — normal condensate discharge). Mixing these two is a guaranteed mark-loser in viva.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Steam Trap — The Universal Logic

Every steam trap, irrespective of its type, must satisfy **three** fundamental functional requirements:

1. **Open** when condensate is present (and ideally, when non-condensables like air, CO₂, N₂ are also present at start-up).
2. **Close** tightly when live steam arrives (zero steam leakage).
3. **Discharge** the condensate at a pressure not less than the downstream return-line pressure, or, where a back-pressure exists, discharge against it.

The decision to open/close is governed by one (or a combination) of the following physical drivers:

| Driver | Property Exploited | Trap Family |
|---|---|---|
| Buoyancy of float | Density difference | **Mechanical** (Float & Thermostatic, Open Float) |
| Inverted bucket weight | Density + vapour pressure | **Mechanical** (Inverted Bucket) |
| Bimetallic / wax element | Thermal expansion | **Thermostatic** |
| Bellows with volatile fluid | Saturated temperature | **Thermostatic** |
| Disc under momentum | Bernoulli / pressure dynamics | **Thermodynamic** |
| Orifice + impulse line | Pressure pulse from condensate | **Thermodynamic** (Impulse) |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Steam Traps (Module 3 — High Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Steam Traps (Density-Operated)

#### (a) **Float & Thermostatic (F&T) Trap** — Most versatile, highest discharge capacity

**Construction:**
- A **float chamber** houses a hollow stainless-steel float.
- The float is connected through a lever/pivot to a **valve** at the top outlet.
- A separate **bimetallic air vent (thermostatic element)** bleeds air and non-condensable gases at start-up.

**Operation:**
- As condensate enters, the float rises in direct proportion to the **liquid level**.
- When the level reaches a pre-set height, the valve is lifted, discharging condensate.
- The valve closes when the level drops (i.e., when live steam reaches the trap inlet).
- The bimetallic air vent opens below saturated steam temperature (typically **5–10 °C below** saturation T) to purge air, and closes sharply once saturated steam arrives.

**Features:**
- **Continuous discharge** modulated by condensate rate.
- Handles wide load variations (0–100 % of capacity).
- Best suited for **process heating** where condensate load varies widely.
- Not suitable for superheated steam mains.

#### (b) **Inverted Bucket (IB) Trap**

**Construction:**
- An **inverted hollow bucket** is pivoted at its top end, connected to a valve at the top.
- The bucket is immersed in condensate inside the trap body.

**Operation:**
- On start-up, the bucket falls down (because it is full of air) → valve **opens** → air + condensate discharge.
- As steam enters the trap, it gets **trapped under the bucket**, making the bucket buoyant → it rises → valve **closes**.
- Condensate continues to accumulate beneath the bucket, eventually overflowing the bucket's lower rim.
- The steam trapped inside the bucket condenses → bucket loses buoyancy → drops → valve re-opens.

**Features:**
- **Intermittent discharge.**
- Robust construction, suitable for high-pressure (up to **30 bar**) and superheated steam.
- A small **vent hole (~3 mm)** at the top of the bucket releases air, otherwise the trap would be air-bound.
- Discharge is **proportional to the rate of steam condensation** under the bucket.

#### (c) **Open Float / Free Float Trap (Bell-shaped)**

- Condensate lifts a hollow bell-shaped float; the float's stem operates the discharge valve.
- No thermostatic air vent — not ideal for systems with high air content.
- Simpler and cheaper than F&T, but rarely used in modern plants.

---

### 2.2.2 Thermostatic Steam Traps (Temperature-Operated)

#### (a) **Bimetallic (Expansion) Trap**

**Construction:**
- Multiple **bimetallic strips** (two metals of different coefficients of thermal expansion, e.g., Invar and brass) are stacked. Cooler metals form the inner layer, hotter metals the outer.

**Operation:**
- When condensate (cool) sits on the stack, strips are **contracted/closed** → valve **open** → condensate discharges.
- When steam arrives, strips **heat up**, the higher-expansion metal expands more, deforming the stack → stack pushes against the valve seat → valve **closes**.
- On cooling, the strips return to original shape → valve re-opens.

**Features:**
- **Discharge is sub-cooled** (typically **5–30 °C below saturation**) — useful where condensate must be cooled before return to avoid water hammer in return lines.
- Excellent for **steam tracing lines** and **drip legs** on long steam mains.
- Resistant to water hammer and superheat up to **+250 °C superheat**.

#### (b) **Balanced Pressure (Bellows) Trap**

**Construction:**
- A **sealed bellows** filled with a volatile fluid (water + alcohol / water + acetone) with a boiling point slightly **below** the saturation temperature of the steam.
- The bellows is connected to a valve at the top.

**Operation:**
- When the trap is cold, fluid is in liquid form → bellows is **collapsed** → valve **open**.
- As hot condensate/saturated steam passes, the volatile fluid **boils and vaporises** → bellows **expands** → valve **closes** sharply at saturation temperature.
- When condensate cools slightly below saturation, fluid condenses back → valve re-opens.

**Features:**
- **Sub-cooled discharge** (typically **5–10 °C** below saturation) — protects return lines.
- Compact, lightweight, ideal for **HVAC** and small tracing lines.
- Cannot tolerate water hammer or superheat.

#### (c) **Liquid Expansion (Wax-Type) Trap**

- Uses **paraffin wax** sealed in a copper cell; wax has a high volumetric expansion on melting.
- When steam arrives, wax melts → pushes a piston → closes the valve.
- Robust, used in petrochemical tracing where temperatures are stable.

---

### 2.2.3 Thermodynamic Steam Traps (Velocity / Momentum-Operated)

#### (a) **Disc (Momentum) Trap — Most Common in Industries**

**Construction:**
- A **flat stainless steel disc** sits on a circular seat inside a chamber.
- A small control chamber sits above the disc, connected to the inlet by a small **control orifice** (or "leak orifice").

**Operation (Cycle — 4 stages):**

1. **Steam entry:** Live steam enters below the disc at high velocity → static pressure **lifts** the disc against the seat → disc lifts → steam rushes through the **outer annular gap** (the "throat"). This is the **"OPEN"** position.
2. **Pressure equalisation:** A small amount of steam leaks through the disc seating lip into the **control chamber** above the disc.
3. **Disc closure:** Pressure in control chamber **rises**, and the velocity of steam through the annular gap **drops** (per Bernoulli). The combination of (a) rising chamber pressure, and (b) loss of dynamic lift, pushes the disc back onto the seat — **CLOSED**.
4. **Condensate collection:** Condensate below the disc cannot lift it (lower density / lower velocity). Condensate accumulates, **reducing** the chamber pressure through the control orifice, and when the condensate head is high enough, the disc is pushed **up again** and a fresh cycle starts.

**Features:**
- **Intermittent discharge** (cycles typically **1–30 times per minute**).
- Compact, light, very high temperature capability (up to **+600 °C superheat**).
- Suitable for **high-pressure mains, drips, tracing**.
- **Disadvantages:** Wear of the disc/seat due to cyclic impact, can blow live steam if seat damaged.

#### (b) **Impulse (Piston) Trap**

- A **piston** in a cylinder is connected to a small **impulse line** sensing inlet pressure.
- Cold condensate cools the piston chamber → piston **moves up** → main valve **opens** → discharge.
- Hot steam heats the chamber → volatile fluid vaporises → piston **moves down** → valve **closes**.
- Used in **compressors, turbines, sterilizers**.

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every formula below is examinable in KTU 2024 ESE — Part A (3-mark) definitions, Part B (14-mark) numericals on energy losses, and lab viva.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning / Engineering Use | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot \lambda$ | Heat delivered by steam = mass × **latent heat of vaporisation** | W or kW |
| 2 | $\lambda \approx 2257 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ (at 1 atm) | Latent heat of steam at **100 °C** saturation | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $\lambda \approx 2086 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ (at 8 bar) | At typical plant pressure (8 bar saturated ≈ 170 °C) | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $m_{leak} = A \cdot \rho_{steam} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho}$ | Orifice flow equation for a leaking/failed trap | kg/s |
| 5 | $E_{loss} = m_{leak} \cdot \lambda \cdot t$ | Annual energy loss due to a single failed trap | kJ/yr (convert to MWh or kWh for billing) |
| 6 | $C_{annual} = m_{leak} \cdot S \cdot 8760$ | Annual steam cost = leak rate × steam tariff × hours/yr | ₹/year |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{C_{fuel} \cdot \eta_{b}}{h_{fg}}$ | Specific steam cost (₹/kg) given fuel cost, boiler efficiency, and latent heat | ₹/kg |
| 8 | $\text{Saving} = N_{failed} \cdot m_{trap} \cdot \lambda \cdot t$ | Savings from replacing N failed traps | kWh or ₹ |
| 9 | $1 \, \text{kWh} = 3600 \, \text{kJ}$ | Conversion constant (essential for energy-audit calculations) | — |
| 10 | $\text{Boiler efficiency} \eta_b = \dfrac{m_s \cdot (h_g - h_f)}{m_{fuel} \cdot GCV}$ | Direct method of boiler efficiency used in steam audit | dimensionless |
| 11 | $h_{fg} = 2501 - 2.42 \cdot T_{sat}$ | Approximate latent heat equation for water (valid 100–200 °C) | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $\Delta P = P_{inlet} - P_{back}$ | Differential pressure across the trap, governs discharge capacity | bar or kPa |
| 13 | $Q_{cap} = A_{orifice} \cdot K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Trap capacity sizing (manufacturer's discharge curves) | kg/h |
| 14 | $\text{Thermodynamic Cycle Ratio} = \dfrac{P_{inlet}}{P_{outlet}}$ | Disc trap cycles faster at high P ratio (this is why disc traps "chatter") | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** If a numerical says "a trap blows 1.5 kg of steam/hour at 7 bar," remember to use $\lambda$ at 7 bar saturation ≈ **2086 kJ/kg**, not 2257 kJ/kg. Many students lose 2–3 marks by using 100 °C latent heat for all problems.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Each Trap Type

| Application / Service | Recommended Trap | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Process heat exchanger** with variable load | Float & Thermostatic (F&T) | Modulated, continuous discharge handles 0–100 % load swings |
| **Steam main drip legs** (large mains) | Thermodynamic Disc | Intermittent, robust, discharges air at start-up, handles superheat |
| **Tracer lines** on piping | Bimetallic or Bellows | Sub-cooled discharge avoids water hammer in return line |
| **Autoclaves / Sterilizers** | Bellows / Balanced Pressure | Tight shut-off, no steam loss (process critical) |
| **Reboiler bottom** (refinery) | Inverted Bucket | High pressure, superheat tolerance, abrasive condensate |
| **Jacketed vessel** with batch operation | F&T or Inverted Bucket | Handles large slug of condensate on batch start |
| **HVAC / Building heating** | Bellows (low pressure) | Compact, cheap, sub-cooled discharge |
| **Turbine drain** | Impulse Trap | High-pressure, superheated, intermittent discharge |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sustainability Angle (Module-3 link to EN-MGT):** Replacing all blown traps in a typical medium-scale plant saves **3–8 %** of total fuel consumption — equivalent to **500–2000 tCO₂/year** avoided emissions. This is a frequent KTU question on "energy conservation opportunities (EnCon)."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantifying Steam Loss from a Failed (Blowing) Trap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU board-favourite 14-mark numerical** on steam-trap energy auditing. We solve it in complete, step-by-step detail.

### **Statement (Sample KTU-style problem)**

A plant has a steam trap that is **stuck fully open**, blowing saturated steam at **7 bar (g)** continuously. The orifice diameter of the trap is **6 mm**, and the downstream is at atmospheric pressure. Calculate:

1. The mass of steam blowing per hour.
2. The heat-energy loss per hour.
3. The annual cost of the leak, given boiler efficiency = **85 %** and furnace oil cost = **₹ 65 / kg**, with GCV = **10,200 kcal/kg**.

### **Given Data (extract and tabulate)**

| Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Inlet pressure $P_1$ | 7 + 1.013 ≈ 8.013 bar ≈ 8 bar (abs) | bar |
| Back pressure $P_2$ | 1.013 bar (atm) | bar |
| Differential pressure $\Delta P$ | 8 − 1.013 = 6.987 ≈ **7 bar** | bar |
| Orifice diameter $d$ | 6 mm = 0.006 m | m |
| Discharge coefficient $C_d$ | **0.6** (assumed standard sharp-edged orifice) | — |
| Steam density at 8 bar sat. $\rho_1$ | **4.16 kg/m³** (from steam tables) | kg/m³ |
| Latent heat at 8 bar sat. $\lambda$ | **2086 kJ/kg** | kJ/kg |
| Boiler efficiency $\eta_b$ | 85 % = 0.85 | — |
| Furnace-oil GCV | 10,200 kcal/kg = 10,200 × 4.184 = **42,676.8 kJ/kg** | kJ/kg |
| Cost of oil | ₹ 65/kg | ₹/kg |

### **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the orifice**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.006)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{4} = \dfrac{1.131 \times 10^{-4}}{4}
$$

$$
A = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Text-row note:** The orifice is the same as the cross-section through which steam escapes; we apply the standard circular area formula.

### **Step 2 — Mass flow rate using the orifice equation**

The general orifice flow equation for compressible fluid (subsonic, choked condition checked):

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}
$$

**Substituting the SI values:**

- $\Delta P$ must be in Pascal: $7 \, \text{bar} = 7 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 700{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$
- $\rho_1 = 4.16 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $C_d = 0.6$, $A = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.6 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{2 \times 4.16 \times 700{,}000}
$$

**Step 2a — Evaluate the inside of the square root:**

$$
2 \times 4.16 \times 700{,}000 = 8.32 \times 700{,}000 = 5.824 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\sqrt{5.824 \times 10^6} = 2413.3
$$

**Step 2b — Multiply all factors:**

$$
\dot{m} = 0.6 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \times 2413.3
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 1.6962 \times 10^{-5} \times 2413.3
$$

$$
\dot{m} \approx 0.04093 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Text-row note:** The mass flow is in **kg per second** because all quantities are in SI. We must convert to kg/hr for the problem.

### **Step 3 — Convert to kg/hr**

$$
\dot{m}_{hr} = 0.04093 \times 3600 = 147.35 \, \text{kg/hr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{hr} \approx 147.4 \, \text{kg of steam per hour}}
$$

### **Step 4 — Heat-energy loss per hour**

$$
Q_{loss/hr} = \dot{m}_{hr} \times \lambda = 147.35 \times 2086
$$

$$
Q_{loss/hr} = 307{,}372.1 \, \text{kJ/hr}
$$

Converting to kWh:

$$
Q_{loss/hr} = \dfrac{307{,}372.1}{3600} = 85.38 \, \text{kWh/hr}
$$

**Per year (8760 hours):**

$$
Q_{loss/yr} = 85.38 \times 8760 = 747{,}929 \, \text{kWh/yr} \approx \textbf{748 MWh/yr}
$$

### **Step 5 — Fuel (oil) required to generate this steam**

Boiler direct efficiency formula:

$$
\eta_b = \dfrac{m_{steam} \cdot (h_g - h_f)}{m_{fuel} \cdot GCV}
$$

Where $(h_g - h_f) = \lambda$ for saturated steam = 2086 kJ/kg.

For the **hourly** fuel consumption:

$$
m_{fuel/hr} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{hr} \cdot \lambda}{\eta_b \cdot GCV_{kJ}} = \dfrac{147.35 \times 2086}{0.85 \times 42{,}676.8}
$$

**Step 5a — Numerator:**

$$
147.35 \times 2086 = 307{,}372.1 \, \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 5b — Denominator:**

$$
0.85 \times 42{,}676.8 = 36{,}275.28 \, \text{kJ/kg of oil}
$$

**Step 5c — Divide:**

$$
m_{fuel/hr} = \dfrac{307{,}372.1}{36{,}275.28} = 8.473 \, \text{kg of oil per hour}
$$

### **Step 6 — Annual cost**

$$
m_{fuel/yr} = 8.473 \times 8760 = 74{,}223.5 \, \text{kg of oil/yr} = 74.22 \, \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

$$
C_{annual} = 74{,}223.5 \times 65 = ₹ 4{,}824{,}528
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{annual} \approx ₹ 48.25 \, \text{Lakhs per year, per single blown trap}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall callout:** Students frequently forget to convert **GCV in kcal/kg to kJ/kg** (multiply by 4.184) — this single error produces a wrong answer by a factor of ~4.2 and is a guaranteed 4-mark loss in ESE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Steam Cost (S in ₹/kg)

This formula is used in many Module-3 audit calculations, including trap savings.

### **Derivation**

Cost of 1 kg of oil = $C_{fuel}$ (₹/kg)
Energy released by 1 kg of oil = $GCV$ (kJ/kg)
Energy reaching steam (after boiler losses) = $\eta_b \cdot GCV$ (kJ/kg of fuel)
Mass of steam generated per kg of fuel = $\dfrac{\eta_b \cdot GCV}{h_{fg}}$ (kg of steam/kg of fuel)

Therefore, **cost of 1 kg of steam** (specific steam cost):

$$
S = \dfrac{C_{fuel}}{\dfrac{\eta_b \cdot GCV}{h_{fg}}} = \dfrac{C_{fuel} \cdot h_{fg}}{\eta_b \cdot GCV}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = \dfrac{C_{fuel} \cdot h_{fg}}{\eta_b \cdot GCV}}
$$

### **Numerical (Quick Verification with above case)**

$$
S = \dfrac{65 \times 2086}{0.85 \times 42{,}676.8} = \dfrac{1{,}35{,}590}{36{,}275.28}
$$

$$
S = 3.737 \, \text{₹/kg of steam}
$$

**Cross-check:** Cost per hour = $147.35 \times 3.737 = 550.65$ ₹/hr, and $550.65 \times 8760 = ₹ 48.23$ Lakhs/yr ✓ (matches Step 6 result, small rounding).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trap Capacity Sizing (Quick Method)

Given: Required condensate load $\dot{m}_{req}$ (kg/h), inlet pressure $P_{in}$ (bar g), back pressure $P_{back}$ (bar g), safety factor $SF$ (typically **2 to 3**).

Required discharge capacity:

$$
\dot{m}_{trap} = SF \times \dot{m}_{req}
$$

The differential pressure across the trap:

$$
\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{back}
$$

From manufacturer's discharge curve, the trap must deliver $\dot{m}_{trap}$ at the calculated $\Delta P$. If the manufacturer's curve shows discharge $Q$ at $\Delta P$ for a given orifice size, select the trap with:

$$
Q \geq \dot{m}_{trap}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why safety factor 2–3?** Steam traps experience **start-up slugs**, **inrush of cold condensate** (during equipment warm-up), and **air-binding** in the first few minutes. A safety factor protects against premature failure.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree (Module 3 Exam Friendly)

| Service Parameter | F&T | Inverted Bucket | Bimetallic | Bellows | Disc (TD) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Variable load | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ |
| Constant load | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| High pressure (>10 bar) | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Superheated steam | ★ (poor) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ (poor) | ★★★★★ |
| Sub-cooled discharge needed | ★ (air vent) | ★ (intermittent) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ (intermittent) |
| Air handling at start-up | ★★★★★ (with vent) | ★★★ (vent hole) | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Water hammer resistance | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ (poor) | ★★★★ |
| Tight shut-off (no steam leak) | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Maintenance interval | 3–5 yr | 3–5 yr | 1–2 yr | 2–3 yr | 1–2 yr |
| Approximate cost (₹) | High | Med-High | Med | Low-Med | Low-Med |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "FATS B"** = **F**loat, **A**ir-vented, **T**emperature-controlled, **S**team-saving, **B**imetallic. Use this in viva to remember categories.

---

## 3.5 Steam Trap Testing & Failure Analysis (Energy Audit Skill)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Energy auditors must use **at least one** of the following methods to detect trap failure.

| Test Method | Procedure | Diagnosis |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual (plume test)** | Observe discharge pipe outdoors for visible white plume | Continuous plume → trap blowing live steam (failed) |
| **Stethoscope / ultrasonic** | Listen to trap on body | Continuous hiss → leaking; silence → choked; ticking → working |
| **Temperature method** | Pyrometer on inlet & outlet sides of trap | $T_{out} < T_{in}$ → working; $T_{out} \approx T_{in}$ → good; $T_{in} \approx T_{out}$ and very high → failed (live steam) |
| **Conductivity probe** | Insert probe in discharge; water completes circuit | No current → live steam leaking; pulsed current → intermittent good operation |
| **Dye / smoke test** | Inject colored dye upstream; observe colour downstream | Dye = condensate (good); clear steam = live steam leak |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The "visual plume" test fails in **cold weather** because flash steam condenses very close to the trap. The auditor must use **ultrasonic + temperature** together for reliable diagnosis — examiners expect this dual-method answer in the 14-mark question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Steam Trap Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **physical and functional architecture** of a generic steam trap system, showing the interaction between upstream steam supply, the trap body, and downstream condensate return. This is the safe Mermaid adaptation of the standard P&ID (piping and instrumentation diagram).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boiler Steam Header at 7 bar g] --> B[Steam Supply Main Pipe]
    B --> C[Isolating Valve 1 - Inlet Strainer]
    C --> D[Steam Trap Body - Working Element]
    D --> E[Trap Inlet Chamber - High Pressure Side]
    E --> F[Disc or Float or Bellows - Actuator]
    F --> G[Valve Seat - Discharge Orifice]
    G --> H[Trap Outlet Chamber - Low Pressure Side]
    H --> I[Isolating Valve 2 - Outlet]
    I --> J[Flash Steam Separator]
    J --> K[Condensate Return Pump or Gravity Return]
    K --> L[Deaerator and Feedwater Tank]
    L --> M[Boiler Feed Pump]
    M --> A
    F -. Condensate Detection .-> N[Sensing Element - Temperature or Density]
    N -. Control Signal .-> F
    A -. Non Condensable Gases Air CO2 .-> D
    D -. Vented Air and Steam at Start Up .-> O[Atmosphere or Vent Line]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Steam flows **right-to-left** from the boiler through the trap and back as condensate — a **closed loop**.
- The **actuator** (disc/float/bellows) is the heart of the trap; it receives a **control signal** from a **sensing element** that detects whether condensate or live steam is present.
- The **flash steam separator** is a critical auxiliary — it captures the small fraction of steam that flashes when high-pressure condensate is reduced to atmospheric pressure in the return line.

---

## 4.2 Working Cycle of a Disc (Thermodynamic) Trap — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SteamEntry
    SteamEntry: STATE 1 - Steam Inlet Below Disc
    SteamEntry: High velocity lifts disc - OPEN
    SteamEntry --> PressureBuilds
    PressureBuilds: STATE 2 - Control Chamber Pressure Rises
    PressureBuilds: Steam leaks through disc lip into upper chamber
    PressureBuilds --> DiscCloses
    DiscCloses: STATE 3 - Disc Pressed onto Seat
    DiscCloses: Chamber pressure equalsises - CLOSED
    DiscCloses --> CondensateCollects
    CondensateCollects: STATE 4 - Condensate Accumulates
    CondensateCollects: Drains chamber pressure through orifice
    CondensateCollects --> SteamEntry
    note right of DiscCloses
        Cycle repeats 1 to 30 times per minute
        depending on condensate load
    end note
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q1 -- Constant --> Q2{Steam pressure greater than 10 bar?}

    Q3 -- No --> IB[Inverted Bucket Trap]

    Q4 -- Yes --> BM[Bimetallic or Bellows Trap]
    Q4 -- No --> DTD2[Disc Thermodynamic Trap]

    IB --> Use2[Best for high pressure reboilers and turbines]
    DTD --> Use3[Best for drip legs on superheated mains]
    BM --> Use4[Best for tracer lines and HVAC]
    DTD2 --> Use5[Best for drip legs on saturated mains]
    style FAT fill:#90EE90
    style IB fill:#FFB6C1

```
