# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define a steam trap. List any four types of steam traps commonly used in process industries.**

**Model Answer:**

> A steam trap is an **automatic, self-actuating valve** that removes **condensate and non-condensable gases** from a steam system while **preventing the escape of live steam**. It requires no external power, operating on density, temperature, or velocity differences between steam and condensate.
>
> The four main types are:
> 1. **Float & Thermostatic (F&T) Trap** — density + temperature operated.
> 2. **Inverted Bucket (IB) Trap** — density operated.
> 3. **Bimetallic / Bellows (Thermostatic) Trap** — temperature operated.
> 4. **Disc / Impulse (Thermodynamic) Trap** — velocity / pressure dynamics operated.
>
> [3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 types, 0.5 each.]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between mechanical and thermostatic steam traps, giving one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Mechanical Trap | Thermostatic Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | **Density difference** between steam and condensate | **Temperature difference** (sub-cooled vs. saturated) |
| Discharge type | Mostly **continuous** (F&T) or **intermittent** (bucket) | Always **intermittent** |
| Air handling | Needs separate air vent (in F&T) or small vent hole (in IB) | Air is naturally discharged until steam temperature reaches saturation |
| Example | **Float & Thermostatic (F&T)** trap | **Bimetallic** or **Bellows** trap |
| Typical use | Process heat exchangers, variable loads | Tracer lines, HVAC, sub-cooled return required |

> [3 marks: 1.5 each for the comparison + 0.5 each for example.]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule 2024:** Part B carries 14 marks and has **internal choice**. The student must attempt **one** of the two alternatives. We provide both options below so that the student can practise either.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working** of a **Float & Thermostatic (F&T) steam trap**. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A plant survey reveals **18 steam traps** stuck in the **fully open** position. The average orifice size is **5 mm**, the upstream pressure is **6 bar (g)**, and the downstream is atmospheric. Estimate the **annual monetary loss** at a steam cost of **₹ 4.50 per kg of steam**, assuming **8000 operating hours/year**. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Construction:**
The F&T trap consists of two main parts — a **float chamber** and a **thermostatic air-venting element**.

1. **Float chamber:** A hollow, **stainless-steel float** is pivoted on a lever. The lever is connected to a **valve plug** at the top of the chamber. As condensate enters the chamber, the float rises in proportion to the condensate level.
2. **Bimetallic air vent:** A bimetallic strip (or bellows) is mounted in a separate compartment at the top, set to open at **5–10 °C below saturated steam temperature**.

**Working:**
- At **start-up**, the system is full of air. Cold condensate + air enters the chamber. The air vent opens (because temperature is below saturation) and **vents air** to the outlet. Condensate level rises; the float lifts the main valve, allowing condensate to discharge. Air is purged, and saturated steam approaches the trap.
- The **bimetallic vent closes** sharply when saturated steam reaches it, preventing steam loss.
- During **normal operation**, only condensate enters. The float modulates the valve opening — **continuous, modulated discharge** proportional to load. As soon as live steam arrives, the float drops and the main valve shuts tight.

**Advantages:**
- Continuous modulated discharge — handles load swings of **0 to 100 %**.
- Excellent air venting at start-up.
- High discharge capacity per unit size.
- Long service life (5–10 years) with simple maintenance.

**Limitations:**
- Cannot tolerate **superheated steam** (bimetallic vent misreads temperature).
- Cannot be used in **dirty / scale-forming condensate** (float can stick).
- Larger and heavier than disc or bellows traps; more expensive.
- Sensitive to **water hammer** (mechanical damage to float).

> **[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for sketch/description, 2 for working, 1.5 for advantages, 1.5 for limitations.]**

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given data (extract for the solution):**
- Number of traps, $N = 18$
- Orifice diameter, $d = 5 \, \text{mm} = 0.005 \, \text{m}$
- Upstream pressure, $P_1 = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013 \approx 7 \, \text{bar (abs)}$
- Back pressure, $P_2 = 1.013 \, \text{bar}$ (atmospheric)
- $\Delta P = 7 - 1.013 = 5.987 \approx 6 \, \text{bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- Steam density at 7 bar sat., $\rho = 3.67 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (from steam tables, since saturation T at 7 bar ≈ 165 °C)
- Discharge coefficient, $C_d = 0.65$ (for sharp-edged orifice)
- Steam cost, $S = ₹ 4.50 / \text{kg}$
- Operating hours, $t = 8000 \, \text{hr/yr}$

**Step 1 — Orifice area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.005)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{4}
$$

$$
A = 1.9635 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Steam mass flow per trap:**

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.65 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{2 \times 3.67 \times 6 \times 10^5}
$$

**Inside the square root:**

$$
2 \times 3.67 \times 6 \times 10^5 = 4.404 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\sqrt{4.404 \times 10^6} = 2098.6
$$

**Multiplying all factors:**

$$
\dot{m} = 0.65 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-5} \times 2098.6
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 1.2763 \times 10^{-5} \times 2098.6 \approx 0.02678 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Per hour, per trap:**

$$
\dot{m}_{hr} = 0.02678 \times 3600 = 96.42 \, \text{kg/hr per trap}
$$

**Step 3 — Total annual steam loss for 18 traps:**

$$
M_{annual} = 96.42 \times 18 \times 8000 = 96.42 \times 144{,}000
$$

$$
M_{annual} = 13{,}884{,}480 \, \text{kg/yr} = 13{,}884.48 \, \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary loss:**

$$
C_{annual} = 13{,}884.48 \times 1000 \times 4.50 = 62.48 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{annual} = ₹ 6.25 \, \text{Crore per year}}
$$

> **[7 marks breakdown: 1 mark for data extraction, 2 marks for orifice equation setup, 2 marks for numerical evaluation, 1 mark for annualisation, 1 mark for cost calculation.]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Q-A(b):**
> 1. Forgetting to convert **bar to Pa** (use $1 \, \text{bar} = 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$) — 2-mark loss.
> 2. Using $\rho$ for water (1000 kg/m³) instead of **steam density (3.67 kg/m³)** — 2-mark loss.
> 3. Using $\Delta P$ in bar directly inside the square root without unit conversion — wrong answer by a factor of $10^5$ — full 7 marks lost.
> 4. Forgetting to multiply by **N = 18 traps** and **t = 8000 hr** — 2-mark loss.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **working of a thermodynamic (disc) steam trap**. Mention its **merits and demerits**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An energy audit of a textile plant identifies a **drip-leg steam trap** on a 100 m long steam main. The trap is fitted with a **3 mm bleed orifice** that is permanently open due to seat erosion. The steam main pressure is **5 bar (g)**, and the **flash steam** from condensate has a latent heat of **2100 kJ/kg**. The plant runs **7200 hr/yr** on a 3-shift schedule. The **boiler efficiency is 80 %**, and the **furnace oil GCV is 42,000 kJ/kg**, costing **₹ 60/kg**. Calculate the **annual cost of the leak** in ₹ lakhs. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Construction:**
The disc (thermodynamic) trap has only **three moving parts**:
1. A **disc-shaped valve** (stainless steel, typically 25–50 mm diameter).
2. A **disc seat** with a sharp edge, located between two chambers.
3. A **control orifice** (small bleed hole) connecting the **lower inlet chamber** to the **upper control chamber** above the disc.

**Working (4-state cycle, refer to Section 4.2 state machine):**

- **State 1 — OPEN:** High-velocity steam entering the lower chamber produces a **dynamic pressure** (Bernoulli effect) that lifts the disc off its seat. Steam rushes through the annular gap to the outlet.
- **State 2 — Pressure build-up:** A small fraction of steam leaks past the disc lip into the **upper control chamber**, raising its pressure.
- **State 3 — CLOSED:** When the upper-chamber pressure rises and the inlet velocity drops (after the slug of steam passes), the disc is **forced back onto the seat** by the chamber pressure. The trap is now **closed**.
- **State 4 — Condensate collection:** Condensate entering cannot lift the disc (lower density and lower velocity). Condensate **pools below the disc**, and slowly drains the upper chamber through the control orifice. When the condensate level pushes the disc, the cycle restarts.

This results in an **intermittent, cyclic discharge** (1–30 cycles/min) — the characteristic **"chatter"** of disc traps.

**Merits:**
- **Small, light, and cheap** — most compact per kg/h capacity.
- Handles **superheat up to 600 °C** above saturation.
- Tolerates **water hammer** and **frozen condensate** (no water-logging).
- Wide pressure range (0.25 to 250 bar).
- Excellent for **drip legs, tracer lines, superheated mains**.

**Demerits:**
- **Discharge is intermittent**, not modulated — not ideal for variable loads.
- **Cyclic hammering** causes wear of disc and seat; typical life **1–2 years**.
- **Cannot handle dirty condensate** (erosion of seat).
- Slightly less tight shut-off than bellows traps; small steam loss at seat.
- Sensitive to **back pressure variations** — back pressure > 30 % of inlet causes hunting.

> **[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks labelled sketch, 2 marks cycle, 1.5 marks merits, 1.5 marks demerits.]**

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given data:**
- Bleed orifice diameter, $d = 3 \, \text{mm} = 0.003 \, \text{m}$
- Upstream pressure $P_1 = 5 + 1.013 \approx 6 \, \text{bar abs}$
- Back pressure $P_2 = 1.013 \, \text{bar}$ (atmospheric)
- $\Delta P = 6 - 1.013 = 4.987 \approx 5 \, \text{bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- Latent heat at 5 bar sat. (≈152 °C), $h_{fg} = 2100 \, \text{kJ/kg}$
- Steam density at 5 bar sat., $\rho = 2.92 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (from steam tables)
- $C_d = 0.62$ (typical for worn seat)
- Boiler efficiency, $\eta_b = 0.80$
- GCV = 42,000 kJ/kg; cost ₹ 60/kg
- Operating hours = 7200 hr/yr

**Step 1 — Orifice area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.003)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 9 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.62 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{2 \times 2.92 \times 5 \times 10^5}
$$

**Inside the square root:**

$$
2 \times 2.92 \times 5 \times 10^5 = 2.92 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\sqrt{2.92 \times 10^6} = 1708.8
$$

**Multiplying:**

$$
\dot{m} = 0.62 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-6} \times 1708.8
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 4.3828 \times 10^{-6} \times 1708.8 = 0.007490 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Per hour:**

$$
\dot{m}_{hr} = 0.007490 \times 3600 = 26.96 \, \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual steam loss:**

$$
M_{annual} = 26.96 \times 7200 = 1{,}94{,}112 \, \text{kg/yr} = 194.11 \, \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy lost per year (kJ):**

$$
E_{loss} = 1{,}94{,}112 \times 2100 = 4.076 \times 10^8 \, \text{kJ/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kWh:**

$$
E_{loss} = \dfrac{4.076 \times 10^8}{3600} = 1.132 \times 10^5 \, \text{kWh/yr} = 113{,}222 \, \text{kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Fuel (oil) required:**

$$
m_{fuel} = \dfrac{E_{loss}}{\eta_b \cdot GCV} = \dfrac{4.076 \times 10^8}{0.80 \times 42{,}000}
$$

$$
m_{fuel} = \dfrac{4.076 \times 10^8}{33{,}600} = 12{,}131.0 \, \text{kg of oil/yr} = 12.13 \, \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 7 — Annual cost:**

$$
C_{annual} = 12{,}131 \times 60 = ₹ 7{,}27{,}860 \approx ₹ 7.28 \, \text{Lakhs/yr}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{annual} \approx ₹ 7.28 \, \text{Lakhs per year}}
$$

> **[7 marks breakdown: 1.5 marks data table, 1.5 marks orifice equation, 1.5 marks numerical substitution, 1 mark annualisation, 1.5 marks final cost.]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Q-B(b):**
> 1. Using $h_{fg} = 2257 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ (latent heat at 100 °C) instead of 2100 kJ/kg (at 5 bar) — examiners at KTU specifically give the value in the problem to test whether the student reads carefully. **1-mark loss.**
> 2. Forgetting to apply **boiler efficiency** when computing fuel consumption — many students compute steam cost = $60 \cdot 0.8 / 42{,}000$ directly without going through energy balance, leading to wrong units. **2-mark loss.**
> 3. Mixing up $\rho$ for steam vs. water. Steam at 5 bar sat. has $\rho = 2.92 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ — not 1000 kg/m³.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this section for the **last 30 minutes** before the exam. Read it once, twice, and you are set.

- **Steam trap = automatic self-actuating valve** that lets condensate out and keeps live steam in. **No external power.**
- **Three functional families:**
  - **Mechanical** → density difference → F&T, Inverted Bucket, Open Float.
  - **Thermostatic** → temperature difference → Bimetallic, Bellows (balanced pressure), Wax.
  - **Thermodynamic** → velocity/momentum → Disc, Impulse.
- **F&T trap** = best for **variable load** process heat exchangers; has separate **bimetallic air vent** for start-up purging.
- **Inverted bucket** = robust, high pressure, superheat tolerant; needs a **3 mm vent hole** at top of bucket to avoid air binding.
- **Bimetallic trap** = stacks of two-metal strips; delivers **sub-cooled** condensate (5–30 °C below saturation) — protects return lines from water hammer.
- **Bellows trap** = volatile fluid in sealed bellows; sharp on-off action; very **tight shut-off**; used in autoclaves, sterilizers.
- **Disc trap** = small, light, cheap, superheat-tolerant; cycles (chatters) **1–30 times/min**; wears out in 1–2 yr.
- **Failure modes:**
  - **Stuck open** (blowing live steam) → loss of condensate + steam.
  - **Stuck closed** (choked) → waterlogging, hammering, process upset.
  - **Internal leak** (seat damage) → small continuous steam loss.
- **Testing methods:** Visual plume, stethoscope / ultrasonic, pyrometer (T in vs. T out), conductivity probe, dye test.
- **Numerical key formulas:**
  - $A = \pi d^2 / 4$
  - $\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$
  - $Q = m \lambda$
  - $S = C_{fuel} h_{fg} / (\eta_b GCV)$
  - $C_{annual} = m_{leak/hr} \times S \times t_{hr/yr}$
- **Units awareness (KTU 2024 examiners are strict!):**
  - **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** — must convert before plugging into SI formula.
  - **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ** — must convert GCV from kcal/kg to kJ/kg.
  - **1 kWh = 3600 kJ** — must convert when computing energy in kWh for billing.
  - **Steam density at 7 bar ≈ 4.16 kg/m³** — NOT 1000 kg/m³ (water).
- **Selection mnemonic:** **"FATS B"** — Float, Air-vented, Temperature-controlled, Steam-saving, Bimetallic.
- **Energy savings:** A properly maintained steam-trap system saves **3–8 % of total fuel** in steam-intensive industries — translates to **500–2000 tCO₂/yr** emissions avoided.
- **High-yield exam point:** A typical blown trap wastes **5–80 kg/hr of steam** ≈ **₹ 2–15 Lakh/yr** depending on pressure and tariff.
- **Sustainability (Module-3 link):** Every trap replaced under a steam-trap management program contributes directly to the plant's **Scope-1 emission reduction targets** and **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme** compliance for energy-intensive industries.
- **Viva one-liner:** "A steam trap is to a steam system what a check-valve-and-strainer is to a pump — it is a **silent guardian of efficiency**; the day it fails silently is the day the plant starts losing money silently."

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Pitfall Callout (one-line):** If a numerical involves **cost**, you must end up in **₹/yr** — work backwards from there and confirm each step's units before writing the final boxed answer. A single unit slip wastes the entire 7 marks allocated to the sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identifying Opportunities for Energy Savings in Steam Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identifying Opportunities for Energy Savings in Steam Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Identifying Opportunities for Energy Savings in Steam Distribution

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Steam Distribution System** refers to the integrated network of pipes, valves, fittings, traps, insulation, headers, and pressure-reducing stations that transport generated steam from the boiler house to the various end-use points (process heating, space heating, power generation, etc.) at the required pressure and temperature. *Energy savings opportunities* in this system are those engineered interventions and housekeeping measures that reduce steam/heat losses, improve thermodynamic efficiency, and recover waste energy without compromising the quality of steam delivered to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Energy savings in steam distribution are evaluated using three primary metrics — (i) **% Steam Leakage**, (ii) **Heat Loss through Uninsulated/Under-insulated Surfaces (kW or MJ/hr)**, and (iii) **Flash Steam Recovery Potential (kg/hr)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a steam distribution system as the **"plumbing network of a hospital"** carrying a precious, life-saving fluid. In a hospital, a leaking IV line wastes medicine and starves the patient; a kinked pipe reduces flow; a poorly insulated line lets the medicine cool. Similarly, in a steam network:
- A leaking steam joint = wasted fuel burned at the boiler.
- A pipe without insulation = heat radiating into the surroundings.
- A failed steam trap = live steam blowing into the condensate line.
- A throttled pressure at the user = wasted exergy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A **1 mm hole** in a steam line at **7 bar(g)** loses approximately **3.2 kg of steam per hour** — that's enough energy to light a 100 W bulb continuously. Multiply this by hundreds of small leaks in a typical plant and the energy bill balloons.

### Why This Topic Matters in Energy Auditing
Steam distribution losses typically account for **5% to 20%** of the total energy input at the boiler. In poorly maintained systems, this can climb above **30%**. The KTU 2024 scheme places this topic under **CO3 — Energy Audit of Utilities** with Bloom's levels **Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Areas of Energy Loss in Steam Distribution
Energy losses in a steam network fall into the following logical categories. Each one represents a **measurable opportunity**:

1. **Steam Leakage** (from joints, flanges, valves, and pipe walls)
2. **Heat Loss through Pipe Surfaces** (radiation + convection from uninsulated pipes)
3. **Steam Trap Malfunction** (live steam blowing into the condensate return)
4. **Flash Steam Losses** (high-pressure condensate flashing to low-pressure steam at vent)
5. **Excess Pressure Drop / Over-pressurization** (wasted throttling energy)
6. **Condensate Sub-cooling** (loss of sensible heat below saturation)
7. **Improper Pipe Sizing** (excessive velocity causing erosion or low velocity causing water hammer)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Energy-Saving Strategies

### A. Steam Leakage Detection and Arresting
- **Why:** Leaks are pure fuel waste — steam that has already consumed boiler fuel is lost to atmosphere.
- **How:** Use ultrasonic leak detectors, soap-bubble tests, and thermal imaging cameras.
- **KTU Quick Estimate Formula:**

$$Q_{leak} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\rho_{steam} \cdot \Delta P}$$

Where:
- $Q_{leak}$ = mass flow rate of steam lost (kg/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically **0.6 to 0.85**)
- $A$ = effective leak area (m²)
- $\rho_{steam}$ = specific volume–based density of steam (kg/m³)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure differential across the leak (Pa)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Loss values for various orifice diameters at different pressures are tabulated in the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) – General Aspects of Energy Management & Energy Audit** book (a mandatory KTU reference for PEEET742).

### B. Insulation of Steam Pipes and Fittings
- **Why:** Bare steam pipes lose 10–15 times more heat than insulated ones.
- **How:** Insulate all surfaces where temperature > 50°C is accessible; use **calcium silicate** or **mineral wool** for high-temperature lines.
- **Heat Loss Formula from a Bare Cylindrical Pipe:**

$$Q_{loss} = \frac{2\pi L \cdot (T_s - T_a)}{\frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_{pipe}} + \frac{1}{h_o D_o}}$$

For an **insulated pipe**, the additional resistance of the insulation layer is added in series:

$$Q_{loss,ins} = \frac{2\pi L \cdot (T_s - T_a)}{\frac{\ln(D_i/D_i^{pipe})}{k_{ins}} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_{pipe}} + \frac{1}{h_o D_o}}$$

Where:
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (°C), $T_a$ = ambient temperature (°C)
- $L$ = pipe length (m)
- $D_i, D_o$ = inner/outer diameter (m)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $h_o$ = outside convective heat transfer coefficient (typically **5–15 W/m²·K** for still air)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Insulation Conductivity:** Mineral wool at 100°C has $k \approx$ **0.038 W/m·K**. Calcium silicate at 200°C has $k \approx$ **0.055 W/m·K**. These are accepted reference values used in BEE audit calculations.

### C. Steam Trap Management
A steam trap's job is to **discharge condensate (and air) but NOT live steam**. Malfunction modes:
- **Stuck Open:** Live steam blows to drain — high loss.
- **Stuck Closed:** Condensate backs up, causes water hammer.
- **Bypassing:** Steam leaks past internal valve seat.

The energy loss from a failed-open trap can be estimated as:

$$\dot{m}_{loss} = A_{orifice} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot P_{trap} \cdot \rho_{steam}}{1 + \beta^4}}$$

Where $\beta$ is the diameter ratio of the orifice (often assumed = 1 for sharp-edged leaks).

### D. Flash Steam Recovery
When high-pressure condensate (e.g., **10 bar(g)**) is discharged to a low-pressure line (**2 bar(g)**), a fraction flashes into steam. This is recoverable.

$$\text{Flash \%} = \frac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $h_{f1}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at high pressure (kJ/kg)
- $h_{f2}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at flash pressure (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fg2}$ = latent heat of vaporization at flash pressure (kJ/kg)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Condensate at 10 bar (h_f1 ≈ 781 kJ/kg) flashing to 2 bar (h_f2 ≈ 504 kJ/kg, h_fg2 ≈ 2201 kJ/kg) gives a flash fraction of approximately **12.6%** — meaning **126 kg of steam can be recovered per 1000 kg of condensate**.

### E. Condensate Recovery
Returning hot condensate to the boiler saves:
1. The fuel needed to reheat the water from cold to feedwater temperature.
2. The water treatment chemicals.
3. The make-up water itself.

$$\text{Energy Saved} = \dot{m}_{cond} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{cond} - T_{makeup})$$

Where $\dot{m}_{cond}$ is condensate mass flow (kg/hr) and $c_p \approx$ **4.186 kJ/kg·K** for water.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Key Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Steam Leakage | $Q_{leak} = C_d A \sqrt{\rho \cdot \Delta P}$ | kg/s; $C_d$ = 0.6–0.85 |
| 2 | Heat Loss Bare Pipe | $Q = 2\pi L (T_s - T_a) / R_{th}$ | W; $R_{th}$ = thermal resistance |
| 3 | Heat Loss Insulated Pipe | Same form, $R_{th} = \sum R_{layer}$ | W |
| 4 | Flash Steam % | $(h_{f1} - h_{f2})/h_{fg2} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Condensate Energy Recovery | $\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ/hr |
| 6 | Annual Fuel Cost Savings | $\Delta Q \times \text{hrs} \times \text{fuel cost} / \eta_{boiler}$ | INR/year |
| 7 | Payback Period | (Investment) / (Annual Savings) | years |
| 8 | Stack + Distribution Loss | $\sum Q_{loss} / Q_{input} \times 100$ | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in the **Steam Master Plus**, **TRACE 700**, and BEE's standard audit spreadsheets. Mastery of these equations is essential for KTU's *design-oriented* and *case-study* questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Loss Through a Composite Insulated Pipe Wall

We consider a steam pipe of inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$, covered by insulation of inner radius $r_2$ and outer radius $r_3$. Steady-state, one-dimensional radial conduction is assumed.

### Step 1: Heat Transfer Through a Cylindrical Wall
For a hollow cylinder, the heat transfer rate through a single layer is given by Fourier's Law integrated over the radius:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= -k \cdot 2\pi r L \cdot \frac{dT}{dr} \\
\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT &= -\frac{Q}{2\pi k L} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} \\
(T_2 - T_1) &= -\frac{Q}{2\pi k L} \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \\
Q &= \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the standard derivation for radial heat conduction, with the sign reversed so that positive $Q$ flows from $T_1$ (hot) to $T_2$ (cold).

### Step 2: Series Resistance for Composite Wall
For pipe + insulation in series, the thermal resistances add:

$$R_{total} = R_{pipe} + R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{pipe} L} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_{ins} L}$$

### Step 3: Final Heat Loss Equation

$$Q_{loss} = \frac{2\pi L (T_s - T_a)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{pipe}} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o r_3}}$$

The third term in the denominator accounts for the **outside convective resistance** to ambient air.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A steam main of 100 mm nominal bore (100 mm ID, 114 mm OD) carries saturated steam at 10 bar(g) (surface temperature ≈ 184°C). The pipe is 50 m long and is currently bare ($k_{pipe} = 45$ W/m·K for carbon steel). It is proposed to insulate with 50 mm thick mineral wool ($k_{ins} = 0.045$ W/m·K). Ambient temperature is 30°C, $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K. Calculate the heat loss before and after insulation, and the annual savings at ₹5/kWh with 8000 operating hours/year.

### Solution:

**Geometry:**
- $r_1 = 0.05$ m, $r_2 = 0.057$ m, $r_3 = 0.057 + 0.050 = 0.107$ m
- $T_s = 184$°C, $T_a = 30$°C, $L = 50$ m

**Bare Pipe (no insulation, so $r_3 = r_2 = 0.057$ m):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{bare} &= \frac{2\pi L (T_s - T_a)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{pipe}} + \frac{1}{h_o r_2}} \\
&= \frac{2\pi (50)(184 - 30)}{\frac{\ln(0.057/0.05)}{45} + \frac{1}{10 \times 0.057}} \\
&= \frac{48361}{0.00293 + 1.7544} \\
&= \frac{48361}{1.7573} \\
&\approx 27517 \text{ W} \approx 27.52 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Insulated Pipe:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{ins} &= \frac{2\pi (50)(154)}{\frac{\ln(0.057/0.05)}{45} + \frac{\ln(0.107/0.057)}{0.045} + \frac{1}{10 \times 0.107}} \\
&= \frac{48361}{0.00293 + 14.169 + 0.9346} \\
&= \frac{48361}{15.107} \\
&\approx 3201 \text{ W} \approx 3.20 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Savings:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q &= 27.52 - 3.20 = 24.32 \text{ kW} \\
\text{Annual Energy Saved} &= 24.32 \times 8000 = 194560 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Annual Cost Saved} &= 194560 \times 5 = ₹9,72,800 \\
\text{Steam Equivalent} &= 194560 / 2200 \approx 88.4 \text{ tonnes of steam/year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Many students forget the **outside convective resistance term** $1/(h_o \cdot r_3)$. Omitting it gives an over-estimate of savings by 30–50%. Always include all three resistances.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Losses to Interventions

| Loss Source | % of Total Distribution Loss | Detection Method | Recommended Intervention | Typical Payback |
|-------------|------------------------------|------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| Steam Leaks | 30–50% | Ultrasonic detector, IR camera | Re-pack joints, replace gaskets | < 3 months |
| Uninsulated Lines | 20–30% | Thermal imaging, hand-touch test | Add mineral wool/calcium silicate | 1–2 years |
| Failed Steam Traps | 15–25% | Trap survey (test plug + pyrometer) | Replace trap, install monitor | < 6 months |
| Flash Steam Loss | 5–10% | Mass balance at condensate lines | Install flash vessel | 1–2 years |
| Sub-cooled Condensate | 5–10% | Temperature at trap discharge | Reduce pipe run length, recover heat | 2–3 years |
| Excess Pressure Drop | 2–5% | Pressure loggers | Re-size line, reduce fittings | 3–5 years |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Steam Distribution System with Energy-Saving Opportunities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boiler House: Fuel + Water] --> B[High-Pressure Steam Header 10 barg]
    B --> C[Pressure Reducing Station to 4 barg]
    C --> D[Medium-Pressure Steam Header]
    B --> E[High-Pressure Steam Main to Process]
    D --> F[Process Equipment Heat Exchangers]
    D --> G[Space Heating and HVAC]
    F --> H[Condensate Return Line Hot 180 C]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Flash Steam Recovery Vessel]
    I --> J[Low-Pressure Flash Steam to Deaerator]
    H --> K[Steam Trap Bank]
    K --> L[Condensate Pump]
    L --> M[Boiler Feedwater Tank]
    M --> A

    %% Energy Saving Opportunities highlighted
    N[Opportunity 1: Leak Arresting via Ultrasonic Testing] -.-> B
    N -.-> C
    N -.-> D
    O[Opportunity 2: Pipe Insulation Mineral Wool] -.-> B
    O -.-> C
    O -.-> E
    P[Opportunity 3: Steam Trap Maintenance Program] -.-> K
    Q[Opportunity 4: Flash Steam Recovery Vessel] -.-> I
    R[Opportunity 5: Condensate Return at Maximum Temperature] -.-> H
    S[Opportunity 6: Pressure Optimization to Match User Demand] -.-> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Tree for Energy Audit of Steam Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Steam Audit at Plant] --> q1{Steam Traps Surveyed?}
    q1 -- No --> a1[Conduct Trap Survey, Identify Failed Units] --> q2
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Pipe Insulation Audit Done?}
    q2 -- No --> a2[Use IR Camera to Map Surface Temperatures] --> q3
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Leak Detection Performed?}
    q3 -- No --> a3[Ultrasonic Leak Survey at All Joints] --> q4
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Flash Steam Recovery Possible?}
    q4 -- No --> q5
    q4 -- Yes --> a4[Install Flash Vessel, Route to Deaerator] --> q5
    q5{Condensate Sub-cooled Below 90 C?}
    q5 -- Yes --> a5[Reduce Pipe Length, Add Condensate Heat Exchanger] --> q6
    q5 -- No --> q6{Operating Pressure Higher Than User Need?}
    q6 -- Yes --> a6[Adjust PRV Setpoint, Recover High-Pressure Condensate] --> end1
    q6 -- No --> end1[End: Compile Audit Report with kWh and INR Savings]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above is a **functional flow schematic**, not a piping isometric. In a real KTU lab/PED class, students may be asked to draw an actual P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram) showing traps, PRVs, and condensate return — but the logic and component flow remain the same.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any six major areas where energy losses occur in a steam distribution system.*

**Model Answer:**
The six major areas of energy loss in a steam distribution system are:
1. Steam leakage from joints, flanges, and valve packings.
2. Heat loss through uninsulated or damaged pipe surfaces.
3. Malfunctioning steam traps (stuck open, leaking live steam).
4. Flash steam loss from high-pressure condensate discharge.
5. Sub-cooling of condensate below saturation temperature.
6. Excessive pressure drop / over-pressurization at the user end.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip (3-mark question):** Students must write **at least 6 distinct points** to score full marks. Writing 4 points only fetches 2 marks in the KTU key.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the function of a steam trap? Name any two common types.*

**Model Answer:**
A **steam trap** is an automatic valve that discharges condensate (and non-condensable gases like air) from a steam system while **preventing the escape of live steam**.
Two common types:
1. **Thermostatic trap** (e.g., bimetallic, balanced pressure)
2. **Thermodynamic trap** (e.g., disc or impulse trap)
3. (Bonus) **Mechanical trap** (e.g., float trap, inverted bucket trap)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question offers internal choice (Q-A OR Q-B). Solve one fully. Each part (a) carries 7 marks and (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**Q-A (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **float-type steam trap**. How is its malfunction detected during an energy audit? (7 Marks)

**Q-A (b)** A high-pressure condensate stream of **5000 kg/hr at 10 bar(g)** is throttled to a flash vessel operating at **2 bar(g)**. Calculate:
(i) The flash steam generated (kg/hr).
(ii) The annual energy recovery in kWh if this steam is used in a low-pressure process (latent heat at 2 bar ≈ 2201 kJ/kg).
(iii) The annual monetary savings at ₹6/kWh and 6000 operating hours/year. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Q-A (a)

**Working of Float-Type Steam Trap:**
- A float-type trap has a **buoyant hollow float** inside a chamber connected to the steam space.
- As condensate accumulates in the chamber, the float rises.
- The rising float is mechanically linked to a valve that opens proportionally to the condensate level.
- When the chamber is full, the valve is fully open, allowing condensate to discharge.
- As condensate is discharged, the float drops and the valve closes — preventing live steam escape.
- Air and non-condensable gases are vented through a small **thermostatic air vent** at the top.

**Malfunction Detection Methods:**
1. **Visual Inspection:** Continuous discharge of steam instead of water.
2. **Pyrometer Test:** Temperature at trap outlet should be near saturation; higher indicates live steam leak.
3. **Ultrasonic Test:** Live steam leaks produce high-frequency sound (>20 kHz).
4. **Trap Survey with Test Plug:** Insert a test valve to check discharge behavior.

**Sketch (verbal description):**
A horizontal cylindrical body with an inlet from the steam space (top), a float chamber, a lever-connected valve at the bottom outlet, and a small air vent at the top.

#### Model Solution — Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{cond} = 5000$ kg/hr
- $P_1 = 10$ bar(g) = 11 bar(a) → $h_{f1} \approx 781$ kJ/kg
- $P_2 = 2$ bar(g) = 3 bar(a) → $h_{f2} \approx 504$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg2} \approx 2201$ kJ/kg

**(i) Flash Steam:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Flash \%} &= \frac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{781 - 504}{2201} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{277}{2201} \times 100 \\
&\approx 12.59\%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\dot{m}_{flash} = 5000 \times 0.1259 \approx 629.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**(ii) Annual Energy Recovery:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{annual} &= \dot{m}_{flash} \times h_{fg2} \times \text{hrs} \\
&= 629.4 \times 2201 \times 6000 \text{ kJ/year} \\
&= 8.31 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ/year} \\
\text{Converting to kWh:} \quad Q_{kWh} &= \frac{8.31 \times 10^9}{3600} \\
&\approx 2,30,8890 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 2,30,889 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Monetary Savings:**

$$\text{Savings} = 2,30,889 \times ₹6 = ₹13,85,334 \text{ per year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write enthalpy values **in J/kg** but forget to **convert kJ → kWh by dividing by 3600**. This single arithmetic error can cost **3 out of 7 marks**. Always state units and show the conversion step explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q-B (a)** Explain the **six-step methodology** for conducting an energy audit of a steam distribution system. (7 Marks)

**Q-B (b)** A 50 m long bare steam pipe (ID = 100 mm, OD = 114 mm) carries saturated steam at 7 bar(g) (surface temperature ≈ 170°C). The pipe is to be insulated with **40 mm thick mineral wool** ($k_{ins} = 0.04$ W/m·K, $k_{steel} = 45$ W/m·K, $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K, $T_a = 32$°C). Calculate:
(i) Heat loss without insulation (kW)
(ii) Heat loss with insulation (kW)
(iii) Annual savings in ₹ at ₹5.5/kWh, 7200 hours/year operation. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Q-B (a)

**Six-Step Energy Audit Methodology for Steam Distribution:**

1. **Inventory & Mapping:** List all steam lines, lengths, diameters, operating pressures, and end-users. Prepare a P&ID.
2. **Baseline Data Collection:** Measure steam flow, pressure, temperature at boiler outlet, PRV stations, and at each user.
3. **Leak & Trap Survey:** Use ultrasonic detectors and pyrometers to identify leaking joints and failed traps. Quantify losses.
4. **Insulation Audit:** Use IR camera to identify hot spots. Calculate heat loss from bare vs. insulated surfaces.
5. **Condensate & Flash Recovery Assessment:** Quantify flash steam generation, sub-cooling, and condensate return temperature.
6. **Recommendation & Economic Analysis:** Prepare a list of energy conservation measures (ECMs) with cost, savings, payback, and CO₂ reduction.

#### Model Solution — Q-B (b)

**Given:**
- $r_1 = 0.050$ m, $r_2 = 0.057$ m, $r_3 = 0.057 + 0.040 = 0.097$ m
- $T_s = 170$°C, $T_a = 32$°C, $\Delta T = 138$°C
- $L = 50$ m, $k_{steel} = 45$ W/m·K, $k_{ins} = 0.04$ W/m·K, $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K

**(i) Heat Loss — Bare Pipe:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{bare} &= \frac{2\pi (50)(138)}{\frac{\ln(0.057/0.05)}{45} + \frac{1}{12 \times 0.057}} \\
&= \frac{43354}{0.00293 + 1.4620} \\
&= \frac{43354}{1.4649} \\
&\approx 29,597 \text{ W} \approx 29.60 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Heat Loss — Insulated Pipe:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{ins} &= \frac{43354}{\frac{0.00293}{1} + \frac{\ln(0.097/0.057)}{0.04} + \frac{1}{12 \times 0.097}} \\
&= \frac{43354}{0.00293 + 13.272 + 0.8591} \\
&= \frac{43354}{14.134} \\
&\approx 3067 \text{ W} \approx 3.07 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Annual Savings:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q &= 29.60 - 3.07 = 26.53 \text{ kW} \\
E_{saved} &= 26.53 \times 7200 = 1,91,016 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Savings} &= 1,91,016 \times ₹5.5 = ₹10,50,588 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When a question gives "7 bar(g) steam" but doesn't state the surface temperature explicitly, many students either (a) leave $T_s$ blank and lose 1–2 marks, or (b) use 100°C (boiling point at 1 atm) which is **wrong** — saturated steam temperature at 7 bar(g) is approximately **170°C**. Always refer to **steam tables** and clearly state the source of the temperature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Energy-saving opportunities in steam distribution** are categorized into **leakage control, insulation, trap management, flash recovery, condensate recovery, and pressure optimization**.
- **Bare pipe heat loss** is governed by **Fourier's law for cylindrical coordinates** with series thermal resistances.
- **Flash steam %** = $(h_{f1} - h_{f2}) / h_{fg2} \times 100$ — always use steam-table values, not approximate numbers.
- **Annual energy savings** = $\Delta Q$ (kW) $\times$ operating hours $\times$ unit cost; always include **boiler efficiency** when translating to fuel savings.
- **Steam traps** are classified as **thermostatic, thermodynamic, and mechanical**; only the trap survey can reveal failure modes.
- **Payback period** = Investment / Annual Savings — most steam ECMs have payback under 2 years.
- **CO₂ reduction** ≈ Energy saved (kWh) $\times$ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (Indian grid average).
- **Condensate recovery** saves both **fuel AND water** — a double dividend.
- **Ultrasonic leak detectors** and **IR thermography** are the two most important non-invasive tools in a steam audit.
- **Pressure reducing valves (PRVs)** must be set to the **minimum required user pressure**; excess pressure is wasted exergy.
- **Critical KTU numbers to memorize:**
  - 1 mm orifice at 7 bar(g) ≈ **3.2 kg/hr steam loss**
  - Bare pipe vs. insulated: **~10× heat loss reduction**
  - Flash % from 10 bar → 2 bar ≈ **12.6%**
  - Latent heat of steam at atmospheric pressure ≈ **2257 kJ/kg**
  - $c_p$ of water ≈ **4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Forgetting to convert **kJ to kWh** (divide by 3600) and forgetting the **outside convective resistance** in pipe heat loss problems.
- **Industry standard reference:** **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** Guide Books — Book 2 (Energy Efficiency in Thermal Utilities) and Book 4 (Energy Performance Assessment for Steam Systems) are **mandatory reading** for KTU PEEET742.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 — Identifying Opportunities for Energy Savings in Steam Distribution.** Proceed to Module 4 only after mastering all numerical problems and the Mermaid-based system diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Furnace: General Fuel Economy Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Furnace: General Fuel Economy Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Furnace in an Energy Audit Context?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Industrial Furnace):**
> A *furnace* is an enclosed combustion chamber designed to deliver controlled, high-temperature heat to a *charge* (raw material or workpiece) by the controlled combustion of a fuel (solid, liquid, or gaseous) with air/oxygen, in order to accomplish a specific metallurgical, chemical, or thermal-processing objective such as melting, heating, re-heating, or heat treatment.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Energy Management & Auditing (PEEET742)** syllabus, a furnace is treated as a **thermal energy conversion system**. It converts the *chemical energy* of a fuel into *sensible + latent heat of flue gases*, and the auditor's job is to identify how much of that heat actually reaches the *charge* versus how much is *lost* to the surroundings, *flue gas*, *walls*, or *coolant*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker vs. The Open Pan"

Imagine you are boiling rice:

- An **ordinary open pan** (a poorly designed furnace) loses enormous heat from the surface, the steam escaping sideways, and the sides of the pan getting hot — the kitchen heats up, but less heat enters the rice.
- A **pressure cooker** (a well-designed, insulated, sealed furnace) keeps the heat trapped, channels the steam (waste heat recovery), and cooks faster with less fuel.

A furnace audit is essentially **turning an "open pan" into a "pressure cooker"** — without rebuilding the equipment, but by tuning its combustion, insulation, draft, charge, and recovery systems.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Furnace Fuel Economy

Every general fuel-economy measure in a furnace falls into one of three pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Pillars of Furnace Fuel Economy**
> 1. **Reducing Input Fuel** — burn less fuel for the same useful output.
> 2. **Reducing Heat Losses** — keep the heat inside the furnace and inside the charge.
> 3. **Recovering Waste Heat** — capture heat that *has* to leave the furnace and reuse it.

All the 20+ general measures (insulation, excess-air trimming, waste-heat recovery, charge preheating, radiant cup burners, etc.) can be mapped onto these three pillars.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | % | Useful heat absorbed by charge ÷ Heat supplied by fuel |
| Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb}$ | % | Heat released in combustion ÷ Heat input from fuel |
| Specific Fuel Consumption | SFC | kg/tonne or m³/tonne | Fuel used per unit mass of charge processed |
| Heat Losses | $Q_{loss}$ | kJ/h or % | Sum of flue-gas, wall, opening, and cooling losses |
| Excess Air | $EA$ | % | (Actual air – Stoichiometric air) ÷ Stoichiometric air × 100 |
| Heat Distribution Factor | $\phi$ | – | Fraction of useful heat that actually enters the charge vs. structural parts |

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Pointer:**
> Module 3 of PEEET742 specifically tests whether a student can (a) list the *general* fuel economy measures applicable to any furnace class, and (b) *quantify* the energy savings. Marks are awarded for **specific named measures** (e.g., "recuperative preheating of combustion air"), **numerical computation of flue-gas losses**, and **classification clarity** (general vs. specific, short-term vs. long-term measures).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Furnace Energy Balance (The Auditor's Master Equation)

The theoretical foundation of every fuel-economy measure is the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the steady-state furnace:

$$
Q_{fuel} \;=\; Q_{charge} \;+\; Q_{flue} \;+\; Q_{wall} \;+\; Q_{opening} \;+\; Q_{cooling} \;+\; Q_{other}
$$

Where:

- $Q_{fuel}$ = Total heat released from fuel combustion (input)
- $Q_{charge}$ = Useful heat absorbed by the **charge** (desired output)
- $Q_{flue}$ = Sensible + latent heat carried away by **flue gases**
- $Q_{wall}$ = Heat lost through **furnace walls, roof, and floor**
- $Q_{opening}$ = Heat lost through **doors, peep-holes, charging openings**
- $Q_{cooling}$ = Heat absorbed by **water-cooled skids, doors, tuyeres, rolls**
- $Q_{other}$ = Surface radiation, unaccounted losses

> **The 'Why' Behind the Equation:** Every fuel-economy measure attacks exactly **one** of the loss terms on the right-hand side. Reducing $EA$ lowers $Q_{flue}$. Adding insulation lowers $Q_{wall}$. A door-curtain reduces $Q_{opening}$. Removing water-cooled panels reduces $Q_{cooling}$. **A student who can map a measure to a loss term earns full marks.**

## 2.2 Combustion Efficiency — The First Diagnostic

Combustion efficiency measures how completely the fuel's chemical energy is converted to **flame heat**, versus how much of that flame heat is immediately swept out as **flue gas sensible heat**.

$$
\eta_{comb} \;=\; 100 \;-\; \frac{m_{fg} \cdot c_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{ref})}{GCV_{fuel} \cdot m_{fuel}} \times 100
$$

Where:

- $m_{fg}$ = mass of flue gas per kg of fuel (kg/kg fuel)
- $c_{p,fg}$ = average specific heat of flue gas ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $T_{fg}$ = flue gas exit temperature (K)
- $T_{ref}$ = reference (combustion air) temperature (K)
- $GCV_{fuel}$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel (kJ/kg)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A 1% drop in $EA$ typically improves $\eta_{comb}$ by **~0.5% to 1.0%** in gaseous/oil-fired furnaces. This is the single most exploited short-term measure.

## 2.3 Excess Air — The Invisible Fuel Thief

$$
EA \;=\; \frac{(A_{actual} - A_{stoich})}{A_{stoich}} \times 100
$$

- **Optimum EA for gaseous fuels:** 5–10%
- **Optimum EA for light oil:** 5–15%
- **Optimum EA for heavy oil:** 15–20%
- **Optimum EA for solid fuels (coal):** 20–30%

> **Why Excess Air is a thief:** Air that does **not** participate in combustion still gets heated from $T_{ref}$ to $T_{fg}$, costing fuel for nothing. Each 1% excess air costs approximately **0.5% of the fuel input** as additional flue-gas loss.

## 2.4 The Siegert Formula for Flue-Gas Loss (High-Yield KTU Formula)

$$
\eta_{flue} \;=\; \frac{T_{fg} - T_{ref}}{T_{fg}} \times \frac{\%CO_2\,\text{max}}{\%CO_2\,\text{actual}}
$$

This **rule-of-thumb** is used by KTU-level auditors to estimate combustion efficiency using **portable flue-gas analyzer readings** ($T_{fg}$ and $\%CO_2$).

## 2.5 The 20 General Fuel-Economy Measures (Theory + Classification)

### A. Combustion-Side Measures (Reducing $Q_{flue}$)
1. **Optimization of excess air** to stoichiometric minimum.
2. **Preheating of combustion air** using waste flue-gas heat (recuperator).
3. **Preheating of fuel** (especially heavy oil to reduce viscosity and improve atomization).
4. **Oxygen enrichment** of combustion air to reduce flue-gas mass.
5. **Use of high-velocity burners / flameless oxidation** to improve heat transfer.
6. **Burner maintenance & tuning** (clean nozzles, correct air–fuel ratio).
7. **Replacement of outdated burners** with regenerative or high-intensity burners.

### B. Heat-Recovery Measures (Reusing $Q_{flue}$)
8. **Recuperators** (gas-to-gas heat exchangers on flue gas → combustion air).
9. **Regenerators** (alternating bed-type heat storage — used in glass/steel furnaces).
10. **Waste-heat boilers (WHB)** to generate steam from flue gases.
11. **Economizers** to preheat boiler feed-water from flue gas.
12. **Exhaust gas → charge preheater** (e.g., rotary hearth furnace).

### C. Charge-Side Measures (Improving $\phi$)
13. **Charge preheating** before insertion into the hot zone.
14. **Improved charge loading density** (more metal per batch).
15. **Hot charging** (transferring hot stock directly from upstream process).
16. **Optimal soak time** (avoiding over-heating).

### D. Furnace-Envelope Measures (Reducing $Q_{wall} + Q_{opening}$)
17. **Ceramic-fibre / refractory insulation** on walls, roof, doors.
18. **Door curtains, self-sealing doors, peek-flaps** (minimise $Q_{opening}$).
19. **Minimization of water-cooling** (use refractory skids, castables, dry-out boxes).
20. **Improved sealing** around charging doors, peep-holes, burner ports.
21. **Draft control** to prevent cold-air infiltration.

### E. Operational & Maintenance Measures
22. **Leak detection & repair (LDAR)** in combustion-air ducts.
23. **Continuous flue-gas monitoring** with O₂/CO trim controls.
24. **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** for furnace start-up, soak, shut-down.
25. **Use of high-emissivity coatings** on internal surfaces to enhance radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification Pattern (Must Memorise):**
> - *Short-term / Low-cost measures:* 1, 2, 5, 6, 17, 18, 21, 22, 23, 24.
> - *Medium-term measures:* 3, 4, 7, 8, 9, 11, 14, 15, 19, 20.
> - *Long-term / Capital-intensive:* 10, 12, 16, 25.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{charge}}{GCV \cdot m_{fuel}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency | % |
| 2 | $\eta_{comb} = 100 - \dfrac{m_{fg} \, c_{p,fg} \, (T_{fg} - T_{ref})}{GCV \cdot m_{fuel}}$ | Combustion efficiency | % |
| 3 | $EA = \dfrac{A_{act} - A_{stoi}}{A_{stoi}} \times 100$ | Excess air | % |
| 4 | $\eta_{flue} = \dfrac{T_{fg} - T_{ref}}{T_{fg}} \cdot \dfrac{\%CO_{2,\max}}{\%CO_{2,act}}$ | Siegert efficiency | % |
| 5 | $SFC = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{m_{charge}}$ | Specific fuel consumption | kg/tonne |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_{rec} = \dot{m}_{fg} \cdot c_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{fg,in} - T_{fg,out}) \cdot \eta_{HX}$ | Recoverable heat from flue gas | kJ/h |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{wall} = \dfrac{k_{ref} \cdot A_{wall}}{t_{wall}} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | Conduction loss through refractory | kJ/h |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{opening} = A_{op} \cdot F_{rad} \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{hot}^4 - T_{amb}^4)$ | Radiation loss through openings | kJ/h |
| 9 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\text{moles O}_2 \text{ needed}}{1} = \sum (n_C + 0.25 n_H - n_O)$ | Stoichiometric O₂ calculation | mol/mol fuel |
| 10 | $\phi = \dfrac{Q_{charge}}{Q_{charge} + Q_{struct}}$ | Heat distribution factor | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Utility:** These formulas are used directly by **BEE-certified Energy Auditors**, **ISO 50001 implementers**, and **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme assessors** in India. PEEET742 trains you for that exact profession.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Industry

| Industry | Furnace Type | Dominant Fuel-Economy Measure |
|---|---|---|
| Steel (Reheating) | Pusher / Walking-beam | Recuperator + Insulation + Hot charge |
| Steel (Coke-making) | Coke oven battery | Waste-heat boiler for tar/COG |
| Aluminum | Melting / Holding | Oxy-fuel burners + Regenerative burners |
| Glass | Glass tank | Regenerative heat recovery (80%+ recovery) |
| Cement | Rotary kiln | Preheater cyclone tower + cooler waste heat |
| Ceramics | Tunnel / Shuttle | High-emissivity roof + waste-heat drying |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### Derivation 1: Combustion Efficiency from First Principles

Starting from the steady-state furnace energy balance, ignoring $Q_{wall}$ and $Q_{opening}$ for a **clean combustion** analysis:

$$
\text{Heat released} \;=\; \text{Heat in flue gas} \;+\; \text{Heat in charge} \;+\; \text{Losses}
$$

If we define **combustion efficiency** as the fraction of the fuel's heat that does *not* leave as sensible heat in the dry flue gas, then:

**Step 1.** Write the heat-balance per kg of fuel fired:

$$
GCV_{fuel} \;=\; m_{fg} \cdot c_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{ref}) \;+\; \text{Other Losses}
$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange to isolate the flue-gas loss term:

$$
\text{Flue gas loss fraction} \;=\; \frac{m_{fg} \cdot c_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{ref})}{GCV_{fuel}}
$$

**Step 3.** Recognise that $\eta_{comb}$ = 1 – loss fraction, and convert to percentage:

$$
\eta_{comb} \;=\; \left( 1 \;-\; \frac{m_{fg} \cdot c_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{ref})}{GCV_{fuel}} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 4.** Note that $m_{fg}$ itself depends on $EA$:

$$
m_{fg} \;=\; m_{prod,\,gas} \;+\; (EA/100) \cdot m_{stoi,\,air} \cdot 1.293
$$

Substituting this into Step 3 gives the full analytical form. The student is *not* expected to integrate this in the exam; the simplified form in Section 2.3 is sufficient.

---

### Derivation 2: Fuel Saving from Combustion-Air Preheating

A recuperator raises combustion air from $T_{ref}$ to $T_{pre}$. The fraction of fuel energy recovered equals the enthalpy gain of the air stream divided by the fuel's GCV.

**Step 1.** Mass of preheated air per kg of fuel (at $EA$):

$$
m_{air} \;=\; m_{stoi,air} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{EA}{100} \right)
$$

**Step 2.** Enthalpy gained by this air in the recuperator:

$$
\Delta H_{air} \;=\; m_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{pre} - T_{ref})
$$

**Step 3.** Fraction of fuel energy replaced (assuming complete substitution):

$$
F_{save} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{air}}{GCV_{fuel}}
$$

**Step 4.** For a typical gas-fired furnace: $EA = 10\%$, $T_{ref} = 30°C$, $T_{pre} = 300°C$:

$$
m_{air} \;\approx\; 17.2 \,\text{kg/kg fuel (for methane)}
$$

$$
c_{p,air} \;\approx\; 1.005 \,\text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{air} \;=\; 17.2 \times 1.005 \times (300 - 30) \;=\; 4667 \,\text{kJ/kg fuel}
$$

$$
F_{save} \;=\; \frac{4667}{55000} \;\approx\; 8.5\%
$$

> **Interpretation (How to present this in the exam):** A simple **recuperator** that preheats combustion air from 30 °C to 300 °C can save about **8–10% of fuel** in a typical gas-fired furnace. This is a 7-marker explanation.

---

### Derivation 3: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a Hydrocarbon Fuel

For a fuel of the form $C_x H_y O_z S_w$, stoichiometric O₂ (and hence air) requirement is computed atom by atom.

**Step 1.** Carbon combustion:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{1 mol O}_2 \text{ per mol C}
$$

**Step 2.** Hydrogen combustion:

$$
\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{2H}_2\text{O} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{0.5 mol O}_2 \text{ per mol H}
$$

**Step 3.** Sulphur combustion:

$$
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \;\rightarrow\; \text{SO}_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{1 mol O}_2 \text{ per mol S}
$$

**Step 4.** Subtract any O already present in the fuel:

$$
N_{O_2,\,min} \;=\; x \;+\; \frac{y}{4} \;+\; w \;-\; \frac{z}{2}
$$

**Step 5.** Convert to air (1 mol O₂ needs 3.77 mol N₂, so 4.77 mol air per mol O₂):

$$
N_{air,\,min} \;=\; 4.77 \times N_{O_2,\,min}
$$

**Numerical example (Methane, CH₄):** $x = 1$, $y = 4$, $z = 0$, $w = 0$:

$$
N_{O_2,\,min} \;=\; 1 + \frac{4}{4} - 0 \;=\; 2
$$

$$
N_{air,\,min} \;=\; 4.77 \times 2 \;=\; 9.54 \,\text{mol air/mol CH}_4
$$

Mass basis (molecular weights: CH₄ = 16, air = 28.97):

$$
m_{air,\,min} \;=\; 9.54 \times \frac{28.97}{16} \;\approx\; 17.27 \,\text{kg air/kg CH}_4
$$

This is the **standard stoichiometric air value for methane = 17.2 kg/kg**, and any deviation above it is the $EA$ overhead.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Each Measure to the Loss It Eliminates

This matrix is the **highest-yield table for KTU 14-mark questions**.

| # | General Fuel-Economy Measure | Loss Term Eliminated | Typical Saving | Payback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optimisation of $EA$ via O₂ trim | $Q_{flue}$ | 1–3% | < 6 months |
| 2 | Recuperative air preheat | $Q_{flue}$ (recovered) | 5–15% | 1–2 years |
| 3 | Ceramic-fibre insulation | $Q_{wall}$ | 3–8% | 1–3 years |
| 4 | Door curtains / self-sealing doors | $Q_{opening}$ | 1–4% | < 1 year |
| 5 | Hot charging of stock | $Q_{charge}$ (recovered) | 8–25% | Process-dependent |
| 6 | Waste-heat boiler / steam gen. | $Q_{flue}$ (recovered) | 10–20% | 2–4 years |
| 7 | Burner replacement (high-velocity) | $Q_{flue}$ + improved $\phi$ | 3–6% | 2–3 years |
| 8 | Removal of water-cooling | $Q_{cooling}$ | 2–6% | 1–2 years |
| 9 | O₂-enrichment (oxy-fuel) | $Q_{flue}$ (reduced volume) | 10–30% | 3–5 years |
| 10 | Charge preheat by flue gas | $Q_{charge}$ (recovered) | 5–12% | 1–3 years |
| 11 | High-emissivity roof coating | Improves $\phi$ | 2–4% | < 1 year |
| 12 | Draft / pressure control | Infiltration loss | 1–3% | < 1 year |
| 13 | Combustion control (CO, O₂) trim | $Q_{flue}$ + unburned loss | 1–2% | < 1 year |
| 14 | Optimal charge loading density | $Q_{wall}$ per unit charge | 2–5% | < 1 year |
| 15 | Soak-time optimisation | $Q_{flue}$ per unit | 1–3% | Immediate |
| 16 | Regenerative burners | $Q_{flue}$ (recovered, 80%+) | 20–40% | 3–5 years |
| 17 | Preheating of oil fuel | $Q_{flue}$ (better comb.) | 1–2% | < 1 year |
| 18 | Seal leaks (LDAR) | Infiltration | 1–2% | < 6 months |
| 19 | Variable-speed drive on fans | Electrical | 5–15% on aux. | 1–2 years |
| 20 | Continuous monitoring & SOPs | Multiple | 2–5% | Immediate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Furnace Fuel-Economy Measure Map

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the *functional architecture* of how general fuel-economy measures attach to the three pillars of furnace energy use. Mermaid is used to keep the diagram text-readable and high-contrast (suitable for B&W printing of KTU notes).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Fuel["FUEL INPUT \n GCV = Chemical Energy"]:::in
    Comb["COMBUSTION \n CHAMBER \n FURNACE CORE"]:::core

    Qflue["FLUE GAS HEAT \n Qflue  30 to 60 percent"]:::loss
    Qwall["WALL / REFRACTORY LOSS \n Qwall  5 to 15 percent"]:::loss
    Qopen["OPENING RADIATION \n Qopening  1 to 5 percent"]:::loss
    Qcool["COOLING WATER LOSS \n Qcooling  2 to 8 percent"]:::loss

    Qcharge["CHARGE USEFUL HEAT \n Qcharge TARGET"]:::useful

    P1["PILLAR 1 \n REDUCE FUEL INPUT"]:::p1
    P2["PILLAR 2 \n REDUCE HEAT LOSS"]:::p2
    P3["PILLAR 3 \n RECOVER WASTE HEAT"]:::p3

    M1["EA Optimisation \n O2 Trim Control \n Burner Tuning"]:::m1
    M2["Ceramic Fibre Lining \n Door Curtains \n Remove Water Cooling \n LDAR Sealing"]:::m2
    M3["Recuperator \n Regenerator \n Waste Heat Boiler \n Charge Preheater"]:::m3

    Fuel --> Comb
    Comb --> Qflue
    Comb --> Qwall
    Comb --> Qopen
    Comb --> Qcool
    Comb --> Qcharge

    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M2
    P3 --> M3

    M1 -. Attacks .-> Qflue
    M2 -. Attacks .-> Qwall
    M2 -. Attacks .-> Qopen
    M2 -. Attacks .-> Qcool
    M3 -. Recovers .-> Qflue

```

    Stack["STACK EXIT \n Tfg2 200 to 400 C"]:::loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **left branch** (blue) is the *cold input* that becomes the *useful output* (green). The **right branch** (red → orange → yellow) is the *waste stream* being de-energised. Every fuel-economy measure in a furnace is a variant of this **counter-flow heat transfer pattern**.
