# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are modelled precisely on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination)** pattern for PEEET742. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year identifier.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
*List any six general fuel-economy measures applicable to a typical industrial re-heating furnace. For each, name the specific loss term it addresses.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Measure | Loss Term Addressed |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optimisation of excess air | Flue-gas sensible loss ($Q_{flue}$) |
| 2 | Ceramic-fibre insulation of walls & roof | Wall conduction + radiation loss ($Q_{wall}$) |
| 3 | Recuperative preheating of combustion air | Recovers part of $Q_{flue}$ |
| 4 | Door curtains / self-sealing charging doors | Opening radiation loss ($Q_{opening}$) |
| 5 | Replacement of water-cooled skids with refractory rolls | Cooling loss ($Q_{cooling}$) |
| 6 | O₂/CO trim combustion control | Flue-gas loss + unburned fuel loss |

*(Examiner's note: 0.5 mark per correct pair; full 3 marks for six correct pairs.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define "Excess Air" in furnace combustion. State the optimum range of excess air for (i) natural gas, (ii) heavy furnace oil, and (iii) pulverised coal.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Excess air is the *percentage of combustion air supplied in excess of the stoichiometric requirement* needed for complete combustion of the fuel.

$$
EA \;=\; \frac{A_{actual} - A_{stoich}}{A_{stoich}} \times 100
$$

**Optimum ranges (2 marks, 2/3 mark each — any two required):**

- (i) Natural gas: **5 – 10 %**
- (ii) Heavy furnace oil: **15 – 20 %**
- (iii) Pulverised coal: **20 – 30 %**

*(Higher $EA$ is required for heavier / solid fuels because mixing with air is inherently poorer.)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

#### Part (a) (7 marks)
*Explain with neat sketches the principle of a **recuperative air-preheater** as a fuel-economy measure in a furnace. Derive the expression for the percentage fuel saving obtained when combustion air is preheated from $T_1$ to $T_2$.*

#### Part (b) (7 marks)
*A gas-fired furnace burns 200 m³/h of natural gas (GCV = 38 MJ/m³) with 12% excess air. The flue gas leaves at 800 K. If the combustion air is preheated from 303 K to 500 K in a recuperator of effectiveness 0.7, calculate (i) the new fuel consumption, assuming the same charge throughput, and (ii) the percentage saving in fuel. Use the standard stoichiometric air value of 17.2 kg/kg for methane-equivalent gas.*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Principle and Derivation (7 marks)

**Principle (4 marks):**
A *recuperator* is a gas-to-gas heat exchanger that transfers sensible heat from the **hot flue gas** leaving the furnace to the **cold combustion air** entering the burners. It is a *static* (non-storage) device, as opposed to a regenerator. The cold and hot streams flow in **counter-flow** through metallic or ceramic tubes, with heat transferred by convection. The preheated air raises flame temperature, accelerates combustion kinetics, and reduces the fuel that would otherwise have been consumed to heat the incoming air up to flame temperature.

```
FLUE GAS (hot)  ===>  ========  ===>  STACK
                          \\\\
                           \\\ (heat transfer)
                            \\\\
COMBUSTION AIR (cold)  ==>  ========  ==>  BURNER
        (preheated)
```

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Enthalpy gained by combustion air per kg of fuel fired:

$$
\Delta H_{air} \;=\; m_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_2 - T_1)
$$

Fuel saved (as a fraction of input):

$$
F_{save} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{air}}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100
$$

With recuperator effectiveness $\varepsilon$:

$$
F_{save} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon \cdot m_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{2,in} - T_1)}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100
$$

**Assumption:** Fuel saving equals the enthalpy supplied to the air, neglecting any change in flue-gas mass flow (valid for low $EA$).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\dot{V}_{fuel,1} = 200 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $GCV = 38 \,000 \,\text{kJ/m}^3$
- $EA_1 = 12\%$, $T_{fg} = 800 \,K$, $T_1 = 303 \,K$, $T_2 = 500 \,K$
- $\varepsilon = 0.7$, $c_{p,air} = 1.005 \,\text{kJ/kg·K}$
- Stoichiometric air = 17.2 kg/kg fuel

**Step 1 — Mass of air per m³ of natural gas (1 mark):**

At $EA = 12\%$, mass of air per kg of fuel:

$$
m_{air} \;=\; 17.2 \times (1 + 0.12) \;=\; 19.264 \,\text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

Assume density of natural gas = 0.8 kg/m³, so per m³:

$$
m_{air} \;=\; 19.264 \times 0.8 \;\approx\; 15.41 \,\text{kg air/m}^3 \text{ fuel}
$$

**Step 2 — Enthalpy actually transferred in the recuperator (1 mark):**

Maximum possible enthalpy rise of air = $15.41 \times 1.005 \times (500 - 303)$

$$
= 15.41 \times 1.005 \times 197 \;\approx\; 3050 \,\text{kJ/m}^3 \text{ fuel}
$$

Actual (with $\varepsilon = 0.7$):

$$
\Delta H_{air,actual} \;=\; 0.7 \times 3050 \;\approx\; 2135 \,\text{kJ/m}^3 \text{ fuel}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent fuel saving per m³ of original fuel (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta V_{saved} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{air,actual}}{GCV} \;=\; \frac{2135}{38000} \;\approx\; 0.0562 \,\text{m}^3 \text{ fuel saved / m}^3 \text{ fired}
$$

**Step 4 — New fuel consumption (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{V}_{fuel,2} \;=\; \dot{V}_{fuel,1} \times (1 - 0.0562) \;=\; 200 \times 0.9438 \;\approx\; 188.8 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage saving (2 marks):**

$$
\% \text{Saving} \;=\; \frac{200 - 188.8}{200} \times 100 \;\approx\; 5.62\%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) New fuel consumption ≈ **188.8 m³/h**
- (ii) Fuel saving ≈ **5.6%**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

#### Part (a) (7 marks)
*Discuss the general fuel-economy measures applicable to a furnace, classifying them as **short-term (no/low cost)** and **long-term (capital-intensive)**. Give at least four examples in each category with justification.*

#### Part (b) (7 marks)
*A furnace has a wall of total area 12 m² made of fireclay brick of thickness 250 mm, thermal conductivity $k = 1.0 \,\text{W/m·K}$. The hot-face temperature is 1200 °C and the outer surface is at 80 °C. Calculate (i) the heat loss through the wall in kW, and (ii) the percentage fuel saving if insulation of 50 mm thickness ($k_{ins} = 0.15 \,\text{W/m·K}$) is added, assuming the inner hot-face temperature remains 1200 °C and the furnace uses 100 kg/h of oil (GCV = 42 MJ/kg).*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Classification (7 marks)

| Category | # | Measure | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-term / No-cost** | 1 | Trim excess air to optimum via O₂ analyser | Pure operational tuning, no hardware |
| | 2 | Clean & tune burners monthly | Restores spray pattern and air-fuel ratio |
| | 3 | Close all peep-holes and use door curtains | Immediate reduction in $Q_{opening}$ |
| | 4 | Stop air infiltration by sealing leaks (LDAR) | No investment, immediate fuel saving |
| **Short-term / Low-cost** | 5 | Apply high-emissivity black coating to internal walls | Coating material is cheap; raises $\phi$ |
| | 6 | Install O₂/CO trim controller | Pays back in <6 months |
| | 7 | Improve charge-loading density | Better scheduling, no capex |
| | 8 | Use SOPs for soak & shutdown | Eliminates wasteful over-heating |
| **Long-term / Capital-intensive** | 9 | Install ceramic-fibre lining on walls & roof | Material + shutdown cost |
| | 10 | Install recuperator for air preheating | Heat-exchanger + ducting capex |
| | 11 | Install regenerator (glass, coke ovens) | Two-bed alternating system, major civil work |
| | 12 | Install waste-heat boiler for steam generation | WHB + piping + auxiliaries |
| | 13 | Replace burners with high-velocity / oxy-fuel types | Capital + retrofit downtime |
| | 14 | Hot-charge upstream stock | Requires process integration |
| | 15 | Replace water-cooled panels with refractory | Long refractory life cycle |

*(Examiner awards: 0.5 mark per correct measure + 0.25 mark for correct justification; full 7 marks for ≥ 4+4 = 8 measures.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $A = 12 \,\text{m}^2$, $L_{brick} = 0.25 \,m$, $k_{brick} = 1.0 \,\text{W/m·K}$
- $T_{hot} = 1200°C$, $T_{cold} = 80°C$, $\Delta T_0 = 1120 \,K$
- $L_{ins} = 0.05 \,m$, $k_{ins} = 0.15 \,\text{W/m·K}$
- $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 100 \,\text{kg/h}$, $GCV = 42\,000 \,\text{kJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — Heat loss through bare wall (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{bare} \;=\; \frac{k_{brick} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_0}{L_{brick}} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \times 12 \times 1120}{0.25}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{bare} \;=\; 53\,760 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 53.76 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to heat loss per hour (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{bare,\,h} \;=\; 53.76 \times 3600 \;\approx\; 193\,536 \,\text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat loss through composite wall (brick + insulation) (2 marks):**

The hot-face is still 1200 °C; the outer face temperature will drop, but for the **same area and same brick inner face**, we compute new $R$-total:

$$
R_{total} \;=\; \frac{L_{brick}}{k_{brick} \cdot A} + \frac{L_{ins}}{k_{ins} \cdot A}
$$

$$
R_{total} \;=\; \frac{0.25}{1.0 \times 12} + \frac{0.05}{0.15 \times 12} \;=\; 0.02083 + 0.02778 \;\approx\; 0.04861 \,\text{K/W}
$$

We need the new cold-face temperature. Assume ambient $T_{\infty} = 30°C = 303 K$, with outer convection $h = 10 \,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, so $R_{conv} = 1/(10 \times 12) = 0.00833 \,\text{K/W}$.

Total $R$ = $0.04861 + 0.00833 = 0.05694 \,\text{K/W}$.

Heat flow:

$$
\dot{Q}_{ins} \;=\; \frac{1120}{0.05694} \;\approx\; 19\,670 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 19.67 \,\text{kW}
$$

*(For KTU-level calculation, the convection term may be neglected, yielding ~22.7 kW; both values are acceptable as long as the calculation path is shown. We use the **conservative simplified form** below for the saving.)*

**Simplified (no convection):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{ins,\,simple} \;=\; \frac{1120}{0.04861} \;\approx\; 23\,030 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 23.0 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat loss reduction (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta \dot{Q} \;=\; 53.76 - 23.0 \;\approx\; 30.76 \,\text{kW}
$$

$$
\Delta \dot{Q}_h \;=\; 30.76 \times 3600 \;\approx\; 110\,736 \,\text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage fuel saving (1 mark):**

Fuel energy input per hour:

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel,h} \;=\; 100 \times 42\,000 \;=\; 4\,200\,000 \,\text{kJ/h}
$$

$$
\%\text{Saving} \;=\; \frac{110\,736}{4\,200\,000} \times 100 \;\approx\; 2.64\%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Bare wall loss ≈ **53.76 kW**
- (ii) Insulation gives ≈ **2.6% fuel saving**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing** "excess air" with "theoretical air" — always subtract stoichiometric from actual.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply $m_{air}$ by $(1+EA/100)$** when air-mass per kg of fuel is asked — this is the single most-lost mark.
> 3. **Using $c_p$ of dry air (1.005 kJ/kg·K) for flue gas** when water-vapour fraction is significant — the moist-flue-gas $c_p$ is closer to 1.05–1.10 kJ/kg·K.
> 4. **In Siegert's formula**, students write $\%CO_2$ *measured* in the numerator — it must be in the **denominator** along with the maximum possible $\%CO_2$.
> 5. **In wall-loss problems**, students forget to include the **outer convection resistance** or assume cold-face temperature stays the same — it drops significantly when insulation is added.
> 6. **Listing measures without naming the loss term** — KTU 14-mark answers require explicit **mapping** of measure → loss term.
> 7. **Unit mix-up**: writing heat loss in kJ/s when the problem asks for kJ/h.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Must Memorise**

- [ ] The **3 pillars** of furnace fuel economy: **Reduce input, Reduce loss, Recover waste heat**.
- [ ] Furnace energy balance: $Q_{fuel} = Q_{charge} + Q_{flue} + Q_{wall} + Q_{opening} + Q_{cooling} + Q_{other}$.
- [ ] **Combustion efficiency** $\eta_{comb} = 100 - \dfrac{m_{fg} \cdot c_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{fg}-T_{ref})}{GCV \cdot m_{fuel}}$.
- [ ] **Excess air** definition and optimum ranges: gas 5–10 %, oil 15–20 %, coal 20–30 %.
- [ ] **Siegert efficiency** $\eta_{flue} = \dfrac{T_{fg} - T_{ref}}{T_{fg}} \cdot \dfrac{\%CO_{2,\max}}{\%CO_{2,act}}$.
- [ ] Stoichiometric air for **CH₄ = 17.2 kg/kg**; for **C₈H₁₈ ≈ 15.1 kg/kg**.
- [ ] A **recuperator** can save **5–15 %** of fuel; **regenerator** can save **20–40 %**.
- [ ] **Ceramic-fibre insulation** typically saves **3–8 %**.
- [ ] **Each 1 %** excess air ≈ **0.5 % extra fuel** lost.
- [ ] 25 general measures to know: 7 combustion-side + 5 heat-recovery + 4 charge-side + 5 envelope + 4 operational.
- [ ] Classification — **Short-term / No-cost** vs **Short-term / Low-cost** vs **Long-term / Capital-intensive**.
- [ ] **SFC = $m_{fuel} / m_{charge}$** is the industry's go-to KPI.
- [ ] **Heat distribution factor** $\phi = Q_{charge} / (Q_{charge} + Q_{struct})$ — the *internal* efficiency indicator.
- [ ] **Waste-heat boiler** + **economiser** + **recuperator** form the standard recovery train.
- [ ] **Oxy-fuel burners** reduce flue-gas volume by ~70 %, giving the largest single-measure saving.
- [ ] **LDAR (Leak Detection and Repair)** is a 0-capex, immediate-saving measure.
- [ ] **Door curtains** + **self-sealing doors** can save **1–4 %** without any engineering.
- [ ] **Hot charging** in steel industry saves **8–25 %** of fuel — a process-integration measure.
- [ ] **Water-cooled panels** consume **2–8 %** of fuel even when no heat is being transferred to them.
- [ ] In KTU answers, **always map** the measure to the loss term — that's where the marks are.
- [ ] Always state the **assumptions** (steady state, neglect of minor terms, reference temperature).
- [ ] The **Siegert formula** is the *only* formula the KTU examiner expects you to memorise verbatim.
- [ ] **Heat balance + at least 8 measures + numerical example** = full 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation Opportunities in Furnaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Conservation Opportunities in Furnaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Furnace (Industrial Definition):** A furnace is an enclosed combustion chamber designed to generate and transfer high-temperature thermal energy to a charge (solid, liquid, or gaseous material) for the purpose of achieving a specific metallurgical, chemical, or thermal transformation such as melting, refining, heat treatment, sintering, or reheating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Conservation Opportunity (ECO) in Furnaces:** A measurable, technically feasible, and economically viable engineering intervention that reduces specific energy consumption (SEC) of a furnace system while maintaining or improving product quality, throughput, and safety standards. ECOs are the cornerstone deliverables of a Type-2 / Type-3 Energy Audit as per the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) framework adopted by KTU-aligned industries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Pressure Cooker Intuition

Imagine a domestic LPG stove where a *pressure cooker* is being used to boil rice. Now think about three common kitchen mistakes:

1. **The Pot is Open** — Heat escapes freely. Closing the lid traps the energy (this is analogous to **sealing furnace openings and improving insulation**).
2. **Burning Too Much Gas** — The blue flame is barely visible; a roaring yellow flame means excess air is being pulled in, carrying heat straight up the chimney. This is exactly what happens in a furnace with **excess combustion air** — hot flue gases escape with wasted enthalpy.
3. **Letting Steam Out Continuously** — Without a regulator, the cooker wastes steam. In a furnace, **waste heat recovery** (like the cooker's gasket or whistle) recaptures flue-gas heat to preheat incoming air or water.

A furnace in a steel or aluminum plant works on the same physics — only the volumes are **10,000 times larger** and the stakes are measured in crores of rupees per year. Energy conservation opportunities simply close these "open lids", trim the "roaring flame", and install "better regulators" at industrial scale.

### 1.3 Classification of Industrial Furnaces (Foundation for ECO Identification)

| Furnace Class | Typical Industry | Energy Source | Operating Temp. Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reverberatory Furnace** | Aluminium, Copper, Glass | Furnace oil / Natural gas | 700 – 1200 °C |
| **Cupola Furnace** | Foundry (Cast Iron) | Coke + Hot blast | 1400 – 1600 °C |
| **Induction Furnace** | Steel, Special alloys | Electrical | 1500 – 1700 °C |
| **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** | Steelmaking | Electrical | 1600 – 1800 °C |
| **Rotary Kiln** | Cement, Lime | Coal / Pet coke | 900 – 1450 °C |
| **Reheating Furnace** | Steel rolling mills | Natural gas / Oil | 1100 – 1250 °C |
| **Heat Treatment Furnace** | Forgings, Castings | Gas / Electric | 600 – 950 °C |
| **Pusher / Walking Beam** | Continuous steel plants | Mixed fuel | 1150 – 1280 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Benchmark (BEE / PAT Scheme):** The typical specific energy consumption for a well-designed gas-fired reheating furnace is **1.2 – 1.8 GCal/tonne** of steel heated. An inefficient unit may consume **2.5 – 3.5 GCal/tonne**, representing an immediate ECO potential of **30 – 50 %**.

### 1.4 The Five Pillars of Furnace Energy Conservation

Every ECO in a furnace falls into one of the following five functional pillars:

1. **Combustion Efficiency Improvement** (air-fuel ratio, burner tuning)
2. **Heat Recovery from Waste Streams** (regenerative, recuperative, economisers)
3. **Thermal Insulation & Refractory Management** (wall losses, door seals)
4. **Process & Charge Optimization** (load scheduling, soaking time)
5. **Control & Automation Upgrade** (PLC, oxygen trim, oxygen-enriched combustion)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Balance of a Furnace — A Simple Statement of Conservation

For any steady-state furnace, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the control volume gives the master equation:

$$\boxed{Q_{fuel} + Q_{air} + Q_{charge} = Q_{useful} + Q_{flue} + Q_{wall} + Q_{opening} + Q_{cooling\;water} + Q_{unaccounted}}$$

Where every term on the right-hand side represents an **irrecoverable or partially recoverable loss** — and every ECO targets one or more of these loss terms.

### 2.2 The Three Primary Energy Losses (Decomposition Logic)

#### 2.2.1 Flue Gas (Stack) Loss — *Usually 50 – 75 % of input energy*

The dominant loss. Hot combustion products leave the chimney carrying both **sensible heat** and **latent heat of water vapour**.

#### 2.2.2 Wall (Surface) Loss — *Usually 5 – 20 % of input energy*

Conduction through refractory walls, radiation through openings, and conduction through furnace furniture.

#### 2.2.3 Other Losses — *Usually 5 – 15 % of input energy*

Cooling water带走 (带走 = carry-away) heat, opening radiation, incomplete combustion, and unaccounted losses (typically 1 – 3 %).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following 14 formulas are the **highest-yield** equations for the KTU End Semester Examination on Furnace ECOs. Memorise units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{combustion} = 100 - \dfrac{L_{flue}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency; $L_{flue}$ = flue-gas enthalpy loss | 70 – 95 % |
| 2 | $AER(\%) = \dfrac{O_2^{actual} - O_2^{stoich}}{O_2^{stoich}} \times 100$ | Air Excess Ratio (also written as **%EA** or $\lambda$) | 5 – 25 % in efficient furnaces |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{21}{21 - O_2^{dry}}$ | Actual air-fuel ratio to stoichiometric ratio | 1.05 – 1.25 |
| 4 | $Q_{flue} = \dot{m}_{flue} \cdot C_{p,flue} \cdot (T_{flue} - T_{ref})$ | Sensible heat loss in flue gas (kW) | — |
| 5 | $L_{flue}(\%) = \dfrac{T_{flue} - T_{ref}}}{\dfrac{CO_2^{max}}{CO_2^{actual}}} \times \text{constant}$ | Siegert's empirical flue-gas loss formula | For natural gas: 0.65 × ($T_{flue}-T_{ref}$) / $CO_2\%$ |
| 6 | $Q_{wall} = \dfrac{k}{t} \cdot A \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | Conduction loss through refractory (Fourier's Law) | 0.5 – 3.0 kW/m² |
| 7 | $q_{rad,opening} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_{opening} \cdot (T_{hot}^{4} - T_{amb}^{4})$ | Radiation heat loss through furnace openings | Stefan-Boltzmann, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ |
| 8 | $\eta_{furnace} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{fuel}} = \dfrac{m_{charge} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{m_{fuel} \cdot GCV} \times 100$ | Overall furnace thermal efficiency | 20 – 60 % typical |
| 9 | $SEC = \dfrac{Q_{fuel}}{m_{product}} \; \left[\dfrac{GCal}{tonne}\right]$ | Specific Energy Consumption | 1.2 – 3.5 GCal/t for reheat |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{recup} = \dot{m}_{flue} \cdot C_{p,flue} \cdot (T_{flue,in} - T_{flue,out}) \cdot \eta_{HX}$ | Recovered heat by recuperator (kW) | 30 – 70 % recovery |
| 11 | $\dot{Q}_{regen} = \dot{m}_{flue} \cdot h_{flue,in}$ (per cycle, divided by cycle time) | Regenerative burner heat recovery (kW) | up to 80 – 90 % |
| 12 | $N_{2,stoich} = 3.76 \times \dfrac{n_{O_2,stoich}}{1}$ | Stoichiometric nitrogen volume (combustion air = 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂) | — |
| 13 | $T_{adiabatic\;flame} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{n_{products} \cdot C_{p,products}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | 1800 – 2300 °C |
| 14 | $O_2^{stoich} = 0 \; ; \quad O_2^{actual} = 21 \times \dfrac{(\lambda - 1)}{\lambda}$ | Theoretical and actual O₂ in dry flue gas | — |

### 2.4 Why Each Pillar Matters — Engineering Utility

* **Combustion tuning** affects both *fuel cost* (direct) and *NOx emissions* (regulatory). A 1 % drop in excess air saves approximately **0.5 – 0.8 %** of fuel input in a gas-fired furnace.
* **Heat recovery** converts a 200 – 400 °C waste stream into 100 – 250 °C preheated combustion air, raising flame temperature and reducing fuel demand by **15 – 35 %**.
* **Refractory selection** with low thermal conductivity (e.g., ceramic fibre, $k \approx 0.05$ W/mK) versus dense firebrick ($k \approx 1.2$ W/mK) reduces wall losses by **60 – 80 %** at the same thickness.
* **Process scheduling** (charging a furnace to full capacity, "just-in-time" discharge) reduces radiation openings and off-load idling losses which can otherwise account for **10 – 25 %** of input.
* **Automation** (oxygen-trim controllers, infrared pyrometers) maintains combustion within ±2 % of the optimum setpoint, eliminating the human-operator variability of **±10 – 15 %**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric Quick Reference (per kg of fuel):**
> - **Natural Gas (CH₄):** 17.2 kg air/kg fuel
> - **Furnace Oil:** 14.3 kg air/kg fuel
> - **Coal (carbon):** 11.5 kg air/kg fuel
> - **Hydrogen (H₂):** 34.5 kg air/kg fuel

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Flue-Gas Loss as a Function of Excess Air

**Statement:** Show that flue-gas loss increases nearly linearly with excess air for a given flue-gas temperature, and identify the economically optimum excess air.

**Step 1 — Mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel.**
Let the fuel be natural gas (CH₄) for which stoichiometric combustion is:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

Per kg of CH₄: 1 kg fuel + 4 kg O₂ + 15.04 kg N₂ → 2.75 kg CO₂ + 2.25 kg H₂O + 15.04 kg N₂ (stoichiometric).

**Step 2 — Introduce excess air multiplier.**
Let the air-fuel ratio be $A$ (kg air/kg fuel), and stoichiometric air = $A_s$. Define $\lambda = A/A_s$.
The actual air supplied is $A = \lambda \cdot A_s$.
The *additional* air beyond stoichiometric is $(\lambda - 1) \cdot A_s$ and exits unchanged in the flue gas.

**Step 3 — Write flue-gas composition per kg of fuel.**
For $\lambda$ = 1.20 (i.e., 20 % excess air):

$$\dot{m}_{flue} = \underbrace{2.75}_{CO_2} + \underbrace{2.25}_{H_2O} + \underbrace{15.04 \cdot 1.20}_{N_2} + \underbrace{4 \cdot 0.20}_{O_2\,excess}$$

$$\dot{m}_{flue} = 2.75 + 2.25 + 18.048 + 0.80 = 23.848 \; \text{kg flue/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Sensible enthalpy of flue gas (assume $C_{p,avg} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K).**
Take flue gas temperature $T_{flue} = 600$ °C, reference $T_{ref} = 27$ °C, so $\Delta T = 573$ K.

$$Q_{flue} = 23.848 \times 1.05 \times 573 = 14{,}348 \; \text{kJ/kg fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to GCal/tonne of steel heated.**
If 1 tonne of steel is heated by 60 kg of natural gas (GCV = 52,000 kJ/kg):

$$SEC_{flue-loss} = \dfrac{14{,}348 \times 60}{10^6} = 0.861 \; GCal/t$$

**Step 6 — Repeat at $\lambda = 1.05$ (5 % EA) for comparison.**
Air supplied = $1.05 \times 19.04 = 19.99$ kg.
Flue mass = 2.75 + 2.25 + 15.04 × 1.05 + 4 × 0.05 = **22.038 kg**.
Flue loss per kg fuel = 22.038 × 1.05 × 573 = **13,259 kJ/kg**.
SEC loss = 13,259 × 60 / 10⁶ = **0.796 GCal/t**.

**Step 7 — Compare savings.**
Savings by trimming EA from 20 % → 5 %:

$$\Delta SEC = 0.861 - 0.796 = 0.065 \; GCal/t$$

For 100,000 tonnes/year at ₹800/GCal:

$$\text{Annual saving} = 0.065 \times 10^5 \times 800 = ₹52{,}00{,}000 / \text{year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Optimising excess air from 20 % to 5 % saves **₹52 lakh per year** on a single furnace producing 100,000 t/yr — purely from a control-loop retrofit. This is a *typical* KTU 14-mark problem.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — ECO Identification vs. Loss Stream

This matrix is **directly tested** in KTU 14-mark questions. Memorise the linkages:

| ECO Category | Specific Measure | Loss Stream Targeted | Typical Payback | SEC Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Combustion tuning | Install O₂ trim controller | Flue-gas sensible heat | 6 – 12 months | 2 – 5 % |
| Burner upgrade | Low-NOx regenerative burners | Flue-gas + wall losses | 1.5 – 3 years | 15 – 30 % |
| Waste heat recovery | Recuperator (metallic/ceramic) | Flue-gas sensible heat | 1 – 3 years | 10 – 20 % |
| Waste heat recovery | Economiser (water-side) | Flue-gas to feedwater | 1 – 2.5 years | 5 – 12 % |
| Insulation | Ceramic-fibre module lining | Wall (conduction) loss | 1 – 2 years | 5 – 15 % |
| Door & openings | Radiation shields, automatic doors | Opening radiation loss | 6 – 18 months | 3 – 8 % |
| Charge preheating | Charge preheater with flue gas | Process sensible heat | 1.5 – 3 years | 8 – 18 % |
| Process control | PLC + IR pyrometer | All losses (stabilisation) | 1 – 2 years | 3 – 10 % |
| Charge optimisation | Full-load scheduling | Idling & opening losses | < 6 months | 5 – 12 % |
| Oxygen enrichment | Oxy-fuel combustion | Flue-gas mass reduction | 2 – 4 years | 20 – 40 % |
| Slag/dross management | Clean slag, fewer re-melts | Process losses | < 1 year | 2 – 6 % |
| Coke replacement | Pulverised coal injection (cupola) | Combustion efficiency | 2 – 4 years | 8 – 15 % |

### 3.3 Derivation: Radiation Loss Through a Furnace Door Opening

**Step 1 — Apply Stefan-Boltzmann Law.**
For a small opening treated as a black-body cavity (emissivity $\varepsilon \approx 1$):

$$q_{rad} = \sigma \cdot A_{opening} \cdot (T_{furnace}^{4} - T_{amb}^{4})$$

with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴.

**Step 2 — Insert typical values.**
$T_{furnace} = 1500$ K; $T_{amb} = 300$ K; $A = 0.5$ m² (a typical 700 mm × 700 mm charging door).

$$\begin{aligned}
T^4_{furnace} &= 1500^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{12} \\
T^4_{amb} &= 300^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9} \\
\Delta &= 5.0544 \times 10^{12} \\
q_{rad} &= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.5 \times 5.0544 \times 10^{12} \\
q_{rad} &= 143{,}300 \; W \approx 143 \; kW
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to annual loss.**
If the door is open 25 % of the operational time (6000 hours/year):

$$Q_{annual} = 143 \times 0.25 \times 6000 = 214{,}500 \; kWh = 0.184 \; GCal$$

**Step 4 — Translate to monetary terms.**
At a fuel cost of ₹2.5/kWh (electrical equivalent of furnace gas):

$$\text{Door-radiation loss} = 214{,}500 \times 2.5 = ₹53{,}62{,}500 \;/\; \text{year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **A single unsealed charging door can cost an Indian industry over ₹50 lakh per year** in wasted radiation. This is why the KTU examiner often tests the simple Stefan-Boltzmann calculation — the *order of magnitude* is the mark-distinguishing insight.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Mapping (Block Description)

**Input Layer (Blue)** — Compressed air, atomised fuel, and cold charge enter the system. This is the *demand side* where ECOs such as combustion-air preheating act.

**Transformation Core (Dark Blue)** — Burners mix air and fuel, refractories contain the heat, and the charge absorbs the energy. ECOs here are *internal* (burner upgrade, refractory change).

**Loss Layer (Red)** — Three streams: stack flue gas, wall conduction, and door radiation. Each is targeted by a *specific* ECO module.

**ECO Layer (Gold)** — Six intervention blocks: O₂ trim, recuperator, regenerative burner, ceramic-fibre insulation, automatic door, and economiser. Each maps bijectively to one or more loss streams.

**Recovery Layer (Green)** — Captured heat returns to the input side as preheated air or preheated water, closing the energy loop and reducing net fuel demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The diagram shows the *bijective* relationship between loss streams and ECO modules — a feature the KTU examiner uses in 14-mark descriptive questions. Memorising this 1-to-1 mapping is worth **3 – 4 marks** by itself.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Set B]**
Define **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for a furnace. State the typical SEC range for a gas-fired reheating furnace and explain why it is a preferred performance metric over absolute fuel consumption.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the quantity of energy consumed per unit mass of product processed, expressed typically in **GCal/tonne** or **MJ/kg**.

For a gas-fired reheating furnace in the steel industry, the typical SEC lies in the range of **1.2 – 1.8 GCal/tonne** for an efficient unit and **2.5 – 3.5 GCal/tonne** for an inefficient one.

SEC is preferred over absolute fuel consumption because it **normalises performance against production output**, allowing direct comparison between furnaces of different capacities, ages, and duty cycles. It also enables benchmarking across plants and against BEE / PAT scheme norms.

*(Word-count: ~70 words. Marks split: 1 for definition, 1 for typical value, 1 for rationale.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
What is **excess air** in furnace combustion? Why is some excess air necessary, and what is the optimum range for a natural-gas-fired furnace?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Excess air** is the volume of combustion air supplied *beyond* the stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage (%EA) or as the air-fuel ratio equivalence ratio $\lambda$.

Some excess air is essential to ensure **complete combustion** and to prevent the formation of CO, soot, and unburnt hydrocarbons. Insufficient air leads to energy losses through *incomplete combustion*, which is far costlier than the small loss incurred from moderate excess air.

For a natural-gas-fired furnace, the optimum excess-air range is **5 – 10 %** ($\lambda \approx 1.05$ – $1.10$). Below 5 %, CO spikes; above 10 %, flue-gas sensible losses rise sharply.

*(Word-count: ~75 words. Marks split: 1 for definition, 1 for necessity, 1 for optimum range with reasoning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **five major categories of energy conservation opportunities** in industrial furnaces. For each category, give one specific engineering measure and its typical fuel-saving percentage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A natural-gas-fired reheating furnace processes 50 tonnes of steel per day. The fuel consumption is 4.0 tonnes/day of natural gas (GCV = 52,000 kJ/kg). The flue gas leaves at 650 °C with an O₂ content of 8 % (dry basis). The reference temperature is 27 °C. Calculate:
   (i) the **actual air-fuel ratio** and the **percentage excess air**, and
   (ii) the **sensible heat loss in the flue gas** per day, in GCal.

Assume the average specific heat of dry flue gas as **1.05 kJ/kg·K** and that the air composition is 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ by volume. Use stoichiometric air for CH₄ as **17.2 kg air/kg fuel**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The five major categories of ECOs in industrial furnaces are:

**1. Combustion Efficiency Improvement (≈ 1 mark)**
Measure: Installation of **oxygen-trim controllers** on the burner air supply.
Typical saving: **2 – 5 %** of fuel input.
Explanation: Continuously adjusts the air register to maintain O₂ at the optimum setpoint, eliminating operator-induced variability.

**2. Waste Heat Recovery (≈ 1.5 marks)**
Measure: Installation of a **recuperative heat exchanger** to preheat combustion air using flue gas.
Typical saving: **10 – 20 %** of fuel input.
Explanation: Recovers 30 – 60 % of the sensible enthalpy of the flue gas and returns it to the process.

**3. Refractory & Insulation Upgrade (≈ 1.5 marks)**
Measure: Replacement of dense firebrick with **ceramic-fibre modules** ($k \approx 0.05$ W/mK).
Typical saving: **5 – 15 %** of fuel input.
Explanation: Reduces conduction losses through the furnace shell by 60 – 80 % at equivalent thickness.

**4. Process & Charge Optimisation (≈ 1.5 marks)**
Measure: **Full-load scheduling** to minimise idle periods and door-open time.
Typical saving: **5 – 12 %** of fuel input.
Explanation: Avoids radiation losses and cycling inefficiencies.

**5. Control & Automation (≈ 1.5 marks)**
Measure: Deployment of **PLC with infrared pyrometers** for closed-loop temperature control.
Typical saving: **3 – 10 %** of fuel input.
Explanation: Eliminates temperature overshoot and reduces soaking time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the Examiner:** "**C**ombustion, **W**aste heat, **R**efractory, **P**rocess, **C**ontrol" — **CWRPC**.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Production: 50 t/day steel
- Fuel: 4.0 t/day natural gas; GCV = 52,000 kJ/kg
- $T_{flue} = 650$ °C, $T_{ref} = 27$ °C
- $O_2^{dry} = 8$ %
- $C_{p,flue} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K
- Stoichiometric air: 17.2 kg/kg fuel
- Air composition: 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂

**Step 1 — Compute $\lambda$ (air-fuel equivalence ratio).** *(1 Mark)*

$$\lambda = \dfrac{21}{21 - O_2^{dry}} = \dfrac{21}{21 - 8} = \dfrac{21}{13} = 1.615$$

**Step 2 — Compute percentage excess air.** *(1 Mark)*

$$\%EA = (\lambda - 1) \times 100 = 0.615 \times 100 = 61.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute actual air-fuel ratio.** *(1 Mark)*

$$A_{actual} = \lambda \times A_{stoich} = 1.615 \times 17.2 = 27.78 \; kg\;air/kg\;fuel$$

**Step 4 — Compute flue-gas mass per kg fuel (conservation of mass).** *(2 Marks)*

For complete combustion of CH₄ + 1.615 × (17.2/4) [O₂] (using $\lambda$), the flue-gas composition per kg CH₄:
- CO₂: 2.75 kg (from stoichiometry)
- H₂O: 2.25 kg
- N₂: $1.615 \times 17.2 \times 0.79 = 21.95$ kg
- O₂ (excess): $0.615 \times 17.2 \times 0.21 = 2.22$ kg

$$\dot{m}_{flue} = 2.75 + 2.25 + 21.95 + 2.22 = 29.17 \; kg\;flue/kg\;fuel$$

**Step 5 — Sensible heat loss per kg fuel.** *(1 Mark)*

$$q_{flue} = 29.17 \times 1.05 \times (650 - 27) = 29.17 \times 1.05 \times 623 = 19{,}082 \; kJ/kg\;fuel$$

**Step 6 — Total daily loss.** *(1 Mark)*

$$Q_{flue,daily} = 19{,}082 \times 4000 = 7.633 \times 10^7 \; kJ/day$$

Convert to GCal (1 GCal = $4.184 \times 10^6$ kJ):

$$Q_{flue,daily} = \dfrac{7.633 \times 10^7}{4.184 \times 10^6} = 18.24 \; GCal/day$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> - **Air-fuel ratio (A/F) = 27.78 kg air/kg fuel**
> - **Excess air = 61.5 %** (this is *very* high — a strong hint to the examiner that the furnace is poorly tuned)
> - **Sensible flue-gas loss = 18.24 GCal/day**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **waste heat recovery techniques** applicable to industrial furnaces. Compare **recuperative** and **regenerative** heat-recovery systems with respect to working principle, heat-transfer effectiveness, capital cost, and payback period. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cupola furnace operates at 1500 °C internal temperature. The charging door measures 600 mm × 600 mm and is opened 15 % of the operating time (operating 6000 hours/year). Using Stefan-Boltzmann law, calculate:
   (i) the **instantaneous radiation heat loss** through the open door, and
   (ii) the **annual energy loss in GCal and in monetary terms**, assuming fuel cost is ₹60/kg of furnace oil with GCV = 42,000 kJ/kg.

Assume the door acts as a black-body cavity, ambient temperature 30 °C, and $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Waste heat recovery (WHR) captures the enthalpy of hot flue gas (typically 200 – 800 °C) and returns it to the process, reducing net fuel demand.

**Recuperative System (3 Marks):**
- **Principle:** Continuous heat exchange through a metallic or ceramic wall separating hot flue gas and cold combustion air.
- **Components:** Metallic tube banks (up to 800 °C), ceramic tube recuperators (up to 1200 °C), or radiation recuperators.
- **Effectiveness:** 40 – 65 % typically; preheated air reaches 200 – 400 °C.
- **Capital cost:** Moderate (₹25 – 50 lakh for medium furnace).
- **Payback:** 1.5 – 3 years.
- **Application:** Continuous furnaces with steady flue gas flow.

**Regenerative System (3 Marks):**
- **Principle:** Cyclic operation — hot flue gas heats a refractory checker bed for ~30 s, then flow is reversed and cold air is preheated by the hot checker for ~30 s.
- **Components:** Two refractory chambers (silica, alumina, or magnesia bricks), switching valves, burners integrated with checkers (RRE burners).
- **Effectiveness:** Up to 80 – 90 %; preheated air reaches 800 – 1200 °C.
- **Capital cost:** High (₹1 – 3 crore).
- **Payback:** 2 – 4 years.
- **Application:** High-temperature furnaces (steel, glass, aluminium).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Comparison:** Regenerative systems are *superior* in heat recovery but *inferior* in control simplicity and capital outlay. Recuperators are the "default" choice for moderate-temperature furnaces.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $T_{furnace} = 1500$ °C = 1773 K
- $T_{amb} = 30$ °C = 303 K
- Door area $A = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36$ m²
- Door-open fraction = 0.15
- Operating time = 6000 h/year
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴

**Step 1 — Stefan-Boltzmann instantaneous loss.** *(2 Marks)*

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{rad} &= \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_{furnace}^{4} - T_{amb}^{4}) \\
T_{furnace}^{4} &= 1773^4 = 9.880 \times 10^{12} \\
T_{amb}^{4} &= 303^4 = 8.43 \times 10^{9} \\
\Delta T^4 &= 9.872 \times 10^{12} \\
q_{rad} &= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.36 \times 9.872 \times 10^{12} \\
q_{rad} &= 2.015 \times 10^5 \; W = 201.5 \; kW
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Annual energy loss (kWh).** *(2 Marks)*

$$E_{annual} = 201.5 \times 6000 \times 0.15 = 181{,}350 \; kWh$$

Convert to kJ:

$$E_{annual} = 181{,}350 \times 3600 = 6.529 \times 10^8 \; kJ$$

**Step 3 — Convert to GCal.** *(1 Mark)*

$$E_{annual} = \dfrac{6.529 \times 10^8}{4.184 \times 10^6} = 156.0 \; GCal$$

**Step 4 — Convert to furnace oil equivalent.** *(1 Mark)*

$$\text{Fuel equivalent} = \dfrac{6.529 \times 10^8}{42{,}000} = 15{,}545 \; kg \approx 15.5 \; \text{tonnes of FO}$$

**Step 5 — Monetary loss.** *(1 Mark)*

$$\text{Rupee loss} = 15{,}545 \times 60 = ₹9{,}32{,}700 \;/\; \text{year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> - **Instantaneous radiation loss = 201.5 kW**
> - **Annual loss = 156 GCal = 15.5 t of FO = ₹9.33 lakh/year**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** if you forget to convert °C to Kelvin before applying the Stefan-Boltzmann law. Always state "$T_{furnace} = 1500 + 273 = 1773$ K" *explicitly*.
> 2. **Forgetting the door-open fraction** — Multiplying by 6000 h *without* the 15 % fraction overestimates the loss by **6.7×**.
> 3. **GCV unit mismatch** — Some students use kcal/kg GCV and forget to convert to kJ/kg. This single error costs **2 marks**.
> 4. **Using furnace emissivity as 1.0 without justification** — In a real cupola, $\varepsilon \approx 0.85$. Acceptable for KTU if the problem states "black-body", but always *state the assumption*.
> 5. **Neglecting convective loss at the door** — In a 14-mark question, mentioning this as a secondary effect (and the reason why the answer is a *lower bound*) earns a bonus mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Card — Memorise This Block:**

- ☐ **SEC benchmark for reheat furnace:** 1.2 – 1.8 GCal/t (efficient) | 2.5 – 3.5 GCal/t (poor)
- ☐ **Optimum excess air:** 5 – 10 % for natural gas, 10 – 15 % for oil, 15 – 25 % for coal
- ☐ **Flue-gas loss dominates:** 50 – 75 % of input energy leaves through the stack
- ☐ **Stoichiometric air:** 17.2 (NG) | 14.3 (FO) | 11.5 (Coke-C) | 34.5 (H₂) — kg air/kg fuel
- ☐ **Five ECO Pillars:** Combustion | Waste Heat | Refractory | Process | Control
- ☐ **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ — always use **Kelvin**
- ☐ **Recuperative vs. Regenerative:** Recuperator is continuous and cheaper; regenerator is cyclic and 80 – 90 % effective
- ☐ **Bijective ECO–Loss mapping:** O₂ trim → flue gas | Recuperator → flue gas | Ceramic fibre → wall | Auto door → radiation | Economiser → flue gas → water
- ☐ **Ceramic-fibre insulation:** $k \approx 0.05$ W/mK — saves 60 – 80 % wall loss vs. firebrick ($k \approx 1.2$ W/mK)
- ☐ **Order-of-magnitude trap:** A 0.5 m² open door at 1500 K loses ~140 kW — easy 3-mark derivation
- ☐ **Key formula to remember:** $\lambda = 21/(21 - O_2^{dry})$ — used in nearly every furnace numerical
- ☐ **PAT scheme reference:** Indian Perform, Achieve, Trade scheme penalises SEC > 2.0 GCal/t for reheat furnaces
- ☐ **Quick money check:** 1 GCal/tonne saved × 1 lakh tonnes/year × ₹800/GCal = **₹80 crore/year** potential
- ☐ **Mnemonic CWRPC:** Combustion, Waste heat, Refractory, Process, Control — the 5 ECO categories
- ☐ **Exam formula list to revise night before:** $\eta_{comb}$, $AER$, $\lambda$, $q_{rad}$, $SEC$, $Q_{flue}$ — 14 formulas in the cheat-sheet table
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HVAC System: Performance and Saving Opportunities in Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HVAC System: Performance and Saving Opportunities in Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning)** is an integrated building system technology that regulates indoor environmental parameters — primarily **temperature**, **humidity**, **air purity (IAQ)**, and **air distribution** — to provide thermal comfort and process-conditioning support. The *performance* of an HVAC/refrigeration system is quantified using thermodynamic and electrical metrics such as the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)**, **Seasonal EER (SEER)**, **Integrated Part Load Value (IPLV)**, and the **kW/TR (kiloWatt per Ton of Refrigeration)** index. *Saving opportunities* refer to the engineered measures, operational best practices, and retrofits identified during an energy audit that reduce the specific energy consumption (SEC) of these systems without compromising the delivered thermal comfort or process output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to (i) compute and interpret HVAC performance indices, (ii) identify wastage points in compressors, condensers, evaporators, chilled-water networks, and air-handling units, and (iii) recommend energy conservation measures (ECMs) with payback justification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your **refrigerator at home** is a tiny HVAC system. It "moves" heat from the inside (cold zone) to the outside (kitchen air). Now, think of a **commercial building's AC** as a *giant refrigerator* cooling an entire office. 

- If the fridge's compressor runs *continuously* at full speed even when the door is mostly closed, it wastes electricity. The smarter design is to run it slower, only as much as needed — that is exactly what a **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** does.
- If the fridge's door seal is broken, cold air leaks out, forcing the compressor to work harder. The HVAC equivalent is **poor building insulation** or **open windows**.
- If the fridge's condenser coil (the hot grille on the back) is covered in dust, heat cannot escape, and the compressor overheats. In building AC, this is the **condenser / cooling tower** choking up.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Universal Energy Equation for HVAC:**
> $$\text{Total Energy Saved} = \text{Reduced Load} + \text{Improved Efficiency} + \text{Recovered Waste Heat}$$
> Any successful HVAC energy audit must attack at least one of these three pillars — preferably all three.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

- **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** = **3.517 kW** of heat removal = **12,000 BTU/hr**
- **Conversion:** 1 kW = 3412.14 BTU/hr
- **Standard Rating Condition (ARI/AHRI):** Evaporator 7.2 °C, Condenser 54.4 °C, Return air 26.7 °C DB / 19.4 °C WB
- **Carnot (Ideal) COP for refrigeration:**
$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$
- **Carnot COP for heat pump:**
$$\text{COP}_{\text{HP,Carnot}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Temperatures in Carnot equations **must** be in Kelvin. Converting °C → K by adding 273.15.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of HVAC Energy Use

**Pillar A — The Cooling Load (Demand Side)**

This is the heat that the building *demands* to be removed. It is composed of:

1. **Envelope Load** — heat conducted through walls, roof, floor, glass.
2. **Solar Load** — direct radiation through glazing and absorbed by the structure.
3. **Internal Load (Occupants & Equipment)** — people, lights, computers, machinery.
4. **Infiltration & Ventilation Load** — uncontrolled outside air leakage and required fresh air.
5. **Process Load** — heat from industrial processes inside the conditioned space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Audit Insight:** If the cooling load itself is reduced (better insulation, LED retrofits, sun-control film), the compressor works less and **automatically** the electricity bill drops. This is the cheapest kWh to save — it is *avoided*, not *generated*.

**Pillar B — The System Efficiency (Supply Side)**

Once the load is determined, the system must deliver cooling efficiently. Losses occur at:

- **Compressor inefficiencies** (isentropic + mechanical + electrical losses)
- **Condenser lift penalty** (higher condensing temperature = more work)
- **Evaporator starvation** (fouled tubes, low air/waterside flow)
- **Distribution losses** (chilled water pumping, duct leakage, friction)
- **Control losses** (simultaneous heating and cooling, hunting, set-point errors)

**Pillar C — Heat Recovery (Reuse Side)**

The heat extracted by the refrigeration cycle is dumped to the condenser. Instead of wasting it to atmosphere, it can be **recovered** for:

- Domestic hot water (DHW) preheating
- Reheat coils in CAV systems
- Heat-driven absorption chillers
- Process heating in industries

### 2.2 Performance Metrics — Step-by-Step Logic

#### (i) Coefficient of Performance (COP)

- **Definition:** Ratio of desired thermal effect to work input.
- **Why it matters:** A COP of 4 means 1 kW of electricity produces 4 kW of cooling — it is the system's *amplification factor*.

$$\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \frac{Q_e}{W_{\text{net,in}}}$$

$$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{Q_h}{W_{\text{net,in}}}$$

**Limitation:** COP is a *single-point* value at a single operating condition. Real HVAC systems operate across a wide range of loads throughout the year.

#### (ii) Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)

Used primarily for **window/split AC** ratings.

$$\text{EER} = \frac{Q_e \,[\text{BTU/hr}]}{W \,[\text{W}]}$$

#### (iii) Seasonal EER (SEER) & IPLV

**SEER** averages efficiency over an entire cooling season; **IPLV** (AHRI Standard 550/590) does it at four discrete part-load points (100%, 75%, 50%, 25%):

$$\text{IPLV} = 0.01 \cdot A + 0.42 \cdot B + 0.45 \cdot C + 0.12 \cdot D$$

where A, B, C, D are EERs at 100%, 75%, 50%, 25% of full load respectively.

#### (iv) kW per Ton (kW/TR)

The most intuitive metric for an energy auditor — *how many kW of electricity does it take to produce 1 TR of cooling?*

$$\text{kW/TR} = \frac{W_{\text{input}} \,[\text{kW}]}{\text{Capacity} \,[\text{TR}]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Benchmark:** A modern screw chiller operates at **0.55 – 0.70 kW/TR**; an old centrifugal may be at **0.90 – 1.10 kW/TR**; a brand-new inverter-driven magnetic-bearing centrifugal chiller can hit **0.45 kW/TR**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Typical Bench-mark | Remarks |
|---|--------|---------|-------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | COP (Refrigeration) | $\dfrac{Q_e}{W_{in}}$ | dimensionless | 3.0 – 5.5 | Single point rating |
| 2 | COP (Heat Pump) | $\dfrac{Q_h}{W_{in}}$ | dimensionless | 3.5 – 5.0 | Heating mode |
| 3 | EER | $\dfrac{Q_e\,[\text{BTU/hr}]}{W\,[\text{W}]}$ | BTU/W·h | 9 – 12 | Air-conditioner rating |
| 4 | SEER | $\dfrac{\sum Q_e}{\sum W}$ over season | BTU/W·h | 13 – 22 | Seasonal efficiency |
| 5 | IPLV | $0.01A+0.42B+0.45C+0.12D$ | BTU/W·h | 14 – 20 | Chiller part-load |
| 6 | kW/TR | $\dfrac{W_{in}\,[\text{kW}]}{\text{TR}}$ | kW/TR | 0.55 – 0.80 | Auditor's favourite |
| 7 | Carnot COP | $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | upper bound | T in Kelvin |
| 8 | Energy Index (EI) | $\dfrac{0.75}{\text{IPLV}}$ | dimensionless | $\leq 1.0$ | AHRI 550/590 limit |
| 9 | Heat Rejection Factor | $\dfrac{Q_c}{Q_e} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\text{COP}}$ | dimensionless | 1.18 – 1.33 | For sizing condenser |
| 10 | Annual kWh | $\text{TR} \times 3.517 \times \text{EFLH} \times \dfrac{1}{\text{COP}}$ | kWh | — | EFLH = Equivalent Full Load Hours |

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute value or "given" expressions in exam answers should be written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert \text{given} \vert$ to avoid markdown table breakage.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Indices

- **Energy Auditors** use **kW/TR** as a quick health check on chillers.
- **HVAC Designers** specify **IPLV** to ensure chiller efficiency at *typical* part-load operation (chillers spend 90% of life below 80% load).
- **Green Building Codes** (ECBC, ASHRAE 90.1) set **minimum EER / COP** limits that systems must meet.
- **Operations Engineers** track **COP monthly** to detect refrigerant leaks, fouling, and sensor drift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Carnot (Ideal) COP from First Law

The maximum possible COP of a refrigeration cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs is derived by combining the **First Law** (energy balance) and **Second Law** (reversible heat transfer).

**Starting Point:** A reversible cycle absorbs heat $Q_L$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$, rejects heat $Q_H$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$, and consumes net work $W_{net}$.

**Step 1 — First Law (Energy Conservation):**

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$

**Step 2 — Second Law (Reversibility implies zero entropy change of universe):**

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q_H$ from Step 2:**

$$Q_H = \frac{T_H}{T_L} \cdot Q_L$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Step 1 to find $W_{net}$:**

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = \frac{T_H}{T_L} \cdot Q_L - Q_L = Q_L \left(\frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1\right)$$

$$W_{net} = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into COP definition ($Q_L / W_{net}$):**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,ref}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

**Conversion rule applied throughout:** $T[\text{K}] = T[^{\circ}\text{C}] + 273.15$. This must be enforced *before* the division, otherwise the result is mathematically wrong (the answer can be negative if °C is used directly when $T_H < T_L$ in °C terms).

### 3.2 Derivation II — Relationship Between COP, kW/TR, and EER

**Given:** A chiller produces $X$ TR of cooling while consuming $P$ kW of electrical input.

**Step 1 — Convert capacity from TR to kW thermal:**

$$Q_e \,[\text{kW}] = X \,[\text{TR}] \times 3.517$$

**Step 2 — Apply COP definition:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_e}{P} = \frac{X \times 3.517}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for kW/TR (the inverse of COP scaled by 3.517):**

$$\text{kW/TR} = \frac{P}{X} = \frac{3.517}{\text{COP}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to EER (BTU/W·h):**

$$\text{EER} = \text{COP} \times 3.412$$

*(where 3.412 is the conversion factor 1 W·h = 3.412 BTU)*

**Numerical sanity-check example:**

A chiller has COP = 4.5. Then:

$$\text{kW/TR} = \frac{3.517}{4.5} = 0.7816 \,\text{kW/TR}$$

$$\text{EER} = 4.5 \times 3.412 = 15.354 \,\text{BTU/W·h}$$

### 3.3 Derivation III — IPLV Computation for an Actual Chiller

**Given measured EERs of a screw chiller at AHRI part-loads:**

| Part Load | EER |
|-----------|-----|
| 100% (A) | 11.8 |
| 75% (B) | 13.6 |
| 50% (C) | 15.4 |
| 25% (D) | 9.2 |

**Step 1 — Apply AHRI 550/590 weighting formula:**

$$\text{IPLV} = 0.01 \times A + 0.42 \times B + 0.45 \times C + 0.12 \times D$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each weighted term:**

$$0.01 \times 11.8 = 0.118$$

$$0.42 \times 13.6 = 5.712$$

$$0.45 \times 15.4 = 6.930$$

$$0.12 \times 9.2 = 1.104$$

**Step 3 — Sum them up:**

$$\text{IPLV} = 0.118 + 5.712 + 6.930 + 1.104 = 13.864 \,\text{BTU/W·h}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Index (EI) per AHRI:**

$$\text{EI} = \frac{0.75}{\text{IPLV}} = \frac{0.75}{13.864} = 0.0541$$

Wait — this is the *wrong* interpretation. AHRI 550/590 actually defines the Energy Index as the ratio of a reference COP/COP₂ to the chiller's COP. For board-exam purposes, the **simpler form** is:

$$\text{EI} = \frac{\text{Reference IPLV}}{\text{Measured IPLV}} \leq 1.0 \text{ (passes)}$$

**Conclusion:** The chiller has an IPLV of **13.86 BTU/W·h**, which is within the typical acceptance range for an air-cooled screw chiller.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Annual Electricity Cost of a Chiller

**Given:** A 200 TR centrifugal chiller, measured COP = 4.0, EFLH (Equivalent Full Load Hours) = 1800 hr/yr, electricity tariff = ₹8/kWh.

**Step 1 — Compute thermal energy delivered per year:**

$$Q_{\text{annual}} = 200 \times 3.517 \times 1800 = 1{,}266{,}120 \,\text{kWh}_{\text{th}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute electrical input per year using COP:**

$$W_{\text{annual}} = \frac{Q_{\text{annual}}}{\text{COP}} = \frac{1{,}266{,}120}{4.0} = 316{,}530 \,\text{kWh}_e$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual cost:**

$$\text{Cost} = 316{,}530 \times 8 = \text{₹ }25{,}32{,}240$$

**Step 4 — Saving if COP improved to 5.0 (e.g., VSD retrofit):**

$$W_{\text{saved}} = Q_{\text{annual}} \times \left(\frac{1}{4.0} - \frac{1}{5.0}\right) = 1{,}266{,}120 \times 0.05 = 63{,}306 \,\text{kWh}_e$$

$$\text{₹ saved} = 63{,}306 \times 8 = \text{₹ }5{,}06{,}448 \,/\,\text{year}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Audit Heuristic:** A 1.0 increase in chiller COP on a 200 TR system running 1800 hrs/yr saves roughly **₹ 5 lakh/year** at ₹ 8/kWh.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — HVAC Energy Audit Decision Tree

The following Mermaid diagram maps the audit logic from initial walk-through to final ECM implementation. Every node uses purely alphanumeric identifiers to comply with the Mermaid compiler safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[HVAC System Entry Point] --> B1[Identify Sub-systems: Chiller AHU Cooling Tower Pump]
    B1 --> C1[Collect Operating Data: TR kW kWh Load Profile]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Performance Indices: COP EER kW-TR IPLV]
    D1 --> E1[Compare with Benchmarks and Rated Values]
    E1 --> F1{Benchmark Gap more than 15 percent}
    F1 -- No --> G1[Document: System Within Acceptable Range]
    F1 -- Yes --> H1[Initiate Detailed Measurements: Temperature Pressure Flow Power]
    H1 --> I1[Pinpoint Loss Sources: Compressor Condenser Evaporator Distribution Controls]
    I1 --> J1[Generate ECM List: VFD Heat Recovery Insulation VAV BMS]
    J1 --> K1[Evaluate Each ECM: Savings kWh Payback years]
    K1 --> L1[Prioritize by Simple Payback less than 2 years]
    L1 --> M1[Implement Selected ECMs]
    M1 --> N1[Post-retrofit Verification M and V]
    N1 --> O1[Update Audit Report with Verified Savings]

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **yellow** node is the audit entry.
- The **sky-blue** node is the calculation step (performance indices).
- The **pink** node is the decision diamond (gap > 15%?).
- The **green** node is the final ECM prioritization.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Major Energy-Saving Opportunities in HVAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Demand Side: Reduce Load] --> P11[Building Envelope Insulation]
    P1 --> P12[Window Films and Shading]
    P1 --> P13[LED Lighting Retrofit]
    P1 --> P14[Occupancy Sensors]

    P2[Supply Side: Improve Efficiency] --> P21[Variable Frequency Drives on Compressors]
    P2 --> P22[Electronically Commutated EC Fans]
    P2 --> P23[Condenser and Evaporator Cleaning]
    P2 --> P24[Refrigerant Charge Optimisation]
    P2 --> P25[Air Side Economizer Cycles]

    P3[Recovery Side: Reuse Waste Heat] --> P31[Condenser Heat to DHW]
    P3 --> P32[Heat Recovery Chillers]
    P3 --> P33[Adsorption Chillers for Waste Heat]

    P4[Control Side: Smart Operation] --> P41[Building Management System BMS]
    P4 --> P42[Chilled Water Reset Schedules]
    P4 --> P43[Optimal Start Stop Algorithms]
    P4 --> P44[Demand Controlled Ventilation DCV]

    P1 --> Q[Lower kWh per TR]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> R[Reduced Net Energy Import]
    P4 --> Q
    Q --> S[Annual Energy Cost Reduction]
    R --> S
```

### 4.3 Sankey-Style Energy Flow (Descriptive Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1[Grid Electricity 100 percent] --> SPL[Distribution at Site]
    SPL --> CHP[Chiller Plant 55 percent]
    SPL --> PUMP[Pumps Chilled and Condenser 18 percent]
    SPL --> AHU[Air Handling Units 20 percent]
    SPL --> OTH[Lighting and Other 7 percent]
    CHP --> LOSS1[Compressor Heat Rejection Loss]
    CHP --> USEFUL[Useful Cooling to Building]
    PUMP --> LOSS2[Pipe Friction and Leakage]
    AHU --> LOSS3[Duct Leakage and Fan Heat]
    USEFUL --> OA[To Occupied Zone 70 percent of input]
    LOSS1 --> REC[Heat Recovery Possible 25 to 30 percent]
    REC --> DHW[Pre-heat Domestic Hot Water]
    DHW --> NET[Net Energy Import Reduced]
    OA --> NET
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

Define **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigeration system. How is it different from **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **COP** is the ratio of the desired effect (heat removed $Q_e$) to the work input ($W_{in}$) to the refrigerator. It is a **dimensionless** number: $\text{COP} = Q_e / W_{in}$.
- **EER** is a similar ratio but expressed in **mixed units** — typically BTU/hr of cooling divided by Watts of electrical input. Numerically, $\text{EER} = 3.412 \times \text{COP}$.
- **Key distinction:** COP is purely dimensionless and is the thermodynamic index used for chillers; EER is used for unitary AC equipment (window/split) and is the regulatory rating metric in many countries.
- *(1 mark each for the three points above.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

State **any three** energy-saving opportunities in a chilled-water air-conditioning system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **VFD on chilled-water pumps and cooling-tower fans** — pump power follows the cube of speed, so a 20% speed reduction cuts power by nearly 50%.
2. **Chilled-water temperature reset** — raising chilled water from 6 °C to 8 °C during part-load lifts chiller COP by 8–12%.
3. **Condenser-tower optimization** — lowering condensing temperature by 1 °C improves chiller COP by ~2–3%.
4. *(Any 3 of the above. 1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A 150 TR water-cooled centrifugal chiller operates with an average COP of 3.8. The system runs for 2400 hours per year at near-full load. Compute the:
    (i) **kW/TR** index
    (ii) **EER** in BTU/W·h
    (iii) **Annual electricity consumption** in kWh
    (iv) **Annual electricity cost** at ₹ 7.50/kWh. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain **five energy conservation measures (ECMs)** applicable to the above chiller plant, indicating the expected percentage saving for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Performance and Cost Calculation**

**(i) kW/TR index:**

$$\text{kW/TR} = \frac{3.517}{\text{COP}} = \frac{3.517}{3.8} = 0.9255 \,\text{kW/TR}$$

Since 1 TR = 3.517 kW, the total power input is $150 \times 0.9255 = 138.83 \,\text{kW}$.

**(ii) EER in BTU/W·h:**

$$\text{EER} = \text{COP} \times 3.412 = 3.8 \times 3.412 = 12.9656 \,\text{BTU/W·h}$$

**(iii) Annual electricity consumption:**

$$W_{\text{annual}} = 138.83 \times 2400 = 3{,}33{,}192 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Alternative method (using the formula from Section 3.4):**

$$W_{\text{annual}} = \frac{150 \times 3.517 \times 2400}{3.8} = \frac{1{,}266{,}120}{3.8} = 3{,}33{,}189 \,\text{kWh} \,(\approx)$$

**(iv) Annual electricity cost:**

$$\text{Cost} = 3{,}33{,}192 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ }24{,}98{,}940$$

---

**Part (b) — Five ECMs for the Chiller Plant**

| # | ECM | Expected Saving |
|---|-----|-----------------|
| 1 | **VFD retrofit on compressor / chilled-water pump** | 15 – 30% |
| 2 | **Condenser tube cleaning & cooling-tower fill replacement** | 5 – 8% |
| 3 | **Chilled-water supply temperature reset (6→8 °C)** | 6 – 10% |
| 4 | **Heat recovery from condenser to DHW** | 4 – 6% (offset) |
| 5 | **Air-side economizer cycle** when outside < 15 °C | 10 – 20% (seasonal) |
| 6 | **BMS-based optimal start/stop** | 3 – 5% |

**Explanation (one-line each, 7 marks = 1.4 marks × 5 ECMs):**

1. **VFD:** Reduces motor speed when load drops. Pump and fan affinity laws: $P \propto N^3$, so partial speed yields cubic power reduction.
2. **Cleaning:** Removes scale and biofilm → improves heat transfer → lower condensing temperature → higher COP.
3. **CHW reset:** Higher leaving chilled water means lower compressor pressure ratio, reducing specific work.
4. **Heat recovery:** Uses condenser waste heat (which would otherwise be rejected) for DHW, offsetting electrical geyser load.
5. **Economizer:** When $T_{oa} < T_{ra}$, use 100% outside air instead of mechanical cooling → chiller off → major savings in mild seasons.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the **Carnot COP** for a refrigeration cycle operating between $-5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (evaporator) and $45\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (condenser). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A building has a measured cooling load of **800 TR** but its installed chiller capacity is **1200 TR**. Discuss the **energy and operational implications** of this oversizing and recommend **four corrective measures** to improve part-load efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Carnot COP Derivation**

*Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:*

$$T_L = -5 + 273.15 = 268.15 \,\text{K}$$

$$T_H = 45 + 273.15 = 318.15 \,\text{K}$$

*Step 2 — Apply the derived formula:*

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268.15}{318.15 - 268.15} = \frac{268.15}{50.00}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = 5.363}$$

*Step 3 — Interpretation:*

- No real refrigerator can exceed 5.363 under these conditions.
- A modern screw chiller achieves 4.5–5.0 → second-law efficiency = $\dfrac{4.7}{5.363} = 87.6\%$ (very good).
- This is a typical KTU-style derivation question; full step-by-step shown is essential for **7 marks**.

---

**Part (b) — Oversizing Implications and Corrective Measures**

**Implications of installing 1200 TR chiller for an 800 TR load:**

1. The chiller will operate at **~67% part load** most of the time. Many chillers (especially constant-speed) lose efficiency below 40–50% load because of **compressor surge** and **poor oil return**.
2. Frequent **on/off cycling** ("short cycling") increases wear and reduces motor life.
3. Higher **capital cost** (₹ 4000/TR extra capacity → ₹ 16 lakh wasted on paper).
4. Higher **standby losses** during shutdown.

**Four Corrective Measures:**

1. **Replace with a smaller chiller** (or run only one of two units if the plant is dual-chiller) — *best long-term solution*.
2. **Install VFD / inverter on the chiller compressor** so it can modulate capacity continuously.
3. **Decouple chilled-water production from peak load** using a **Thermal Energy Storage (TES) tank** — produce cooling at night and store in chilled water or ice.
4. **Add a smaller dedicated chiller** for the *base load* and keep the large chiller as a *trim/peak* unit.

**Combined effect:** Can lift effective part-load COP by **30–40%**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes observed in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Using °C instead of K** in Carnot COP — this gives a wrong (sometimes negative!) result. *Always convert first.*
> 2. **Mixing up COP and EER** — examiner deducts 1 mark per such instance.
> 3. **Skipping the unit conversion 1 TR = 3.517 kW** in kW/TR problems — student writes the inverse by mistake.
> 4. **Forgetting EFLH** in annual-energy problems — full marks only if EFLH is mentioned and used.
> 5. **In Part B, writing "improper maintenance" as an ECM** without quantifying the savings — examiners expect **numerical estimates** of kWh saved or % improvement.
> 6. **In derivation questions, jumping to the final answer** — KTU gives step-wise marks. Always show 4–6 steps.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **COP = $Q_e / W_{in}$** is dimensionless; **EER = $3.412 \times \text{COP}$** in BTU/W·h.
- [ ] **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr** — memorize this.
- [ ] **kW/TR = 3.517 / COP** — a *lower* value means a *better* chiller.
- [ ] **Carnot COP** uses **Kelvin** — always convert °C → K by adding 273.15.
- [ ] **IPLV** uses AHRI 550/590 weights: **0.01, 0.42, 0.45, 0.12** at 100/75/50/25% load.
- [ ] **Energy Saving in HVAC** comes from 3 sources: (i) reducing load, (ii) improving efficiency, (iii) recovering waste heat.
- [ ] **VFD** gives cubic energy savings on pumps/fans: $P \propto N^3$.
- [ ] **1 °C rise in condensing temperature** → ~2–3% drop in chiller COP.
- [ ] **1 °C rise in chilled-water temperature** → ~1.5–2% improvement in COP.
- [ ] **ECMs with payback < 2 years** are typically prioritized in audit reports.
- [ ] **Heat recovery from condenser** to DHW can offset 4–6% of net HVAC energy use.
- [ ] **Oversized chillers** are a common audit finding — VFD or TES is the standard fix.
- [ ] Always quote **EFLH** and **tariff** when computing annual savings.
- [ ] Compare measured values with **ASHRAE / ECBC** benchmarks, not just nameplate.
- [ ] For board exam, **step-by-step derivations** > one-line answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Recovery Systems: Waste Heat Recovery System, Energy Saving Opportunities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Recovery Systems: Waste Heat Recovery System, Energy Saving Opportunities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** is the process of capturing **heat energy** that is normally lost through hot exhaust gases, hot air, cooling water, or other waste streams from industrial processes, and **reusing** it either within the same process (recirculation) or in a different process, thereby improving the overall **energy efficiency** of the system.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus context:
$$Q_{recovered} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \quad [\text{kW}]$$

where the recoverable heat is computed from the mass flow rate, specific heat, and temperature drop of the waste stream.

## 1.2 Sources and Classification of Waste Heat

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **U.S. Department of Energy (DOE)** classification, waste heat is broadly classified by temperature level:

| Temperature Range | Classification | Common Sources |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **High Grade** (650 °C and above) | Class I | Furnace exhausts, cement kilns, steel re-heat furnaces, gas turbines |
| **Medium Grade** (230 °C – 650 °C) | Class II | Steam boiler exhausts, diesel engine exhausts, process heaters |
| **Low Grade** (up to 230 °C) | Class III | Hot wastewater, warm exhaust air, cooling water streams, condenser reject |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reuse the Bath Water" Idea

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you just boiled water to cook noodles. You drain the boiling water down the sink — that is the *typical industrial process* (wasting heat). Now imagine you use that same hot water to wash your utensils or pre-warm the next pot of water. That is *Waste Heat Recovery*. In a factory, the hot exhaust gas leaving a boiler is full of energy that can preheat the incoming boiler feedwater, saving **fuel** (and money).

## 1.4 Energy Saving Opportunities — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Saving Opportunities (ESO)** in the context of energy management are the **identifiable modifications** in equipment, processes, or operational practices that lead to a measurable reduction in energy consumption per unit of output, without compromising product quality or safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two complementary pillars of this module are:
> 1. **Hardware-based recovery** — installing physical devices (economizers, heat pipes, WHB).
> 2. **Process integration / ESO** — identifying *where* and *how* the recovered energy can be utilized most efficiently (Pinch Analysis, co-generation, insulation upgrades).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Waste Heat Recovery Important?

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** dictates that energy cannot be destroyed. In any thermal process, the energy that *enters* must equal the energy that *leaves* (as useful output + losses). Industrial processes typically exhibit:

- **Stack losses** — 5% to 30% of input energy escapes as hot flue gas.
- **Radiation & convection losses** — 1% to 5% from hot surfaces.
- **Cooling water losses** — energy carried away in cooling circuits.
- **Unburnt fuel losses** — 0% to 5% due to incomplete combustion.

The "why" of WHR is therefore **purely economic and environmental**: every kilowatt-hour recovered is a kilowatt-hour **not purchased** from the utility, and a kilogram of **CO₂ not emitted**.

## 2.2 Pinch Analysis — The Heart of Process Integration

**Pinch Analysis** is a systematic methodology for determining the **minimum energy requirement** of a process by constructing composite curves of hot and cold streams.

### 2.2.1 Key Terms
- **Hot Stream**: A process stream that needs to be cooled (releases heat).
- **Cold Stream**: A process stream that needs to be heated (absorbs heat).
- **Pinch Point**: The location of **minimum temperature difference** ($\Delta T_{min}$) between hot and cold composite curves.
- **Minimum Energy Target**: The theoretical minimum heating and cooling utilities required.

### 2.2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Procedure

1. Extract all hot and cold streams from the process flow diagram (PFD).
2. For each stream, determine supply temperature ($T_S$), target temperature ($T_T$), and enthalpy change ($\Delta H$).
3. Shift cold stream temperatures upward by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ to maintain the minimum temperature approach.
4. Divide the temperature range into intervals and calculate **net heat** in each interval.
5. Plot the **Composite Curve** (hot and cold) and identify the **pinch point**.
6. Read off:
   - **Minimum Hot Utility (Q_H,min)**: heat required above the pinch.
   - **Minimum Cold Utility (Q_C,min)**: heat rejected below the pinch.
7. Design a **Heat Exchanger Network (HEN)** above and below the pinch — never crossing the pinch with utilities.

## 2.3 Types of Waste Heat Recovery Systems

### 2.3.1 Recoupers / Recuperators
- **Definition**: Direct-contact or indirect-contact heat exchangers where hot and cold fluids flow simultaneously on either side of a wall.
- **Examples**: Economizers, air-preheaters, gas-to-gas heaters.
- **Best for**: Continuous, steady-state waste heat streams.

### 2.3.2 Regenerators
- **Definition**: Heat is stored temporarily in a solid matrix (refractory bricks, ceramic balls) and then released to the cold stream in a cyclic fashion.
- **Examples**: Cowper stoves in steel plants, Ljungström air preheaters.
- **Best for**: High-temperature periodic processes (blast furnaces).

### 2.3.3 Heat Pipe Heat Exchangers (HPHE)
- **Definition**: Sealed tubes containing a working fluid that evaporates at the hot end and condenses at the cold end, transferring heat via latent heat of vaporization.
- **Best for**: Flue gas-to-air heat recovery, small-to-medium temperature ranges.

### 2.3.4 Waste Heat Boilers (WHB)
- **Definition**: A boiler specifically designed to recover heat from hot flue gases (e.g., from an internal combustion engine or gas turbine) to produce steam.
- **Application**: CHP/cogeneration plants.

### 2.3.5 Economizers
- **Definition**: A finned-tube heat exchanger that recovers heat from boiler flue gas to preheat the boiler feedwater.
- **Typical Savings**: 5%–10% of fuel input.

### 2.3.6 Run-Around Coils
- **Definition**: Two or more coil units (one in exhaust, one in supply) connected by a pumped fluid loop.
- **Best for**: Distant exhaust and supply streams in HVAC systems.

## 2.4 Co-generation (Combined Heat and Power — CHP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cogeneration** is the simultaneous production of **electrical (or mechanical)** energy and **useful thermal energy** from a single fuel input. Typical overall efficiency reaches **70%–80%**, compared with 30%–40% for separate generation.

| Configuration | Prime Mover | Typical Electrical Efficiency | Total Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Topping Cycle | Gas turbine / Diesel engine first | 25%–35% | 70%–85% |
| Bottoming Cycle | Steam turbine after process heat | 20%–30% | 75%–90% |

## 2.5 Energy Saving Opportunities (ESO) — Identification Matrix

| Opportunity Area | Typical Savings | Payback Period |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Installing an economizer on boiler flue gas | 5%–10% | 1–2 years |
| Using waste heat boiler for process steam | 10%–20% | 2–3 years |
| Cogeneration system installation | 20%–30% | 3–5 years |
| Heat pipe air preheater | 3%–8% | 1–2 years |
| Insulation of steam pipes and boilers | 2%–5% | < 1 year |
| Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on motors | 20%–50% on motor load | 1–3 years |
| Pinch analysis-based HEN retrofit | 10%–30% | 2–4 years |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master formula reference** for Module 3 numerical problems. Memorize the units and assumptions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat recovery rate | kW |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat recovery (condensing) | kW |
| 3 | $\eta_{combustion} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (\text{losses})}{Q_{input}}$ | Combustion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam}(h_{steam} - h_{fw})}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Boiler efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{S_{theoretical}}$ | Stoichiometric fuel-air ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Exergy} = \dot{m}[(h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)]$ | Specific exergy | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $\text{Energy saved} = \dfrac{Q_{recovered}}{\eta_{boiler}} \times 3600$ | Annual energy saved (kJ/hr to kCal/hr) | varies |
| 8 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_{H}}{W_{in}}$ | Coefficient of performance of heat pump | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Capital cost}}{\text{Annual savings}}$ | Simple payback period | years |
| 10 | $E_{p} = E_{c} \left(1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_{H}}\right)$ | Maximum (Carnot) work from heat | kW |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

WHR and ESO principles are foundational to:
- **Industrial energy audits** (mandatory under the Indian **PAT** scheme for Designated Consumers).
- **ISO 50001** Energy Management System certification.
- **Green building design** (ECBC, GRIHA ratings).
- **Process plant retrofit** and debottlenecking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Waste Heat Available from Flue Gas

### Problem Setup
A boiler burns furnace oil. The flue gas leaves at $T_g = 280\,°C$. The ambient is $T_a = 30\,°C$. The flue gas mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_g = 1.5\,\text{kg/s}$ and the average specific heat is $c_{p,g} = 1.05\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The "available" waste heat is the heat content above ambient (the maximum recoverable portion):

$$Q_{waste} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_g - T_a)$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$Q_{waste} = 1.5\,\text{kg/s} \times 1.05\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)} \times (280 - 30)\,\text{K}$$

$$Q_{waste} = 1.5 \times 1.05 \times 250$$

$$Q_{waste} = 1.575 \times 250 = 393.75\,\text{kW}$$

### Comment
This is the **maximum theoretical** recoverable heat. In practice, due to finite temperature difference ($\Delta T_{min}$) and exchanger inefficiency, only **60%–80%** of this is realistically captured.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Monetary Savings from a Waste Heat Boiler

### Given
- Recovered heat: $Q_{r} = 300\,\text{kW}$
- Boiler efficiency (without WHR): $\eta_{b} = 0.75$
