## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Energy Utilization Factor" (EUF) of a cogeneration plant. A plant generates 8 MW power and 20 MW of useful heat from a fuel input of 30 MW (thermal). Calculate the EUF.**

**Model Answer:**

The Energy Utilization Factor (EUF), also called the overall efficiency or total efficiency, is the ratio of the sum of useful power output and useful heat output to the fuel energy input.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{P + Q}{F}$$

**Calculation:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{8 + 20}{30} = \frac{28}{30} = 0.9333 = 93.33\,\%$$

This is far higher than a conventional power plant (35–40 %) and demonstrates the fundamental advantage of cogeneration. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the "heat-to-power ratio" (β) of a cogeneration plant? Why is it a critical parameter for optimal operation?**

**Model Answer:**

The heat-to-power ratio (β) is defined as the ratio of useful thermal energy output to the electrical (or mechanical) power output of a cogeneration plant:

$$\beta = \frac{Q}{P}$$

It is a critical parameter because:

1. The back-pressure or extraction-condensing turbine has a **physically fixed** β, determined by its design (inlet pressure, exhaust pressure, extraction point).
2. The plant's β **must match** the consumer's demand ratio $\beta_D = Q_D/P_D$ for maximum efficiency with zero import or supplementary heat.
3. Mismatch forces the operator to either buy grid power or fire an auxiliary boiler — both suboptimal.
4. The operating strategy (Case A or Case B) is decided solely by comparing $\beta_{cogen}$ with $\beta_D$. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Question A (14 Marks)**

An industrial process requires $P_D = 10\,\text{MW}$ electrical power and $Q_D = 40\,\text{MW}$ of process steam. Two alternatives are available:

- **Option 1:** Purchase power from the grid at ₹ 7/kWh and produce steam in a coal-fired boiler with efficiency 0.80 (fuel cost ₹ 3/kWh thermal).
- **Option 2:** Install a back-pressure cogeneration plant with $\beta = 4$, electrical efficiency 0.20, fuel cost ₹ 3/kWh (thermal), and the heat is fully utilized.

**(a)** Compute the hourly operating cost under both options.

**(b)** Which option is economically optimal and what is the hourly saving?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Option 1 — Grid + Stand-alone Boiler**

Grid power cost per hour:

$$C_{grid} = 7 \times 10{,}000 = ₹\,70{,}000 \text{ per hour}$$

Boiler fuel input:

$$F_{boiler} = \frac{Q_D}{\eta_b} = \frac{40{,}000}{0.80} = 50{,}000\,\text{kW (thermal)}$$

Boiler fuel cost:

$$C_{boiler} = 3 \times 50{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per hour}$$

**Total Option 1 cost:**

$$C_1 = 70{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹\,2{,}20{,}000 \text{ per hour} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

---

**(a) Option 2 — Cogeneration**

Compare β's:

$$\beta_D = \frac{40}{10} = 4 = \beta_{cogen}$$

**Match!** Cogen meets both demands exactly with no grid import and no auxiliary boiler.

Power generated: $P_{cogen} = 10\,\text{MW}$
Heat generated: $Q_{cogen} = 40\,\text{MW}$

Fuel input:

$$F_{cogen} = \frac{P_{cogen}}{\eta_e} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.20} = 50{,}000\,\text{kW (thermal)}$$

**Total Option 2 cost:**

$$C_2 = 3 \times 50{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per hour} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

---

**(b) Optimal Choice and Saving**

$$\text{Saving} = C_1 - C_2 = 2{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹\,70{,}000 \text{ per hour}$$

$$\text{Annual saving} = 70{,}000 \times 8760 = ₹\,61.32\,\text{Crore per year}$$

**Option 2 (Cogeneration) is optimal.** The plant not only halves its bill but also **eliminates grid dependency**. *(14 marks total)*

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

A sugar mill cogeneration plant operates with the following data:

- Bagasse-fired boiler steam flow: 30 TPH at 40 bar, 440 °C
- Turbine exhaust: 2.5 bar (process steam)
- Generator efficiency: 0.97
- Enthalpies: $h_{in} = 3330\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{out} = 2750\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{proc} = 2710\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated steam at 2.5 bar), $h_{fw} = 535\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (90 °C feed water)

**(a)** Calculate the heat-to-power ratio, electrical output, useful heat output, and the Energy Utilization Factor.

**(b)** If the same plant were replaced by a separate grid + boiler setup, what fuel-energy would the boiler consume? Compute the Fuel Energy Saving Ratio (FESR).

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cogeneration Plant Performance**

Step 1 — Heat-to-power ratio:

$$\beta = \frac{h_{proc} - h_{fw}}{\eta_{gen}(h_{in} - h_{out})} = \frac{2710 - 535}{0.97 \times (3330 - 2750)} = \frac{2175}{562.6} = 3.866$$

Step 2 — Steam flow conversion:

$$m_s = 30\,\text{TPH} = \frac{30{,}000}{3600} = 8.333\,\text{kg/s}$$

Step 3 — Power output:

$$P = \eta_{gen} \cdot m_s \cdot (h_{in} - h_{out}) = 0.97 \times 8.333 \times (3330 - 2750) = 0.97 \times 8.333 \times 580$$

$$P = 4688.6\,\text{kW} \approx 4.69\,\text{MW}$$

Step 4 — Heat output:

$$Q = m_s \cdot (h_{proc} - h_{fw}) = 8.333 \times (2710 - 535) = 8.333 \times 2175 = 18{,}124\,\text{kW} \approx 18.12\,\text{MW}$$

Step 5 — Fuel input (using plant thermal efficiency ~ 0.85 for bagasse boiler):

$$F = \frac{P + Q}{\eta_{boiler}} = \frac{4688.6 + 18{,}124}{0.85} = \frac{22{,}812.6}{0.85} = 26{,}838.4\,\text{kW (thermal)}$$

Step 6 — Energy Utilization Factor:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{P + Q}{F} = \frac{22{,}812.6}{26{,}838.4} = 0.85 = 85\,\% \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

---

**(b) FESR Calculation**

In a separate-generation scenario:
- Power is generated in a condenser plant with efficiency $\eta_{sep,power} = 0.35$ (typical):
$$F_{sep,power} = \frac{P}{0.35} = \frac{4688.6}{0.35} = 13{,}396\,\text{kW (thermal)}$$

- Heat is generated in an industrial boiler with $\eta_{sep,heat} = 0.80$:
$$F_{sep,heat} = \frac{Q}{0.80} = \frac{18{,}124}{0.80} = 22{,}655\,\text{kW (thermal)}$$

Total separate-generation fuel:

$$F_{sep} = 13{,}396 + 22{,}655 = 36{,}051\,\text{kW (thermal)}$$

FESR:

$$\text{FESR} = 1 - \frac{F_{CHP}}{F_{sep}} = 1 - \frac{26{,}838.4}{36{,}051} = 1 - 0.7445 = 0.2555 = 25.55\,\%$$

**Interpretation:** Cogeneration saves **25.55 %** of the fuel that would otherwise be burnt in two separate systems — a typical figure for sugar mill cogens. *(7 marks)* *(14 marks total)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cogeneration Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks):** Mixing kW and MW without scaling; forgetting $1\,\text{MW} = 1000\,\text{kW}$ when multiplying by ₹/kWh.
> 2. **Steam Flow Units (–2 marks):** TPH (tonnes per hour) must be converted to **kg/s** by dividing by 3.6, not 3600. Many students incorrectly divide by 3600 directly.
> 3. **Forgetting Generator Efficiency (–1 mark):** The formula $P = m_s (h_{in} - h_{out})$ gives the *shaft* power, not the *electrical* power. You must multiply by $\eta_{gen}$ (typically 0.96–0.98).
> 4. **Wrong Sign of Heat Credit (–2 marks):** The "heat-credit" method must **subtract** $C_h \cdot Q$ from the total fuel cost, not add it. A negative net cost of electricity is **legitimate** and indicates strong cogen economics — do not panic if it goes negative.
> 5. **Skipping the β Comparison (–3 marks):** Always begin a load-allocation problem by computing $\beta_D$ and comparing it with $\beta_{cogen}$ to determine the operating mode. Skipping this step leaves the examiner with no clue of your logic.
> 6. **Ignoring Boiler Efficiency (–1 mark):** Auxiliary heat fuel is $Q_{aux}/\eta_b$, **not** $Q_{aux}$. The boiler is never 100 % efficient.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cogeneration = Two useful outputs from one fuel input** (power + heat).
- **Topping cycle** is the most common in industry; **bottoming cycle** is used where high-grade waste heat is available (steel, cement, glass).
- **β = Q/P** is the single most important parameter — it decides operating mode.
- **Optimal operation** = minimum cost of meeting $P_D$ and $Q_D$ simultaneously.
- **Energy Utilization Factor (ε)** typically lies between **70 % and 85 %** for industrial cogens.
- **Decision rule:** $\beta_{cogen} > \beta_D$ → meet heat first, import power; $\beta_{cogen} < \beta_D$ → meet power first, fire aux. boiler.
- **Heat-credit method** for unit cost of cogen electricity:
  $$C_{e} = \frac{C_f \cdot F - C_h \cdot Q}{P}$$
- **FESR** of 20–35 % is a strong indicator of fuel-saving potential of any proposed cogen project.
- **Auxiliary boiler fuel** is always $Q_{aux}/\eta_b$, never $Q_{aux}$.
- **Steam flow conversion:** $1\,\text{TPH} = 1000/3600 = 0.2778\,\text{kg/s}$.
- **Generator efficiency** of 0.96–0.98 must be applied to convert shaft power to electrical power.
- **EUFs above 80 %** automatically qualify a plant for "captive cogen" benefits in most Indian industrial tariff norms.
- Always draw a **mass/energy balance diagram** at the start of the solution — examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear, labelled schematic.
- For the KTU 14-mark question, the **expected solution length** is approximately **1.5–2 pages**, with all formulas written explicitly and each numerical step shown.
- **Memorize the formula sheet (Section 2.3)** — it covers ~90 % of all KTU numericals on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Cycle Electricity Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combined Cycle Electricity Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Cycle Electricity Generation** is a highly efficient thermodynamic configuration in which **two distinct prime-mover cycles operate in cascade on the same fuel input**. The high-temperature exhaust gases from a **Gas Turbine (Brayton Cycle)** — acting as the *topping cycle* — are routed through a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)**, which extracts the otherwise-wasted thermal energy to produce steam that drives a **Steam Turbine (Rankine Cycle)** — the *bottoming cycle*. The mechanical outputs of both turbines are coupled to electrical generators, yielding a **net plant efficiency of 55–62%** compared to the **33–42%** achieved by a standalone gas turbine.

The arrangement is termed "combined" because it **combines the high-temperature advantages of the Brayton cycle with the high specific-heat-rejection capability of the Rankine cycle**, producing more useful work per kilogram of fuel burned.

### The Two-Cycle Architecture

| Cycle Role | Cycle Type | Working Fluid | Energy Range | Reason for Placement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topping Cycle | Brayton (Gas Turbine) | Air + Combustion Gas | High Temperature (≈ 500–600 °C exhaust) | Best suited to extract work from high-grade heat |
| Bottoming Cycle | Rankine (Steam Turbine) | Water/Steam | Low-to-Moderate Temperature (≈ 150–250 °C) | Can economically recover moderate-grade waste heat |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Coffee Mug Problem**
>
> Imagine you brew a fresh cup of hot coffee. You drink the liquid (the *primary useful work*), but the empty mug is still scalding hot. If you simply throw the mug away, the residual heat is wasted. But what if you used that hot mug to warm up a second, cooler drink? You'd be getting **two useful outputs from one energy input**.
>
> In a Combined Cycle Power Plant (CCPP), the **hot exhaust of the gas turbine** is the *scalding mug*. The HRSG acts as a heat exchanger that transfers this waste heat to water, producing **steam — a brand-new, useful working fluid** that drives a second turbine. **No fuel is burned a second time**, yet we get extra electrical output almost for free.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

> [!TIP]
> The following benchmark numbers are the standard values used in **KTU Energy Management and Auditing** problems and in real-world energy audits (per ASHRAE and ISO 50001 conventions):
>
> - **Simple Open-Cycle Gas Turbine Efficiency (η_GT):** **0.30 – 0.42** (i.e., 30%–42%)
> - **Simple Rankine Steam Turbine Efficiency (η_ST):** **0.33 – 0.40**
> - **Combined Cycle Net Plant Efficiency (η_CC):** **0.55 – 0.62**
> - **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) Exhaust Temperature:** **100 °C – 150 °C** (must stay above acid dew point of ≈ 60–95 °C to avoid corrosion)
> - **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) for CCPP:** **0.20 – 0.25 kg of oil-equivalent / kWh**
> - **Standard Reference Ambient Temperature (ISO):** **15 °C**, **1.013 bar**, **60% Relative Humidity**

## 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context

The concept of cascading thermodynamic cycles dates back to **1929** but achieved commercial viability only in the 1970s with the development of high-temperature ceramic coatings and HRSG metallurgy that could withstand the corrosive exhaust of modern gas turbines. Today, combined cycle plants dominate new utility-scale power installations worldwide because they offer the **lowest levelized cost of electricity (LCOE)** among thermal plants while also enabling **fast ramp-up (≈ 30 minutes)** — making them the preferred bridge technology between coal and renewables.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Components and Their Functions

The combined cycle plant is a **multi-component, integrated thermodynamic assembly**. Each block has a specific role and a measurable energy interaction with the system boundary.

### 2.1.1 Component-Wise Operational Logic

1. **Air Intake & Compressor**
   - Atmospheric air enters the axial-flow compressor (typically **15–20 stages**).
   - It is compressed to a pressure ratio of **r_p = 10 to 25** (modern F-class turbines).
   - **Power consumed (negative work)**: $W_C = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p (T_2 - T_1)$.
   - *Why this matters for energy audit:* The compressor can consume **50–60% of the gross turbine work**, so a 1% drop in isentropic efficiency equals a measurable drop in net output.

2. **Combustion Chamber**
   - Compressed air mixes with fuel (natural gas, naphtha, or distillate oil).
   - **Combustion temperature (Turbine Inlet Temperature, TIT)**: **1300 °C – 1600 °C**.
   - Modern turbines use **Dry Low NOx (DLN)** burners to keep NOx below **25 ppm** at 15% O₂.
   - Energy released per kg of fuel: $Q_{in} = m_f \cdot CV$, where **CV = Calorific Value (≈ 50 MJ/kg for natural gas)**.

3. **Gas Turbine (Brayton Topping Cycle)**
   - Hot gases expand through **3–4 stages** of turbine blades.
   - Part of the work output drives the compressor; the remainder drives the **Generator 1**.
   - Net work: $W_{GT,net} = W_{Turbine} - W_{Compressor}$.

4. **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)**
   - This is the **heart of the combined cycle** — a once-through or natural-circulation heat exchanger with **three pressure levels** in modern designs:
     - **High-Pressure (HP) Evaporator:** Steam at **100–130 bar, 500–565 °C**.
     - **Intermediate-Pressure (IP) Evaporator:** Steam at **25–40 bar**.
     - **Low-Pressure (LP) Evaporator:** Steam at **4–10 bar**, often used to preheat feedwater via economizers.
   - Includes **superheaters**, **evaporators**, **economizers**, and a **deaerator**.

5. **Steam Turbine (Rankine Bottoming Cycle)**
   - Multi-stage (typically **1 HP + 1 IP + 1 LP**) condensing turbine.
   - Exhausts to a **condenser** at vacuum pressure **0.05 – 0.10 bar abs**.
   - Drives **Generator 2** (or both turbines may share a single shaft on a **single-shaft CCPP**).

6. **Cooling System (Condenser)**
   - **Once-through cooling**, **cooling tower (wet)**, or **air-cooled condenser (ACC)**.
   - Condenser pressure directly affects steam turbine efficiency.

7. **Electrical Generators**
   - Synchronous 3-phase AC generators at **50 Hz, 11 kV / 22 kV** (India).
   - Stepped up to **220 kV / 400 kV** for grid transmission.

## 2.2 Energy Flow Hierarchy

The energy cascade follows this rigid sequence. The student must understand that **the bottoming cycle receives no fuel of its own**; it operates purely on the *exhaust enthalpy* of the topping cycle.

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} \rightarrow W_{GT,net} + \dot{Q}_{HRSG,recovered} \rightarrow W_{ST,net} + \dot{Q}_{condenser}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations constitute the **core analytical toolkit** for any numerical problem on combined cycle efficiency in KTU examinations. Memorize the **input variables, units, and boundary conditions** for each.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Meaning | Standard Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor Work | $W_C = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pa} (T_2 - T_1)$ | $\dot{m}_a$ = air mass flow, $c_{pa}$ = air specific heat | kW | T₂ = T₁ · r_p^{(γ-1)/γ} for ideal |
| 2 | Compressor Pressure Ratio | $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ | P₁ = ambient, P₂ = compressor discharge | bar | Modern F-class: 18–23 |
| 3 | Ideal Brayton Efficiency | $\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - (1/r_p)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | γ = 1.4 for air | dimensionless | Purely a function of r_p |
| 4 | Gas Turbine Net Work | $W_{GT,net} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{pg} (T_3 - T_4) - \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pa} (T_2 - T_1)$ | subscript g = gas, 3 = TIT, 4 = exhaust | kW | $\dot{m}_g \approx \dot{m}_a$ for lean mixtures |
| 5 | Gas Turbine Heat Input | $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV$ | $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel flow, CV = calorific value | kW | CV ≈ 50 MJ/kg for CH₄ |
| 6 | Gas Turbine Efficiency | $\eta_{GT} = W_{GT,net} / \dot{Q}_{in}$ | ratio of net work to fuel energy | dimensionless | Real: 0.30–0.42 |
| 7 | HRSG Heat Recovery | $\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{pg} (T_4 - T_{stack})$ | T_stack = exhaust gas leaving stack | kW | T_stack ≥ acid dew point |
| 8 | Steam Turbine Work | $W_{ST} = \dot{m}_s (h_5 - h_6)$ | h₅ = HP steam enthalpy, h₆ = exhaust enthalpy | kW | h from steam tables |
| 9 | Steam Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Rankine} = W_{ST} / \dot{Q}_{HRSG}$ | work out / heat in to steam | dimensionless | 0.30–0.40 for subcritical |
| 10 | **Combined Cycle Efficiency (THE KEY EQUATION)** | $\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT,net} + W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}$ | total useful work / fuel energy | dimensionless | 0.55–0.62 in practice |
| 11 | Combined Cycle Alt. Form | $\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{Rankine} \cdot (1 - \eta_{GT}) \cdot \eta_{HRSG}$ | when η_HRSG is the heat-capture factor | dimensionless | Used for energy audits |
| 12 | Plant Auxiliary Consumption | $P_{aux} = 0.03 \text{ to } 0.07 \cdot P_{gross}$ | pumps, fans, controls, lighting | kW | Deduct from gross output |
| 13 | Net Plant Efficiency | $\eta_{net} = (P_{gross} - P_{aux}) / \dot{Q}_{in}$ | net output / fuel | dimensionless | Used in energy audit reports |
| 14 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = 3600 / (\eta_{net} \cdot CV_{fuel})$ | fuel kg per kWh | kg/kWh | For oil: ≈ 0.20–0.25 |
| 15 | Heat Rate | $HR = 3600 / \eta_{net}$ | kJ of fuel per kWh | kJ/kWh | Lower = better |
| 16 | Capacity Utilization Factor | $CUF = E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \cdot 8760)$ | energy actually generated | fraction | Used in plant performance |
| 17 | Plant Load Factor | $PLF = E_{actual} / (P_{max,actual} \cdot t)$ | ratio of actual to max possible | fraction | Distinguishes from CUF |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real energy audit (per the **BEE - Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India** framework), the combined cycle is analyzed under three operating regimes:

1. **Design Point Operation** — full load, ISO conditions.
2. **Part-Load Operation** — the gas turbine maintains high efficiency, but the HRSG steam production drops non-linearly, reducing the bottoming cycle's share.
3. **Duct-Firing** — additional burners are placed in the HRSG exhaust duct to boost steam output during peak demand. This is **not** a true combined cycle efficiency gain (it is a topping modification), but it provides **operational flexibility**.

The audit officer must also verify that the **condenser cleanliness (Cleanliness Factor ≥ 0.85)**, **HRSG tube fouling**, and **compressor washing schedule** are properly maintained — each of these can drop η_CC by **1 to 3 percentage points** without any obvious malfunction.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Combined Cycle Net Efficiency From First Principles

We derive the combined cycle efficiency using **control-volume energy balances** on each component, with the standard steady-state assumption of negligible kinetic and potential energy changes and no heat loss to surroundings (other than the HRSG stack and condenser).

### Step 1 — Define the System Boundaries

- **System 1:** Gas turbine (compressor + combustor + turbine).
- **System 2:** Heat Recovery Steam Generator + Steam Turbine + Condenser.

### Step 2 — Energy Balance Across the Gas Turbine

The fuel releases heat $\dot{Q}_{in}$. A fraction leaves as net shaft work, and the rest leaves as exhaust gas enthalpy:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = W_{GT,net} + \dot{m}_g c_{pg} (T_4 - T_{ref})
$$

We define **gas turbine efficiency** as the ratio of useful work to fuel energy:

$$
\eta_{GT} = \frac{W_{GT,net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}
$$

### Step 3 — Energy Balance Across the HRSG

The exhaust gas cools from $T_4$ (≈ 540 °C) to the stack temperature $T_{stack}$ (≈ 120 °C). The heat extracted is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_g c_{pg} (T_4 - T_{stack})
$$

The HRSG heat-capture effectiveness is defined as:

$$
\eta_{HRSG} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}}{\dot{m}_g c_{pg} (T_4 - T_{ref})}
$$

For an ideal, perfectly insulated HRSG, $\eta_{HRSG} \to 1$.

### Step 4 — Energy Balance Across the Steam Turbine

Steam enters at enthalpy $h_5$ and exits the condenser at $h_6$. The steam turbine work is:

$$
W_{ST} = \dot{m}_s (h_5 - h_6)
$$

The bottoming Rankine cycle efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{Rankine} = \frac{W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Two Cycles

The total useful work output of the plant is:

$$
W_{total} = W_{GT,net} + W_{ST}
$$

The total fuel energy input is only $\dot{Q}_{in}$ (the steam cycle receives no additional fuel, only recovered waste heat). Therefore:

$$
\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT,net} + W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}
$$

### Step 6 — Rewrite in Terms of Component Efficiencies

Substituting Steps 2 and 4 into Step 5:

$$
\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT,net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} + \frac{W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}
$$

The first term is $\eta_{GT}$. The second term is:

$$
\frac{W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}} \cdot \frac{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}
$$

$$
= \eta_{Rankine} \cdot \frac{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}
$$

Now, the heat not converted to work by the gas turbine becomes the HRSG input (assuming no other losses):

$$
\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{Q}_{in} (1 - \eta_{GT})
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{Rankine} \cdot (1 - \eta_{GT})}
$$

This is the **canonical combined cycle efficiency equation** in KTU textbooks. Notice that the equation does not contain $\eta_{HRSG}$ explicitly — it is **implicitly embedded in the assumption that all rejected heat from the gas turbine is fully captured**.

When $\eta_{HRSG} < 1$, the corrected form is:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{Rankine} \cdot (1 - \eta_{GT}) \cdot \eta_{HRSG}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (modeled on KTU pattern):**
> A combined cycle power plant has a gas turbine with net electrical output of **80 MW** and a steam turbine output of **50 MW**. The HRSG recovers heat from the gas turbine exhaust at the rate of **180 MW** (thermal). The fuel input to the gas turbine combustor is **280 MW** (thermal, based on LHV). Compute:
> (a) Efficiency of the gas turbine cycle.
> (b) Efficiency of the steam (Rankine) cycle.
> (c) Overall combined cycle efficiency.
> (d) Net plant efficiency assuming an auxiliary consumption of **6 MW**.

### Part (a) — Gas Turbine Efficiency

$$
\eta_{GT} = \frac{W_{GT,net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{80 \text{ MW}}{280 \text{ MW}} = 0.2857
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{GT} = 28.57\%}
$$

### Part (b) — Steam (Rankine) Cycle Efficiency

The HRSG supplies 180 MW of heat to the steam cycle. The steam turbine produces 50 MW of work.

$$
\eta_{Rankine} = \frac{W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}} = \frac{50 \text{ MW}}{180 \text{ MW}} = 0.2778
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{Rankine} = 27.78\%}
$$

### Part (c) — Combined Cycle Efficiency (Direct Method)

$$
\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT,net} + W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{80 + 50}{280} = \frac{130}{280} = 0.4643
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CC} = 46.43\%}
$$

### Part (d) — Net Plant Efficiency

Auxiliary consumption: $P_{aux} = 6$ MW.

$$
P_{net} = P_{gross} - P_{aux} = (80 + 50) - 6 = 124 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\eta_{net} = \frac{P_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{124}{280} = 0.4429
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{net} = 44.29\%}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Verification Using the Formula of Section 3.1

Let us confirm the result of Part (c) using the derived formula:

$$
\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{Rankine} \cdot (1 - \eta_{GT})
$$

$$
\eta_{CC} = 0.2857 + 0.2778 \cdot (1 - 0.2857)
$$

$$
\eta_{CC} = 0.2857 + 0.2778 \cdot 0.7143
$$

$$
\eta_{CC} = 0.2857 + 0.1984
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CC} = 0.4841 \approx 48.41\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrepancy Notice:** The direct calculation gave 46.43%, while the formula gave 48.41%. The reason: the direct method implicitly assumed that the *total fuel input (280 MW)* equals the *sum of GT work + HRSG input (80 + 180 = 260 MW)*, but in fact there is an **unaccounted 20 MW loss** (likely radiation, generator loss, or compressor inefficiency not absorbed by the GT work term). In a **real KTU problem, students must state this assumption explicitly** or use whichever value the question provides. This is one of the most common trap questions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cycle Configurations

| Configuration | η Typical Range | Capital Cost (₹/kW, India 2024) | Ramp-Up Time | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Open-Cycle GT | 30 – 42% | 3 – 4 Crore | 10 – 15 min | Peak shaving, standby |
| Simple Rankine (coal) | 33 – 42% | 5 – 6 Crore | 4 – 8 hours | Baseload coal |
| **Combined Cycle (NG)** | **55 – 62%** | **4 – 5 Crore** | **30 – 60 min** | **Baseload + Intermediate** |
| CCPP with Duct Firing | 50 – 55% | + 0.3 Crore | 30 min | Peak augmentation |
| Triple-Pressure Reheat CC | 60 – 62% | 5 – 6 Crore | 60 min | High-efficiency baseload |
| STIG (Steam Injected GT) | 42 – 48% | 3.5 Crore | 20 min | Industrial CHP |

## 3.5 Key Analytical Insight: Why Efficiency Jumps From 40% to 60%

> [!TIP]
> **The Carnot Connection:**
> The maximum theoretical efficiency of any heat engine is the Carnot efficiency: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$.
> For a gas turbine, the effective $T_H$ is high (1600 K) but the heat is added at **constant pressure** (not isothermal), so the Brayton cycle is always less than Carnot. For a steam cycle, $T_H$ is limited by steam metallurgical limits (565 °C → 838 K), so even though it is a Rankine cycle, it is far below the Brayton temperature.
>
> By **cascading** the two cycles, we effectively use:
> - The **high temperature** portion → Brayton topping.
> - The **low-to-moderate temperature** rejection → Rankine bottoming.
>
> This approach **approximates an Ericsson-like cycle** that has higher theoretical efficiency than either cycle alone. The improvement factor is mathematically:
>
> $$
> \text{Improvement Factor} = \frac{\eta_{CC}}{\eta_{GT}} = 1 + \frac{\eta_{Rankine} (1 - \eta_{GT})}{\eta_{GT}}
> $$
>
> For $\eta_{GT} = 0.35$ and $\eta_{Rankine} = 0.33$, this factor is $1.57$ — meaning **57% more electrical output from the same fuel**.

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete energy and mass flow architecture** of a 1:1:1 (one gas turbine : one HRSG : one steam turbine) combined cycle configuration. Each block represents a major component; arrows represent the flow of working fluid (air, gas, steam, water) or electrical power.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> A2[Axial Flow Compressor]

    A2 -.drives.-> A4
    A4 --> A5[Exhaust Gas at 540 C]
    A4 --> G1[Synchronous Generator 1]
    G1 --> E1[Electrical Output Bus 50 Hz]

    A5 --> B1[HRSG HP Section]
    B1 --> B2[HRSG IP Section]
    B2 --> B3[HRSG LP Section]
    B3 --> B4[Stack Gas to Atmosphere 120 C]

    FW1[Feedwater Pump] --> B3
    FW1 --> B2
    FW1 --> B1

    B1 --> C1[HP Steam Turbine]
    C1 --> C2[IP Steam Turbine]
    C2 --> C3[LP Steam Turbine]
    C3 --> D1[Condenser Vacuum 0.08 bar]
    D1 --> FW1

    C1 --> G2[Synchronous Generator 2]
    C2 --> G2
    C3 --> G2
    G2 --> E1

    AUX[Auxiliary Loads Pumps Fans Controls] -.consumes.-> E1
    GRID[220 kV or 400 kV Transmission Grid] -.receives.-> E1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FUEL[Fuel Energy Input 280 MW thermal] --> COMBUST[Combustor]

    COMBUST -->|direct fuel heating| HRSG2[HRSG Duct Firing Optional]

    GT -->|80 MW net electrical| GEN1[Generator 1 Output]
    GT -->|170 MW hot exhaust| HRSG[HRSG Heat Exchanger]

    HRSG -->|50 MW steam turbine work| ST[STEAM TURBINE]

    ST -->|50 MW net electrical| GEN2[Generator 2 Output]

    GEN2 --> TOTAL
    TOTAL -->|minus 6 MW| AUX[Auxiliaries]
```

## 4.4 Reading the Diagrams — KTU Exam Tip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU Energy Audit examination, when asked to *"draw the block diagram of a combined cycle power plant"*, the student **must** include:
>
> 1. The **air intake and filter** at the compressor inlet.
> 2. The **fuel input** arrow to the combustor.
> 3. The **HRSG with at least two pressure levels** (HP and LP).
> 4. The **condenser** with cooling water flow.
> 5. **Two separate generators** (or a clear note of single-shaft configuration).
> 6. The **stack** showing exhaust to atmosphere.
> 7. The **electrical output bus** connecting to the grid.
>
> Missing the **condenser return line to the feedwater pump** is the most common reason students lose 1–2 marks in such diagram questions.
