# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define combined cycle power generation. Why is its efficiency higher than a simple gas turbine plant?**

**Model Answer:**

Combined cycle power generation is a system in which the **waste heat from the exhaust of a gas turbine (Brayton cycle)** is recovered in a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** and used to generate steam, which then drives a **steam turbine (Rankine cycle)**. The electrical outputs of both turbines are combined.

The efficiency is higher because the **simple gas turbine rejects about 60–70% of the fuel energy as exhaust heat**. In a combined cycle, this rejected heat is **partially converted to additional electrical work** by the steam turbine, **without burning additional fuel**. While a simple gas turbine achieves **30–42% efficiency**, a combined cycle plant achieves **55–62% efficiency**, an improvement of nearly **20 percentage points** for the same fuel input.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)? List its main sections.**

**Model Answer:**

A Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) is a **heat exchanger device that captures thermal energy from the hot exhaust gases of a gas turbine** and uses it to convert feedwater into steam at conditions suitable for driving a steam turbine. It typically operates at **3 pressure levels** (HP, IP, LP) and contains the following main sections:

1. **Economizer** — preheats the feedwater using residual flue-gas heat.
2. **Evaporator (or Steam Drum section)** — converts water to saturated steam.
3. **Superheater** — raises the steam temperature above saturation.
4. **Reheater** (in reheat designs) — reheats partially expanded steam.
5. **Stack / Gas Duct** — releases cooled exhaust to the atmosphere.

The HRSG is the **critical link** between the Brayton topping cycle and the Rankine bottoming cycle.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU ESE pattern**, each long-answer question is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Two alternative choices (Q-A and Q-B) are provided so the student can attempt the one of their choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a combined cycle power plant. Discuss the role of the HRSG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A combined cycle power plant uses a gas turbine with a heat input of **300 MJ/s** and generates **100 MW** of electrical power. The exhaust heat is recovered in an HRSG, which supplies **160 MJ/s** of heat to a steam turbine that generates **55 MW** of electrical power. Calculate: (i) Gas turbine efficiency, (ii) Steam cycle efficiency, (iii) Overall combined cycle efficiency, (iv) Net plant efficiency assuming an auxiliary consumption of **4 MW**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The combined cycle plant consists of the following major components arranged in series and parallel:

- **Air Compressor → Combustion Chamber → Gas Turbine → Generator 1** (the Brayton topping cycle).
- **Gas Turbine Exhaust → HRSG → Steam Turbine → Generator 2** (the Rankine bottoming cycle).
- **Condenser → Feedwater Pump → HRSG** (the steam cycle water loop).

**Step 2 — Working Explanation (3 marks):**

Atmospheric air is drawn into the **compressor** and pressurized to a ratio of 10–20. Fuel is burned in the **combustion chamber**, raising the gas temperature to **1300–1600 °C**. The hot gases expand through the **gas turbine**, producing mechanical work. Part of this work drives the compressor; the remainder drives **Generator 1**. The exhaust gases, still at **500–600 °C**, enter the **HRSG**, where they transfer heat to feedwater. Steam generated at high pressure drives the **steam turbine**, which drives **Generator 2**. Spent steam is condensed and returned as feedwater.

**Step 3 — Role of the HRSG (1 mark):**

The HRSG is the **energy-coupling device** between the two cycles. It captures waste heat that would otherwise be lost in the stack and converts it into useful steam energy. It is the **key component that makes the combined cycle possible**, transforming a 35%-efficient gas turbine into a 55–60%-efficient combined plant.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{Q}_{in} = 300$ MJ/s
- $W_{GT,net} = 100$ MW
- $\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = 160$ MJ/s
- $W_{ST} = 55$ MW
- $P_{aux} = 4$ MW

**(i) Gas Turbine Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{GT} = \frac{W_{GT,net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{100}{300} = 0.3333
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{GT} = 33.33\%} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Steam Cycle Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{Rankine} = \frac{W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{HRSG}} = \frac{55}{160} = 0.3438
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{Rankine} = 34.38\%} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Overall Combined Cycle Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT,net} + W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{100 + 55}{300} = \frac{155}{300} = 0.5167
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CC} = 51.67\%} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iv) Net Plant Efficiency:**

$$
P_{net} = (W_{GT,net} + W_{ST}) - P_{aux} = 155 - 4 = 151 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\eta_{net} = \frac{P_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{151}{300} = 0.5033
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{net} = 50.33\%} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the working of a **Combined Cycle Power Plant (CCPP)**. Mention the **typical efficiencies** of the gas turbine, steam turbine, and combined cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas turbine in a combined cycle plant has the following data:
- Compressor pressure ratio: **12**
- Turbine inlet temperature: **1500 K**
- Exhaust gas temperature to HRSG: **850 K**
- Mass flow of exhaust gas: **80 kg/s**
- Specific heat of exhaust gas: **1.05 kJ/kg·K**

Calculate: (i) Heat input to the gas turbine, (ii) Heat recovered in the HRSG, (iii) If the HRSG converts 85% of recovered heat to steam turbine work, find the combined cycle efficiency, given the gas turbine produces **25 MW**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

(Reference: Section 4.1 of these notes — show compressor, combustor, gas turbine, HRSG with HP/IP/LP levels, steam turbine, condenser, two generators, and stack.)

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**

Atmospheric air is compressed in the **axial compressor** (pressure ratio ≈ 12–20). Fuel is injected and burned in the **combustion chamber**, raising the gas temperature to the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** of **1300–1600 °C**. The hot gases expand through the **gas turbine**, producing mechanical work that drives both the compressor and **Generator 1**. The exhaust gas, still hot at **500–600 °C**, enters the **HRSG**, transferring its heat to feedwater. The produced high-pressure steam drives the **steam turbine** connected to **Generator 2**. Spent steam is condensed in a **condenser** at high vacuum and recycled by the **feedwater pump**.

**Typical Efficiencies (2 marks):**

- Simple gas turbine: **30 – 42%**
- Simple steam turbine (subcritical): **33 – 40%**
- Combined cycle: **55 – 62%**
- Combined cycle with triple-pressure reheat: up to **62%**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $r_p = 12$
- $T_3 = 1500$ K (TIT)
- $T_4 = 850$ K (exhaust to HRSG)
- $\dot{m}_g = 80$ kg/s
- $c_{pg} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K
- $W_{GT,net} = 25$ MW
- HRSG-to-work conversion efficiency = 85% = 0.85

**(i) Heat Input to the Gas Turbine:**

The heat input equals the fuel energy released in the combustor, which raises the gas from compressor discharge temperature $T_2$ to the turbine inlet temperature $T_3$.

For a pressure ratio of 12 with $\gamma = 1.4$ for air:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}
$$

Assuming $T_1 = 300$ K:

$$
T_2 = 300 \cdot 12^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \cdot 12^{0.2857} = 300 \cdot 2.034 = 610.2 \text{ K}
$$

Heat input:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_3 - T_2)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 80 \cdot 1.05 \cdot (1500 - 610.2)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 80 \cdot 1.05 \cdot 889.8
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 84 \cdot 889.8 = 74,743.2 \text{ kW} \approx 74.74 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{in} \approx 74.74 \text{ MW}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Heat Recovered in the HRSG:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_4 - T_{stack})
$$

Assuming a typical stack temperature of $T_{stack} = 400$ K (since the problem does not specify, we use 400 K as a reasonable CCPP value):

$$
\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = 80 \cdot 1.05 \cdot (850 - 400)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = 84 \cdot 450 = 37,800 \text{ kW} = 37.8 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{HRSG} = 37.8 \text{ MW}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Combined Cycle Efficiency:**

Steam turbine work output (with 85% conversion):

$$
W_{ST} = 0.85 \cdot \dot{Q}_{HRSG} = 0.85 \cdot 37.8 = 32.13 \text{ MW}
$$

Combined cycle efficiency:

$$
\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT,net} + W_{ST}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{25 + 32.13}{74.74} = \frac{57.13}{74.74}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{CC} = 0.7644 = 76.44\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Stack Temperature Assumption (Critical):** The problem does not specify $T_{stack}$. Students must **state the assumed value explicitly** (typically 400 K for a well-designed HRSG). If the student assumes 300 K (which is unrealistic due to acid dew point), they get a higher but practically wrong answer. **Valuator deducts 1 mark** for not stating the assumption.
>
> 2. **Mass Flow Equality:** Many students incorrectly use different $\dot{m}$ for compressor and turbine. For lean combustion, $\dot{m}_{air} \approx \dot{m}_{gas}$. State this assumption. **Valuator deducts 0.5 mark** for omission.
>
> 3. **Gas Turbine Work vs Heat Input:** A common trap is using $W_{GT,net} = 25$ MW as $\dot{Q}_{in}$. This is **wrong** — 25 MW is the *output*, not the *input*. **Valuator deducts 2 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 4. **The 76% Result Is Unrealistic:** Real combined cycles top out at ~62%. The 76% value here arises because we ignored compressor work, generator losses, mechanical losses, and auxiliary loads. In a KTU exam, if the answer is "too high", the student should **mention the simplifying assumptions**. **Valuator appreciates** this awareness even if no marks are explicitly allocated.

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Scheme Mark Distribution Reminder

| Question Type | Marks | Cognitive Level (Revised Bloom) | Course Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part A — Short Answer | 3 × 2 = 6 | Remember / Understand | CO2, CO3 |
| Part B — Long Answer | 14 (choice of 1) | Apply / Analyze / Evaluate | CO3, CO4 |
| Total Module Weightage | 20 marks | — | — |

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Before the exam, ensure you can confidently answer "yes" to every item below:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Combined cycle = Gas turbine (Brayton) + Steam turbine (Rankine) sharing one fuel input.
- [ ] **Topping cycle** is the gas turbine; **bottoming cycle** is the steam turbine.
- [ ] **HRSG** = Heat Recovery Steam Generator — the bridge between the two cycles.
- [ ] **Typical efficiencies:** GT = 30–42%, ST = 33–40%, **CC = 55–62%**.
- [ ] **Key equation:** $\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{Rankine} (1 - \eta_{GT}) \eta_{HRSG}$.
- [ ] **Exhaust gas temperature entering HRSG** is around **500–600 °C**; leaving at **100–150 °C**.
- [ ] **HRSG sections** (in order of gas flow): Economizer → Evaporator → Superheater → Reheater → Stack.
- [ ] **Three pressure levels** (HP, IP, LP) are used in modern CCPP for higher efficiency.
- [ ] **Compressor pressure ratio (r_p):** modern F-class GT = 18–23.
- [ ] **TIT (Turbine Inlet Temperature):** 1300–1600 °C.
- [ ] **Condenser vacuum:** 0.05 – 0.10 bar absolute.
- [ ] **Single-shaft** vs **multi-shaft** configurations — know the difference.
- [ ] **Duct firing** adds burners downstream of GT to boost steam — *not* a true CC efficiency improvement.
- [ ] **STIG (Steam Injected Gas Turbine)** is a related but distinct configuration.
- [ ] **Auxiliary consumption** is typically **3–7%** of gross output.
- [ ] **SFC** of CCPP = 0.20–0.25 kg oil-equivalent / kWh.
- [ ] **Heat rate** = 3600 / $\eta_{net}$ (in kJ/kWh).
- [ ] **Standard reference conditions (ISO):** 15 °C, 1.013 bar, 60% RH.
- [ ] **Block diagram must include:** compressor, combustor, GT, HRSG, ST, condenser, feedwater pump, two generators (or single), and stack.
- [ ] **Acid dew point** of exhaust ≈ 60–95 °C — must keep stack temperature above this to avoid corrosion.
- [ ] **The two cycles share one fuel input** — the steam cycle does NOT burn any additional fuel in a pure combined cycle.
- [ ] **Energy audit KPI:** Combined cycle plants have a Plant Load Factor (PLF) of 70–85% and CUF of 60–80% in Indian grid conditions.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Combined Cycle Electricity Generation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Analysis: Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economic Analysis: Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Economic Analysis Methods** in the context of Energy Management and Auditing (EMA) are a structured set of quantitative financial engineering techniques used to evaluate the economic viability, profitability, and long-term cost-effectiveness of proposed energy conservation projects, retrofits, and capital investments. These methods convert future streams of energy savings, operating costs, and capital expenditures into a common, comparable monetary framework — incorporating the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** — so that competing energy projects can be objectively ranked and justified for board-level approval.

According to the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) and KTU 2024 scheme guidelines, the four canonical methods mandated for energy project appraisal are:

1. **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** — a non-discounted screening method.
2. **Net Present Value (NPV)** — a discounted profitability indicator.
3. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — a discount rate that drives NPV to zero.
4. **Life Cycle Cost (LCC) / Annualized Cost Analysis** — a total cost-of-ownership method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Economic analysis questions are typically tagged to **Course Outcome CO4** (Develop energy-efficient solutions through appropriate audit instruments and economic justification) and tested at **Revised Bloom's Level Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)**. Always mention the **assumed discount rate** (commonly 10%–15% in Indian energy projects unless specified).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mango Tree" Intuition

Imagine you are given two choices:

- **Plan A:** A neighbour offers you **₹1,00,000 right now** to never plant a mango tree.
- **Plan B:** You plant a mango tree today for **₹50,000**, and for the next 20 years it gives you mangoes worth **₹15,000 per year**.

A naive person picks Plan A because ₹1,00,000 > ₹50,000. But the wise evaluator realizes that **₹15,000 received 5 years from now is not worth ₹15,000 received today** — because today's money could have been invested at, say, 10% interest in a Fixed Deposit. The **discounted present value** of those future mango-revenues may be less than, equal to, or greater than the initial ₹50,000. This is exactly what economic analysis methods compute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Truth:** Money has a *time dimension*. Two cash flows of identical nominal value but different timings are **not economically equivalent**. Economic methods are the "translation engine" that brings all future cash flows back to a single, comparable moment — usually **today (Year 0)**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Discount Rate ($i$ or $r$):** The opportunity cost of capital, usually expressed as a percentage per annum. For KTU problems, common values are **$i = 10\%$, $i = 12\%$, or $i = 15\%$**.
- **Present Value (PV):** The current worth of a future cash flow.
- **Future Value (FV):** The worth of a present cash flow at a future date.
- **Cash Flow ($CF_t$):** The net monetary inflow (positive) or outflow (negative) at the end of year $t$.
- **Project Life ($n$):** The useful life of the energy equipment in years.
- **Salvage Value ($SV$):** The residual market value of the equipment at the end of its useful life.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP)

**Concept:** Measures the time (in years) required for the cumulative annual savings to recover the initial investment. It is the **most widely used screening tool** in industry because of its simplicity, but it **ignores the time value of money** and any cash flows beyond the payback year.

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Determine the **Initial Investment** ($I_0$ or CapEx).
2. Identify the **Net Annual Savings** ($S$) = (Energy Cost Savings) − (Incremental O&M Costs).
3. Compute $SPP = I_0 / S$ assuming savings are uniform.
4. For non-uniform savings, accumulate year-by-year until cumulative savings $\geq I_0$.

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if SPP is less than the **target payback** set by the company's financial policy (typically 2–3 years for energy projects in India).

## 2.2 Method 2 — Net Present Value (NPV)

**Concept:** NPV discounts every future cash flow back to **Year 0** using the discount rate $i$, and then sums them. It directly answers: *"How much wealth does this project add to the firm in today's rupees?"*

**Operating Logic:**
1. Forecast the net cash flow $CF_t$ for each year $t = 1, 2, \dots, n$.
2. Discount each $CF_t$ using the factor $(1+i)^{-t}$.
3. Subtract the initial investment $I_0$.
4. The sum is the NPV.

**Decision Rule:**
- $NPV > 0$ → **Accept** (project earns more than the discount rate).
- $NPV < 0$ → **Reject** (project earns less than the discount rate).
- $NPV = 0$ → Indifferent (project earns exactly the discount rate).

## 2.3 Method 3 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

**Concept:** IRR is the discount rate $i^*$ at which the NPV of the project equals exactly zero. It is the **break-even discount rate** — the maximum interest rate a project can afford to pay on its borrowed capital and still break even.

**Decision Rule:**
- $IRR \geq$ company's Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR) → **Accept**.
- $IRR <$ MARR → **Reject**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disadvantages of IRR:** When cash flows change sign more than once, IRR can yield **multiple mathematical roots**, and it cannot rank mutually exclusive projects of different sizes. In such cases, **NPV is the superior method** — a fact KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.4 Method 4 — Life Cycle Cost (LCC) Analysis

**Concept:** LCC computes the **total owning cost** of a piece of equipment over its entire life, including purchase, installation, operation, maintenance, and disposal costs. It is the preferred method when comparing **competing equipment options** (e.g., standard motor vs. premium-efficiency motor) that deliver identical service but differ in cost profile.

**Two Equivalent Forms:**
- **Present Worth Method:** Sum the present values of all costs.
- **Annualized Cost Method:** Convert the LCC into a uniform equivalent annual cost (EUAC) using the capital recovery factor (CRF).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on Economic Analysis Methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Reading Note:** Vertical bars (absolute value) are rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Method | Master Formula | Key Variables & Units | Decision Rule |
|---|--------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Simple Payback Period (Uniform) | $$SPP = \dfrac{I_0}{S}$$ | $I_0$ = Initial Investment (₹); $S$ = Net Annual Savings (₹/yr) | $SPP \leq$ Target Payback → Accept |
| 2 | Present Value of a Single Future Cash Flow | $$PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+i)^n}$$ | $FV$ = Future Value (₹); $i$ = Discount Rate (decimal); $n$ = Number of Years | Comparison metric |
| 3 | Present Value of a Uniform Annual Series | $$PV = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$ | $A$ = Annual Amount (₹/yr); denominator = Present Worth Factor (PWF or P/A, i, n) | Comparison metric |
| 4 | Future Value of a Uniform Annual Series | $$FV = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$ | Numerator structure = Future Worth Factor (F/A, i, n) | Used for sinking fund calculation |
| 5 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $$CRF = \dfrac{i \cdot (1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$ | Converts Present Value to Equivalent Annual Cost | Used in EUAC method |
| 6 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$ | $CF_t$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$ (₹) | $NPV > 0$ → Accept |
| 7 | Net Present Worth (NPW) of a Series | $$NPW = -I_0 + S \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+i)^{-n}}{i} + \dfrac{SV}{(1+i)^n}$$ | $SV$ = Salvage Value (₹) | Same as NPV |
| 8 | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | $$0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$$ | Solve iteratively or by interpolation | $IRR \geq$ MARR → Accept |
| 9 | Benefit–Cost Ratio (BCR) / SIR | $$SIR = \dfrac{PV \text{ of Savings}}{PV \text{ of Costs}}$$ | Ratio of benefits to costs | $SIR > 1$ → Accept |
| 10 | Life Cycle Cost (LCC) — Present Worth | $$LCC = I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t} - \dfrac{SV}{(1+i)^n}$$ | $C_t$ = Annual O&M Cost in year $t$ | Choose option with lowest LCC |
| 11 | Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC) | $$EUAC = LCC \cdot CRF$$ | All costs annualized | Choose lowest EUAC |
| 12 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $$SFF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$ | Used to compute annual deposit for future liability | Auxiliary factor |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These methods are the **language of capital budgeting** in every energy-intensive industry — steel plants (savings from waste-heat recovery), cement plants (VFDs on kiln fans), commercial buildings (LED retrofits, chiller replacements), and power utilities (repowering vs. refurbishment). Without these methods, an Energy Manager cannot defend a ₹10 Crore retrofit to the board of directors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Present Worth Factor (P/A, i, n)

We start with the most fundamental identity. A sum of ₹1 received $n$ years from now is worth less than ₹1 today. The discount factor applied is $(1+i)^{-n}$.

**Step 1 — Single Cash Flow Discount:**
The present value of a cash flow $A$ received at the end of year $t$ is:

$$
PV = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^t}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum Across a Uniform Series:**
If the same cash flow $A$ is received at the end of every year from $t = 1$ to $t = n$, the total present value is the sum of a geometric progression:

$$
PV = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \dots + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor Out the Common Term:**
Let $x = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$. Then:

$$
PV = A \cdot (x + x^2 + \dots + x^n)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Geometric Series Formula:**
For a finite geometric series with first term $x$, common ratio $x$, and $n$ terms, the sum is:

$$
PV = A \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{1 - x^n}{1 - x}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Back $x = (1+i)^{-1}$:**

$$
PV = A \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+i} \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the Denominator:**

$$
1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i} = \dfrac{1+i - 1}{1+i} = \dfrac{i}{1+i}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$
PV = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

This is the celebrated **(P/A, i, n) factor** — the Present Worth of a Uniform Annual Series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Insight:** Notice that every discounted cash flow method in the formula sheet is a *variation* of this single identity. If you memorize how to derive this, you can re-derive the rest in the exam hall even under stress.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

The CRF converts a **Present Value** into an **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost** over $n$ years at interest rate $i$. Mathematically, it is the reciprocal of the (P/A, i, n) factor.

**Step 1 — Start with PWF Expression:**

$$
PV = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $A$ (the annual equivalent):**

$$
A = PV \cdot \dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $(1+i)^n$:**

$$
A = PV \cdot \dfrac{i \cdot (1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Final CRF Expression:**

$$
CRF(i, n) = \dfrac{i \cdot (1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

**Numerical Verification:** At $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$ years:

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \dfrac{0.25937}{1.5937} \approx 0.16275
$$

So ₹1,00,000 invested today is equivalent to paying **₹16,275 per year for 10 years** at 10% interest. Sanity check: 10 × 16,275 = 1,62,750 > 1,00,000 ✓ (the excess is the interest paid).

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**

> A manufacturing unit is evaluating a power-factor correction project. The data is as follows:
> - Initial Investment ($I_0$) = **₹5,00,000**
> - Net Annual Energy Savings ($S$) = **₹1,80,000 per year** (uniform for 5 years)
> - Salvage Value at end of Year 5 = **₹50,000**
> - Discount Rate = **12% per annum**
>
> Compute: (a) Simple Payback Period, (b) Net Present Value, (c) Internal Rate of Return, and (d) Benefit–Cost Ratio. Advise whether the project should be accepted if the MARR is 12%.

### Part (a) — Simple Payback Period

$$
SPP = \dfrac{I_0}{S} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000} = 2.778 \text{ years} \approx 2 \text{ years, 9 months}
$$

### Part (b) — Net Present Value

We need three components: PV of uniform savings, PV of salvage, and the initial outlay.

**Step 1 — Compute the PWF at 12% for 5 years:**

$$
PWF = \dfrac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = \dfrac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \dfrac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048
$$

**Step 2 — PV of Savings:**

$$
PV_{\text{Savings}} = 1{,}80{,}000 \times 3.6048 = 6{,}48{,}864
$$

**Step 3 — PV of Salvage Value (single future cash flow at $t = 5$):**

$$
PV_{SV} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{(1.12)^5} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{1.7623} = 28{,}371
$$

**Step 4 — Total NPV:**

$$
NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}48{,}864 + 28{,}371 = +1{,}77{,}235
$$

Since **$NPV = +₹1,77,235 > 0$**, the project earns more than 12% per year and should be **ACCEPTED**.

### Part (c) — Internal Rate of Return (Trial & Error with Interpolation)

We need to find $i^*$ such that NPV = 0. We test two bracketing rates.

**Trial 1 — at $i = 20\%$:**

$$
PWF_{20\%,5} = \dfrac{1 - (1.20)^{-5}}{0.20} = \dfrac{1 - 0.4019}{0.20} = \dfrac{0.5981}{0.20} = 2.9906
$$

$$
PV_{\text{Savings at 20\%}} = 1{,}80{,}000 \times 2.9906 = 5{,}38{,}311
$$

$$
PV_{SV \text{ at 20\%}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{(1.20)^5} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{2.4883} = 20{,}094
$$

$$
NPV_{20\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}38{,}311 + 20{,}094 = +58{,}405
$$

**Trial 2 — at $i = 25\%$:**

$$
PWF_{25\%,5} = \dfrac{1 - (1.25)^{-5}}{0.25} = \dfrac{1 - 0.3277}{0.25} = \dfrac{0.6723}{0.25} = 2.6893
$$

$$
PV_{\text{Savings at 25\%}} = 1{,}80{,}000 \times 2.6893 = 4{,}84{,}074
$$

$$
PV_{SV \text{ at 25\%}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{(1.25)^5} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{3.0518} = 16{,}384
$$

$$
NPV_{25\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}84{,}074 + 16{,}384 = +458
$$

**Trial 3 — at $i = 26\%$:**

$$
PWF_{26\%,5} = \dfrac{1 - (1.26)^{-5}}{0.26} = \dfrac{1 - 0.3154}{0.26} = \dfrac{0.6846}{0.26} = 2.6351
$$

$$
PV_{\text{Savings at 26\%}} = 1{,}80{,}000 \times 2.6351 = 4{,}74{,}318
$$

$$
PV_{SV \text{ at 26\%}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{(1.26)^5} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{3.1758} = 15{,}745
$$

$$
NPV_{26\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}74{,}318 + 15{,}745 = -9{,}937
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Linear Interpolation:**

$$
IRR = 25\% + \dfrac{NPV_{25\%}}{NPV_{25\%} - NPV_{26\%}} \times (26\% - 25\%)
$$

$$
IRR = 25\% + \dfrac{458}{458 - (-9{,}937)} \times 1\% = 25\% + \dfrac{458}{10{,}395} \times 1\%
$$

$$
IRR = 25\% + 0.0441\% \approx 25.04\%
$$

**Conclusion:** $IRR \approx 25.04\% \gg$ MARR of 12% → **ACCEPT** the project.

### Part (d) — Benefit–Cost Ratio (SIR)

$$
SIR = \dfrac{PV \text{ of Savings (including Salvage)}}{I_0} = \dfrac{6{,}48{,}864 + 28{,}371}{5{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{6{,}77{,}235}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.354
$$

Since $SIR = 1.354 > 1$, the project **PASSES** the BCR test.

### Final Decision Matrix (Summary)

| Method | Computed Value | Decision Criterion | Verdict |
|--------|----------------|---------------------|----------|
| SPP | 2.78 years | < 3 years | **Accept** |
| NPV | +₹1,77,235 | > 0 | **Accept** |
| IRR | 25.04% | > 12% MARR | **Accept** |
| SIR | 1.354 | > 1.0 | **Accept** |

> [!NOTE]
> **All four methods unanimously recommend ACCEPTANCE**, which is the ideal scenario in a KTU exam. When methods disagree, give precedence to **NPV**, then IRR, then SIR, then SPP — in that order.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Economic Method

This Mermaid block maps the analytical decision tree a student should mentally follow when an Energy Management problem is presented.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Energy Project Proposal Received"] --> B{"Is initial\ninvestment known?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Reject Proposal\nReturn to Feasibility Stage"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Are annual\nsavings uniform?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Compute Simple Payback Period\nSPP = I0 divide S"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Compute Cumulative Payback\nYear by Year Accumulation"]
    E --> G{"SPP less than\ntarget payback?"}
    F --> G
    G -- "No" --> H["Reject Project\nSeek Lower CapEx Option"]
    G -- "Yes" --> I["Apply Discounted Cash Flow Methods"]
    I --> J["Compute Net Present Value NPV"]
    J --> K{"NPV greater\nthan zero?"}
    K -- "No" --> L["Reject Project\nInsufficient Return"]
    K -- "Yes" --> M["Compute Internal Rate of Return IRR"]
    M --> N{"IRR greater than\nMARR discount rate?"}
    N -- "Yes" --> O["ACCEPT PROJECT\nFinal Recommendation to Board"]
    N -- "No" --> P["Reject Project\nReallocate Capital"]
    L --> Q["Document Decision in\nAudit Report"]
    H --> Q
    P --> Q
    O --> R["Proceed to Implementation\nPhase and Monitor Savings"]
```

## 4.2 Method Classification Architecture

This block diagram illustrates the **hierarchical taxonomy** of the four methods studied, classified by whether they incorporate the time value of money.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Economic Analysis Methods"] --> B["Static Methods\nIgnore Time Value of Money"]
    A --> C["Dynamic Methods\nIncorporate Time Value of Money"]
    B --> B1["Simple Payback Period SPP"]
    B --> B2["Return on Investment ROI"]
    C --> C1["Net Present Value NPV"]
    C --> C2["Internal Rate of Return IRR"]
    C --> C3["Benefit Cost Ratio SIR"]
    C --> C4["Life Cycle Cost LCC"]
    C1 --> D1["Ranks Profitability in Rupees"]
    C2 --> D2["Ranks Profitability in Percent"]
    C3 --> D3["Ranks Profitability as Ratio"]
    C4 --> D4["Ranks Lowest Total Cost of Ownership"]
```

## 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline for the Worked Example

This Gantt-style block diagram visualizes the temporal pattern of cash flows in the worked example, helping students see **when** each rupee enters or leaves the project.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0["Year 0\nOutflow 500000"] --> Y1["Year 1\nInflow 180000"]
    Y1 --> Y2["Year 2\nInflow 180000"]
    Y2 --> Y3["Year 3\nInflow 180000"]
    Y3 --> Y4["Year 4\nInflow 180000"]
    Y4 --> Y5["Year 5\nInflow 180000 plus Salvage 50000"]
    style Y0 fill:#FFB3B3
    style Y1 fill:#B3FFB3
    style Y2 fill:#B3FFB3
    style Y3 fill:#B3FFB3
    style Y4 fill:#B3FFB3
    style Y5 fill:#B3D9FF
```

**Reading Key:**
- **Red** = Cash Outflow (Investment)
- **Green** = Uniform Annual Savings
- **Blue** = Year 5 combines operating savings and salvage recovery

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**"Define the term 'Net Present Value' as applied to energy conservation projects. State the decision rule based on NPV."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — word count target: ~60 words):**

> Net Present Value (NPV) is the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash inflows and outflows associated with an energy project over its entire useful life, discounted at a specified rate of interest (the cost of capital). Mathematically, it is the difference between the **discounted future savings** and the **initial capital investment**.
>
> **Decision Rule:** A project is **economically accepted if NPV > 0**, meaning the project earns a return greater than the discount rate. If NPV < 0, the project is rejected. When NPV = 0, the firm is financially indifferent.
>
> *(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula/implication, 1 mark decision rule.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]
**"Differentiate between Simple Payback Period and Discounted Payback Period. Why is the latter preferred in energy auditing?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — word count target: ~70 words):**

> The **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** computes the time required to recover the initial investment from **undiscounted** annual savings. It ignores the time value of money and any post-payback cash flows.
>
> The **Discounted Payback Period (DPP)** applies the same logic but uses **present-valued** annual savings, requiring the use of a discount rate.
>
> **Why DPP is preferred:** Energy projects typically span 10–20 years, during which inflation, interest rates, and risk profiles shift. DPP accounts for these factors, providing a more **realistic and risk-adjusted** recovery horizon. It is therefore a **more conservative and prudent** metric for long-term energy retrofits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks on Part B questions by: (a) forgetting to state the **units** of the final answer, (b) skipping the **decision rule verdict** (Accept/Reject) at the end, (c) using the wrong cash flow sign convention (cash outflows must be negative), and (d) failing to mention the **assumed salvage value handling**. A 14-mark KTU answer demands *all four* evaluation methods with a **concluding recommendation paragraph**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]**

> A steel plant is evaluating the installation of a waste-heat recovery (WHR) boiler. The investment and savings data are:
>
> - Initial Investment ($I_0$) = **₹25,00,000**
> - Net Annual Savings (uniform, for 8 years) = **₹5,50,000 per year**
> - Salvage Value at end of Year 8 = **₹2,00,000**
> - Discount Rate ($i$) = **12% per annum**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of the project. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Simple Payback Period** and the **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** assuming an additional annual O&M cost of ₹50,000. Comment on the economic viability of the WHR project. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Present Worth Factor at 12% for 8 years:**

$$
PWF_{12\%,8} = \dfrac{1 - (1.12)^{-8}}{0.12} = \dfrac{1 - 0.40388}{0.12} = \dfrac{0.59612}{0.12} = 4.9676
$$

**Step 2 — PV of Annual Savings:**

$$
PV_{\text{Savings}} = 5{,}50{,}000 \times 4.9676 = 27{,}32{,}180
$$

**Step 3 — PV of Salvage Value (at $t = 8$):**

$$
PV_{SV} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^8} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{2.4760} = 80{,}775
$$

**Step 4 — Total NPV:**

$$
NPV = -25{,}00{,}000 + 27{,}32{,}180 + 80{,}775 = +3{,}12{,}955
$$

**Decision:** $NPV = +₹3,12,955 > 0$ → Project is **economically viable** at 12% discount rate. **(1 mark for verdict)**

**Step 5 — IRR by Interpolation:**

We test two rates where NPV changes sign.

*At $i = 18\%$:*

$$
PWF_{18\%,8} = \dfrac{1 - (1.18)^{-8}}{0.18} = \dfrac{1 - 0.2660}{0.18} = 4.0776
$$

$$
PV_{\text{Savings at 18\%}} = 5{,}50{,}000 \times 4.0776 = 22{,}42{,}680
$$

$$
PV_{SV \text{ at 18\%}} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1.18)^8} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{3.7589} = 53{,}207
$$

$$
NPV_{18\%} = -25{,}00{,}000 + 22{,}42{,}680 + 53{,}207 = -2{,}04{,}113
$$

*At $i = 15\%$:*

$$
PWF_{15\%,8} = \dfrac{1 - (1.15)^{-8}}{0.15} = \dfrac{1 - 0.3269}{0.15} = 4.4873
$$

$$
PV_{\text{Savings at 15\%}} = 5{,}50{,}000 \times 4.4873 = 24{,}68{,}015
$$

$$
PV_{SV \text{ at 15\%}} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1.15)^8} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{3.0590} = 65{,}381
$$

$$
NPV_{15\%} = -25{,}00{,}000 + 24{,}68{,}015 + 65{,}381 = +33{,}396
$$

**Step 6 — Linear Interpolation:**

$$
IRR = 15\% + \dfrac{33{,}396}{33{,}396 - (-2{,}04{,}113)} \times (18\% - 15\%)
$$

$$
IRR = 15\% + \dfrac{33{,}396}{2{,}37{,}509} \times 3\% = 15\% + 0.422\% \approx 15.42\%
$$

**Verdict:** $IRR \approx 15.42\% > 12\%$ MARR → **Accept**. **(1 mark for verdict)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Simple Payback Period (using net annual savings of ₹5,50,000):**

$$
SPP = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} = 4.545 \text{ years} \approx 4 \text{ years, 7 months}
$$

**Step 2 — Life Cycle Cost Computation:**

LCC = Initial Cost + PV of O&M Costs − PV of Salvage Value.

Net Annual Cost = ₹5,50,000 (savings offset by ₹50,000 O&M)... actually, for **LCC**, we compute costs only.

> **Approach:** Since the WHR system *generates* savings of ₹5,50,000 and *incurs* O&M of ₹50,000, the net annual cash inflow is ₹5,00,000. The LCC of *cost elements only* is:
>
> $$
> LCC_{\text{cost}} = I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{8} \dfrac{50{,}000}{(1.12)^t} - \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^8}
> $$
>
> $$
> PV_{O\&M} = 50{,}000 \times 4.9676 = 2{,}48{,}380
> $$
>
> $$
> LCC_{\text{net}} = 25{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}48{,}380 - 80{,}775 = 26{,}67{,}605
> $$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC):**

$$
CRF_{12\%,8} = \dfrac{0.12 \times (1.12)^8}{(1.12)^8 - 1} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{1.4760} = 0.20130
$$

$$
EUAC = 26{,}67{,}605 \times 0.20130 = 5{,}36{,}988 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4 — Viability Comment (paragraph form for the remaining 2 marks):**

> The Simple Payback Period of approximately **4.55 years** is well within the typical industrial target of 5 years for energy projects, providing an early break-even. The NPV of **+₹3,12,955** indicates that the project will generate wealth above the 12% hurdle rate, and the IRR of **15.42%** confirms a return that exceeds the company's MARR. The EUAC of ₹5,36,988/year is lower than the uniform annual savings of ₹5,50,000, confirming positive net annualized benefit. **All four metrics unanimously support the acceptance and implementation of the WHR boiler project.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]**

> A textile mill is comparing two energy-efficient motors for replacement of an existing 50 HP motor:
>
> | Parameter | Motor X (Standard) | Motor Y (Premium) |
> |-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
> | Purchase Price | ₹1,20,000 | ₹1,80,000 |
> | Annual Energy Cost | ₹1,00,000 | ₹60,000 |
> | Annual O&M Cost | ₹10,000 | ₹8,000 |
> | Life | 10 years | 12 years |
> | Salvage Value | ₹10,000 | ₹15,000 |
> | Discount Rate | 10% | 10% |
>
> **(a)** Using the **Life Cycle Cost (Present Worth) method**, determine which motor is more economical. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Net Present Value of Savings** for the Premium Motor (Y) over the Standard Motor (X) and the **Benefit–Cost Ratio** of the incremental investment. Advise the mill owner. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Note on Different Lives:** When competing equipment have **unequal lives**, the standard KTU approach is to use a **common analysis period** (the LCM of the two lives) or to convert to **EUAC** using each equipment's own CRF. We will use the **EUAC method** for direct comparison.

**Step 1 — Compute CRF for each motor.**

For Motor X (n = 10, i = 10%):

$$
CRF_X = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{1.5937} = 0.16275
$$

For Motor Y (n = 12, i = 10%):

$$
CRF_Y = \dfrac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{12}}{(1.10)^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 3.1384}{2.1384} = 0.14676
$$

**Step 2 — LCC (Present Worth) for Motor X:**

$$
PWF_{X} = \dfrac{1}{CRF_X} = 6.1446
$$

$$
LCC_X = 1{,}20{,}000 + (1{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}000) \times 6.1446 - \dfrac{10{,}000}{(1.10)^{10}}
$$

$$
LCC_X = 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}10{,}000 \times 6.1446 - 3{,}855
$$

$$
LCC_X = 1{,}20{,}000 + 6{,}75{,}906 - 3{,}855 = 7{,}92{,}051
$$

**Step 3 — LCC (Present Worth) for Motor Y:**

$$
PWF_{Y} = \dfrac{1}{CRF_Y} = 6.8137
$$

$$
LCC_Y = 1{,}80{,}000 + (60{,}000 + 8{,}000) \times 6.8137 - \dfrac{15{,}000}{(1.10)^{12}}
$$

$$
LCC_Y = 1{,}80{,}000 + 68{,}000 \times 6.8137 - \dfrac{15{,}000}{3.1384}
$$

$$
LCC_Y = 1{,}80{,}000 + 4{,}63{,}332 - 4{,}779 = 6{,}38{,}553
$$

**Step 4 — EUAC Comparison:**

$$
EUAC_X = 7{,}92{,}051 \times 0.16275 = 1{,}28{,}882 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
EUAC_Y = 6{,}38{,}553 \times 0.14676 = 93{,}715 \text{ per year}
$$

**Decision:** $LCC_Y < LCC_X$ and $EUAC_Y < EUAC_X$. **Premium Motor Y is more economical** by approximately **₹35,167 per year** in equivalent annual cost terms. **(2 marks for verdict with numerical justification)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

The two motors have different lives (10 vs 12 years), so incremental analysis is non-trivial. We adopt the **Equivalent Annual Savings (EAS)** approach — converting everything to a common annual basis.

**Step 1 — Incremental Capital Cost:**

$$
\Delta I_0 = 1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 60{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Incremental Annual Savings (Energy + O&M):**

$$
\Delta S = (1{,}10{,}000 - 68{,}000) = 42{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

But this comparison is for different lives. We use the **perpetual (infinite horizon) approach**: annualize the salvage too.

For Motor X, annualized salvage = $10{,}000 \times SFF_{10\%,10}$:

$$
SFF_{10\%,10} = \dfrac{0.10}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10}{1.5937} = 0.06275
$$

$$
\text{Annualized Salvage}_X = 10{,}000 \times 0.06275 = 627
$$

For Motor Y, annualized salvage = $15{,}000 \times SFF_{10\%,12}$:

$$
SFF_{10\%,12} = \dfrac{0.10}{(1.10)^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10}{2.1384} = 0.04676
$$

$$
\text{Annualized Salvage}_Y = 15{,}000 \times 0.04676 = 701
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Annual Cost of Incremental Investment:**

$$
EUAC_{\Delta} = 60{,}000 \times CRF_{Y} = 60{,}000 \times 0.14676 = 8{,}806 \text{ per year}
$$

(We use Motor Y's CRF because it has the longer life, ensuring the comparison is conservative.)

**Step 4 — NPV of Incremental Savings (using Motor Y's life of 12 years):**

$$
PWF_{Y} = 6.8137
$$

$$
NPV_{\Delta} = -60{,}000 + 42{,}000 \times 6.8137 + (701 - 627) \times \text{adjustment}
$$

Treating this simply: NPV of incremental savings = 42,000 × 6.8137 − 60,000 + (701−627) / 0.14676 × 0.14676... let's simplify to a clean NPV:

$$
NPV_{\Delta} \approx -60{,}000 + 42{,}000 \times 6.8137 = -60{,}000 + 2{,}86{,}175 = +2{,}26{,}175
$$

**Step 5 — Benefit–Cost Ratio (Incremental):**

$$
SIR_{\Delta} = \dfrac{PV \text{ of Incremental Savings}}{\Delta I_0} = \dfrac{2{,}86{,}175}{60{,}000} = 4.77
$$

**Verdict (Advisory):** Since $NPV_{\Delta} = +₹2{,}26{,}175 > 0$ **and** $SIR_{\Delta} = 4.77 \gg 1.0$, the incremental investment in **Premium Motor Y is strongly justified**. Despite its higher initial cost of ₹60,000 over the standard motor, the Premium Motor will save the textile mill approximately ₹42,000 per year in energy and O&M combined — paying back the premium in less than 2 years on a discounted basis. The mill owner should **proceed with the Premium Motor Y** for long-term economic benefit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Economic Analysis Methods**

- **Four canonical methods:** Simple Payback, Net Present Value, Internal Rate of Return, Life Cycle Cost. Memorize all four formulas and decision rules.
- **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the bedrock concept. Always discount future cash flows at the specified $i$ before summing.
- **Sign Convention:** Initial Investment is always **negative** (outflow); annual savings are **positive** (inflow); salvage is **positive** (inflow at end of life).
- **Discount Rate ($i$):** Convert percentages to decimal before substituting. Indian energy projects typically use $i = 10\%$, $12\%$, or $15\%$.
- **Present Worth Factor (PWF):** The single most-used factor. Formula: $\dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$. Memorize it.
- **CRF (Capital Recovery Factor):** The reciprocal companion of PWF. Formula: $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$. Used in EUAC method.
- **Decision Rules:** SPP → shorter is better. NPV → must be positive. IRR → must exceed MARR. SIR → must exceed 1.0. LCC → choose lowest LCC.
- **Interpolation for IRR:** Always bracket the IRR between two rates where NPV changes sign, then apply linear interpolation. Do not extrapolate.
- **Method Priority (KTU Convention):** NPV > IRR > SIR > SPP. When methods disagree, defer to NPV.
- **Unequal Lives Problem:** When comparing equipment with different lives, use the **EUAC method** or extend analysis to the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of the lives.
- **Salvage Value:** Must be discounted to present value before adding to NPV. Forgetting this costs 1–2 marks.
- **Examiner Traps:** (1) Forgetting the sign of the initial investment, (2) using nominal $i$ without converting to decimal, (3) concluding the numerical answer without stating **Accept/Reject**, (4) forgetting the unit "years" in payback, "₹" in NPV, "%" in IRR.
- **Real-World Heuristic:** SPP < 3 years → quick screening approval. IRR > 15% → attractive energy project. NPV > 0 → passes basic hurdle. LCC < competing alternative → switch.
- **Common KTU Past Year Pattern:** A 14-mark question will always combine *one discounted method* (NPV or IRR) with *one non-discounted method* (SPP) and conclude with a *viability paragraph*. Practice this exact structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cash Flow Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Energy Management and Auditing (PEEET742)**, a **Cash Flow Model** is a quantitative financial framework used to evaluate the economic viability of an energy conservation project (ECP) or an energy efficiency investment over its entire operational life. It systematically tracks all **inflows** (energy cost savings, revenue from waste heat recovery, incentives/subsidies) and **outflows** (initial capital investment, recurring O&M costs, fuel costs, decommissioning costs) on a year-by-year basis, applying the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle to arrive at a single, comparable economic indicator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> The Cash Flow Model is the mathematical representation of the timing and magnitude of cash receipts and disbursements attributable to an energy project. It is the foundation upon which all discounted and non-discounted economic evaluation metrics — **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**, **Discounted Payback Period (DPP)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)**, and **Annual Equivalent Value (AEV)** — are computed.

The governing equation for the Net Cash Flow in year *t* is:

$$NCF_t = \text{Savings}_t + \text{Revenue}_t - \text{O\&M}_t - \text{Fuel}_t - \text{Tax}_t$$

where **NCF_t** is the **Net Cash Flow** in year *t* and all terms are expressed in the **monetary unit of the base year (e.g., ₹ or $)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a small tea stall, and you want to install a **solar panel** on the roof. Today, you must spend **₹1,00,000** to buy the panel. After that, every month your electricity bill drops by **₹1,500** for the next **10 years**.

A **Cash Flow Model** is essentially a **ledger book** spread across time:
- **Year 0 (today)**: You hand over a big chunk of money → a *negative* cash flow.
- **Years 1 to 10**: The sun pays you back in the form of reduced bills → *positive* cash flows.

But here is the catch: **₹1,000 received next year is NOT worth the same as ₹1,000 today.** Why? Because if you had the ₹1,000 today, you could put it in a fixed deposit and earn interest. Therefore, future savings must be **discounted** to bring them to "today's value" before summing them up. The cash flow model is the disciplined spreadsheet that lets you do exactly this for an industrial energy project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A Cash Flow Model converts a *stream* of future savings into a *single number* that tells a manager — "Is this energy project financially worth doing?"

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Assumptions

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Discount Rate (Weighted Avg. Cost of Capital) | **i** or **r** | 8% – 15% (India industrial) | % per annum |
| Project Life | **n** | 5 – 25 years | years |
| Inflation Rate (energy prices) | **f** | 4% – 8% | % per annum |
| Tax Rate (Corporate) | **t** | 25% – 30% | % |
| Depreciation Rate | **d** | 10% – 80% (WDV) | % per annum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For numerical problems, KTU typically assumes a **constant discount rate (i)**, **uniform annual savings**, **zero salvage value**, and **no mid-life replacement**, unless explicitly stated otherwise in the question stem.

---

## 1.4 Why is the Cash Flow Model Important in Energy Auditing?

- It forms the **economic backbone** of the **Investment Grade Audit (IGA)** report.
- It allows the auditor to **compare competing EE measures** (e.g., a VFD retrofit vs. a heat exchanger retrofit) on a common, apples-to-apples basis.
- It satisfies the **ISO 50001** requirement for demonstrating the financial viability of Energy Performance Improvement Actions (EPIAs).
- It is the prerequisite for every KTU numerical problem in Module 4 of the syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of a Cash Flow Model

A cash flow model for an energy project consists of **four time regions** on the project timeline:

1. **Year 0 — The Investment Year:** The *single largest negative* cash flow, equal to the **Initial Capital Investment (P)**. It may include equipment cost, installation, commissioning, and interest during construction.
2. **Years 1 to (n−1) — The Operating Years:** A *sequence* of positive net cash flows (NCF) representing **annual energy cost savings net of O&M, fuel, and tax**.
3. **Year n — The Terminal Year:** Includes the *final year of operations* plus any **salvage value (S)** of the equipment and the **reversal of working capital**.
4. **Mid-Life (optional):** In some long-life projects (e.g., a 20-year chiller), a **mid-life overhaul cost** may be inserted at year 10, creating a negative spike.

---

## 2.2 The Logic of Time Value of Money (TVM)

The Time Value of Money is the *philosophical core* of the cash flow model. The logic chain is:

- **Premise 1:** Money can earn a risk-free return *r* (the discount rate) in the market.
- **Premise 2:** Therefore, **₹1 today > ₹1 tomorrow**.
- **Premise 3:** Future cash flows must be *scaled down* by a discount factor to express them in **Present Value (PV)**.
- **Premise 4:** Only Present Values can be *legitimately summed* across different time periods.

The mathematical embodiment of Premise 3 is the **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)**:

$$(P/F, i, n) = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$$

and its counterpart, the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)**:

$$(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^n}$$

---

## 2.3 Decision Criteria Derived from the Cash Flow Model

Once the cash flow stream is built, the engineer computes **one or more** of the following KTU-favorite metrics:

- **Simple Payback Period (SPP):** The number of years required for *undiscounted cumulative* cash flows to equal the initial investment.
- **Discounted Payback Period (DPP):** The number of years required for *discounted cumulative* cash flows to equal the initial investment.
- **Net Present Value (NPV):** The present value of all cash flows **minus** the initial investment. **Decision rule:** *Accept if NPV > 0.*
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** The discount rate at which **NPV = 0**. **Decision rule:** *Accept if IRR > MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).*
- **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR):** Ratio of PV of benefits to PV of costs. **Decision rule:** *Accept if BCR > 1.*
- **Annual Equivalent Value (AEV) / Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC):** The cash flow model converted into a *uniform annual series*, allowing comparison of projects with unequal lives.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU Module 4 numerical questions. Memorize the standard factor notation `(X/Y, i, n)`.

| # | Metric | Formula (Standard) | Decision Rule | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Present Worth of a Future Single Sum | $P = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$ | — | ₹ |
| 2 | Future Worth of a Present Single Sum | $F = P \cdot (1+i)^{n}$ | — | ₹ |
| 3 | Present Worth of a Uniform Annual Series | $P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$ | — | ₹ |
| 4 | Capital Recovery (A from P) | $A = P \cdot \frac{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | — | ₹/year |
| 5 | Sinking Fund (A from F) | $A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | — | ₹/year |
| 6 | Future Worth of a Uniform Series | $F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | — | ₹ |
| 7 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^{t}} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | Accept if $NPV > 0$ | ₹ |
| 8 | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | $0 = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+IRR)^{t}}$ | Accept if $IRR > MARR$ | % |
| 9 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \frac{P_0}{\text{Annual undiscounted NCF}}$ | Accept if $SPP < \text{Target}$ | years |
| 10 | Discounted Payback Period | $DPP = \text{Year before recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered at start of year}}{\text{Discounted NCF in that year}}$ | Accept if $DPP < \text{Target}$ | years |
| 11 | Benefit-Cost Ratio (B/C) | $B/C = \frac{\sum \frac{B_t}{(1+i)^{t}}}{\sum \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^{t}} + P_0}$ | Accept if $B/C > 1$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Annual Equivalent Value (AEV) | $AEV = NPV \cdot \frac{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Used for unequal-life comparison | ₹/year |
| 13 | Inflated Discount Rate (Fisher) | $i_{real} = \frac{1+i_{nom}}{1+f} - 1$ | Use when energy prices inflate | % |
| 14 | Present Worth with Gradient | $P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + G \cdot \frac{(P/A, i, n) - n \cdot (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | For escalating savings | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the notation $(P/A, i, n)$, $(P/F, i, n)$ etc. represents the standard **engineering economics factor notation** taught in every KTU-affiliated engineering economics module. Example: $(P/A, 10\%, 5) = 3.7908$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial practice, the cash flow model is the *lingua franca* between the energy auditor and the **CFO (Chief Financial Officer)** of the client company. Most Fortune 500 manufacturers will not sanction an EE project unless its **NPV is positive at the corporate hurdle rate (typically 12–18% in India) and its IRR exceeds the MARR**. Furthermore, energy service companies (ESCOs) use the cash flow model to structure **Energy Performance Contracts (EPCs)** and to compute **shared-savings** payments to the client. Thus, mastering the cash flow model is essential for any energy auditor who wishes to translate technical recommendations into bankable projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Net Present Value (NPV) Equation

The NPV equation is the cornerstone of the cash flow model. Below is its **exhaustive derivation** from first principles, which is a frequent KTU Part B question.

**Starting Premise:** A sum of money *F* received after *n* years, when discounted at rate *i*, is worth $F \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$ today.

**Step 1 — Define the discount factor for year *t*:**

The discount factor for a cash flow occurring at the end of year *t* is given by:

$$DF_t = (1+i)^{-t} = \frac{1}{(1+i)^{t}}$$

**Step 2 — Discount every annual cash flow to Year 0:**

The present value of the cash flow in year 1 is $\frac{NCF_1}{(1+i)^1}$; in year 2 it is $\frac{NCF_2}{(1+i)^2}$, and so on until year $n$ where it is $\frac{NCF_n}{(1+i)^n}$.

**Step 3 — Sum all discounted cash flows and subtract the initial outlay:**

The algebraic sum of all present values of inflows minus the initial investment $P_0$ gives:

$$NPV = -P_0 + \frac{NCF_1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{NCF_2}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{NCF_n}{(1+i)^n} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^n}$$

where $S$ is the salvage value at the end of year $n$.

**Step 4 — Express in summation (sigma) form:**

This is the **canonical NPV equation** used universally:

$$NPV = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^{t}} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

**Step 5 — Special case: Uniform annual cash flow ($NCF_t = A$ for all $t$):**

When the cash flow is a *uniform annual series*, we can factor out *A* and use the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor**:

$$NPV = -P_0 + A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

$$\Rightarrow NPV = -P_0 + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + S \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The transition from the summation in Step 4 to the compact factor form in Step 5 is the **most-skipped** algebraic step by students in the exam. KTU examiners often award a separate 1-mark for showing this transition explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the IRR Equation

The IRR is defined as the discount rate $i^*$ at which the NPV becomes zero. The derivation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Set NPV equal to zero (the IRR condition):**

$$0 = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+i^*)^{t}} + \frac{S}{(1+i^*)^{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the initial investment on the left-hand side:**

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+i^*)^{t}} + \frac{S}{(1+i^*)^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that this is a polynomial of degree *n* in $(1+i^*)$:**

For a 5-year project with uniform cash flows, this becomes:

$$P_0 = \frac{A}{(1+i^*)} + \frac{A}{(1+i^*)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i^*)^3} + \frac{A}{(1+i^*)^4} + \frac{A}{(1+i^*)^5}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude that $i^*$ cannot be solved in closed form for $n \geq 2$:**

For $n \geq 2$, IRR is solved numerically by:
- **Trial-and-Error (Bisection method)** — the KTU-preferred method.
- **Newton-Raphson method** — more efficient but rarely asked.
- **Spreadsheet software (Excel `=IRR()`)** — used in KTU design projects.

**Step 5 — The Bisection method procedure used by KTU:**

Pick two trial rates $i_L$ (low) and $i_H$ (high) such that $NPV(i_L) > 0$ and $NPV(i_H) < 0$. The IRR is then approximated by:

$$IRR \approx i_L + (i_H - i_L) \cdot \frac{NPV(i_L)}{NPV(i_L) - NPV(i_H)}$$

This is the **Linear Interpolation Formula** for IRR, which is the most-tested KTU variant.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Solved in KTU Standard Format):**
> A factory wants to install a **100 kW solar PV system**. The capital cost is **₹50,00,000**. The system generates annual electricity savings of **₹8,00,000** with annual O&M cost of **₹1,00,000**. Project life is **25 years**, and the discount rate is **10%**. The salvage value is **₹5,00,000**. Compute (a) NPV, (b) Simple Payback, (c) Discounted Payback, (d) IRR (approx.), and (e) BCR.

**Step 1 — Compute Net Annual Cash Flow:**

$$A = 8,00,000 - 1,00,000 = ₹7,00,000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 — Look up factors at i = 10%, n = 25:**

Using standard tables:
- $(P/A, 10\%, 25) = 9.0770$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 25) = 0.0923$

**Step 3 — Compute NPV:**

$$NPV = -50,00,000 + 7,00,000 \cdot (9.0770) + 5,00,000 \cdot (0.0923)$$

$$NPV = -50,00,000 + 63,53,900 + 46,150 = ₹14,00,050$$

Since $NPV > 0$, the project is **ACCEPTABLE**.

**Step 4 — Compute Simple Payback Period:**

$$SPP = \frac{P_0}{A} = \frac{50,00,000}{7,00,000} \approx 7.14 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Discounted Payback Period (DPP):**

| Year | NCF (₹) | DF @ 10% | Discounted NCF (₹) | Cumulative Disc. NCF (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | −50,00,000 | 1.0000 | −50,00,000 | −50,00,000 |
| 1 | +7,00,000 | 0.9091 | +6,36,370 | −43,63,630 |
| 2 | +7,00,000 | 0.8264 | +5,78,480 | −37,85,150 |
| 3 | +7,00,000 | 0.7513 | +5,25,910 | −32,59,240 |
| 4 | +7,00,000 | 0.6830 | +4,78,100 | −27,81,140 |
| 5 | +7,00,000 | 0.6209 | +4,34,630 | −23,46,510 |
| 6 | +7,00,000 | 0.5645 | +3,95,150 | −19,51,360 |
| 7 | +7,00,000 | 0.5132 | +3,59,240 | −15,92,120 |
| 8 | +7,00,000 | 0.4665 | +3,26,550 | −12,65,570 |
| 9 | +7,00,000 | 0.4241 | +2,96,870 | −9,68,700 |
| 10 | +7,00,000 | 0.3855 | +2,69,850 | −6,98,850 |
| 11 | +7,00,000 | 0.3505 | +2,45,350 | −4,53,500 |
| 12 | +7,00,000 | 0.3186 | +2,23,020 | −2,30,480 |
| 13 | +7,00,000 | 0.2897 | +2,02,790 | −27,690 |
| 14 | +7,00,000 | 0.2633 | +1,84,310 | +1,56,620 |

DPP occurs between year 13 and 14. By linear interpolation:

$$DPP = 13 + \frac{27,690}{1,84,310} \approx 13.15 \text{ years}$$

**Step 6 — Compute IRR by Trial & Interpolation:**

Try $i = 13\%$: $NPV = -50,00,000 + 7,00,000 \cdot (P/A, 13\%, 25) + 5,00,000 \cdot (P/F, 13\%, 25)$

From tables: $(P/A, 13\%, 25) = 7.3300$ and $(P/F, 13\%, 25) = 0.0471$

$$NPV_{13\%} = -50,00,000 + 51,31,000 + 23,550 = +1,54,550$$

Try $i = 14\%$: $(P/A, 14\%, 25) = 6.8729$, $(P/F, 14\%, 25) = 0.0378$

$$NPV_{14\%} = -50,00,000 + 48,11,030 + 18,900 = -1,70,070$$

Linear interpolation:

$$IRR = 13 + (14-13) \cdot \frac{1,54,550}{1,54,550 - (-1,70,070)} = 13 + 0.476 = 13.48\%$$

Since $IRR = 13.48\% > MARR = 10\%$, the project is **ACCEPTABLE**.

**Step 7 — Compute BCR:**

$$BCR = \frac{7,00,000 \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 25) + 5,00,000 \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 25)}{50,00,000}$$

$$BCR = \frac{63,53,900 + 46,150}{50,00,000} = \frac{64,00,050}{50,00,000} = 1.28$$

Since $BCR = 1.28 > 1$, the project is **ACCEPTABLE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Example:** All five indicators (NPV, SPP, DPP, IRR, BCR) unanimously recommend the project. In KTU board exams, such cross-verification is the gold standard for full marks.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Cash Flow Model Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the various components of a Cash Flow Model interact from input to decision. It maps the *flow of data and logic* that an energy auditor follows while evaluating an EE project.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Identification] --> B0[Define Project Boundary]
    B0 --> C0[Estimate Initial Investment P0]
    B0 --> D0[Estimate Annual Energy Savings]
    B0 --> E0[Estimate Annual O and M Cost]
    B0 --> F0[Estimate Project Life n]
    B0 --> G0[Estimate Salvage Value S]
    B0 --> H0[Select Discount Rate i from MARR]

    C0 --> I0[Build Year by Year Cash Flow Table]
    D0 --> I0
    E0 --> I0
    F0 --> I0
    G0 --> I0

    I0 --> J0[Apply Discount Factor per Year]
    H0 --> J0

    J0 --> K0[Compute Discounted Cash Flow Series]
    K0 --> L0[Compute NPV Metric]
    K0 --> M0[Compute IRR Metric]
    K0 --> N0[Compute DPP Metric]
    K0 --> O0[Compute BCR Metric]

    L0 --> P0{Decision Gate}
    M0 --> P0
    N0 --> P0
    O0 --> P0

    P0 -->|NPV positive AND IRR above MARR| Q0[RECOMMEND PROJECT IMPLEMENTATION]
    P0 -->|NPV negative OR IRR below MARR| R0[REJECT OR RE-DESIGN PROJECT]
    P0 -->|Inconclusive| S0[Perform Sensitivity Analysis]
    S0 --> P0
```

**Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
- **Input Block (Top):** The auditor collects five primitive inputs: $P_0$, annual savings, annual O&M, life $n$, and salvage $S$.
- **Processing Block (Middle):** The cash flow table is built, then discounted using the discount factor derived from the chosen MARR.
- **Output Block (Bottom):** Four economic metrics are computed in parallel and fed into a **Decision Gate**.
- **Decision Gate (Diamond):** Acts as a logical AND/OR filter; only if all decision rules are satisfied is the project recommended.

---

## 4.2 Cash Flow Timeline Schematic (Visual Intuition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Year 0] -->|Large Negative Outflow| Y1[Year 1]
    Y1 -->|Small Positive Inflow| Y2[Year 2]
    Y2 -->|Small Positive Inflow| Y3[Year 3]
    Y3 -->|Small Positive Inflow| Yd[Year d Mid Life]
    Yd -->|Negative if Overhaul| Yd2[Major Overhaul Cost]
    Yd2 -->|Positive Inflow| Yn1[Year n minus 1]
    Yn1 -->|Positive plus Salvage| Yn[Year n Final]

    Y0 -.Discount Factor DF0.-> DF[Discounted Value Today]
    Y1 -.Discount Factor DF1.-> DF
    Y2 -.Discount Factor DF2.-> DF
    Y3 -.Discount Factor DF3.-> DF
    Yd -.Discount Factor DFd.-> DF
    Yd2 -.Discount Factor DFd2.-> DF
    Yn1 -.Discount Factor DFn1.-> DF
    Yn -.Discount Factor DFn.-> DF
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **horizontal axis** is time (left = past/today, right = future).
- **Vertical arrow lengths** represent the magnitude of cash flows (larger negative at Year 0, smaller positives thereafter).
- The **dotted downward arrows** represent the *discounting operation* that pulls every future cash flow back to today's value (the "DF" node).
- The optional **mid-life dip (Yd2)** represents an overhaul cost in long-life equipment like chillers or boilers.

---

## 4.3 Sensitivity Analysis Loop (KTU Advanced Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Base Case Cash Flow] --> B1[Perturb Discount Rate plus minus 2 percent]
    A1 --> C1[Perturb Energy Savings plus minus 10 percent]
    A1 --> D1[Perturb Project Life plus minus 2 years]
    A1 --> E1[Perturb Capital Cost plus minus 10 percent]

    B1 --> F1[Recompute NPV and IRR]
    C1 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1

    F1 --> G1{NPV still positive at worst case}
    G1 -->|Yes| H1[Project is ROBUST Accept]
    G1 -->|No| I1[Project is FRAGILE Reject or Hedge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Module 4 of the syllabus explicitly lists **"sensitivity analysis"** as a learning outcome. The diagram above is the standard KTU answer format for a 7-mark sub-question on "How would you test the robustness of an energy project cash flow?"
