# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"What is a Cash Flow Model in the context of Energy Management? List its three essential components."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Cash Flow Model in energy management is a structured, year-by-year financial representation of all monetary receipts and expenditures associated with an energy conservation project over its operational life.

The three essential components are:

1. **Initial Investment (Year 0 Outflow):** The upfront capital cost of the energy-efficient equipment, including installation and commissioning.
2. **Operating Cash Flows (Years 1 to n):** The annual net savings, computed as *energy cost savings minus annual O&M costs* (and minus tax, where applicable).
3. **Terminal Cash Flow (Year n):** The final-year operating savings plus any salvage value and recovery of working capital.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *omit the discount rate* from the components list. The discount rate *i* is the fourth essential element because without it, the model degenerates into a simple accounting ledger and loses its time-value-of-money capability. Deduct **1 mark** if *i* is not mentioned.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between Simple Payback Period (SPP) and Discounted Payback Period (DPP). When is DPP preferred over SPP?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | Discounted Payback Period (DPP) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Discounting | Uses **undiscounted** (nominal) annual cash flows | Uses **discounted** (present-value) cash flows |
| Computation | $SPP = P_0 / A$ directly | $DPP = \text{Year before recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered discounted NCF}}{\text{Discounted NCF in that year}}$ |
| Time Value of Money | **Ignored** — treats ₹1 today = ₹1 tomorrow | **Respected** — applies the discount rate |
| Accuracy for Long-Life Projects | Understates the true recovery time | More accurate and conservative |

**DPP is preferred over SPP** when:
- The project has a **long life (n > 10 years)** where the time value of money is significant.
- The annual savings are **non-uniform** (escalating due to energy price inflation).
- The auditor needs a **more conservative and realistic** estimate of the capital recovery time for managerial decision-making.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B (Choice A) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

A textile mill is evaluating two mutually exclusive energy projects:

| Parameter | Project X (VFD on motors) | Project Y (Heat Recovery Unit) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Initial Investment (₹) | 15,00,000 | 25,00,000 |
| Annual Energy Savings (₹) | 4,00,000 | 6,50,000 |
| Annual O&M (₹) | 50,000 | 1,00,000 |
| Project Life (years) | 10 | 10 |
| Salvage Value (₹) | 1,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| MARR (Discount Rate) | 12% | 12% |

**(a)** Construct the Cash Flow Model for both projects. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Compute NPV, IRR (by interpolation), and Discounted Payback Period for both projects, and recommend the optimal choice using incremental analysis. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Cash Flow Model Construction [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Net Annual Cash Flow (A) for each project:**

$$A_X = 4,00,000 - 50,000 = ₹3,50,000$$

$$A_Y = 6,50,000 - 1,00,000 = ₹5,50,000$$

**Step 2 — Build the Cash Flow Table (showing Year 0, Years 1–9, and Year 10):**

For **Project X:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | −15,00,000 | Initial Investment |
| 1 to 9 | +3,50,000 | Net Annual Savings |
| 10 | +3,50,000 + 1,00,000 = +4,50,000 | Final Year + Salvage |

For **Project Y:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | −25,00,000 | Initial Investment |
| 1 to 9 | +5,50,000 | Net Annual Savings |
| 10 | +5,50,000 + 2,00,000 = +7,50,000 | Final Year + Salvage |

**Step 3 — State the discount factors used:**

At $i = 12\%$ and $n = 10$:
- $(P/A, 12\%, 10) = 5.6502$
- $(P/F, 12\%, 10) = 0.3220$

---

#### Part (b) — NPV, IRR, DPP & Recommendation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute NPV:**

$$NPV_X = -15,00,000 + 3,50,000 \cdot (5.6502) + 1,00,000 \cdot (0.3220)$$

$$NPV_X = -15,00,000 + 19,77,570 + 32,200 = ₹5,09,770$$

$$NPV_Y = -25,00,000 + 5,50,000 \cdot (5.6502) + 2,00,000 \cdot (0.3220)$$

$$NPV_Y = -25,00,000 + 31,07,610 + 64,400 = ₹6,72,010$$

**Step 2 — Compute IRR by Linear Interpolation:**

**For Project X:** Try $i_L = 18\%$ and $i_H = 20\%$.

At 18%, $(P/A, 18\%, 10) = 4.4941$: $NPV = -15,00,000 + 3,50,000 \cdot 4.4941 + 1,00,000 \cdot 0.1911 = +71,793$ (positive)

At 20%, $(P/A, 20\%, 10) = 4.1925$: $NPV = -15,00,000 + 3,50,000 \cdot 4.1925 + 1,00,000 \cdot 0.1615 = -11,475$ (negative)

$$IRR_X = 18 + (20-18) \cdot \frac{71,793}{71,793 + 11,475} = 18 + 1.73 = 19.73\%$$

**For Project Y:** Try $i_L = 18\%$ and $i_H = 20\%$.

At 18%: $NPV = -25,00,000 + 5,50,000 \cdot 4.4941 + 2,00,000 \cdot 0.1911 = +11,517$ (positive)

At 20%: $NPV = -25,00,000 + 5,50,000 \cdot 4.1925 + 2,00,000 \cdot 0.1615 = -87,975$ (negative)

$$IRR_Y = 18 + 2 \cdot \frac{11,517}{11,517 + 87,975} = 18 + 0.23 = 18.23\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute Discounted Payback Period (Year-by-Year Method):**

For **Project X** (Discounted NCF in year $t$ = $3,50,000 \cdot (1.12)^{-t}$):

| Year | Discounted NCF (₹) | Cumulative (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | −15,00,000 | −15,00,000 |
| 1 | +3,12,500 | −11,87,500 |
| 2 | +2,78,950 | −9,08,550 |
| 3 | +2,49,070 | −6,59,480 |
| 4 | +2,22,330 | −4,37,150 |
| 5 | +1,98,510 | −2,38,640 |
| 6 | +1,77,240 | −61,400 |
| 7 | +1,58,250 | +96,850 |

DPP occurs between year 6 and 7:

$$DPP_X = 6 + \frac{61,400}{1,58,250} = 6.39 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4 — Incremental Analysis and Recommendation:**

Since both projects are acceptable individually (NPV > 0, IRR > 12%), we perform **incremental analysis** of the extra investment in Y over X:

- $\Delta P_0 = 25,00,000 - 15,00,000 = ₹10,00,000$ (extra investment)
- $\Delta A = 5,50,000 - 3,50,000 = ₹2,00,000$ (extra annual savings)
- $\Delta NPV = 6,72,010 - 5,09,770 = ₹1,62,240 > 0$ (extra NPV is positive)
- $\Delta IRR \approx 16.5\% > MARR = 12\%$

**Recommendation:** **Choose Project Y** (the heat recovery unit) because the extra ₹10 lakh investment yields a positive incremental NPV of ₹1,62,240 and an incremental IRR exceeding the MARR. Project Y is the **economically superior choice** in the long run despite its higher initial outlay.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students simply pick the project with the higher NPV and stop there. KTU board examiners specifically look for the phrase **"incremental analysis"** and the calculation of $\Delta$IRR or $\Delta$NPV. Missing this step typically results in a **2-mark deduction**.

---

### Question B (Choice B) — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

A hospital is considering the installation of a **co-generation (CHP) system** with the following data:

- Initial Investment: **₹80,00,000**
- Annual Electricity Savings: **₹18,00,000**
- Annual Fuel Cost Increase: **₹8,00,000**
- Annual O&M: **₹2,00,000**
- Project Life: **15 years**
- Salvage Value: **₹5,00,000**
- MARR: **10%**
- Corporate Tax Rate: **30%** (apply straight-line depreciation for simplicity, life = 15 yrs)

**(a)** Build the after-tax Cash Flow Model. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Compute the NPV, Benefit-Cost Ratio, and discuss whether the project should be accepted. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — After-Tax Cash Flow Model [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the Net Annual Savings BEFORE tax and depreciation:**

$$\text{Gross Savings} = 18,00,000 - 8,00,000 - 2,00,000 = ₹8,00,000$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Depreciation (Straight-Line):**

$$D = \frac{P_0 - S}{n} = \frac{80,00,000 - 5,00,000}{15} = ₹5,00,000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Taxable Income:**

$$\text{Taxable Income} = \text{Gross Savings} - D = 8,00,000 - 5,00,000 = ₹3,00,000$$

**Step 4 — Compute Tax:**

$$\text{Tax} = 3,00,000 \cdot 0.30 = ₹90,000$$

**Step 5 — Compute After-Tax Net Cash Flow:**

$$A_{AT} = \text{Gross Savings} - \text{Tax} = 8,00,000 - 90,000 = ₹7,10,000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6 — Construct the Cash Flow Table:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | −80,00,000 | Initial Investment |
| 1 to 14 | +7,10,000 | After-Tax Annual Savings |
| 15 | +7,10,000 + 5,00,000 = +12,10,000 | Final Year + Salvage |

---

#### Part (b) — NPV, BCR, and Recommendation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Look up factors at i = 10%, n = 15:**

- $(P/A, 10\%, 15) = 7.6061$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 15) = 0.2394$

**Step 2 — Compute NPV:**

$$NPV = -80,00,000 + 7,10,000 \cdot (7.6061) + 5,00,000 \cdot (0.2394)$$

$$NPV = -80,00,000 + 54,00,331 + 1,19,700 = -₹24,79,969$$

**Step 3 — Compute BCR:**

$$\text{PV of Benefits} = 7,10,000 \cdot 7.6061 + 5,00,000 \cdot 0.2394 = 55,20,031$$

$$\text{PV of Costs} = 80,00,000 \text{ (assuming no other discounted costs beyond Year 0)}$$

$$BCR = \frac{55,20,031}{80,00,000} = 0.69$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and Recommendation:**

- $NPV = -₹24,79,969 < 0$ → Project is **NOT acceptable** on an NPV basis.
- $BCR = 0.69 < 1$ → The present value of benefits is only 69% of the present value of costs; the project destroys ₹31 of value for every ₹100 invested.
- $IRR$ (computed by interpolation between 4% and 5%) would be **less than 5%**, far below the MARR of 10%.

**Recommendation:** The hospital should **REJECT** the CHP project under the given assumptions. Possible redesign options to make it viable: negotiate a **lower capital cost**, secure a **government subsidy (e.g., from SECI or MNRE schemes)**, increase electricity tariff savings by **expanding the load** served by the CHP, or use a **higher-quality, cheaper fuel** like natural gas instead of diesel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently make the mistake of using the **pre-tax savings (₹8 lakh)** as the annual cash flow in the NPV formula, which would yield a **falsely positive NPV** of about +₹5 lakh. KTU board examiners specifically test whether students can correctly subtract **depreciation-adjusted tax** from the gross savings. Using pre-tax cash flow is a **3-mark error** — it reflects a fundamental misunderstanding of after-tax cash flow modeling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Cash Flow Model (Module 4 of PEEET742):**

- **Definition:** A Cash Flow Model is a year-by-year accounting of inflows and outflows for an energy project, discounted to present value.
- **Standard Equation:** $NPV = -P_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{NCF_t}{(1+i)^{t}} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^{n}}$
- **Five Key Inputs to Memorize:** $P_0$, $A$ (net annual savings), $n$, $i$ (MARR), and $S$ (salvage value).
- **Six Key Outputs (Decision Metrics):** NPV, IRR, SPP, DPP, BCR, AEV.
- **Factor Notation is Sacred:** Always write $(P/A, i, n)$, $(P/F, i, n)$ explicitly in the KTU answer sheet — examiners award a 0.5 mark bonus for using the correct factor notation.
- **Decision Rules:**
  - Accept if $NPV > 0$
  - Accept if $IRR > MARR$
  - Accept if $BCR > 1$
  - Accept if $SPP \leq$ Target Payback
  - Accept if $DPP \leq$ Target Payback
- **Incremental Analysis is Mandatory** when comparing two mutually exclusive projects. Simply choosing the higher-NPV project is **incomplete**; you must verify the incremental NPV/IRR.
- **For After-Tax Cash Flows:** First compute gross savings, then subtract depreciation to get taxable income, multiply by tax rate to get tax, and finally subtract tax from gross savings.
- **Sensitivity Analysis:** Always test the NPV by perturbing the discount rate (±2%), energy savings (±10%), and project life (±2 years) to demonstrate the robustness of the recommendation.
- **Common KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting to add the **salvage value** in the terminal year. The salvage value is discounted using $(P/F, i, n)$ and added to the NPV.
- **Real-World Link:** The Cash Flow Model is the financial engine behind every ESCO-based Energy Performance Contract (EPC) and every PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) cycle of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Value of Money
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Time Value of Money

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the foundational financial principle in engineering economics that states **a unit of currency (e.g., ₹1, $1) received today is worth more than the same unit of currency received at a future date**, because money possesses three inherent productive capacities: **earning potential, liquidity premium, and risk-adjusted purchasing power protection**.

In the context of the **PEEET742 – Energy Management and Auditing** course, TVM is the mathematical cornerstone used to evaluate the long-term economic viability of energy efficiency retrofits, renewable energy installations, and demand-side management programs. The syllabus categorizes this concept under **Module 4: Economic Analysis of Energy Projects**.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Present Value** | $PV$ | The current worth of a future sum of money, discounted at a specified rate |
| **Future Value** | $FV$ | The value of a present asset at a specified future date, grown at a given rate |
| **Discount Rate** | $i$ | The annual percentage rate used to convert future cash flows to present value (often MARR) |
| **Compounding Periods** | $n$ | The total number of interest application periods over the project lifetime |
| **Payment / Cash Flow** | $PMT$ / $CF$ | A uniform or non-uniform monetary transaction occurring at regular intervals |
| **Annuity** | $A$ | A series of equal payments made at fixed intervals over a specified duration |
| **Net Present Value** | $NPV$ | The algebraic sum of all discounted cash flows minus the initial investment |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mango Lend-Out"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you lend your friend **₹1,000 today**. A year later, your friend returns **₹1,000**. Have you broken even? **No!** Why? Because if you had the ₹1,000, you could have:
> 1. Deposited it in a bank and earned interest (earning potential).
> 2. Used it to buy a mango (immediate consumption value).
> 3. Invested in a small business (capital growth).
> 4. Avoided the risk of inflation eroding its purchasing power.
>
> Hence, **₹1,000 today > ₹1,000 tomorrow**. To make the lender truly indifferent, the borrower must pay **₹1,000 + Interest** in the future. This interest is the "rent" the borrower pays for using the lender's money over time.

### Why TVM is Indispensable in Energy Management

In real-world energy projects, the following practical constraints **always** exist:
- **Capital costs** (e.g., ₹10 lakh for a solar plant) occur **at $t = 0$** (today).
- **Operating savings** (e.g., ₹2 lakh/year in reduced electricity bills) occur at **$t = 1, 2, 3, ... n$ years** in the future.
- **Discounting** is the only mathematically rigorous way to compare these **temporally mismatched** cash flows on a common scale (Present Value).
- **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** is the threshold that converts the abstract concept of "earning potential" into a concrete numerical input.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In energy audits, every recommendation (VFD installation, LED retrofit, capacitor banks, waste heat recovery) is evaluated using TVM-based metrics such as **NPV, BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio), and IRR (Internal Rate of Return)**. Without a firm grasp of TVM, no engineer can justify a single energy-saving proposal to management.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Foundational Principles — The Six Pillars of TVM

The entire framework of TVM rests upon six interconnected pillars. Mastery of these is mandatory for KTU board examinations.

### Pillar 1: Simple Interest (Linear Growth)
- Interest is calculated **only on the original principal** for every period.
- Mathematically: $I = P \cdot r \cdot t$
- The total accumulated value: $A = P + I = P(1 + rt)$
- **Application in Energy:** Rarely used in real engineering economics; forms the conceptual base for compound interest.

### Pillar 2: Compound Interest (Exponential Growth)
- Interest earned in each period is **added back to the principal**, so subsequent periods earn interest on previously earned interest (interest on interest).
- Compounding frequency can be **annual, semi-annual, quarterly, monthly, or continuous**.
- **Application in Energy:** Standard assumption for project cash flow analysis; closely mirrors real bank deposits and loan amortization schedules.

### Pillar 3: Discounting (Reverse of Compounding)
- The process of converting a future monetary value to its present equivalent using the discount rate $i$.
- This is the **inverse mathematical operation** of compounding.
- **Application in Energy:** Central to NPV analysis of energy projects.

### Pillar 4: Annuities (Uniform Series)
- A sequence of equal payments made at equal time intervals.
- **Ordinary Annuity:** Payments occur at the **end** of each period (most common in engineering economics).
- **Annuity Due:** Payments occur at the **beginning** of each period.

### Pillar 5: Cash Flow Diagrams (Visualization)
- Time on the horizontal axis; cash flows (positive = receipts, negative = disbursements) as vertical arrows.
- **Application in Energy:** Used in KTU theory questions to identify PV, FV, and annuity positions.

### Pillar 6: Net Present Value (Project Acceptance Criterion)
- The present worth of all future net cash flows minus the initial investment.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if $NPV > 0$ at the MARR; reject if $NPV < 0$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these 8 formulas account for ~80% of all numerical questions in PEEET742 TVM sections.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Formula | Variables Explained | Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Interest | $A = P(1 + rt)$ | $P$: principal, $r$: rate per period, $t$: time | Educational baseline; rare in industry |
| 2 | Future Value (Single Sum) | $F = P(1 + i)^{n}$ | $i$: interest rate, $n$: number of periods | Lump-sum future cost of equipment |
| 3 | Present Value (Single Sum) | $P = F(1 + i)^{-n}$ | $P$: present worth | Discounting future maintenance cost |
| 4 | Future Value of Annuity (FVA) | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | $A$: uniform payment | Sinking fund for future boiler replacement |
| 5 | Present Value of Annuity (PVA) | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | $A$: uniform payment | Capital recovery of energy savings |
| 6 | FVA of Annuity Due | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)$ | Payment at $t=0, 1, ..., n-1$ | Lease payments at start of year |
| 7 | PVA of Annuity Due | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \cdot (1+i)$ | Payment at $t=0, 1, ..., n-1$ | Insurance premiums paid upfront |
| 8 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Converts $P$ to equivalent $A$ | Annualized cost of solar PV system |
| 9 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Saves lump sum $F$ via equal deposits | Reserved for future major overhaul |
| 10 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | $CF_{t}$: cash flow at year $t$ | Core metric for project acceptance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Used:** In the table above, $\vert$ for absolute value has been replaced with $\mid$ where required to preserve LaTeX/markdown table compatibility.

## 2.3 The Two Master Reciprocal Relationships (Frequently Tested!)

**Relationship A:** Single-Payment Compound Amount Factor (SPCAF) and Single-Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF) are reciprocals.

$$\text{SPCAF} \cdot \text{SPPWF} = 1$$

$$(1+i)^{n} \cdot (1+i)^{-n} = 1$$

**Relationship B:** Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (USCAF) and Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) are reciprocals.

$$\text{USCAF} \cdot \text{SFF} = 1$$

$$\frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i} \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1} = 1$$

**Relationship C:** Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF) and Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) are reciprocals.

$$\text{USPWF} \cdot \text{CRF} = 1$$

$$\frac{1-(1+i)^{-n}}{i} \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} = 1$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When you forget a formula during an exam, **derive it from a known reciprocal relationship** — this is a tested life-saver technique.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Energy Engineering Fields

| Sector | Application of TVM |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Energy Audits** | Comparing high-efficiency motors vs. standard motors using NPV |
| **Renewable Energy (Solar PV, Wind)** | Calculating LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) using CRF |
| **Building HVAC Retrofits** | Justifying chiller replacement with 15-year lifecycle analysis |
| **Power Plants** | Setting aside depreciation funds via Sinking Fund Factor |
| **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** | Evaluating TOU (Time-of-Use) tariff switching economics |
| **Combined Heat & Power (CHP)** | Comparing annualized cost of reciprocating engine vs. gas turbine |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Future Value of a Single Sum ($F = P(1+i)^n$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Origin:** We start with the simple interest formula and then iteratively add "interest on interest" — this yields the geometric series that defines compounding.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the initial principal be $P$, interest rate per period be $i$, and total periods be $n$.

**Step 1: Value at end of period 1 ($t = 1$)**

$$F_1 = P + Pi = P(1+i)$$

Explanation: The principal earns interest for one period. Total amount is the original sum plus the simple interest.

**Step 2: Value at end of period 2 ($t = 2$)**

$$F_2 = F_1 + F_1 \cdot i = F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$

Explanation: The amount at the end of period 1 ($F_1$) now becomes the new principal for period 2, and it earns interest again.

**Step 3: Inductive Hypothesis at $t = k$**

$$F_k = P(1+i)^{k}$$

Explanation: Assume the formula holds for the $k$-th period. This is the inductive assumption.

**Step 4: Recursive Step at $t = k+1$**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{k+1} &= F_k + F_k \cdot i \\
&= F_k(1+i) \\
&= P(1+i)^{k}(1+i) \\
&= P(1+i)^{k+1}
\end{aligned}$$

Explanation: The amount from period $k$ earns interest for one more period. The mathematical identity $(1+i)^k \cdot (1+i) = (1+i)^{k+1}$ is the key exponential growth property.

**Step 5: Final Form at $t = n$**

$$\boxed{F = P(1+i)^{n}}$$

Explanation: By mathematical induction, the formula holds for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$. This is the **Single-Payment Compound Amount Factor (SPCAF)** or $(F/P, i, n)$ notation in KTU textbooks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Present Value of a Single Sum ($P = F(1+i)^{-n}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Origin:** This is the algebraic inverse of the future value formula. We simply solve for $P$ in terms of $F$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start with the FV formula**

$$F = P(1+i)^{n}$$

Explanation: This is the previously derived result.

**Step 2: Isolate $P$ algebraically**

$$P = \frac{F}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

Explanation: Divide both sides by $(1+i)^n$ using the division property of equality.

**Step 3: Apply the negative exponent rule**

$$P = F(1+i)^{-n}$$

Explanation: By the law of exponents, $1/(1+i)^n = (1+i)^{-n}$. This is the **Single-Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)** or $(P/F, i, n)$ notation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity ($F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Origin:** Sum the future values of $n$ equal payments, each deposited at the end of successive periods, to a common focal date at $t = n$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $A$ be the uniform end-of-period payment, and let us find the total accumulated value at $t = n$.

**Step 1: Identify individual payment positions**

Payment 1 is at $t = 1$ → it compounds for $(n-1)$ periods.
Payment 2 is at $t = 2$ → it compounds for $(n-2)$ periods.
Payment $k$ is at $t = k$ → it compounds for $(n-k)$ periods.
Payment $n$ is at $t = n$ → it compounds for $0$ periods (no growth).

**Step 2: Write the future value of each payment at $t = n$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Value of Payment 1 at } t=n: \quad & A(1+i)^{n-1} \\
\text{Value of Payment 2 at } t=n: \quad & A(1+i)^{n-2} \\
\vdots \quad & \vdots \\
\text{Value of Payment } k \text{ at } t=n: \quad & A(1+i)^{n-k} \\
\vdots \quad & \vdots \\
\text{Value of Payment } n \text{ at } t=n: \quad & A(1+i)^{0} = A
\end{aligned}$$

Explanation: Each payment is moved forward in time using the single-sum FV formula.

**Step 3: Sum the geometric series**

$$F = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A(1+i)^{n-k} = A\left[(1+i)^{n-1} + (1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + (1+i)^{0}\right]$$

Explanation: Factor out the constant $A$ and observe that we have a geometric series.

**Step 4: Apply the geometric series sum formula**

$$\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} r^{j} = \frac{r^{n}-1}{r-1}$$

With $r = (1+i)$ and $j = 0$ to $n-1$:

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$$

**Step 5: Final simplification**

$$\boxed{F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}}$$

Explanation: The denominator $(1+i) - 1 = i$. This is the **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (USCAF)** or $(F/A, i, n)$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity ($P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Origin:** Sum the present values of $n$ equal payments to a common focal date at $t = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the present value of each payment at $t = 0$**

Payment 1 at $t=1$ → discounted by 1 period.
Payment $k$ at $t=k$ → discounted by $k$ periods.
Payment $n$ at $t=n$ → discounted by $n$ periods.

**Step 2: Express individual present values**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV \text{ of Payment 1: } \quad & A(1+i)^{-1} \\
PV \text{ of Payment 2: } \quad & A(1+i)^{-2} \\
\vdots \quad & \vdots \\
PV \text{ of Payment } n: \quad & A(1+i)^{-n}
\end{aligned}$$

Explanation: Each payment is moved backward in time using the single-sum PV formula.

**Step 3: Sum the geometric series**

$$P = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A(1+i)^{-k} = A\left[(1+i)^{-1} + (1+i)^{-2} + \cdots + (1+i)^{-n}\right]$$

**Step 4: Apply the geometric series sum formula with common ratio $(1+i)^{-1}$**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} r^{k} = \frac{r(1-r^{n})}{1-r}$$

With $r = (1+i)^{-1}$:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{-1}\left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}$$

**Step 5: Simplify the denominator**

$$1 - (1+i)^{-1} = 1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}$$

**Step 6: Substitute and cancel**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{-1}\left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{i/(1+i)} \\
&= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{-1} \cdot (1+i)}{i} \cdot \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right] \\
&= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{0}}{i} \cdot \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Final result**

$$\boxed{P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}}$$

Explanation: This is the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)** or $(P/A, i, n)$. It is the most frequently used factor in energy project NPV calculations.

## 3.5 Analytic Worked Example (Without Numerical Codes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Standard Problem:** An industrial plant invests **₹8,00,000** in energy-efficient LED lighting that generates uniform annual savings of **₹1,50,000** for **6 years**. If the **MARR is 9% per annum**, evaluate the project using NPV.

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step 1: Identify the variables**

$$P_{\text{initial}} = 8,00,000, \quad A = 1,50,000, \quad n = 6, \quad i = 0.09$$

Explanation: Initial outflow is the investment; uniform annual cash inflow is the energy saving.

**Step 2: Look up or compute the USPWF**

$$(P/A, 9\%, 6) = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-6}}{0.09}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $(1.09)^6$**

$$(1.09)^6 = 1.6771$$

**Step 4: Calculate $(1.09)^{-6}$**

$$(1.09)^{-6} = \frac{1}{1.6771} = 0.5963$$

**Step 5: Compute the USPWF**

$$\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 9\%, 6) &= \frac{1 - 0.5963}{0.09} \\
&= \frac{0.4037}{0.09} \\
&= 4.4859
\end{aligned}$$

Explanation: This factor means that every ₹1 received annually for 6 years is equivalent to ₹4.4859 today.

**Step 6: Calculate the Present Worth of the savings (PV of annuity)**

$$PW_{\text{savings}} = 1,50,000 \times 4.4859 = 6,72,885$$

Explanation: The total discounted value of the energy savings at $t=0$ is approximately ₹6.73 lakh.

**Step 7: Calculate the NPV**

$$NPV = PW_{\text{savings}} - P_{\text{initial}} = 6,72,885 - 8,00,000 = -1,27,115$$

**Step 8: Decision**

$$NPV = -1,27,115 < 0$$

**Conclusion:** At a 9% MARR, the project is **NOT economically viable** because the present value of savings is less than the initial investment. The company should either **negotiate a lower capital cost**, **secure higher annual savings**, or **reject the project**.

> [!TIP]
> **Sensitivity Insight:** Try recalculating with $i = 7\%$. You will find that the NPV becomes positive. This illustrates the critical role of the discount rate in energy project evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define the term 'Time Value of Money'. Why is it a necessary concept in the economic analysis of energy projects?"**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Time Value of Money is the financial principle that a rupee (or any unit of currency) available today is worth more than the same rupee available at a future date, due to its earning capacity, liquidity, and inflation-protected purchasing power.

> **Necessity in Energy Projects:**
> 1. Energy projects involve large **initial capital expenditures** (e.g., solar PV, VFD, retrofitting) at $t = 0$ and **gradual energy savings** distributed over 10–20 years.
> 2. These temporally mismatched cash flows must be brought to a **common time scale** (Present Value) for fair comparison.
> 3. It allows engineers to compute **NPV, IRR, BCR**, and **Payback Period** — the four standard metrics mandated by KTU's energy management curriculum.
> 4. It enables comparison of **mutually exclusive alternatives** (e.g., gas turbine vs. diesel generator) on a uniform economic basis.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 2-4 necessity points]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Differentiate between Simple Interest and Compound Interest. State the formulas for the accumulated amount in each case."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Simple Interest | Compound Interest |
|---|---|---|
| **Interest Base** | Calculated on **original principal only** | Calculated on **principal + accumulated interest** |
| **Growth Type** | **Linear** (straight line) | **Exponential** (curved growth) |
| **Formula (Amount)** | $A = P(1 + rt)$ | $A = P(1+i)^{n}$ |
| **Interest Earned (I)** | $I = Prt$ | $I = P[(1+i)^{n} - 1]$ |
| **Use in Energy Audits** | Theoretical baseline; rarely used | Standard assumption in NPV analysis |
| **Effect of Time** | Interest grows proportionally with $t$ | Interest grows as a **power function** of $n$ |

> **Example:** ₹1,00,000 at 10% for 3 years gives SI = ₹30,000 (Amount = ₹1,30,000) and CI = ₹33,100 (Amount = ₹1,33,100). The ₹3,100 difference is the "interest on interest" earned via compounding.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 mark for tabular differentiation + 1 mark for both formulas + 1 mark for example]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Format:** Part B contains choice-based questions worth 14 marks each. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry 7 marks each. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two options provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the formula for the **Future Value of a Single Sum** under compound interest. Explain the role of compounding frequency with a numerical illustration. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Start with the definition: Future Value of Single Sum, $F$, at interest rate $i$ for $n$ periods, with initial principal $P$.

2. Period 1: $F_1 = P + Pi = P(1+i)$. *(1 mark for first-period derivation)*

3. Period 2: $F_2 = F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$. *(1 mark for second-period expansion)*

4. By mathematical induction, at $t = n$: $\boxed{F = P(1+i)^n}$. *(2 marks for general proof using induction)*

5. **Compounding Frequency Illustration:** For ₹1,00,000 at 10% for 1 year:
   - Annual compounding: $F = 1,00,000(1.10)^1 = ₹1,10,000$
   - Semi-annual: $F = 1,00,000(1.05)^2 = ₹1,10,250$
   - Quarterly: $F = 1,00,000(1.025)^4 = ₹1,10,381$
   - Monthly: $F = 1,00,000(1+0.10/12)^{12} = ₹1,10,471$
   - Continuous: $F = 1,00,000 \cdot e^{0.10} = ₹1,10,517$

6. **Conclusion:** As compounding frequency increases, future value increases (for the same nominal rate), approaching the continuous compounding limit. *(1 mark for inference)*

**(b)** A company invests **₹6,00,000** in a power factor correction project. The project yields **uniform annual savings of ₹1,80,000** for **5 years**. The MARR is **12% per annum**. Evaluate the project using **Net Present Value (NPV)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Variable Identification**

$$P = 6,00,000, \quad A = 1,80,000, \quad n = 5, \quad i = 12\% = 0.12$$

**Step 2: Compute the Present Worth Factor (P/A, 12%, 5)**

$$(1.12)^{5} = 1.7623$$
$$(1.12)^{-5} = 0.5674$$
$$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048$$

**Step 3: Compute Present Worth of Savings**

$$PW_{\text{savings}} = 1,80,000 \times 3.6048 = 6,48,864$$

**Step 4: Compute NPV**

$$NPV = PW_{\text{savings}} - P_{\text{initial}} = 6,48,864 - 6,00,000 = +48,864$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $NPV = +₹48,864 > 0$, the project is **economically viable and recommended for acceptance** at a 12% MARR.

**Step 6: Sensitivity Check (Bonus Insight)**
Even with a 1% increase in MARR (to 13%), the NPV remains positive (₹27,000 approximately), indicating the project's robustness.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Annuities**. Derive the formula for the **Present Worth of an Ordinary Annuity** with $n$ equal end-of-period payments. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Annuities** *(2 marks)*
An annuity is a series of equal monetary payments made at equal intervals of time. The two main types are:
- **Ordinary Annuity:** Payments occur at the **end** of each period (e.g., loan EMIs, energy savings realized at year-end).
- **Annuity Due:** Payments occur at the **beginning** of each period (e.g., lease rentals, annual insurance premiums paid in advance).

**2. Derivation Setup** *(1 mark)*
Let $A$ be the uniform end-of-period payment, $i$ the interest rate, and $n$ the number of periods. We aim to find $P$, the present worth at $t = 0$.

**3. Individual Present Values** *(2 marks)*

$$P = A(1+i)^{-1} + A(1+i)^{-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^{-n}$$

**4. Sum the Geometric Series** *(1 mark)*

Using the sum formula with common ratio $r = (1+i)^{-1}$:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{-1}\left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}$$

**5. Simplification and Final Form** *(1 mark)*

After algebraic simplification (dividing numerator and denominator by $(1+i)$):

$$\boxed{P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}}$$

This is the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)**, denoted as $(P/A, i, n)$.

**(b)** A manufacturing plant is evaluating the installation of a **500 kW rooftop solar PV system**. The total installed cost is **₹20,00,000**, and the expected annual energy bill savings are **₹4,50,000**. The annual Operation & Maintenance (O&M) cost is **₹50,000**. The system has a useful life of **15 years** with negligible salvage value. Using a discount rate of **10% per annum**, compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of the project and state whether the investment is justified. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Net Annual Cash Flow**

$$A_{\text{net}} = \text{Savings} - \text{O\&M} = 4,50,000 - 50,000 = ₹4,00,000$$

**Step 2: Identify Parameters**

$$P = 20,00,000, \quad A_{\text{net}} = 4,00,000, \quad n = 15, \quad i = 10\% = 0.10$$

**Step 3: Compute $(P/A, 10\%, 15)$**

$$(1.10)^{15} = 4.1772$$
$$(1.10)^{-15} = 0.2394$$
$$(P/A, 10\%, 15) = \frac{1 - 0.2394}{0.10} = \frac{0.7606}{0.10} = 7.6061$$

**Step 4: Compute Present Worth of Net Cash Inflows**

$$PW = 4,00,000 \times 7.6061 = 30,42,440$$

**Step 5: Compute NPV**

$$NPV = PW - P_{\text{initial}} = 30,42,440 - 20,00,000 = +10,42,440$$

**Step 6: Decision**

Since $NPV = +₹10,42,440 > 0$, the rooftop solar PV investment is **economically justified** and should be accepted.

**Step 7: Bonus Insight — Approximate Payback**

$$P/A = 20,00,000 / 4,00,000 = 5 \text{ years (simple payback)}$$

This is a strong project with a quick payback and substantial positive NPV.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU BOARD EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — AVOID THESE MISTAKES:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Annuity vs. Annuity Due:** Forgetting to multiply by $(1+i)$ when the problem specifies "beginning of period" payments. This is the #1 most common error — costs **2 marks instantly**.
>
> 2. **Discount Rate vs. Interest Rate Confusion:** Some students use the inflation rate instead of MARR. Always re-read the problem carefully: if "MARR" or "required rate of return" is given, use that as $i$.
>
> 3. **Algebraic Sign Errors in NPV:** Forgetting to subtract the initial investment (which is a cash outflow at $t=0$). NPV = PV(inflows) − PV(outflows).
>
> 4. **Wrong Compounding Period:** The problem says "monthly" but the student annualizes. Check if $n$ and $i$ are in matching time units.
>
> 5. **Not Showing Intermediate Steps:** KTU examiners award **step marks**. Even if the final answer is wrong, intermediate formulas (e.g., correctly setting up $(1+i)^{-n}$) fetch partial credit.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Reciprocal Relationships:** A common question asks to derive CRF from P/A. Use the reciprocal: $CRF = 1 / (P/A, i, n)$.
>
> 7. **Unit Mismatch:** Interest rate given as **0.10** in some textbooks and **10%** in others. The formula $i$ MUST be a decimal, not a percentage.
>
> 8. **Cash Flow Diagram Absence:** KTU award 1 extra mark for a well-labeled cash flow diagram. Always draw it, even for numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — TIME VALUE OF MONEY**

### 🔑 Core Concept Recap
- **TVM** = Money has time-dependent value due to earning potential, liquidity, and inflation effects.
- The two universal operations: **Compounding** (PV → FV) and **Discounting** (FV → PV).
- The single most important factor in any TVM calculation is the **discount rate $i$**.

### 📚 Must-Memorize Formulas (8 of them)
1. $F = P(1+i)^n$ — Single sum future value
2. $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ — Single sum present value
3. $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ — Future value of ordinary annuity
4. $P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ — Present value of ordinary annuity
5. $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — Capital recovery factor
6. $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — Sinking fund factor
7. $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ — Net present value
8. $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)$ — Future value of annuity due

### 🧠 Reciprocal Pairs to Remember
- $(F/P, i, n) \times (P/F, i, n) = 1$
- $(F/A, i, n) \times (A/F, i, n) = 1$
- $(P/A, i, n) \times (A/P, i, n) = 1$

### 🎯 Energy Engineering Application Hooks
- **NPV > 0** → Accept the energy project.
- **NPV = 0** → Break-even; indifferent decision.
- **NPV < 0** → Reject the energy project.
- **Higher MARR** → Lower NPV (always).
- **Longer project life ($n$)** → Higher NPV (for positive cash flows).
- **Larger discount rate differential** → Greater impact on NPV (compounding amplification).

### ⚠️ Quick Sanity Checks Before Submission
- ✅ Is the cash flow diagram drawn and labeled?
- ✅ Is the discount rate converted from percentage to decimal?
- ✅ Is the annuity ordinary (end-of-period) or due (begin-of-period)?
- ✅ Are units of $n$ and $i$ consistent (e.g., both in years)?
- ✅ Have you shown $(1+i)^n$ computation explicitly?
- ✅ Is the final NPV decision (accept/reject) clearly stated?

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When in doubt, draw a **cash flow diagram** first. It clarifies 90% of confusion about whether to use PV, FV, or annuity factors. Master this skill and TVM becomes a high-scoring topic in PEEET742.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Proposals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evaluation of Proposals** in Energy Management refers to the systematic, analytical process of appraising competing energy conservation projects (ECPs) and capital investment alternatives to determine their technical feasibility, economic viability, and overall worthiness before committing organizational capital. It is the financial and engineering decision-support framework that converts raw audit findings into prioritized, bankable projects.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this module equips the future energy manager with the analytical toolkit to:
- Rank investment options when capital is constrained.
- Justify expenditures to top management and financial controllers.
- Compare projects with differing lifespans, cash-flow patterns, and risk profiles.
- Translate engineering savings (kWh, tonnes of oil equivalent, kL of water) into monetary and temporal metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Verbatim):**
> *"Evaluation of Proposals – Payback period, Return on Investment (ROI), Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), Life Cycle Costing (LCC), Sensitivity and Risk Analysis."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Shopkeeper" Intuition

Imagine a small shopkeeper, **Mr. KTU**, who has saved ₹5,00,000. He has three investment options:

1. **Buy a new refrigerator** — saves ₹8,000/month in spoilage, lasts 10 years.
2. **Install solar panels** — saves ₹6,000/month, lasts 25 years.
3. **Replace old delivery bike** — saves ₹4,000/month, lasts 4 years.

A naïve shopkeeper would just pick the one that "saves the most per month" (the refrigerator). But a wise shopkeeper asks three deeper questions:

- **How fast will I get my money back?** *(Payback Period)*
- **What is my yearly return compared to the cost?** *(ROI)*
- **What is the true value of money over time, considering I could invest it elsewhere?** *(NPV/IRR)*
- **What if my savings estimates are wrong, or electricity prices rise, or the equipment fails early?** *(Sensitivity/Risk Analysis)*

**Evaluation of Proposals is exactly this "wise shopkeeper" thinking, but applied rigorously to industrial energy projects with mathematical precision.** It is the lens through which an Energy Manager defends every rupee of capex demanded from the boardroom.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is THE Heart of Energy Management

> [!NOTE]
> Without **Evaluation of Proposals**, an energy audit is just a *shopping list* of ideas. With evaluation, it becomes a **prioritized investment roadmap** that CFOs approve in minutes.

The lifecycle of an energy project looks like this:

```
Energy Audit → Identification of ECMs → Evaluation of Proposals → Implementation → M&V
```

The "Evaluation" stage is the **gatekeeper** that converts technical opportunity into financial commitment.

## 1.4 The Two Families of Evaluation Methods

| Family | Logic | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Discounted (Simple) Methods** | Ignore the time value of money | Quick screening, small-ticket items |
| **Discounted (Time-Adjusted) Methods** | Account for the fact that ₹100 today > ₹100 in 5 years | Large capital projects, board-level decisions |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP)

### Definition
The **Simple Payback Period** is the time (usually expressed in years or months) required for the cumulative gross cash savings from a project to equal the initial capital investment.

### Core Logic
- **Concept:** "How long until I recover my money?"
- **Why use it:** Extremely intuitive, easy to compute, ideal for communicating with non-finance stakeholders.
- **Limitation:** Ignores cash flows *after* payback and ignores the time value of money.

### Formula Derivation

Let:
- $C_0$ = Initial Capital Investment (₹)
- $S_t$ = Net Annual Savings in year $t$ (₹/year)
- $n$ = Payback Period (years)

If savings are uniform ($S_t = S$ constant):

$$
\text{SPP} = \frac{C_0}{S}
$$

If savings are non-uniform (typical of phased retrofits):

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} S_t \;\geq\; C_0
$$

The smallest integer $n$ that satisfies this inequality is the payback period.

### Decision Rule
- **Accept** if SPP $\leq$ Target Payback (often set by management, e.g., 3 years).
- **Reject** if SPP $>$ Target Payback.

> [!NOTE]
> Many Indian PSUs and energy auditors adopt a **maximum acceptable payback of 3 to 5 years** for energy projects due to the rapidly changing technology landscape.

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — Discounted Payback Period (DPP)

### Definition
A refinement of SPP where future cash flows are *discounted* back to present value before summation. It answers the question: "Considering the time value of money, how long until I recover my investment?"

### Formula

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{S_t}{(1+i)^{t}} \;\geq\; C_0
$$

where $i$ = discount rate (often the Weighted Average Cost of Capital, WACC).

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — Return on Investment (ROI)

### Definition
ROI expresses the **average annual net savings as a percentage of the initial investment**. It is a profitability ratio, not a time-based metric.

### Formula

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{\text{Average Annual Net Savings}}{C_0} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

Alternative formulation (accounting-based):

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Cost of Investment}} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

### Decision Rule
- **Accept** if ROI $\geq$ Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR), often 15–20% for industrial energy projects in India.

---

## 2.4 Method 4 — Net Present Value (NPV)

### Definition
The **gold standard** of capital budgeting. NPV is the algebraic sum of all discounted cash inflows minus all discounted cash outflows over the project life.

If NPV > 0, the project earns **more** than the discount rate (i.e., it creates wealth). If NPV < 0, it destroys wealth.

### Formula

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^{t}}
$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$ (positive = inflow, negative = outflow)
- $i$ = Discount rate (cost of capital or hurdle rate)
- $n$ = Project life in years
- $t=0$ represents the initial investment (a negative value).

### Decision Rule
- **NPV > 0** → ACCEPT (project is financially viable)
- **NPV = 0** → Indifferent (project exactly earns the discount rate)
- **NPV < 0** → REJECT

### Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) Variant
When comparing projects of unequal lives, convert NPV to an annualized stream using the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF):

$$
\text{EAC} \;=\; \text{NPV} \times \text{CRF} \;=\; \text{NPV} \times \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}
$$

---

## 2.5 Method 5 — Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

### Definition
IRR is the discount rate $r$ that makes the NPV of a project exactly equal to **zero**. It is the project's "intrinsic" rate of return — the rate at which the invested money grows internally.

### Formula

$$
0 \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^{t}}
$$

Solve for $r$.

### Decision Rule
- **IRR > Hurdle Rate ($i$)** → ACCEPT
- **IRR = Hurdle Rate** → Indifferent
- **IRR < Hurdle Rate** → REJECT

### Approximation via Trial & Error (Interpolative Method)

$$
\text{IRR} \;\approx\; r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)
$$

Where:
- $r_L$ = lower discount rate (giving positive NPV)
- $r_H$ = higher discount rate (giving negative NPV)
- $\text{NPV}_L$ = NPV at $r_L$
- $\text{NPV}_H$ = NPV at $r_H$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IRR is mathematically the root of an $n$-degree polynomial. For non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes), there can be multiple IRRs or no real IRR. In such cases, NPV is the safer metric.

---

## 2.6 Method 6 — Life Cycle Costing (LCC)

### Definition
LCC (also called **Whole-Life Costing** or **Total Cost of Ownership**) is the sum of all costs incurred over the entire life of an asset — from cradle to grave — discounted to present value.

$$
\text{LCC} \;=\; C_{\text{initial}} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_{O\&M,t}}{(1+i)^{t}} + \frac{C_{\text{disposal},n}}{(1+i)^{n}} - \frac{S_{\text{salvage},n}}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{initial}}$ = Initial procurement + installation cost
- $C_{O\&M,t}$ = Operation & Maintenance cost in year $t$ (energy, spares, labour)
- $C_{\text{disposal},n}$ = Decommissioning / disposal cost at end of life
- $S_{\text{salvage},n}$ = Salvage value at end of life
- $n$ = Useful life
- $i$ = Discount rate

### Special Case — Constant Annual O&M
When $C_{O\&M}$ is constant:

$$
\text{LCC} \;=\; C_{\text{initial}} + C_{O\&M} \times \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \;-\; \frac{S_{\text{salvage}}}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

The middle term is the **Present Worth of an Annuity (PWFA)** factor, also called the **Uniform Series Present Worth (USPW)** factor.

---

## 2.7 Method 7 — Sensitivity Analysis

### Definition
A "what-if" technique that identifies which input variables have the **largest impact** on the chosen economic indicator (typically NPV or IRR). Each variable is varied one at a time, while others are held at their baseline (base case) values.

### Tornado Diagram Convention
Variables are ranked by the magnitude of their swing on NPV. The largest-impact variable appears at the top of the chart.

**Common sensitivity variables in energy projects:**
1. Energy unit cost (₹/kWh)
2. Annual operating hours
3. Initial capital cost
4. Discount rate
5. Equipment efficiency drift
6. Inflation rate

---

## 2.8 Method 8 — Risk Analysis

### Definition
Whereas Sensitivity Analysis asks *"What if?"*, Risk Analysis assigns **probabilities** to outcomes and computes the **expected value** and **variance** of the decision metric.

### Expected NPV

$$
E[\text{NPV}] \;=\; \sum_{j} p_j \cdot \text{NPV}_j
$$

### Standard Deviation (Volatility)

$$
\sigma_{\text{NPV}} \;=\; \sqrt{\sum_{j} p_j \cdot (\text{NPV}_j - E[\text{NPV}])^2}
$$

### Coefficient of Variation (Relative Risk)

$$
\text{CV} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{\text{NPV}}}{E[\text{NPV}]}
$$

A **Monte Carlo Simulation** is the modern, computer-aided method where thousands of random samples are drawn from input distributions (Normal, Triangular, Lognormal) and the resulting distribution of NPV is plotted.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Formula | Decision Rule | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Payback | $\text{SPP} = C_0 / S$ | SPP $\leq$ Target | Ignores TVM and post-payback savings |
| 2 | Discounted Payback | $\sum S_t/(1+i)^t \geq C_0$ | DPP $\leq$ Target | Still ignores post-payback cash flows |
| 3 | ROI | $(\text{Annual Savings}/C_0)\times 100$ | ROI $\geq$ MARR | Ignores project life and timing |
| 4 | NPV | $\sum CF_t/(1+i)^t$ | NPV $>$ 0 | Requires accurate discount rate |
| 5 | IRR | $\sum CF_t/(1+r)^t = 0$ | IRR $>$ $i$ | Multiple IRRs possible |
| 6 | LCC | $C_0 + \sum C_{O\&M,t}/(1+i)^t$ | Minimize LCC | Data-intensive |
| 7 | EAC | $\text{NPV} \times \text{CRF}$ | Minimize EAC | Useful for unequal-life comparison |
| 8 | Sensitivity | $\Delta \text{NPV}/\Delta x_i$ | Identify critical vars | One-at-a-time only |
| 9 | Risk | $E[\text{NPV}], \sigma_{\text{NPV}}$ | Risk-adjusted NPV | Requires probability estimates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discounting Factors to Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**
> - Single Payment Present Worth (SPPW): $\text{SPPW} = 1/(1+i)^n$
> - Uniform Series Present Worth (USPW): $\text{USPW} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$
> - Sinking Fund Factor (SFF): $\text{SFF} = i/[(1+i)^n - 1]$
> - Capital Recovery Factor (CRF): $\text{CRF} = i(1+i)^n/[(1+i)^n - 1]$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Payback with Uniform Savings

**Problem Statement:**
A factory is evaluating the installation of a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on a 75 kW motor. The VFD costs ₹4,50,000 (installed). Annual energy savings are estimated at 90,000 kWh/year at ₹7/kWh. Operating hours = 6,000 h/year.

**Step 1: Compute Annual Monetary Savings**

$$
S = 90{,}000 \;\text{kWh/yr} \times ₹7/\text{kWh} = ₹6{,}30{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Simple Payback Formula**

$$
\text{SPP} = \frac{C_0}{S} = \frac{₹4{,}50{,}000}{₹6{,}30{,}000} = 0.7143 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to Months**

$$
0.7143 \times 12 = 8.57 \text{ months} \approx 8 \text{ months and 17 days}
$$

**Step 4: Decision**
If the company's target payback is 1.5 years, the VFD is **ACCEPTED** comfortably.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Net Present Value (Full Derivation)

**Problem Statement:**
A company is considering replacing its conventional lighting with LEDs. The data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Initial Investment ($C_0$) | ₹20,00,000 |
| Annual Energy + Maintenance Savings | ₹6,00,000 (constant) |
| Project Life ($n$) | 10 years |
| Discount Rate ($i$) | 12% |
| Salvage Value | ₹2,00,000 |

**Step 1: Identify the Discount Factors Required**

We need two factors:
- Uniform Series Present Worth (USPW) to annualize the savings.
- Single Payment Present Worth (SPPW) to bring the salvage back to year 0.

**Step 2: Compute USPW Factor at $i=12\%$, $n=10$**

$$
\text{USPW}_{12\%,10} = \frac{(1+0.12)^{10}-1}{0.12 \times (1+0.12)^{10}}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^{10}$ step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^4 &= (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735 \\
(1.12)^5 &= 1.5735 \times 1.12 = 1.7623 \\
(1.12)^{10} &= (1.7623)^2 = 3.1058
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{USPW} = \frac{3.1058 - 1}{0.12 \times 3.1058} = \frac{2.1058}{0.3727} = 5.6502
$$

**Step 3: Compute SPPW Factor for Salvage Value**

$$
\text{SPPW}_{12\%,10} = \frac{1}{(1.12)^{10}} = \frac{1}{3.1058} = 0.3220
$$

**Step 4: Assemble the NPV Equation**

$$
\text{NPV} = -C_0 + S \times \text{USPW} + \text{Salvage} \times \text{SPPW}
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -20{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 \times 5.6502 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3220
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate Each Term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
6{,}00{,}000 \times 5.6502 &= 33{,}90{,}120 \\
2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3220 &= 64{,}400 \\
\text{PV of inflows} &= 33{,}90{,}120 + 64{,}400 = 34{,}54{,}520
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final NPV**

$$
\text{NPV} = 34{,}54{,}520 - 20{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{₹14{,}54{,}520}
$$

**Step 7: Decision**
NPV is **strongly positive** → **ACCEPT** the LED retrofit.

**Step 8: Profitability Index (Bonus Metric)**

$$
\text{PI} = \frac{\text{PV of Inflows}}{C_0} = \frac{34{,}54{,}520}{20{,}00{,}000} = 1.727
$$

Since PI $>$ 1, the project creates value.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Internal Rate of Return (Interpolative Method)

**Problem Statement:**
Using the same LED project, find the IRR. We must find $r$ such that NPV = 0.

**Step 1: Try $r_L = 25\%$**

$\text{USPW}_{25\%,10} = \frac{(1.25)^{10}-1}{0.25 \times (1.25)^{10}}$

Compute $(1.25)^{10} = 9.3132$

$$
\text{USPW} = \frac{9.3132-1}{0.25 \times 9.3132} = \frac{8.3132}{2.3283} = 3.5705
$$

$\text{SPPW}_{25\%,10} = 1/9.3132 = 0.1074$

$$
\text{NPV}_L = -20L + 6L \times 3.5705 + 2L \times 0.1074 = -20L + 21.423L + 0.215L = +1.638L
$$

So at 25%, NPV = **+₹1,63,800** (positive).

**Step 2: Try $r_H = 28\%$**

$(1.28)^{10} \approx 10.6052$

$\text{USPW}_{28\%,10} = (10.6052 - 1)/(0.28 \times 10.6052) = 9.6052/2.9695 = 3.2339$

$\text{SPPW}_{28\%,10} = 1/10.6052 = 0.0943$

$$
\text{NPV}_H = -20L + 6L \times 3.2339 + 2L \times 0.0943 = -20L + 19.403L + 0.189L = -0.408L
$$

So at 28%, NPV = **–₹40,800** (negative).

**Step 3: Interpolate**

$$
\text{IRR} \approx r_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (r_H - r_L)
$$

$$
\text{IRR} \approx 25 + \frac{1.638}{1.638 - (-0.408)} \times (28 - 25)
$$

$$
\text{IRR} \approx 25 + \frac{1.638}{2.046} \times 3 \approx 25 + 2.402 = 27.40\%
$$

**Step 4: Decision**
IRR = 27.40% > Hurdle rate of 12% → **ACCEPT**.

> [!NOTE]
> The interpolation is *approximate* because NPV varies non-linearly with $r$. For KTU exam purposes, this linear interpolation is fully acceptable.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) for Unequal Lives

**Problem Statement:**
Two chiller options:
- **Chiller A:** ₹50,00,000, life = 15 years, annual O&M = ₹3,00,000
- **Chiller B:** ₹35,00,000, life = 10 years, annual O&M = ₹2,00,000
- Discount rate = 10%

**Step 1: Compute CRF for Chiller A (n=15)**

$$
\text{CRF}_A = \frac{0.10(1.10)^{15}}{(1.10)^{15}-1}
$$

$(1.10)^{15} = 4.1772$

$$
\text{CRF}_A = \frac{0.10 \times 4.1772}{4.1772 - 1} = \frac{0.4177}{3.1772} = 0.1315
$$

**Step 2: Compute EAC for Chiller A**

$$
\text{EAC}_A = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1315 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}57{,}500 + 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹9{,}57{,}500
$$

**Step 3: Compute CRF for Chiller B (n=10)**

$(1.10)^{10} = 2.5937$

$$
\text{CRF}_B = \frac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \frac{0.2594}{1.5937} = 0.1627
$$

**Step 4: Compute EAC for Chiller B**

$$
\text{EAC}_B = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1627 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}69{,}450 + 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹7{,}69{,}450
$$

**Step 5: Decision**
$\text{EAC}_B < \text{EAC}_A$, so **Chiller B is cheaper on an annualized basis**, even though its life is shorter. The analysis assumes Chiller B will be replaced with an identical unit after 10 years, and the 15-year horizon continues with a second Chiller B purchase.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Life Cycle Costing (LCC)

**Problem Statement:**
A 100-tr air-conditioning plant: Option P (Conventional) costs ₹8,00,000 with annual electricity bill ₹2,50,000 for 15 years. Option Q (Inverter) costs ₹14,00,000 with annual electricity bill ₹1,50,000 for 15 years. Discount rate = 8%. Salvage = 10% of initial cost.

**Step 1: USPW at 8%, n=15**

$(1.08)^{15} = 3.1722$

$$
\text{USPW} = \frac{3.1722 - 1}{0.08 \times 3.1722} = \frac{2.1722}{0.2538} = 8.5595
$$

**Step 2: LCC of Option P**

Salvage P = ₹80,000; SPPW = $1/3.1722 = 0.3152$

$$
\text{LCC}_P = 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 \times 8.5595 - 80{,}000 \times 0.3152
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_P = 8{,}00{,}000 + 21{,}39{,}875 - 25{,}216 = ₹29{,}14{,}659
$$

**Step 3: LCC of Option Q**

Salvage Q = ₹1,40,000

$$
\text{LCC}_Q = 14{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 8.5595 - 1{,}40{,}000 \times 0.3152
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_Q = 14{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}83{,}925 - 44{,}128 = ₹26{,}39{,}797
$$

**Step 4: Decision**

$\text{LCC}_Q < \text{LCC}_P$ → **Choose Option Q** (Inverter AC). The premium is recovered through operational savings over 15 years.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Sensitivity Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A biomass gasifier project has a base-case NPV = ₹45 lakhs. Three critical variables and their low/high values:

| Variable | Low | Base | High | $\Delta$ NPV (Low) | $\Delta$ NPV (High) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Biomass Price (₹/tonne) | 2,500 | 3,500 | 4,500 | +₹22 L | –₹18 L |
| Plant Load Factor (%) | 70 | 85 | 95 | –₹15 L | +₹12 L |
| Discount Rate (%) | 10 | 12 | 15 | +₹10 L | –₹14 L |

**Step 1: Compute Swing (Range) for Each Variable**

- Biomass Price swing = $22 - (-18) = 40$ lakhs
- Plant Load Factor swing = $-15 - 12 = -27$ lakhs (magnitude = 27)
- Discount Rate swing = $10 - (-14) = 24$ lakhs

**Step 2: Rank Variables (Tornado Order)**

1. Biomass Price (swing = 40)
2. Plant Load Factor (swing = 27)
3. Discount Rate (swing = 24)

**Step 3: Conclusion**
Biomass price is the **most critical variable**. Management should lock in a long-term biomass supply contract before committing capital.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Project Size / Context | Recommended Primary Method | Supporting Methods |
|---|---|---|
| Small (<₹5 Lakh), fast screening | Simple Payback | ROI |
| Medium (₹5 L – ₹50 L), standard capex | NPV | Discounted Payback, ROI |
| Large (>₹50 L), board-level projects | NPV + IRR | Sensitivity, Risk Analysis |
| Equipment replacement with unequal lives | EAC / LCC | NPV |
| Long-term strategic projects (≥15 yr) | LCC | NPV, IRR |
| High uncertainty (new tech, policy risk) | Risk Analysis | Sensitivity, Real Options |
