# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **"What is meant by Net Present Value (NPV) of an energy project? State its decision rule."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Net Present Value (NPV):** NPV is the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash inflows and outflows associated with a project over its entire economic life, discounted at an appropriate hurdle rate (cost of capital). It converts future rupee savings into today's money.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^{t}}
$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If **NPV > 0** → ACCEPT (project creates wealth above the discount rate).
- If **NPV = 0** → Indifferent.
- If **NPV < 0** → REJECT.

NPV is considered the most reliable single criterion for capital budgeting in energy management because it directly measures wealth creation in monetary terms. *(3 marks: definition 1.5, formula 0.5, decision rule 1)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **"Distinguish between Simple Payback Period (SPP) and Discounted Payback Period (DPP)."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Basis | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | Discounted Payback Period (DPP) |
|---|---|---|
| Time value of money | Ignored | Considered via discount rate $i$ |
| Cash flow treatment | Undiscounted nominal cash flows | Discounted (present-valued) cash flows |
| Formula | $\sum S_t \geq C_0$ | $\sum S_t/(1+i)^t \geq C_0$ |
| Result | Always shorter than DPP | Always longer than SPP |
| Accuracy | Approximate, conservative on payback | More realistic, accounts for opportunity cost |

**Key Point:** DPP is the more theoretically correct metric, while SPP is preferred for quick screening due to its simplicity. *(3 marks: tabulated distinction with formulae and conceptual point)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question carries internal choice. We provide both alternatives with full solutions.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a)** A factory plans to install a 500 kW rooftop solar PV system. Initial cost is ₹2,50,00,000. Annual generation = 7,50,000 kWh at ₹6.50/kWh tariff. Project life = 25 years. Discount rate = 10%. Salvage value = ₹15,00,000. Annual O&M = ₹4,00,000. Calculate the **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** using the interpolative method. Decide on project acceptance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Life Cycle Costing (LCC)** with a suitable formula. How is it different from simple payback analysis? Discuss its significance in evaluating energy-efficient equipment. **(7 marks)**

### Solution (a) — NPV and IRR Calculation

**Step 1: Compute Net Annual Cash Inflow**
- Gross annual savings = $7,50,000 \times 6.50 = ₹48,75,000$
- Less: O&M = ₹4,00,000
- **Net annual cash savings** = ₹44,75,000

**Step 2: Compute USPW at $i = 10\%$, $n = 25$**

$(1.10)^{25} = 10.8347$

$$
\text{USPW} = \frac{10.8347 - 1}{0.10 \times 10.8347} = \frac{9.8347}{1.0835} = 9.0770
$$

**Step 3: Compute SPPW for Salvage**

$\text{SPPW} = 1/10.8347 = 0.0923$

**Step 4: Assemble NPV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -2{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 44{,}75{,}000 \times 9.0770 + 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.0923 \\
&= -2{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}06{,}19{,}575 + 1{,}38{,}450 \\
&= -2{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}07{,}58{,}025 \\
&= ₹1{,}57{,}58{,}025
\end{aligned}
$$

**NPV = ₹1.576 crores (positive) → Project is financially viable at 10% discount rate.**

**Step 5: Find IRR by Trial**

Try $r = 20\%$: $(1.20)^{25} = 95.396$

$\text{USPW}_{20\%,25} = (95.396-1)/(0.20 \times 95.396) = 94.396/19.079 = 4.948$

$\text{SPPW} = 1/95.396 = 0.0105$

$\text{NPV}_{20\%} = -2.5\text{Cr} + 44.75\text{L} \times 4.948 + 15\text{L} \times 0.0105 = -2.5\text{Cr} + 2.214\text{Cr} + 0.0158\text{Cr} = -0.27\text{Cr}$ (Negative)

Try $r = 18\%$: $(1.18)^{25} = 53.108$

$\text{USPW}_{18\%,25} = (53.108-1)/(0.18 \times 53.108) = 52.108/9.559 = 5.451$

$\text{SPPW} = 1/53.108 = 0.0188$

$\text{NPV}_{18\%} = -2.5\text{Cr} + 44.75\text{L} \times 5.451 + 15\text{L} \times 0.0188 = -2.5\text{Cr} + 2.439\text{Cr} + 0.0282\text{Cr} = -0.033\text{Cr}$ (Slightly Negative)

Try $r = 17\%$: $(1.17)^{25} \approx 44.99$

$\text{USPW}_{17\%,25} \approx (44.99-1)/(0.17 \times 44.99) = 43.99/7.648 = 5.752$

$\text{SPPW} = 1/44.99 = 0.0222$

$\text{NPV}_{17\%} = -2.5\text{Cr} + 44.75\text{L} \times 5.752 + 15\text{L} \times 0.0222 = -2.5\text{Cr} + 2.574\text{Cr} + 0.0333\text{Cr} = +0.107\text{Cr}$ (Positive)

**Step 6: Interpolate between 17% and 18%**

$$
\text{IRR} \approx 17 + \frac{0.107}{0.107 - (-0.033)} \times (18-17) = 17 + \frac{0.107}{0.140} \times 1 = 17 + 0.764 = 17.76\%
$$

**IRR ≈ 17.76% > Hurdle rate of 10% → ACCEPT.** *(7 marks)*

---

### Solution (b) — Life Cycle Costing

**Definition:** Life Cycle Costing (LCC) is the systematic methodology of summing **all costs** incurred during the entire economic life of an asset — acquisition, installation, operation, maintenance, and disposal — brought to a common present-value basis using an appropriate discount rate. The goal is to identify the option with the **lowest total cost of ownership**.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{LCC} = C_{\text{init}} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_{O\&M,t}}{(1+i)^{t}} - \frac{S_{\text{salvage}}}{(1+i)^{n}} + \frac{C_{\text{disposal}}}{(1+i)^{n}}
$$

**Difference from Simple Payback:**

| Aspect | Simple Payback | LCC |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | Only until capital is recovered | Entire project life |
| Cash inflows | Ignored after payback | All savings included |
| Discounting | Not applied | Discounting is fundamental |
| Decision metric | Time (years) | Money (₹) |
| Suitability | Quick screening | Strategic equipment selection |

**Significance in evaluating energy-efficient equipment:**

1. **Energy-efficient equipment** typically has higher upfront costs but dramatically lower running costs. Payback alone misses this.
2. LCC captures the **total cost of ownership**, making it the fairest comparison between, say, a conventional motor vs. a premium-efficiency (IE3/IE4) motor.
3. LCC is mandated by **ISO 15686-5** and **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** guidelines for government and PSU procurement.
4. It reveals the **true hidden cost** of cheap equipment — high electricity bills over 15–20 years.
5. It supports **green procurement policies** by quantifying lifetime energy cost savings in monetary terms. *(7 marks: definition 1.5, formula 1, comparison table 2.5, significance 2)*

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a)** A company must choose between two motors for a continuous-duty application:
>
> | Parameter | Motor X (Standard) | Motor Y (Premium IE4) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Price | ₹1,20,000 | ₹2,00,000 |
> | Efficiency | 88% | 94% |
> | Annual Energy Cost @ 8000 hr | ₹6,00,000 | ₹5,60,000 |
> | Life | 15 years | 15 years |
> | Salvage | ₹10,000 | ₹20,000 |
>
> Discount rate = 12%. Using **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)**, recommend the better option. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **Sensitivity Analysis**? With reference to an energy project, explain how a **Tornado Diagram** is constructed and interpreted. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

### Solution (a) — EAC Comparison

**Step 1: Compute CRF at $i=12\%$, $n=15$**

$(1.12)^{15} = 5.4736$

$$
\text{CRF} = \frac{0.12 \times 5.4736}{5.4736 - 1} = \frac{0.6568}{4.4736} = 0.1468
$$

**Step 2: Annualized Capital Cost for Motor X**

$$
A_{\text{cap,X}} = (1{,}20{,}000 - 10{,}000) \times 0.1468 + 10{,}000 \times 0 \; \text{(if salvage treated separately)}
$$

Equivalent treatment:

$$
A_{\text{cap,X}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.1468 - 10{,}000 \times 0.1468 + 10{,}000 \times \text{(SFF)}
$$

For simplicity, combine initial + salvage in CRF:

$$
A_{\text{cap,X}} = (1{,}20{,}000 - 10{,}000) \times 0.1468 = ₹16{,}162
$$

(Note: Salvage adjustment is minor; CRF method over the depreciable base.)

**Step 3: EAC of Motor X**

$$
\text{EAC}_X = A_{\text{cap,X}} + \text{Annual Energy Cost}_X = 16{,}162 + 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹6{,}16{,}162
$$

**Step 4: Annualized Capital Cost for Motor Y**

$$
A_{\text{cap,Y}} = (2{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000) \times 0.1468 = ₹26{,}424
$$

**Step 5: EAC of Motor Y**

$$
\text{EAC}_Y = 26{,}424 + 5{,}60{,}000 = ₹5{,}86{,}424
$$

**Step 6: Decision**

$\text{EAC}_Y = ₹5{,}86{,}424 < \text{EAC}_X = ₹6{,}16{,}162$

**Premium IE4 motor is cheaper by ~₹29,738/year.** Recommend Motor Y. *(7 marks)*

---

### Solution (b) — Sensitivity Analysis & Tornado Diagram

**Definition:** Sensitivity Analysis is a one-at-a-time (OAT) testing procedure that quantifies how much the **output** of a financial model (typically NPV or IRR) changes when a single **input variable** is varied while all other variables are held at their base-case values. It identifies the *critical variables* — those whose uncertainty most threatens the project's viability.

**Construction of a Tornado Diagram:**

1. List 5–8 key input variables (e.g., energy tariff, O&M cost, discount rate, capacity factor, capex, project life, salvage).
2. For each variable, define a plausible low and high value (often ±20% or based on worst-best scenarios).
3. Recompute NPV at the low and high values, holding others constant.
4. Compute the **swing** = NPV(high) − NPV(low) for each variable.
5. Order the variables from **largest swing at top to smallest at bottom** — this creates the characteristic tornado shape.

**Interpretation:**
- The bar at the top = the variable whose uncertainty most affects NPV.
- A variable whose low-case still yields positive NPV is **robust**.
- A variable whose high-case NPV goes negative is a **killer variable** requiring risk mitigation (e.g., fixed-price contracts, insurance).

**Limitations:**
1. **One-at-a-time only** — cannot capture interaction effects between variables.
2. **Subjective ranges** — the choice of ±X% is arbitrary.
3. **No probability weighting** — does not tell which scenario is most likely.
4. **Linear assumption** — implicitly assumes small variations; large swings may produce non-linear NPV responses.
5. **Decision paralysis** — too many sensitive variables can make a project look riskier than it actually is.

> For more rigorous risk quantification, Sensitivity Analysis must be paired with **Scenario Analysis** and **Monte Carlo Simulation**. *(7 marks: definition 1.5, construction steps 2, interpretation 1.5, limitations 2)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign on $C_0$** — A ₹20 lakh investment must be entered as **−₹20,00,000**, not positive. Mixing signs flips NPV sign and changes the entire decision.
> 2. **Using SPP formula on non-uniform savings** — When annual savings vary year to year (e.g., phased LED retrofit), the simple $C_0/S$ formula is invalid. Use the cumulative summation method.
> 3. **Wrong discount factor used** — Students often confuse USPW with SPPW, or use the wrong $n$. Always re-verify: are you discounting an *annuity* (use USPW/CRF) or a *future lump sum* (use SPPW/SFF)?
> 4. **Interpolation trap with IRR** — The linear interpolation is approximate. Examiners accept it, but do not present it as "exact IRR". State clearly it is an estimate.
> 5. **EAC without equal-life assumption** — EAC is valid only when the shorter-life asset is replaced by an equivalent at the end of its life. State this assumption explicitly.
> 6. **Skipping units in tables** — Always annotate currency (₹), units (kWh, tonnes), and time (years) to gain easy presentation marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- [ ] **SPP** = Capex ÷ Annual Savings (only for uniform savings). Quick screening tool.
- [ ] **DPP** = Same but with discounted cash flows. Slightly more rigorous.
- [ ] **ROI** = (Avg Annual Savings ÷ Capex) × 100. A profitability ratio, not a time.
- [ ] **NPV** is the **king** of capital budgeting. Decision: NPV > 0 → Accept.
- [ ] **NPV Formula:** $\sum CF_t/(1+i)^t$. Don't forget the negative $C_0$.
- [ ] **USPW Factor:** $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — discount a constant annual series.
- [ ] **SPPW Factor:** $\frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ — discount a future lump sum (e.g., salvage).
- [ ] **CRF Factor:** $\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — convert present value to annual equivalent (EAC).
- [ ] **EAC** is the right metric for comparing options with **unequal lives**.
- [ ] **IRR** = discount rate that makes NPV = 0. Use interpolation between $r_L$ (NPV+) and $r_H$ (NPV−).
- [ ] **IRR Decision:** IRR > Hurdle Rate → Accept. Multiple sign changes in cash flow = use NPV instead.
- [ ] **LCC** = Initial + PV(O&M) − PV(Salvage) + PV(Disposal). Used for long-lived equipment.
- [ ] **Sensitivity Analysis** = one variable at a time. **Tornado Diagram** ranks variables by swing.
- [ ] **Risk Analysis** = assign probabilities; compute Expected NPV and standard deviation. **Monte Carlo** for complex projects.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** — discount rate, project life, salvage, O&M, energy unit cost.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** Don't use SPP when savings are non-uniform; don't use IRR for non-conventional cash flows; don't forget negative sign on capex.

> **End of Module 4 Topic — Evaluation of Proposals**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pay-Back Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pay-Back Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Pay-Back Period: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pay-Back Period (PBP)** is the length of time (usually expressed in **years** or **months**) required for the **cumulative net cash inflows** generated by an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) to exactly equal the **initial capital investment** (capital outlay) incurred to implement that project.

In the context of an **Energy Audit** and **Investment Grade Audit (IGA)**, the Pay-Back Period is the most widely used **"first-pass" screening tool** by industries, Energy Service Companies (ESCOs), and financial controllers to rank competing energy-efficiency projects before performing deeper financial appraisals such as **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, or **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** analysis.

Mathematically, PBP is defined as the smallest integer $n$ satisfying:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} CF_k \;\geq\; I_0$$

where $I_0$ is the initial investment and $CF_k$ is the net cash flow in year $k$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Loan Repayment View"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you borrow **₹5,00,000** from a bank to install a **solar PV system** on your factory roof. Every month, the electricity bill you *no longer pay* becomes your "income" from the system. The **Pay-Back Period** is simply the month at which your cumulative electricity savings have "paid off" the ₹5,00,000 loan in full — just like the EMIs repaying the loan, except here the project itself is repaying you.

The moment cumulative savings **cross** the initial investment line on a graph, the project is said to have "broken even." From that instant onwards, every rupee saved is **pure profit**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Pay-Back Methods

There are **two principal variants** recognized by KTU and standard Energy Management literature:

| # | Method | Accounts for Time Value of Money (TVM)? | Complexity |
|---|--------|------------------------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Simple (Undiscounted) Pay-Back Period** | ❌ No | Very Low |
| 2 | **Discounted Pay-Back Period** | ✅ Yes | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metric:** Acceptable Pay-Back Period thresholds vary by sector — **2–3 years** for lighting retrofits, **3–5 years** for HVAC/VFD projects, and **5–7 years** for major cogeneration or waste-heat-recovery systems. Any project exceeding the corporate "**Maximum Acceptable Pay-Back Period (MAPBP)**" is normally rejected during the preliminary screening stage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The Pay-Back Period method follows a strict, deterministic algorithm:

1. **Identify the Initial Investment ($I_0$):** Sum of all capital costs — equipment, installation, commissioning, design, and training expenses incurred at Year 0.
2. **Forecast the Annual Net Cash Flow ($CF_k$):** The annual monetary benefit, calculated as:
   $$CF_k = \text{Annual Energy Cost Savings} + \text{O\&M Cost Savings} - \text{Incremental O\&M Cost}$$
3. **Build a Cumulative Cash Flow Table:** Year-wise sum of net cash flows starting from $-I_0$ at Year 0.
4. **Locate the Break-Even Year:** Identify the year at which the cumulative total transitions from negative to positive (or zero).
5. **Decision Rule:**
   - **Accept** the project if PBP $\leq$ MAPBP (Maximum Acceptable Pay-Back Period set by management).
   - **Reject** the project if PBP $>$ MAPBP.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Method

- **Speed of Decision:** Industries often have dozens of ECMs competing for limited capital. PBP gives a **single-number, easily comparable metric**.
- **Risk Aversion:** Projects with short pay-back are inherently **less risky** because the uncertainty horizon is shorter.
- **Cash-Flow Recovery Focus:** Aligns with the **liquidity preference** of CFOs who want their capital back quickly.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all exam-critical formulas** for the Pay-Back Period topic. These are the equations you must memorize verbatim for the **University ESE (End Semester Examination)**.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple Pay-Back Period (Uniform Savings)** | $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{I_0}{S}$ | $I_0$ = Initial Investment (**₹**); $S$ = Annual Net Savings (**₹/year**) | Only valid when annual savings are **constant** every year |
| 2 | **Simple Pay-Back Period (Non-Uniform Savings)** | $\text{PBP} = A + \dfrac{\vert B \vert}{C}$ | $A$ = last year with negative cumulative CF (**years**); $B$ = absolute value of cumulative CF at end of year $A$ (**₹**); $C$ = cash flow in year $A+1$ (**₹**) | Used when savings vary year-to-year; interpolation between two consecutive years |
| 3 | **Discounted Pay-Back Period (Uniform Savings)** | $\text{DPBP} = \dfrac{-\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{I_0 \cdot r}{S}\right)}{\ln(1+r)}$ | $r$ = discount rate (**dimensionless, decimal**) | Valid only when $I_0 \cdot r < S$ (otherwise DPBP is **infinite** — savings never recover principal at real terms) |
| 4 | **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** | $\text{PVAF}(r,n) = \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Used to equate the discounted annuity to $I_0$ | $r \neq 0$ |
| 5 | **Discounted Cash Flow in Year $k$** | $DCF_k = \dfrac{CF_k}{(1+r)^{k}}$ | $CF_k$ = nominal cash flow in year $k$ | TVM-adjusted value |
| 6 | **Decision Criterion** | Accept if $\text{PBP} \leq \text{MAPBP}$ | MAPBP = corporate threshold | Reject otherwise |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ESCO Project Financing:** Energy Service Companies quote PBP to clients as the headline metric of contract viability.
- **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) – PAT Scheme:** Industries compute PBP for notified energy-intensive units to assess compliance investments.
- **CDM / Carbon Credit Projects:** PBP is used to evaluate whether the additionality of emission-reduction projects is financially viable.
- **Plant-Level Capex Justification:** Maintenance and plant engineers must always justify any energy-related capex with a PBP $\leq$ **3 years** in many Indian manufacturing firms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Pay-Back Period Formula (Uniform Savings)

### Statement

For a project with constant annual savings $S$ (₹/year) and initial capital $I_0$ (₹), derive the closed-form expression for the Simple Pay-Back Period.

### Derivation

**Step 1 —** Write the cumulative cash flow equation. Let $n$ be the number of years elapsed. The cumulative net cash flow at year $n$ is:

$$\text{Cumulative CF}(n) = -I_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} S$$

**Step 2 —** Factor out the constant $S$:

$$\text{Cumulative CF}(n) = -I_0 + n \cdot S$$

**Step 3 —** At the Pay-Back Period, by definition, the cumulative cash flow equals zero:

$$-I_0 + n \cdot S = 0$$

**Step 4 —** Solve algebraically for $n$:

$$n \cdot S = I_0$$

$$n = \frac{I_0}{S}$$

**Step 5 —** Therefore, the **Simple Pay-Back Period** is:

$$\boxed{\text{PBP} = \frac{I_0}{S}}$$

**Step 6 —** Dimensional check:

$$[\text{PBP}] = \frac{[\text{₹}]}{[\text{₹/year}]} = [\text{year}] \;\;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Interpolated Pay-Back Period Formula (Non-Uniform Savings)

### Statement

When yearly savings are unequal, the cumulative cash flow crosses zero *between* two years. Derive the interpolation formula.

### Derivation

**Step 1 —** Let Year $A$ be the last year with a negative cumulative cash flow, and Year $A+1$ be the first year with a positive cumulative cash flow.

**Step 2 —** Define:
- $B$ = absolute value of the (negative) cumulative cash flow at the **end** of Year $A$.
- $C$ = net cash flow occurring **during** Year $A+1$.

**Step 3 —** Assume a linear cash-flow pattern within Year $A+1$. The fraction of Year $A+1$ required to absorb the deficit $B$ is:

$$f = \frac{B}{C}$$

**Step 4 —** The Pay-Back Period is therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{PBP} = A + \frac{B}{C}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This interpolation is **only an approximation**. Strictly speaking, PBP under non-uniform savings should be computed by building a year-by-year cumulative table and locating the sign change.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Discounted Pay-Back Period Formula

### Statement

For uniform annual savings $S$ and discount rate $r$, derive the closed-form expression for the Discounted Pay-Back Period.

### Derivation

**Step 1 —** The present value of an annuity of $S$ per year for $n$ years at discount rate $r$ is:

$$\text{PV} = S \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Step 2 —** Set this equal to the initial investment (break-even condition):

$$I_0 = S \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Step 3 —** Isolate the bracket term:

$$\frac{I_0 \cdot r}{S} = 1 - (1+r)^{-n}$$

**Step 4 —** Rearrange to isolate $(1+r)^{-n}$:

$$(1+r)^{-n} = 1 - \frac{I_0 \cdot r}{S}$$

**Step 5 —** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-n \cdot \ln(1+r) = \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{I_0 \cdot r}{S}\right)$$

**Step 6 —** Solve for $n$:

$$\boxed{\text{DPBP} = n = \frac{-\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{I_0 \cdot r}{S}\right)}{\ln(1+r)}}$$

**Step 7 —** Convergence condition:

$$1 - \frac{I_0 \cdot r}{S} > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; S > I_0 \cdot r$$

If $S \leq I_0 \cdot r$, the denominator inside the logarithm becomes zero or negative, meaning **the project can never pay back at the discounted rate** — it is **financially non-viable** under TVM considerations.

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example — Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Typical 7-mark sub-part):**
>
> A manufacturing unit invests **₹8,00,000** to install a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on its cooling-water pump. The projected annual energy savings are:
> - Year 1: ₹2,50,000
> - Year 2: ₹3,00,000
> - Year 3: ₹3,50,000
> - Year 4: ₹4,00,000
> - Year 5 onwards: ₹4,00,000 / year
>
> The corporate Maximum Acceptable Pay-Back Period is **3 years**. Determine the Simple Pay-Back Period and decide on project acceptance.

### Solution — Cumulative Cash Flow Table

| Year | Annual Savings (₹) | Cumulative CF (₹) | Status |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|--------|
| 0    | $-8,00,000$       | $-8,00,000$       | Negative |
| 1    | $+2,50,000$       | $-5,50,000$       | Negative |
| 2    | $+3,00,000$       | $-2,50,000$       | Negative |
| 3    | $+3,50,000$       | $+1,00,000$       | **Positive** ✅ |
| 4    | $+4,00,000$       | $+5,00,000$       | Positive |
| 5    | $+4,00,000$       | $+9,00,000$       | Positive |

**Step 1 —** Sign change occurs between **Year 2** and **Year 3**.
- Last negative year: $A = 2$
- Cumulative deficit at end of Year 2: $B = \vert -2,50,000 \vert = 2,50,000$
- Cash flow in Year 3: $C = 3,50,000$

**Step 2 —** Apply the interpolation formula:

$$\text{PBP} = A + \frac{B}{C} = 2 + \frac{2,50,000}{3,50,000}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{2,50,000}{3,50,000} = \frac{25}{35} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143$$

**Step 4 —** Final answer:

$$\boxed{\text{PBP} = 2.71 \;\text{years} \;\approx\; 2\;\text{years},\; 8.5\;\text{months}}$$

**Step 5 —** Decision: Since **2.71 years $<$ 3 years (MAPBP)**, the project is **ACCEPTED** ✅.

---

## 3.5 Solved Numerical — Discounted Pay-Back Period

> **Additional Worked Example:**
>
> A factory invests **₹10,00,000** in a heat-recovery system yielding uniform annual savings of **₹3,00,000**. The bank discount rate is **12% p.a.** Compute the Discounted Pay-Back Period.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $I_0 = 10,00,000$
- $S = 3,00,000$
- $r = 0.12$

**Step 1 —** Check feasibility condition:

$$I_0 \cdot r = 10,00,000 \times 0.12 = 1,20,000$$

Since $S = 3,00,000 > 1,20,000$ → **Project is viable under TVM** ✅

**Step 2 —** Compute the argument of the outer logarithm:

$$1 - \frac{I_0 \cdot r}{S} = 1 - \frac{1,20,000}{3,00,000} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the natural logarithms:

$$\ln(0.60) = -0.5108$$

$$\ln(1.12) = 0.1133$$

**Step 4 —** Apply the derived DPBP formula:

$$\text{DPBP} = \frac{-\ln(0.60)}{\ln(1.12)} = \frac{-(-0.5108)}{0.1133} = \frac{0.5108}{0.1133}$$

**Step 5 —** Final answer:

$$\boxed{\text{DPBP} \approx 4.51 \;\text{years}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how DPBP (4.51 years) is **longer** than the simple PBP (3.33 years). This illustrates the core principle: **once you account for the time value of money, recovery takes longer than nominal savings suggest.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pay-Back Period Decision Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the end-to-end **decision-making algorithm** an energy manager follows when evaluating a project using the Pay-Back Period method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: ECM Identified] --> B[List Initial Investment I0]
    B --> C[Forecast Annual Savings Sk for k = 1 to N]
    C --> D{Are Annual Savings Uniform?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Simple PBP Formula: PBP = I0 divided by S]
    D -- No --> F[Build Cumulative CF Table Year-by-Year]
    F --> G[Locate Sign Change Between Years]
    G --> H[Apply Interpolation: PBP = A + B divided by C]
    E --> I{Apply Discount Rate r?}
    H --> I
    I -- Yes --> J[Use DPBP Formula with Logarithmic Form]
    I -- No --> K[Compute Simple PBP Only]
    J --> L[Compare PBP with MAPBP Threshold]
    K --> L
    L --> M{PBP Less than or Equal to MAPBP?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Accept ECM: Proceed to Detailed NPV and IRR Analysis]
    M -- No --> O[Reject ECM: Archive with Justification Notes]
    N --> P[End: Add to Project Pipeline]
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Simple vs Discounted Pay-Back — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPBP[Simple Pay Back Period]
        A1[Initial Investment I0] --> B1[Constant Annual Savings S]
        B1 --> C1[PBP equals I0 divided by S]
        C1 --> D1[Ignores Time Value of Money]
        D1 --> E1[Quick but Naive Estimate]
    end
    subgraph DPBP[Discounted Pay Back Period]
        A2[Initial Investment I0] --> B2[Annual Savings S]
        B2 --> C2[Apply Discount Factor 1 over 1+r to power k]
        C2 --> D2[Cumulative Discounted CF Table]
        D2 --> E2[DPBP equals minus ln of bracket over ln of 1+r]
        E2 --> F2[Realistic Financial Recovery Time]
    end
    E1 -. Compared with .-> F2
```

## 4.3 Cumulative Cash Flow Visualization (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Y0[Year 0: Negative I0] -->|Add S1| Y1[Year 1: Still Negative]
    Y1 -->|Add S2| Y2[Year 2: Approaches Zero]
    Y2 -->|Add S3| Y3[Year 3: Crosses Zero — Pay-Back Point]
    Y3 -->|Add S4| Y4[Year 4: Profit Zone]
    Y4 -->|Add S5| Y5[Year 5: Growing Profit]
    style Y3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style Y4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style Y5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style Y0 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style Y1 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style Y2 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 4 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **"Pay-Back Period"** as applied in energy management. Mention its main **limitation**.

**Model Answer:**
The Pay-Back Period is the time (in years or months) required for the cumulative net cash savings from an energy conservation project to equal the initial capital investment. Mathematically, $\text{PBP} = I_0 / S$ for uniform savings. **Main Limitation:** It ignores the **time value of money** and **disregards any savings occurring after the pay-back point**, hence it cannot measure the true profitability of a project. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Simple Pay-Back Period** and **Discounted Pay-Back Period**.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple PBP | Discounted PBP |
|---|---|---|
| Time Value of Money | Not considered | Considered using discount rate $r$ |
| Formula (Uniform Savings) | $I_0 / S$ | $-\ln(1 - I_0 r/S) \;/\; \ln(1+r)$ |
| Result | Shorter (optimistic) | Longer (realistic) |
| Use Case | Quick screening | Detailed financial appraisal |

**(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Explain the concept of Pay-Back Period. Derive the formula for Simple Pay-Back Period with **uniform annual savings**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A textile mill invests **₹6,00,000** in a power-factor-correction capacitor bank. The annual savings due to reduced kVAH penalty and lower line losses are estimated as **₹2,40,000** in Year 1, **₹2,80,000** in Year 2, and **₹3,20,000** from Year 3 onwards. The MAPBP is **2.5 years**. **(i)** Determine the Simple Pay-Back Period. **(ii)** State with reasons whether the project should be accepted. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Concept & Derivation (7 marks):**

The Pay-Back Period is the duration needed for cumulative savings to recover the initial capital outlay. Let $I_0$ be the initial investment and $S$ the uniform annual savings.

Cumulative cash flow at year $n$:

$$\text{CCF}(n) = -I_0 + n \cdot S$$

Setting CCF to zero:

$$-I_0 + n \cdot S = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{PBP} = \frac{I_0}{S} = \frac{6,00,000}{2,40,000} = 2.50 \;\text{years (hypothetical)}$$ 

*(Note: For part b the savings are non-uniform, hence the table method is applied.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 —** Build the cumulative cash flow table:

| Year | Annual Savings (₹) | Cumulative CF (₹) |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 0    | $-6,00,000$        | $-6,00,000$        |
| 1    | $+2,40,000$        | $-3,60,000$        |
| 2    | $+2,80,000$        | $-80,000$          |
| 3    | $+3,20,000$        | $+2,40,000$        |

**Step 2 —** Identify the sign-change year:
- $A = 2$ (last negative year)
- $B = \vert -80,000 \vert = 80,000$
- $C = 3,20,000$ (cash flow in Year 3)

**Step 3 —** Apply interpolation:

$$\text{PBP} = A + \frac{B}{C} = 2 + \frac{80,000}{3,20,000} = 2 + 0.25 = 2.25 \;\text{years}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PBP} = 2.25 \;\text{years} \;\equiv\; 2 \;\text{years},\; 3 \;\text{months}}$$

**Step 4 —** Decision:
Since **PBP = 2.25 years $<$ MAPBP = 2.5 years**, the project is **ACCEPTED** ✅.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **(a)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of the Pay-Back Period method in evaluating energy-efficiency projects? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An industry invests **₹12,00,000** in a waste-heat-recovery boiler that yields **uniform annual savings of ₹3,50,000**. If the cost of capital (discount rate) is **15% per annum**, compute the **Discounted Pay-Back Period** and comment on the financial viability of the project. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Advantages & Limitations (7 marks):**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Simplicity** — Easy to compute and explain to non-financial managers.
2. **Risk Indicator** — Short PBP implies shorter exposure to forecasting errors.
3. **Liquidity Focus** — Aligns with corporate preference for early capital recovery.

**Limitations (4 marks):**
1. **Ignores Time Value of Money** — Treats ₹1 today = ₹1 in 5 years (unrealistic).
2. **Ignores Post-Pay-Back Cash Flows** — A 2-year PBP project with 1-year life is ranked equally with one having a 20-year life.
3. **No Profitability Measure** — Cannot distinguish between projects of different NPVs.
4. **Arbitrary Threshold** — The MAPBP is management's subjective choice, not market-driven.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $I_0 = 12,00,000$
- $S = 3,50,000$
- $r = 0.15$

**Step 1 —** Feasibility check:

$$I_0 \cdot r = 12,00,000 \times 0.15 = 1,80,000$$

Since $S = 3,50,000 > 1,80,000$ → **Viable** ✅

**Step 2 —** Compute bracket:

$$1 - \frac{I_0 \cdot r}{S} = 1 - \frac{1,80,000}{3,50,000} = 1 - 0.5143 = 0.4857$$

**Step 3 —** Compute logs:

$$\ln(0.4857) = -0.7218 \quad ; \quad \ln(1.15) = 0.1398$$

**Step 4 —** Apply formula:

$$\text{DPBP} = \frac{-(-0.7218)}{0.1398} = \frac{0.7218}{0.1398} = 5.16 \;\text{years}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DPBP} \approx 5.16 \;\text{years}}$$

**Step 5 —** Comparison:
- Simple PBP = $12,00,000 \;/\; 3,50,000 = 3.43$ years
- Discounted PBP = 5.16 years

**Step 6 —** Comment:
The project takes **5.16 years** to recover when time-value-of-money is properly accounted for. If MAPBP > 5.16 years, the project is acceptable on a discounted basis. The 1.7-year gap between Simple and Discounted PBP emphasizes that **ignoring TVM gives an overly optimistic recovery estimate**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students frequently forget that Year 0 carries $-I_0$ and add the first year's savings incorrectly, getting PBP off by one year. Always show the **cumulative column explicitly**.
> 2. **Interpolation Misapplication:** The formula $A + B/C$ is valid **only** for the year *immediately after* the sign change. Using any other year pair costs full marks.
> 3. **Discounted vs Simple Confusion:** Examiners often give a problem with a discount rate mentioned — if the student computes Simple PBP without acknowledging $r$, the answer is treated as incomplete and **up to 3 marks are deducted**.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing **months** and **years** (e.g., writing PBP = 2.25 months instead of 2.25 years) is a common arithmetic slip — verify before finalizing.
> 5. **Decision Justification Missing:** Even when PBP is correctly computed, KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark if the student does **not explicitly state** "Accepted" or "Rejected" with comparison against MAPBP.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** PBP = time for cumulative savings to equal initial investment.
- **Simple PBP formula (uniform):** $\text{PBP} = I_0 / S$.
- **Simple PBP formula (non-uniform):** $\text{PBP} = A + B/C$ via interpolation.
- **Discounted PBP formula:** $\text{DPBP} = -\ln(1 - I_0 r/S) \;/\; \ln(1+r)$.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if PBP $\leq$ MAPBP; Reject otherwise.
- **Feasibility Condition for DPBP:** $S > I_0 \cdot r$ (otherwise infinite DPBP).
- **Always discount** when the problem mentions a **cost of capital**, **interest rate**, or **discount rate**.
- **Simple PBP $<$ Discounted PBP** always (TVM always penalizes future savings).
- **Build a year-wise cumulative table** — never skip this step in the exam.
- **Mention both Simple and Discounted** PBP if data permits — it earns bonus conceptual marks.
- **DPBP does not replace NPV/IRR** — it only complements them as a screening metric.
- **Common industry MAPBP ranges:** 2 yrs (lighting) — 3 yrs (motors/VFD) — 5 yrs (boilers) — 7 yrs (cogeneration).
- **Units check:** Investment in ₹, Savings in ₹/year → PBP in years. If monthly, convert savings accordingly.
- **Remember the constant sign convention:** Year 0 is always $-I_0$; subsequent years are $+CF_k$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Rate of Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Average Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Average Rate of Return (ARR) Method** is a classical, non-discounted **capital budgeting technique** used in engineering economic analysis to evaluate the profitability of energy conservation projects. It expresses the **average annual accounting profit** earned on the **average investment** committed to a project, expressed as a percentage.

The ARR is fundamentally an **accounting-based profitability ratio** (not a cash-flow ratio) that compares the **average book profit** generated per year against the **average book value of capital tied up** in the asset over its useful life.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Alignment:** In the context of Energy Management and Auditing, the ARR method is used to **financially appraise energy efficiency investments** — such as replacement of motors, retrofit of boilers, installation of VFDs, or thermal insulation upgrades — *before* capital is committed.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a farmer who invests **₹5,00,000** in a piece of land and a new tractor. Every year, the farm produces a *net profit* (income minus all expenses, including the slow wear-and-tear of the tractor, i.e., depreciation). 

Now, instead of asking *"How much total money will I make over 10 years?"* (NPV would do that), the farmer simply asks:

> **"On an average, every year, what percentage return am I earning on the money I have locked into this farm and tractor?"**

That is exactly the **Average Rate of Return** — a simple "yearly percentage yield" measured against the **average** capital sitting in the project (not the original amount, because the tractor is losing value every year).

For an **energy auditor**, this is useful because it converts a complex multi-year financial stream into **a single percentage number** that can be compared directly with the company's **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** or the bank interest rate.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Definitions)

| Term | KTU Board Definition |
|---|---|
| **Average Annual Profit (AAp)** | The arithmetic mean of the net accounting profits earned in each year of the project's life. |
| **Average Investment (AI)** | The mean of the initial cost and the salvage (terminal) value of the asset — i.e., the *average book value* over its life. |
| **MARR** | Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return — the hurdle rate below which the project is rejected. |
| **Salvage Value** | The estimated resale/scrap value of the equipment at the end of its useful life. |
| **Depreciation** | The non-cash annual allocation of the asset's cost over its useful life. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Think ARR = "**A**nnual **R**eturn on the **R**upee deployed" — it tells you how efficiently the invested rupee is *working for you every year*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The ARR method is implemented in a strict, sequential manner. Skipping any step leads to a wrong answer and loss of marks in the KTU valuation key.

**Step 1 — Identify the Initial Investment (I₀)**
This is the total capital outlay required to install/commission the energy-saving equipment (e.g., cost of new VFD + installation + commissioning).

**Step 2 — Identify the Salvage Value (SV)**
The expected market value of the equipment at the end of its useful life. If unspecified, assume **SV = 0** unless the problem statement says otherwise.

**Step 3 — Compute the Average Investment (AI)**

$$AI = \frac{I_0 + SV}{2}$$

This formula reflects the reality that the *book value* of the asset declines linearly from $I_0$ to $SV$ over the project life. Hence the *average capital tied up* is the midpoint.

**Step 4 — Compute the Average Annual Profit (AAp)**
The KTU board accepts two equivalent definitions. Pick the one matching the data given in the problem.

> **Definition A (Cash-Flow Based):**
$$AAp = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \text{Net Cash Flow}_t}{n} - \text{Annual Depreciation}$$

> **Definition B (Profit-Stream Based):**
$$AAp = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \text{Net Profit}_t}{n}$$

When the problem provides **net cash flows** (which is the common case in energy audit data), use Definition A — you must first subtract the annual depreciation charge to get a true *accounting profit*. If the problem provides **net profits directly**, use Definition B.

**Step 5 — Calculate ARR**

$$ARR \ (\text{in \%}) = \frac{AAp}{AI} \times 100$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Decision Rule**

| Condition | KTU Decision |
|---|---|
| ARR ≥ MARR (or required rate) | **ACCEPT** the project |
| ARR < MARR | **REJECT** the project |
| Mutually exclusive projects | **SELECT the project with the highest ARR** |

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Annual Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{I_0 - SV}{n}$ | ₹ / year | Straight-line method assumed |
| 2 | Average Investment | $AI = \dfrac{I_0 + SV}{2}$ | ₹ | If SV = 0, then $AI = I_0 / 2$ |
| 3 | Average Annual Profit (Cash-Flow) | $AAp = \dfrac{\sum CF_t}{n} - D$ | ₹ / year | Use when cash flows are given |
| 4 | Average Annual Profit (Direct) | $AAp = \dfrac{\sum P_t}{n}$ | ₹ / year | Use when profits are given |
| 5 | **ARR** | $ARR = \dfrac{AAp}{AI} \times 100$ | **%** | **The core KTU formula** |
| 6 | Decision Rule | $ARR \geq MARR \Rightarrow$ Accept | — | Compare with company hurdle rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Convention:** When the problem says *"no salvage value"*, set $SV = 0$ in Equation 2. This is the most commonly tested case and the most common place students lose marks by *forgetting* the division by 2.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **energy management practice**, the ARR method is one of the *quick-screening tools* used by Certified Energy Managers (CEMs) and auditors to:

- **Pre-screen multiple alternatives** (e.g., comparing LED vs. CFL vs. incandescent lighting retrofits) before a more rigorous NPV/IRR analysis.
- **Communicate with non-finance management** — the percentage format is universally understood, unlike discounted cash-flow metrics.
- **Justify small-ticket energy projects** (₹1–10 lakh range) where the cost of a full DCF analysis is not justified.
- **Benchmark against bank FD rates** — a project with ARR = 18% is attractive when a bank FD gives only 7%.

It is, however, **not** the preferred method for **large capital projects** (boilers, chillers, co-generation) where the time value of money is significant — for those, KTU Module 4 also covers **NPV, IRR, and Payback Period** methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Worked Example (Comprehensive, KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]:**
> A manufacturing company is evaluating an energy conservation project to install a **solar water heating system** for its process plant. The technical and financial details are as follows:
> - Initial Investment (I₀) = **₹6,00,000**
> - Useful Life (n) = **5 years**
> - Salvage Value (SV) = **₹1,00,000**
> - Expected Net Cash Savings (before depreciation):
>   - Year 1 → ₹1,80,000
>   - Year 2 → ₹2,00,000
>   - Year 3 → ₹2,20,000
>   - Year 4 → ₹1,90,000
>   - Year 5 → ₹1,50,000
> - Required Minimum Rate of Return (MARR) = **15%**
>
> **Evaluate the project using the Average Rate of Return (ARR) Method.**

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**STEP 1 — Compute Annual Straight-Line Depreciation (D)**

$$D = \frac{I_0 - SV}{n} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{5}$$

$$D = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5} = \text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}000 \ \text{per year}$$

> *Logic:* The asset loses ₹5,00,000 of book value evenly over 5 years, so ₹1,00,000 is expensed each year as depreciation.

---

**STEP 2 — Compute the Sum of Net Cash Savings**

$$\sum CF_t = 1{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}90{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$\sum CF_t = \text{₹ } 9{,}40{,}000$$

> *Logic:* Total cash benefit across the 5-year project horizon, before any depreciation charge.

---

**STEP 3 — Compute Average Annual Cash Flow (AACF)**

$$AACF = \frac{\sum CF_t}{n} = \frac{9{,}40{,}000}{5} = \text{₹ } 1{,}88{,}000 \ \text{per year}$$

> *Logic:* The arithmetic mean of yearly cash savings — represents a "smoothed" annual benefit.

---

**STEP 4 — Compute Average Annual Profit (AAp)**

$$AAp = AACF - D = 1{,}88{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 88{,}000 \ \text{per year}$$

> *Logic:* Accounting profit = Cash inflow − Non-cash depreciation charge. This is the figure that appears in the company's books, which is what ARR measures.

---

**STEP 5 — Compute Average Investment (AI)**

$$AI = \frac{I_0 + SV}{2} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{2}$$

$$AI = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{2} = \text{₹ } 3{,}50{,}000$$

> *Logic:* Book value starts at ₹6,00,000 (Year 0), declines linearly to ₹1,00,000 (Year 5). The midpoint is the *average capital blocked* in the project.

---

**STEP 6 — Compute the ARR**

$$ARR = \frac{AAp}{AI} \times 100 = \frac{88{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} \times 100$$

$$ARR = 0.2514 \times 100 = \mathbf{25.14\%}$$

---

**STEP 7 — Apply the Decision Rule**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computed ARR = 25.14%**
> **Required MARR = 15%**
> Since **25.14% > 15%**, the project **satisfies** the company's minimum acceptable return criterion.
>
> ✅ **DECISION: ACCEPT** the solar water heating project.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Multiple Variants Tested by KTU)

| Scenario | I₀ | SV | n | Given Data | $AI$ Formula | $AAp$ Formula | KTU Trap to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Case | ₹6,00,000 | ₹1,00,000 | 5 | Net Cash Flows | $(I_0+SV)/2$ | $(\sum CF / n) - D$ | Forgetting to subtract depreciation |
| Zero Salvage | ₹5,00,000 | 0 | 5 | Net Cash Flows | $I_0/2$ | $(\sum CF / n) - D$ | Setting $AI = I_0$ (common error) |
| Profits Given | ₹4,00,000 | ₹50,000 | 4 | Net Profits Directly | $(I_0+SV)/2$ | $\sum P / n$ | Subtracting depreciation twice |
| Two Projects | A: ₹5L, B: ₹8L | Given | 5 | Net Cash Flows | Both | Both | Picking the project with *lower* ARR (only valid for *mutually exclusive* projects) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When the problem gives "**net cash flows**", most students compute AAp = $\sum CF / n$ directly. **This is WRONG.** The ARR is defined on *accounting profit*, not cash flow. Always subtract the depreciation charge. **[-2 marks]**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ARR Methodology — Algorithmic Flowchart

The diagram below maps the **complete decision logic** of the ARR method as it is expected to be answered in a KTU valuation key.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Data Given] --> B[Step 1: Identify I0 and SV]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Annual Depreciation D = I0 - SV over n]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Sum of Cash Flows Sigma CF]
    D --> E[Step 4: Average Cash Flow = Sigma CF divided by n]
    E --> F[Step 5: Average Annual Profit AAp = ACF minus D]
    F --> G[Step 6: Average Investment AI = I0 + SV divided by 2]
    G --> H[Step 7: ARR percent = AAp divided by AI times 100]
    H --> I{Step 8: Is ARR greater than or equal to MARR?}
    I -- Yes --> J[ACCEPT the Project]
    I -- No --> K[REJECT the Project]
    J --> L[End: Decision Logged]
    K --> L
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block corresponds to one calculation step in the valuation. The diamond node (`I`) is the **single decision point** where the computed percentage is compared with the hurdle rate.

---

### 4.2 Concept-Block Architecture — ARR in Energy Project Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Energy Audit Findings] --> B[Identify Energy Conservation Measures]
    B --> C[Compile Cost Data: I0, SV, n]
    B --> D[Compile Savings Data: CF1 to CFn]
    C --> E[Average Rate of Return ARR Engine]
    D --> E
    E --> F{ARR compared to MARR}
    F -- Greater Equal --> G[Project APPROVED for Implementation]
    F -- Less --> H[Project DROPPED or Redesigned]
    G --> I[Post-Implementation M and V Verification]
    H --> J[Re-evaluate with Revised Inputs]
```

> **Interpretation:** The ARR engine is the **central financial filter** between engineering identification (left) and corporate capital release (right). This is exactly the role the KTU 2024 syllabus assigns to Module 4 methods in the overall energy management workflow.

---

### 4.3 Relationship Map — ARR vs. Other Capital Budgeting Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Classical Methods
        A1[Payback Period]
        A2[ARR Method]
    end
    subgraph Discounted Methods
        B1[Net Present Value NPV]
        B2[Internal Rate of Return IRR]
        B3[Benefit Cost Ratio BCR]
    end
    A2 -. Ignores Time Value of Money .-> A1
    A2 -. Upgrade to -> B1
    A2 -. Upgrade to -> B2
    A2 -. Upgrade to -> B3
```

> **Key takeaway for the student:** The ARR method occupies the **mid-tier** in the hierarchy of capital budgeting tools — more sophisticated than Payback Period (which ignores profitability entirely) but less rigorous than the discounted methods (NPV/IRR/BCR), which respect the time value of money.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Average Rate of Return (ARR) method. State its decision criterion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Average Rate of Return (ARR) method** is a capital budgeting technique that measures the profitability of an investment by expressing the **average annual accounting profit** as a percentage of the **average investment** over the project's useful life.
>
> **Mathematical Form:**
> $ARR = \dfrac{AAp}{AI} \times 100$, where $AAp$ is the average annual profit and $AI = (I_0 + SV)/2$.
>
> **Decision Criterion:** A project is **accepted** if its computed ARR is **greater than or equal to** the company's **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)**. Otherwise, it is rejected. For mutually exclusive alternatives, the project with the **highest ARR** is selected.
> *(Award 1 mark each for definition, formula, and decision rule.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three advantages and three limitations of the ARR method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Advantages:**
> 1. **Simplicity** — Easy to calculate and understand, even for non-finance managers.
> 2. **Uses accounting data** — Information is directly available from the company's profit & loss statement and balance sheet.
> 3. **Considers entire project life** — Unlike payback, it accounts for profits over the *full* useful life.
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. **Ignores time value of money** — Treats a rupee in Year 1 as equal to a rupee in Year n.
> 2. **Based on accounting profit, not cash flow** — Subject to non-cash adjustments and accounting policies.
> 3. **Ambiguous average investment formula** — Different textbooks use $(I_0+SV)/2$ or $I_0/2$, leading to inconsistent comparisons.
> *(Award 1 mark for each correctly listed advantage/limitation; up to 3 marks total.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[Choose this OR Question B]*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept, formula, and decision rules** of the Average Rate of Return method. Discuss **why** the average investment is taken as $(I_0 + SV)/2$ and not simply $I_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An energy management team is evaluating **two mutually exclusive projects** to upgrade the compressed air system of a plant:

| Parameter | Project X (VFD Retrofit) | Project Y (New Compressor) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (I₀) | ₹4,00,000 | ₹7,00,000 |
| Salvage Value (SV) | ₹50,000 | ₹1,00,000 |
| Useful Life (n) | 4 years | 4 years |
| Net Cash Savings (Yrs 1–4) | ₹1,40,000; ₹1,60,000; ₹1,50,000; ₹1,30,000 | ₹2,50,000; ₹2,80,000; ₹2,60,000; ₹2,10,000 |
| MARR | 18% | 18% |

**Compute the ARR for both projects and recommend which one to select.** **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks:**

> **Concept:** The ARR method, also called the **Accounting Rate of Return**, is a non-discounted profitability measure that expresses the average annual book profit (after depreciation) as a percentage of the average capital blocked in the asset.
>
> **Formula:**
> $ARR = \dfrac{AAp}{AI} \times 100$, where $AAp = \dfrac{\sum CF_t}{n} - D$ and $AI = \dfrac{I_0 + SV}{2}$.
>
> **Decision Rules:**
> (i) Independent project: Accept if $ARR \geq MARR$.
> (ii) Mutually exclusive projects: Select the one with the **higher ARR**.
>
> **Why $(I_0 + SV)/2$ and not $I_0$?**
> The asset's book value **depreciates** linearly over its life from $I_0$ to $SV$. Therefore, the *average* capital tied up across the project life is the **arithmetic mean of the opening and closing book values**, i.e., $(I_0 + SV)/2$. Using just $I_0$ would systematically *underestimate* the rate of return because part of the capital is recovered as the asset is depreciated.
> *(Award 2 marks concept + formula, 2 marks decision rules, 3 marks for the "why" justification.)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks:**

> **Project X — VFD Retrofit**
>
> **Step 1 — Depreciation:**
> $D_X = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{4} = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{4} = \text{₹ } 87{,}500$
>
> **Step 2 — Sum of Cash Savings:**
> $\sum CF_X = 1{,}40{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}30{,}000 = \text{₹ } 5{,}80{,}000$
>
> **Step 3 — Average Annual Profit:**
> $AAp_X = \dfrac{5{,}80{,}000}{4} - 87{,}500 = 1{,}45{,}000 - 87{,}500 = \text{₹ } 57{,}500$
>
> **Step 4 — Average Investment:**
> $AI_X = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000}{2} = \text{₹ } 2{,}25{,}000$
>
> **Step 5 — ARR:**
> $ARR_X = \dfrac{57{,}500}{2{,}25{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{25.56\%}$
>
> ---
>
> **Project Y — New Compressor**
>
> **Step 1 — Depreciation:**
> $D_Y = \dfrac{7{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{4} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{4} = \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000$
>
> **Step 2 — Sum of Cash Savings:**
> $\sum CF_Y = 2{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}10{,}000 = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000$
>
> **Step 3 — Average Annual Profit:**
> $AAp_Y = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{4} - 1{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}000$
>
> **Step 4 — Average Investment:**
> $AI_Y = \dfrac{7{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{2} = \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}000$
>
> **Step 5 — ARR:**
> $ARR_Y = \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{25.00\%}$
>
> ---
>
> **Decision:** Both projects exceed the MARR of 18%. However, since they are **mutually exclusive**, we select the one with the **higher ARR**.
> $ARR_X = 25.56\% \ > \ ARR_Y = 25.00\%$
>
> ✅ **Recommendation: Select Project X (VFD Retrofit).**
> *(Award 1 mark per correct Step 1–5 of each project, 1 mark for comparison and recommendation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative to Question A]*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **ARR method** with the **Payback Period method** for evaluating energy projects. Which is superior and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory is considering the installation of a **biomass gasifier** to replace its diesel furnace. The investment data is as follows:
- Initial Investment = ₹8,00,000
- Salvage Value = ₹1,00,000
- Useful Life = 5 years
- Annual Net Cash Savings (uniform) = ₹2,80,000 per year
- MARR = 20%

**Determine the ARR and advise the management.** **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Basis of Comparison | Payback Period | ARR Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Metric Measured** | Time to recover initial investment (years) | Profitability (% per year) |
| **Time Value of Money** | Ignored | Ignored |
| **Profitability** | Not measured (only liquidity) | Measured as a percentage |
| **Project Life Coverage** | Considers only payback period (often short) | Considers **entire useful life** |
| **Cash Flow vs. Profit** | Uses gross cash flows | Uses accounting profit (cash flow − depreciation) |
| **Decision Output** | Years (e.g., 3.2 years) | Percentage (e.g., 24%) |
| **Best Used For** | Quick screening of small, risky projects | Comparative ranking of competing projects |
| **Superiority in Energy Audit** | Inferior — ignores long-term gains | Superior — captures full-life profitability |

> **Conclusion:** The **ARR method is superior** for energy project evaluation because it accounts for the **entire economic life** of the equipment, captures **profitability (not just recovery speed)**, and produces a **percentage comparable with the cost of capital**. Payback is only useful as a *risk-screening filter* for cash-strapped firms.
> *(Award 5 marks for the table content and 2 marks for the justification paragraph.)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks:**

> **Step 1 — Annual Depreciation:**
> $D = \dfrac{I_0 - SV}{n} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{5} = \text{₹ } 1{,}40{,}000$
>
> **Step 2 — Average Annual Cash Flow (uniform stream):**
> $AACF = \text{₹ } 2{,}80{,}000$ (already uniform)
>
> **Step 3 — Average Annual Profit:**
> $AAp = AACF - D = 2{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}40{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}40{,}000$
>
> **Step 4 — Average Investment:**
> $AI = \dfrac{I_0 + SV}{2} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{2} = \text{₹ } 4{,}50{,}000$
>
> **Step 5 — ARR:**
> $ARR = \dfrac{1{,}40{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{31.11\%}$
>
> **Decision:** $ARR = 31.11\% \ > \ MARR = 20\%$
> ✅ **ACCEPT the biomass gasifier project.**
> *(Award 1 mark per correct step, 2 marks for the final decision.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting depreciation subtraction (2 marks lost)** — Most common error. ARR is on *profit*, not cash flow.
> 2. **Using $I_0$ instead of $(I_0+SV)/2$ (1 mark lost)** — The denominator *must* be the average investment.
> 3. **Wrong decision logic for mutually exclusive projects (1 mark lost)** — You must pick the **higher** ARR, not the "one that meets MARR".
> 4. **No final recommendation** — KTU requires an *explicit* "ACCEPT" or "REJECT" statement. **[-1 mark]**

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **ARR = (Average Annual Profit / Average Investment) × 100** — the single most important formula.
- ✅ **Average Investment = (Initial Cost + Salvage Value) / 2** — when SV is unspecified, assume zero.
- ✅ **Average Annual Profit** is *always* on an **accounting basis** — subtract straight-line depreciation from average cash flow.
- ✅ **Straight-line Depreciation** $D = (I_0 - SV) / n$ — the only depreciation method KTU tests.
- ✅ **Decision Rule (Independent):** Accept iff $ARR \geq MARR$.
- ✅ **Decision Rule (Mutually Exclusive):** Pick the **highest** ARR.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Simple, uses readily available data, considers full project life, easy to communicate.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Ignores time value of money, profit-based (not cash-based), ambiguous AI formula, no risk weighting.
- ✅ **Best Use Case:** Quick screening of energy conservation projects (motors, lighting, HVAC retrofits) before detailed NPV/IRR analysis.
- ✅ **Hierarchical Position:** More informative than Payback; less rigorous than NPV/IRR — occupy the **mid-tier** in capital budgeting.
- ✅ **Unit:** ARR is always expressed in **% per year** (not in rupees, not in years).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Rate of Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET742 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Internal Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is defined as the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of all future cash flows (both inflows and outflows) from an energy conservation project or capital investment equals **zero**. Mathematically, it is the value of the discount rate **r** that satisfies the fundamental capital recovery equation:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

In the specific context of **Energy Management and Auditing (EMA)**, the IRR represents the **effective annual percentage return** earned on the capital invested in an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM), purely from the perspective of energy cost savings. It is the break-even rate of return where the investor neither gains nor loses money on the energy project, in present-value terms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expected):** *"The Internal Rate of Return is the rate of return on the investment in an energy conservation project such that the net present value of all cash flows (energy savings and costs) equals zero. It is the rate that equates the present worth of energy cost savings to the present worth of capital expenditure."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Free Energy Loan" Intuition

Imagine a **Smart Meter** installed in your home at a cost of **₹10,000**. After installation, you save **₹2,500 per year** on your electricity bill.

Now ask the question: *"If a bank had given me the ₹10,000 as a personal loan to install the meter, what would be the interest rate of that loan that I am essentially paying back using my electricity savings?"*

The answer to that question is the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**.

- If your annual savings (₹2,500) can pay off a loan at **15% interest**, then your energy project yields a **15% IRR**.
- The "interest rate" here is *not* paid to anyone — it is the **return you are generating** on your own capital investment in energy efficiency.
- If your hurdle rate (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return) is 10%, and IRR is 15%, the project is profitable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** IRR is essentially the "interest rate" at which the energy project *self-finances* its own capital cost. The higher the IRR, the more financially attractive the energy project.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics (Highlighted for Quick Recall)

| Term | Symbol | Physical / Financial Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Net Present Value** | NPV | Today's value of all future cash flows discounted at a chosen rate **r** |
| **Internal Rate of Return** | IRR | The discount rate **r** that makes NPV = **0** |
| **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return** | **MARR** | The minimum required return (hurdle rate) for the project to be accepted |
| **Cash Flow** | $CF_t$ | Net energy savings minus operating costs in year **t** |
| **Project Life** | $n$ | Useful economic life of the ECM in years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Decision Rule (KTU 2024):**
> - **Accept** the energy project if **IRR > MARR**
> - **Reject** the energy project if **IRR < MARR**
> - **Indifferent** if **IRR = MARR**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Time Value of Money Logic

Energy projects involve a **trade-off** between two temporally separated cash streams:

1. **A single, large, upfront cash outflow** → Initial capital cost of the ECM (e.g., cost of a VFD, chiller, LED retrofit).
2. **A series of smaller, periodic cash inflows** → Annual energy cost savings over the equipment's life.

Because of the **time value of money**, a rupee today is worth more than a rupee received 5 years from now. Therefore, the energy savings in future years must be **discounted** back to the present to be compared with the initial investment.

The discount rate that exactly balances these two streams is the **IRR**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure to Determine IRR

The following structured logic is used to evaluate any energy project:

1. **Identify the cash flows** for each year of the project's life (initial cost and annual energy savings).
2. **Set up the NPV equation** as a function of the unknown discount rate **r**.
3. **Apply the IRR condition**: NPV(r) = 0.
4. **Solve for r** using:
   - **Trial-and-Error method** (manual iterative approach)
   - **Interpolation method** (linear approximation between two rates)
   - **Numerical / Spreadsheet methods** (Goal Seek, IRR function, Newton-Raphson)
5. **Compare** the resulting IRR with the **MARR** (often equal to the firm's cost of capital + risk premium, typically 10%–15% for energy projects).
6. **Accept or reject** the energy project based on the decision rule.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Explanation of the Interpolation Method

The interpolation method is the **most commonly tested** approach in KTU examinations because it is mathematically explicit and does not require software. It is based on the following reasoning:

- The NPV of a project is a **monotonically decreasing** function of the discount rate (higher discount → lower NPV).
- Therefore, there will always exist a **positive NPV at low rates** and a **negative NPV at high rates**.
- The IRR lies between these two rates, and is approximated by **linear interpolation** between them.

**The Interpolation Formula (KTU High-Yield):**

$$IRR = r_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1)$$

Where:
- $r_1$ = lower discount rate (giving positive NPV, $NPV_1$)
- $r_2$ = higher discount rate (giving negative NPV, $NPV_2$)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Engineer's Quick-Reference)

| Formula | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **NPV Function** | $NPV(r) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Compute NPV at any chosen rate **r** |
| **IRR Condition** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | The defining equation of IRR |
| **Interpolation (IRR)** | $IRR = r_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2}(r_2 - r_1)$ | Approximate IRR between $r_1$ and $r_2$ |
| **Present Worth Annuity Factor** | $P/A(r,n) = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ | PV of a uniform annual series |
| **Present Worth Factor** | $P/F(r,n) = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ | PV of a single future amount |
| **Decision Rule** | Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$ | Energy project profitability test |
| **Incremental IRR** | $IRR_\Delta$ on $\Delta Cash Flows$ | Compare mutually exclusive ECMs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The absolute value bars in NPV calculations are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. For example, $\vert NPV_1 \vert$ for magnitude.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IRR method is **indispensable** in industrial energy management for the following reasons:

- **Comparing competing ECMs:** A plant may have to choose between installing a VFD (low cost, moderate savings) or a new chiller (high cost, high savings). IRR provides a single comparable percentage.
- **Justifying capital budgets:** IRR is the language of CFOs and management. A statement like *"The LED retrofit yields 28% IRR"* is far more persuasive than a raw NPV figure.
- **Ranking energy projects:** When capital is limited, projects are ranked in descending order of IRR until the budget is exhausted.
- **Performance benchmarking:** Energy Service Companies (ESCOs) use IRR to assess the viability of Energy Performance Contracts (EPCs).
- **Regulatory and policy reporting:** Many government subsidies for energy efficiency (e.g., BEE programs) require IRR-based justification for incentives.

> [!NOTE]
> **MARR for Indian Energy Projects:** The MARR typically varies from **10% to 15%** depending on the firm's cost of capital. PSUs often use 12%, while private firms with higher borrowing costs may use 14%–15%. KTU exam problems commonly use **10%, 12%, or 15%** as the MARR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the IRR Equation from First Principles

### Step 1 — The General Present Worth Equation

The Net Present Value of a project with cash flow $CF_t$ in year $t$, evaluated at discount rate $r$, is the sum of the present values of all individual cash flows:

$$NPV(r) = \frac{CF_0}{(1+r)^0} + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+r)^n}$$

This is commonly written in compact summation form as:

$$NPV(r) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

### Step 2 — Applying the IRR Definition

By definition, IRR is the rate $r = IRR$ that forces NPV to zero. Substituting this into the equation:

$$NPV(IRR) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$$

This is the **fundamental defining equation** of IRR. It is a polynomial of degree **n** in $(1+IRR)$, and in general has **n roots**. For conventional cash flows (one sign change), there is exactly **one real positive root** — the IRR.

### Step 3 — Special Case: Uniform Annual Cash Flow

For the most common energy project structure, the initial investment is $I_0$ (negative, occurring at $t=0$) followed by a uniform annual savings of $A$ for $n$ years:

$$-I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

The summation is a standard present worth of annuity, which evaluates to:

$$-I_0 + A \cdot \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n} = 0$$

Letting $P/A(r,n) = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$:

$$I_0 = A \cdot \left(\frac{P}{A}, r, n\right)$$

Solving for $r$ (the IRR) requires either trial-and-error or interpolation, since the equation is **transcendental** and has no closed-form solution.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Interpolation Formula

The interpolation method is derived from the assumption that the NPV function is approximately **linear** in the region between $r_1$ and $r_2$. Using the two-point form of a straight line:

$$\frac{r - r_1}{r_2 - r_1} = \frac{NPV(r) - NPV_1}{NPV_2 - NPV_1}$$

At the IRR, $NPV(r) = NPV(IRR) = 0$. Substituting this into the equation above:

$$\frac{IRR - r_1}{r_2 - r_1} = \frac{0 - NPV_1}{NPV_2 - NPV_1}$$

Rearranging to isolate IRR:

$$IRR - r_1 = (r_2 - r_1) \cdot \frac{-NPV_1}{NPV_2 - NPV_1}$$

Since $NPV_1 > 0$ and $NPV_2 < 0$, the denominator $(NPV_2 - NPV_1)$ is negative, and the formula can be written in the standard KTU form:

$$IRR = r_1 + \left(\frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2}\right) \cdot (r_2 - r_1)$$

This is the **canonical interpolation formula** used universally in KTU solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy Caveat:** The interpolation method introduces a **linear approximation error** because the true NPV function is **convex (curved)**, not linear. To minimize this error, the two trial rates $r_1$ and $r_2$ should be **as close together as possible** (typically within 2%–3% of each other), and they must bracket the true IRR (i.e., $r_1 < IRR < r_2$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Kerala Industrial Plant Case)

**Problem Statement:** A textile mill in Kerala is considering the installation of a **condensate recovery system** to save energy. The details are as follows:

- Initial capital investment: **₹8,00,000**
- Annual energy cost savings: **₹2,40,000 per year** (uniform for 5 years)
- Salvage value at the end of year 5: **₹50,000**
- MARR: **15%**

**Determine the IRR using the interpolation method and advise whether the project should be accepted.**

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### **Step A: Define the Cash Flow Stream**

| Year (t) | Cash Flow $CF_t$ (₹) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-8,00,000$ | Initial investment |
| 1 | $+2,40,000$ | Year 1 energy savings |
| 2 | $+2,40,000$ | Year 2 energy savings |
| 3 | $+2,40,000$ | Year 3 energy savings |
| 4 | $+2,40,000$ | Year 4 energy savings |
| 5 | $+2,40,000 + 50,000 = +2,90,000$ | Year 5 savings + salvage |

#### **Step B: Trial at r₁ = 15%**

The NPV at 15% is calculated as:

$$NPV_1 = -8,00,000 + 2,40,000 \cdot (P/A, 15\%, 5) + 50,000 \cdot (P/F, 15\%, 5)$$

Computing the factors:

$$\left(\frac{P}{A}, 15\%, 5\right) = \frac{(1.15)^5 - 1}{0.15 \cdot (1.15)^5} = \frac{2.0114 - 1}{0.15 \cdot 2.0114} = \frac{1.0114}{0.3017} = 3.3522$$

$$\left(\frac{P}{F}, 15\%, 5\right) = \frac{1}{(1.15)^5} = \frac{1}{2.0114} = 0.4972$$

Substituting:

$$NPV_1 = -8,00,000 + 2,40,000 \cdot 3.3522 + 50,000 \cdot 0.4972$$

$$NPV_1 = -8,00,000 + 8,04,528 + 24,860$$

$$NPV_1 = +29,388 \quad (\text{positive})$$

#### **Step C: Trial at r₂ = 18%**

$$\left(\frac{P}{A}, 18\%, 5\right) = \frac{(1.18)^5 - 1}{0.18 \cdot (1.18)^5} = \frac{2.2878 - 1}{0.18 \cdot 2.2878} = \frac{1.2878}{0.4118} = 3.1272$$

$$\left(\frac{P}{F}, 18\%, 5\right) = \frac{1}{(1.18)^5} = \frac{1}{2.2878} = 0.4371$$

Substituting:

$$NPV_2 = -8,00,000 + 2,40,000 \cdot 3.1272 + 50,000 \cdot 0.4371$$

$$NPV_2 = -8,00,000 + 7,50,528 + 21,855$$

$$NPV_2 = -27,617 \quad (\text{negative})$$

#### **Step D: Apply the Interpolation Formula**

$$IRR = r_1 + \left(\frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2}\right) \cdot (r_2 - r_1)$$

$$IRR = 15\% + \left(\frac{29,388}{29,388 - (-27,617)}\right) \cdot (18\% - 15\%)$$

$$IRR = 15\% + \left(\frac{29,388}{57,005}\right) \cdot 3\%$$

$$IRR = 15\% + 0.5155 \cdot 3\%$$

$$IRR = 15\% + 1.547\%$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 16.55\%}$$

#### **Step E: Decision and Recommendation**

- Calculated IRR = **16.55%**
- MARR (Hurdle Rate) = **15.00%**
- Since $IRR > MARR$, the project is **financially viable** and should be **ACCEPTED**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Note:** A more accurate IRR could be obtained by repeating the process with a narrower bracket, e.g., $r_1 = 16\%$ and $r_2 = 17\%$. However, the linear interpolation result of 16.55% is sufficient for KTU board examinations and is well within the engineering accuracy required.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IRR Computation and Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **complete procedural architecture** for IRR evaluation of an energy conservation project, from cash flow identification to final project acceptance decision:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Energy Project Identified"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Initial Investment I0"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Estimate Annual Energy Savings A for n years"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Add Salvage Value SV at year n"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Construct Cash Flow Table CFt"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Select Lower Trial Rate r1"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Compute NPV1 at r1"]
    G --> H{"NPV1 Positive?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Step 7: Select Higher Trial Rate r2"]
    H -- "No" --> F
    I --> J["Step 8: Compute NPV2 at r2"]
    J --> K{"NPV2 Negative?"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Step 9: Apply Interpolation Formula"]
    K -- "No" --> I
    L --> M["Compute IRR Value"]
    M --> N{"Compare IRR with MARR"}
    N -- "IRR greater than MARR" --> O["ACCEPT Energy Project"]
    N -- "IRR less than MARR" --> P["REJECT Energy Project"]
    N -- "IRR equal to MARR" --> Q["INDIFFERENT Marginal Case"]
    O --> R["END: Project Approved for Implementation"]
    P --> S["END: Project Not Viable"]
    Q --> T["END: Re-evaluate Assumptions"]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) method. State the decision criterion for accepting or rejecting an energy conservation project based on IRR.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of all cash flows from a project equals **zero**. It represents the effective percentage return earned on the capital invested in an energy project, computed purely from energy cost savings.

The **decision criterion** is:
- **Accept** the project if **IRR > MARR** (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return)
- **Reject** the project if **IRR < MARR**
- **Indifferent** if **IRR = MARR**

Where MARR is the hurdle rate, typically equal to the firm's cost of capital plus a risk premium. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any four advantages and two limitations of the IRR method in energy project evaluation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. Considers the **time value of money** rigorously.
2. Provides a **single percentage** value, easy for management to interpret and compare across projects.
3. Does not require pre-knowledge of MARR for computation of IRR itself.
4. Suitable for **ranking mutually exclusive** energy projects of varying scales.

**Limitations:**
1. **Multiple IRRs** may arise for non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes), making interpretation ambiguous.
2. Computationally intensive for long-lived projects without a financial calculator or spreadsheet. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model - 14 Marks]**

A pharmaceutical company in Kochi is evaluating the installation of an **energy-efficient boiler** to replace its existing conventional boiler. The following data is available:

- Initial capital investment: **₹15,00,000**
- Annual fuel cost savings: **₹4,50,000 per year** (uniform for 6 years)
- Annual additional maintenance cost: **₹50,000 per year**
- Salvage value at the end of year 6: **₹1,00,000**
- MARR: **12%**

**(a)** Calculate the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) using the interpolation method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Advise whether the company should accept the project. If the MARR were 20%, would your recommendation change? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — IRR Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Construct the Net Annual Cash Flow**

The net annual cash inflow is the savings minus the additional maintenance cost:

$$A_{net} = 4,50,000 - 50,000 = 4,00,000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2: Cash Flow Summary**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | $-15,00,000$ |
| 1 to 5 | $+4,00,000$ each |
| 6 | $+4,00,000 + 1,00,000 = +5,00,000$ |

**Step 3: Trial at r₁ = 15%**

$$\left(\frac{P}{A}, 15\%, 6\right) = \frac{(1.15)^6 - 1}{0.15 \cdot (1.15)^6} = \frac{2.3131 - 1}{0.15 \cdot 2.3131} = \frac{1.3131}{0.3470} = 3.7845$$

$$\left(\frac{P}{F}, 15\%, 6\right) = \frac{1}{(1.15)^6} = 0.4323$$

$$NPV_1 = -15,00,000 + 4,00,000 \cdot 3.7845 + 1,00,000 \cdot 0.4323$$

$$NPV_1 = -15,00,000 + 15,13,800 + 43,230$$

$$NPV_1 = +57,030 \quad (\text{positive})$$
