```mermaid
        REP1[Reports ISO 50001 EnPI M and V]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUTS FOR ENERGY MANAGER"]
        KPI1[KPI SEC kWh per ton]
        ALM1[Real Time Alarms]
        CFG1[Automatic Demand Response]
        ESG1[Carbon Accounting ESG Report]
    end

    SM1 --> RTU1
    CT1 --> RTU1
    PT1 --> RTU1
    TH1 --> PLC1
    FL1 --> PLC1

    RTU1 --> PRT1

    EMS1 --> CFG1

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Sensors and Meters] --> B[RTU and PLC]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid
    D --> S
    E --> S
    P[Post Retrofit Energy Use E post] --> V[Savings Verification]
    S --> V
    V --> R[Verified Savings kWh or MJ]

    R --> ESC[ESCo Performance Contract Billing]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the three layers of a Computer Aided Energy Management System and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A Computer Aided EMS has three layers:

1. **Field Instrumentation Layer:** Comprises smart energy meters, CTs/PTs, temperature, flow, and pressure sensors. It physically measures the energy and process variables.

2. **Communication & Data Acquisition Layer:** Consists of RTUs, PLCs, data loggers, edge gateways, and protocols (Modbus, BACnet, MQTT). It transmits the measured data to the central system.

3. **Application & Analytics Layer:** Includes SCADA, EMS software, historian, and cloud analytics. It performs real-time visualization, control, analysis, and reporting.

(1 mark for each layer + 1 mark for examples)

### **Question 1.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between SCADA and EMIS in an Energy Management System?**

**Model Answer:**

- **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):** A real-time system used for **monitoring and control** of industrial processes. It can send control commands back to field devices (e.g., switching a pump ON/OFF).

- **EMIS (Energy Management Information System):** A **read-only or analytical** system used by energy managers. It focuses on **data analysis, KPI generation, EnPI tracking, and reporting** (ISO 50001, M&V). It typically does not control equipment.

Key difference: SCADA = **real-time control**, EMIS = **analytical reporting**.

(2 marks comparison + 1 mark example)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module 4 Internal Choice)

### **Question 2A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of a Computer Aided Energy Management System** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of SCADA, RTU, PLC, and historian in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory's smart meter logs 15-minute average demand for 30 days. The sum of all 15-min interval powers is 3,60,000 kW. The maximum recorded 15-min demand is 650 kW. Calculate:
  (i) Total energy consumed in the month (kWh)
  (ii) Average load (kW)
  (iii) Load Factor (%) **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The architecture has three tiers (refer Section 4.1 diagram):

**Tier 1 – Field Layer (2 marks):**
- Smart energy meters (Class 0.5/1.0 accuracy)
- CTs, PTs for current/voltage sensing
- Process sensors: temperature, pressure, flow
- These devices output 4-20 mA / 0-10 V / digital pulse signals.

**Tier 2 – Communication Layer (2 marks):**
- **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):** Used in geographically spread facilities (e.g., wind farms, substations). It polls field devices and transmits data to the central server via GSM/4G/radio.
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Used in process plants for fast, deterministic control. Performs local logic (e.g., trip logic for a motor).
- **Protocols:** Modbus RTU/TCP, BACnet (HVAC), DNP3 (utilities), MQTT (IoT).
- **Edge Gateways:** Aggregate data from multiple meters, perform local processing, push to cloud.

**Tier 3 – Application Layer (3 marks):**
- **SCADA:** Real-time HMI for operators, alarms, and supervisory control. MTU/Server runs SCADA software (e.g., Wonderware, WinCC, Ignition).
- **Historian:** Specialized time-series database (e.g., OSIsoft PI, Wonderware Historian) that stores years of data efficiently.
- **EMS Software:** Dashboards, KPI calculations, EnPI tracking, reports.
- **Analytics:** ML-based forecasting, anomaly detection, energy benchmarking.
- **Integration with MES/ERP:** For production-based energy KPIs and sustainability reporting.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $\Delta t = 15$ min $= 0.25$ hr
- $N = 30 \times 24 \times 4 = 2880$ intervals
- $\sum P_i = 3,60,000$ kW
- $MD = 650$ kW

**(i) Total energy (3 marks):**

$$E_{total} = \Delta t \times \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$$

$$E_{total} = 0.25 \times 3,60,000 = 90,000 \text{ kWh}$$

**(ii) Average load (2 marks):**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{total}}{T_{total}} = \frac{90,000}{30 \times 24} = \frac{90,000}{720} = 125 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Load Factor (2 marks):**

$$LF = \frac{P_{avg}}{MD} = \frac{125}{650} = 0.1923 = 19.23\%$$

**Interpretation:** A load factor of 19.23% indicates significant scope for load shifting, peak shaving, and demand-side management using the EMS.

---

### **Question 2B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4 Choice 2]`

**(a)** What is **IPMVP**? Explain its **four measurement options (A, B, C, D)** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit installs a **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** on a 75 kW motor to save energy. The IPMVP Option A protocol is applied. Data collected for one year:
  - Baseline annual energy: 4,50,000 kWh
  - Post-retrofit annual energy: 3,75,000 kWh
  - Operating hours post-retrofit: 5,800 hrs (baseline: 6,000 hrs)
  - Weather adjustment: -1.5%
  - Production adjustment: +2.0%

Compute the **IPMVP-verified annual savings** in kWh. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** is an M&V protocol developed by **EVO (Efficiency Valuation Organization)** that provides standardized methods for quantifying the savings from energy efficiency projects. **(1 mark)**

**Four Options (6 marks):**

| Option | Name | Measurement Boundary | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Retrofit Isolation – Key Parameter | Energy of one equipment; key parameter measured, others assumed constant | Lighting retrofit – measure kWh; assume light output constant |
| **B** | Retrofit Isolation – All Parameters | Energy of one equipment; all parameters measured | Boiler retrofit – measure fuel flow, NCV, return temperature |
| **C** | Whole Facility | Total energy of the entire facility | Multiple retrofits at one plant – measure main meter |
| **D** | Calibrated Simulation | Energy use from a computer simulation model | New construction – no historical data available |

The choice depends on project complexity, cost, and accuracy requirements. Option A is the cheapest; Option C captures interactive effects but is costlier.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $E_{base} = 4,50,000$ kWh
- $E_{post} = 3,75,000$ kWh
- $H_{base} = 6,000$ hr; $H_{post} = 5,800$ hr
- Weather adjustment $=-1.5\%$
- Production adjustment $=+2.0\%$

**Step 1: Raw Savings (1 mark)**

$$Savings_{raw} = E_{base} - E_{post} = 4,50,000 - 3,75,000 = 75,000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2: Operating Hours Normalization (2 marks)**

The motor ran 200 fewer hours after retrofit. So baseline energy must be adjusted:

$$E_{base,adj} = E_{base} \times \frac{H_{post}}{H_{base}} = 4,50,000 \times \frac{5,800}{6,000}$$

$$E_{base,adj} = 4,50,000 \times 0.9667 = 4,35,000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3: Apply weather and production adjustments (2 marks)**

$$\Delta E_{weather} = -1.5\% \text{ of } E_{base,adj} = -0.015 \times 4,35,000 = -6,525 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\Delta E_{production} = +2.0\% \text{ of } E_{base,adj} = +0.02 \times 4,35,000 = +8,700 \text{ kWh}$$

Net adjustment:

$$\Delta E_{total} = -6,525 + 8,700 = +2,175 \text{ kWh}$$

This means the corrected baseline consumes **less** because the weather was warmer (reducing HVAC load) but **more** because production increased.

Wait — careful. The sign convention: in IPMVP, adjustments convert the baseline to "what it would have been under post-retrofit conditions."

**Step 4: Adjusted Baseline (1 mark)**

$$E_{base,adj,total} = 4,35,000 + 2,175 = 4,37,175 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5: Verified Savings (1 mark)**

$$Savings_{IPMVP} = E_{base,adj,total} - E_{post} = 4,37,175 - 3,75,000 = 62,175 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Interpretation:** The VFD saves **62,175 kWh/year**, which at ₹8/kWh = **₹4,97,400/year** in cost savings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the hours normalization** in IPMVP Option A/B — students often compute raw savings without adjusting baseline for different operating hours. **Loses 2-3 marks.**
> 2. **Sign errors in adjustments:** A "weather adjustment of -1.5%" means subtract 1.5%, not that the savings are negative. Always write: $E_{base,adj} = E_{base} \times (1 + \text{adj}\%)$.
> 3. **Mixing up SCADA and EMIS** in definitions — they are NOT the same. SCADA is for control; EMIS is for analysis.
> 4. **Confusing RTU and PLC:** RTU = remote telemetry (substations, wind farms); PLC = local industrial control. They are NOT interchangeable.
> 5. **Forgetting units in Load Factor calculation:** Always state the final answer in **%**, not just a decimal.
> 6. **IPMVP Option D** is often missed by students — remember it is the only option for **new construction** with no historical data.
> 7. **Historian is not SCADA:** Historian is a database; SCADA is the visualization & control software. They work together but are distinct components.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EMS = Field + Communication + Application** (three layers) — remember the mnemonic **"Field Cars Always Move"**.
- **SCADA = real-time control; EMIS = analytical reporting.**
- **RTU** is for remote sites; **PLC** is for local process control.
- **Historian** stores time-series data for years; it is the memory of the EMS.
- **Smart meters** are the eyes and ears of an EMS; they measure kWh, kVAh, kVArh, MD, PF.
- **Protocols:** Modbus (industrial), BACnet (buildings), DNP3 (utilities), MQTT (IoT/cloud).
- **Load Factor** is the most-used EMS KPI: $LF = P_{avg} / P_{max}$.
- **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption)** is a key EnPI: $SEC = E / Production$.
- **IPMVP has 4 options:** A (key param), B (all param), C (whole facility), D (calibrated simulation).
- **CUSUM** detects drift in energy data when $\vert S_k \vert > 4\sigma$ — useful for early fault detection.
- **KSEB billing uses 15-min block MD** — a 15-minute spike in demand can cost lakhs of rupees per month.
- **ISO 50001** requires continuous EnPI tracking — only an EMS can deliver this at scale.
- **Always state the unit** (kWh, MJ, %, tCO₂) in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit omission.
- **M&V is a legally binding protocol** for ESCo projects; you MUST apply the adjustments.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for EMS:** **"Smart CTs PTs sense; RTUs PLCs transmit; SCADA historian stores; EMIS reports."**
